topics.title: Biomass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biomass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomass** is any organic, non-fossil matter of biological origin, derived from living or recently living organisms (plants, animals, and their by-products/wastes), which contains stored chemical energy (primarily in the form of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen) and can be converted into useful energy carriers such as heat, electricity, solid fuels, liquid fuels (bioethanol, biodiesel), or gaseous fuels (biogas, syngas).

In fuel engineering, biomass is categorized as a **renewable, carbon-neutral** solid/liquid/gaseous fuel. Its energy content originates from the photosynthetic conversion of solar radiation into chemical energy, represented by the simplified reaction:

$$6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight + Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$$

## 1.2 Chemical Composition of Biomass

| Component | Formula/Identity | Mass Fraction (dry basis) | Energy Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cellulose** | $(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n$ | 40 – 60 % | Primary |
| **Hemicellulose** | $(C_5H_8O_4)_m$ | 20 – 40 % | Secondary |
| **Lignin** | Complex aromatic polymer | 10 – 25 % | High (HHV up to **25 MJ/kg**) |
| **Extractives** | Sugars, starches, oils, resins | 1 – 15 % | Variable |
| **Ash (Inorganics)** | K, Ca, Si, Mg oxides | 1 – 10 % | None (penalty) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Bank Account of Nature"**
> Think of a tree as a *savings account* in a "solar bank." Every day, sunlight (deposit), CO₂ (raw material), and water (solvent) are processed by the tree's leaves to manufacture high-energy molecules like cellulose and sugars. This stored "wealth" can be "withdrawn" (released) by burning the wood, digesting it in a biogas plant, or fermenting sugarcane juice. The key difference from a coal "savings account" is that the carbon released is reabsorbed by new plants, closing the **CO₂ cycle** in years (not millennia) — hence, **carbon neutral**.

## 1.4 Classification of Biomass (KTU High-Yield Taxonomy)

1. **Woody Biomass** – Forest residues, sawdust, wood chips, bark, pellets.
2. **Herbaceous Biomass** – Grasses, straws (rice/wheat), bagasse, alfalfa.
3. **Aquatic Biomass** – Microalgae, macroalgae (seaweed), water hyacinth.
4. **Agricultural Residues** – Rice husk, coconut shell, groundnut shell, corn stover.
5. **Animal & Municipal Wastes** – Cattle dung, poultry litter, sewage sludge, MSW organic fraction.
6. **Energy Crops** – Dedicated plantations of poplar, eucalyptus, jatropha, sugarcane.

## 1.5 Why Biomass Matters in the 2024 KTU Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Kerala's agricultural economy (coconut, rice, areca) generates **>10 million tonnes/year** of biomass residues. Understanding their fuel value, conversion pathways, and energy auditing is a direct industrial application aligned with KTU Module 1 syllabus outcomes: *CO1 – Characterize solid, liquid, and gaseous fuels.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fuel Characterization of Biomass (Operational Breakdown)

Biomass evaluation follows the same **two-pillar analysis** used for coal:

### A. Proximate Analysis (Engineering Composition)
Determines the *physical/operational* fractions of biomass:
- **Moisture Content (M, %)** – Reduces CV; adds drying cost.
- **Volatile Matter (VM, %)** – Gases released on heating to **950 °C** in inert atmosphere. High VM = easy ignition, smoky flame.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC, %)** – Solid combustible residue after VM release; high FC = high char yield.
- **Ash Content (A, %)** – Non-combustible mineral residue; causes slagging, fouling, and erosion.

**Closure Check:** $M + VM + FC + A = 100\%$

### B. Ultimate Analysis (Elemental Composition)
Determines the *chemical elements*: **C, H, O, N, S** (and sometimes Cl).
- Reported on **dry, ash-free (daf)** basis for comparison.
- High **O/C** ratio → Low CV (oxygen is "inert diluent" in combustion).
- High **N, S** → Pollutant emissions (NOₓ, SO₂).

## 2.2 Calorific Value (CV) — The Energy Currency

The **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** and **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** are fundamental:

- **HHV (or GCV)** – Heat released assuming all water vapor in flue gas condenses to liquid (latent heat recovered).
- **LHV (or NCV)** – Heat released when water leaves as vapor (typical for hot exhaust gases in industrial furnaces).

$$LHV = HHV - h_{fg} \cdot \left( \frac{9H}{100} + \frac{M}{100} \right) \quad \text{[MJ/kg]}$$

Where:
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water = **2.442 MJ/kg** (at 25 °C)
- $H$ = Hydrogen mass percentage (ultimate analysis)
- $M$ = Moisture mass percentage

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **9H/100** arises because combustion of 1 kg of hydrogen produces **9 kg of water**: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \Rightarrow (2 \text{ kg } H_2) \to (18 \text{ kg } H_2O)$, so per kg of H → 9 kg of H₂O.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Use `vert` for Absolute Value Symbols)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dulong's HHV (Biomass) | $HHV = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$ | MJ/kg | Predict CV from ultimate analysis |
| 2 | LHV from HHV | $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \left[ \tfrac{9H + M}{100} \right]$ | MJ/kg | Industrial furnace design |
| 3 | Stoichiometric O₂ demand | $O_2^{min} = \tfrac{32}{12}C + \tfrac{32}{4}\left(\tfrac{H}{2}\right) + \tfrac{32}{32}S - \tfrac{O}{100}$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel | Combustion calculation |
| 4 | Stoichiometric Air | $A_{st} = \dfrac{O_2^{min}}{0.232} = 4.31 \cdot O_2^{min}$ | kg air / kg fuel | Burner / boiler sizing |
| 5 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $AFR = \dfrac{\text{Mass of air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}}$ | – | Combustion efficiency |
| 6 | Theoretical CO₂ (max) | $CO_2^{max} = \dfrac{44}{12} \cdot C \cdot \dfrac{1}{AFR + 1}$ | vol. fraction | Flue gas analysis |
| 7 | Energy density comparison | $\text{Specific energy} = \dfrac{LHV}{\rho}$ | MJ/m³ | Storage / transport |
| 8 | Carbon neutrality index | $CN = \dfrac{CO_2^{\text{absorbed (growth)}}}{CO_2^{\text{emitted (combustion)}}}$ | dimensionless | Sustainability check |
| 9 | Moisture heating load | $Q_{dry} = M \cdot (c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T + h_{fg})$ | kJ/kg fuel | Pre-drying energy audit |
| 10 | Biogas yield (BMP) | $Y_{CH_4} = V_{CH_4} \cdot \dfrac{p \cdot M_{CH_4}}{R \cdot T}$ | kg CH₄/kg VS | Anaerobic digester sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 2.442 MJ/kg?** At 25 °C, the enthalpy of vaporization of water is **2442 kJ/kg**. This is the "penalty" subtracted from HHV every time the products leave above 100 °C (i.e., nearly always in real plants).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants**: 1 tonne of dry bagasse ≈ **1.8 MWh** of electricity (sugar mill cogeneration in Kerala).
- **Domestic**: Biogas from 1 m³ digester displaces ~**1.5 LPG cylinders/year** for a rural family.
- **Transport**: 1 tonne of biodiesel (from waste cooking oil) replaces ~**0.95 tonne of fossil diesel**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio from Ultimate Analysis

**Starting Premise:** Complete combustion of a fuel with mass fractions (per kg):
- C → CO₂ : $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ (1 mole C consumes 1 mole O₂ → 32/12 kg O₂ per kg C)
- H₂ → H₂O : $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$ (per kg H₂ consumes 8 kg O₂; per kg H consumes 8 kg O₂)
- S → SO₂ : $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$ (32/32 kg O₂ per kg S)
- O → reduces O₂ demand (the fuel's own oxygen is "internal")

**Step 1 — O₂ consumed by each element (per kg of fuel, mass fractions as decimals):**

$$O_2^{C} = \frac{32}{12} \cdot C = 2.6667 \, C$$

$$O_2^{H} = \frac{32}{4} \cdot H = 8 \, H$$

$$O_2^{S} = \frac{32}{32} \cdot S = 1 \, S$$

**Step 2 — Internal O₂ credit (oxygen already in fuel):**

$$O_2^{available} = \frac{32}{32} \cdot O = 1 \cdot O$$

**Step 3 — Net O₂ required:**

$$O_2^{min} = 2.6667C + 8H + 1S - O \quad \text{[kg O}_2 \text{ / kg fuel]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert O₂ demand to Air demand (dry air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass):**

$$A_{st} = \frac{O_2^{min}}{0.232} = 4.31 \cdot O_2^{min} \quad \text{[kg air / kg fuel]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 23.2 %?** Standard dry atmospheric air by mass = 75.5 % N₂ + 23.2 % O₂ + 1.3 % Ar. We exclude Ar from O₂ calculation and assume N₂ passes through as inert.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Model 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A biomass fuel has the following ultimate analysis (mass %, as-received basis):
- C = 42.0 %, H = 5.5 %, O = 38.0 %, N = 1.5 %, S = 0.2 %, Ash = 3.0 %, Moisture = 9.8 %

**Find:** (a) HHV using Dulong's formula, (b) LHV, (c) Stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel, (d) Mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel.

### Solution

**Part (a) — HHV using Dulong:**

$$HHV = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$$

Substituting values (C = 42.0, H = 5.5, S = 0.2, O = 38.0, N = 1.5, A = 3.0):

$$\begin{aligned}
HHV &= (0.3491 \times 42.0) + (1.1783 \times 5.5) + (0.1005 \times 0.2) \\
&\quad - (0.1034 \times 38.0) - (0.0151 \times 1.5) - (0.0211 \times 3.0) \\[4pt]
&= 14.6622 + 6.4807 + 0.0201 - 3.9292 - 0.0227 - 0.0633 \\[4pt]
&= 17.148 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — LHV:**

$$LHV = HHV - 2.442 \left[ \frac{9H + M}{100} \right]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
LHV &= 17.148 - 2.442 \left[ \frac{(9 \times 5.5) + 9.8}{100} \right] \\[4pt]
&= 17.148 - 2.442 \times \left[ \frac{49.5 + 9.8}{100} \right] \\[4pt]
&= 17.148 - 2.442 \times 0.593 \\[4pt]
&= 17.148 - 1.448 = 15.700 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c) — Stoichiometric Air:**

$$\begin{aligned}
O_2^{min} &= 2.6667C + 8H + 1S - O \quad \text{(using percentages/100)} \\
&= (2.6667 \times 0.42) + (8 \times 0.055) + (1 \times 0.002) - 0.38 \\
&= 1.1200 + 0.4400 + 0.0020 - 0.3800 \\
&= 1.1820 \;\text{kg O}_2 \text{/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

$$A_{st} = \frac{1.1820}{0.232} = 5.095 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Part (d) — Mass of dry flue gas per kg fuel:**

Dry flue gas = N₂ from air + CO₂ from C + SO₂ from S

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{N_2} &= A_{st} \times 0.768 = 5.095 \times 0.768 = 3.913 \;\text{kg} \\
m_{CO_2} &= \frac{44}{12} \times C = 3.667 \times 0.42 = 1.540 \;\text{kg} \\
m_{SO_2} &= \frac{64}{32} \times S = 2.0 \times 0.002 = 0.004 \;\text{kg} \\[4pt]
m_{flue,\,dry} &= 3.913 + 1.540 + 0.004 = 5.457 \;\text{kg/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check**: $m_{air} + m_{fuel,\,dry} = m_{flue}$ → $5.095 + 0.902 = 5.997$ kg (≈ 5.457 + 0.540 water vapor). ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Biomass vs Fossil Fuels

| Property | Wood (dry) | Bagasse (dry) | Coal (Bituminous) | Natural Gas (CH₄) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| HHV (MJ/kg) | 18 – 21 | 17 – 20 | 24 – 35 | 50 – 55 |
| Volatile Matter (%) | 70 – 85 | 75 – 80 | 15 – 35 | (Gas) |
| Fixed Carbon (%) | 15 – 25 | 10 – 20 | 45 – 70 | (Gas) |
| Ash (%) | 0.5 – 5 | 1 – 8 | 5 – 15 | 0 |
| Moisture (as received) | 20 – 60 | 40 – 50 | 5 – 15 | – |
| O/C (atomic) | 0.7 – 0.9 | 0.8 – 1.0 | 0.05 – 0.2 | 0 |
| CO₂ footprint (kg/GJ) | 0 (net) | 0 (net) | 90 – 100 | 50 – 60 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Biomass has *much higher volatile matter* than coal, making it easier to ignite but also creating challenges in flame stability and tar formation during gasification.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    DIG --> ST1[Stage 1: Hydrolysis]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define biomass. List any four types of biomass with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
Biomass is defined as any biodegradable organic material derived from plants, animals, or their by-products that can be used as a source of energy through combustion, biochemical, or thermochemical conversion. It is considered a **renewable** and **carbon-neutral** fuel.

Four types with examples:
1. **Woody biomass** – Sawdust from rubber plantations.
2. **Herbaceous biomass** – Rice straw from paddy fields.
3. **Agricultural residue** – Coconut shell from coir industry.
4. **Animal waste** – Cattle dung from dairy farms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner tip:** Use precise definitions; do not confuse biomass with biogas.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of a fuel. Why is LHV used in industrial furnace design?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | HHV (GCV) | LHV (NCV) |
|---|---|---|
| Water in products | Condensed to liquid | Remains as vapor |
| Heat recovered | Includes latent heat of condensation | Excludes latent heat |
| Magnitude | HHV > LHV | LHV < HHV |
| Relation | $HHV = LHV + h_{fg}\left(\frac{9H+M}{100}\right)$ | – |

**LHV is used in industrial practice** because the exhaust gases of furnaces and boilers leave the system at temperatures well above **100 °C**, so the latent heat of condensation of water vapor is **not recovered** under real operating conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

#### **Q.A(a) [7 Marks] — Classify biomass in detail and explain the proximate and ultimate analysis of biomass with significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Biomass (3 marks):**
Biomass can be classified into four major categories:
- **Natural biomass** – Forest growth, grasslands (unmanaged).
- **Residue biomass** – Agricultural residues (rice husk, bagasse, coconut shell), forestry residues, agro-industrial by-products.
- **Energy crops** – Dedicated plantations like *Jatropha* (oil), *Saccharum* (sugar for ethanol), *Pennisetum* (miscanthus grass).
- **Waste biomass** – Municipal solid waste (organic fraction), animal manure, sewage sludge, food processing waste.

**Proximate Analysis (2 marks):**
Proximate analysis determines the **operational** composition in four fractions:
- **Moisture (M)**: Determined by oven drying at 105 °C; affects handling, storage, and net CV.
- **Volatile Matter (VM)**: Released at 950 °C in inert atmosphere; high VM → easy ignition, high flame.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC)**: Solid combustible residue; calculated by difference.
- **Ash (A)**: Non-combustible mineral residue; causes slagging, fouling, and disposal issues.

**Ultimate Analysis (2 marks):**
Reports elemental composition: **C, H, O, N, S (and sometimes Cl)**. Significance:
- Determines stoichiometric air and combustion products.
- High N → NOₓ emissions; High S → SO₂, acid rain.
- Oxygen content inversely proportional to calorific value.
- Used as input to **Dulong's formula** for CV prediction.

---

#### **Q.A(b) [7 Marks] — A biomass sample has the following ultimate analysis (mass % dry basis): C = 48, H = 6, O = 42, N = 1.5, S = 0.5, Ash = 2. If the moisture content is 8 %, calculate (i) HHV using Dulong's formula, (ii) LHV, and (iii) Stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) HHV by Dulong's Formula:**

$$HHV = 0.3491(48) + 1.1783(6) + 0.1005(0.5) - 0.1034(42) - 0.0151(1.5) - 0.0211(2)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
HHV &= 16.7568 + 7.0698 + 0.0503 - 4.3428 - 0.0227 - 0.0422 \\
&= 19.469 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) LHV:**

$$LHV = 19.469 - 2.442 \left[ \frac{9 \times 6 + 8}{100} \right] = 19.469 - 2.442 \times 0.62 = 17.955 \;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Stoichiometric Air:**

$$O_2^{min} = 2.6667 \times 0.48 + 8 \times 0.06 + 0.005 - 0.42 = 1.280 + 0.480 + 0.005 - 0.420 = 1.345 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$A_{st} = \frac{1.345}{0.232} = 5.80 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

#### **Q.B(a) [7 Marks] — Explain the various thermochemical and biochemical conversion routes of biomass with neat block diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Thermochemical Conversion Routes (4 marks):**
- **Direct Combustion:** Oldest and most common. Biomass burnt in excess air at 800 – 1200 °C in fixed-bed, fluidized-bed, or spreader-stoker furnaces. Produces heat → steam → electricity. Efficiency: 25 – 40 %.
- **Pyrolysis:** Thermal decomposition in *absence of air* at 400 – 800 °C. Products: biochar (solid), bio-oil (liquid), pyrolysis gas. *Fast pyrolysis* maximizes bio-oil (up to 75 % wt).
- **Gasification:** Partial oxidation with limited air/oxygen/steam at 800 – 1200 °C. Produces **syngas** (CO + H₂) for engines, turbines, or Fischer–Tropsch synthesis.
- **Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL):** Uses hot compressed water (300 – 350 °C, 10 – 20 MPa) to convert wet biomass (algae, sludge) directly into **biocrude**, bypassing drying.

**2. Biochemical Conversion Routes (3 marks):**
- **Anaerobic Digestion (AD):** Microbial breakdown in absence of O₂ at 35 – 55 °C (mesophilic/thermophilic). Stages: hydrolysis → acidogenesis → acetogenesis → methanogenesis. Output: **biogas** (50 – 70 % CH₄) + nutrient-rich digestate.
- **Fermentation:** Sugar/starch-rich biomass (sugarcane, molasses, corn) → bioethanol via *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*. Two-step: hydrolysis of polysaccharides to sugars, then yeast fermentation to ethanol (6 – 12 % v/v), followed by distillation and dehydration.
- **Enzymatic Hydrolysis + Fermentation:** Cellulosic biomass pre-treated and hydrolyzed by cellulase enzymes to glucose, then fermented to ethanol (2G ethanol).

---

#### **Q.B(b) [7 Marks] — Describe the process of biogas production through anaerobic digestion. What are the major constituents of biogas and its applications?**

**Model Answer:**

**Process (5 marks):**
Anaerobic digestion (AD) is the microbial decomposition of organic matter in the *absence of oxygen* inside a sealed, stirred reactor called a **digester**, maintained at mesophilic (35 °C) or thermophilic (55 °C) temperature and near-neutral pH (6.8 – 7.5). The biochemical pathway proceeds in **four sequential stages**:

1. **Hydrolysis:** Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down by extracellular enzymes into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids).
2. **Acidogenesis:** Acidogenic bacteria convert these monomers into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂.
3. **Acetogenesis:** VFAs and alcohols are further oxidized to **acetic acid**, H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria.
4. **Methanogenesis:** *Methanogenic archaea* convert acetate and H₂/CO₂ into **methane (CH₄)** and CO₂.

Overall simplified reaction:
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$$

**Typical retention time:** 20 – 40 days; **biogas yield:** 0.2 – 0.5 m³/kg VS (volatile solids).

**Composition of Biogas (1 mark):**
- CH₄: 50 – 70 %
- CO₂: 30 – 40 %
- H₂S: 0.1 – 0.5 % (must be removed before engine use)
- N₂, H₂O, NH₃: traces

**Applications (1 mark):**
Domestic cooking, lighting, electricity generation (gas engines/turbines), dual-fuel diesel engines, vehicle fuel (after CO₂/H₂S scrubbing), and slurry as **organic fertilizer**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing HHV with LHV** without specifying reference state. Always state "HHV at 25 °C" or "LHV at 25 °C".
> 2. **Using percentages directly** in stoichiometric air formula instead of converting to decimal mass fractions (e.g., 42 % → 0.42). This alone causes 1–2 mark deductions.
> 3. **Forgetting ash credit in Dulong's formula** (coefficient −0.0211A). Students often omit ash.
> 4. **Mixing up anaerobic digestion stages** — note that hydrolysis is *first*, not methanogenesis.
> 5. **Writing "biogas = natural gas"** — wrong; they differ in composition (biogas has CO₂, H₂S).
> 6. **Forgetting the 9H/100 term** in LHV derivation. The "9" comes from the 18:2 mass ratio of H₂O:H₂.
> 7. **Skipping unit consistency** in numerical problems (MJ/kg vs kJ/kg vs kcal/kg).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Points to Memorize for KTU ESE**

- ✅ **Biomass** = biodegradable organic matter; **renewable + carbon-neutral** (CO₂ released on burning is reabsorbed by regrowth).
- ✅ **Three primary structural components:** Cellulose (40–60 %), Hemicellulose (20–40 %), Lignin (10–25 %).
- ✅ **Proximate analysis** = M + VM + FC + A = 100 %; **Ultimate** = C, H, O, N, S, (Cl).
- ✅ **Dulong's formula coefficients** (in MJ/kg): C→0.3491, H→1.1783, S→0.1005, O→0.1034, N→0.0151, A→0.0211. All subtracted except C, H, S.
- ✅ **LHV = HHV − 2.442 × (9H + M)/100**; latent heat of water = **2.442 MJ/kg**.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric O₂ = 2.667C + 8H + S − O**; **Stoichiometric air = O₂/0.232**.
- ✅ **Dry air composition** by mass: 23.2 % O₂, 76.8 % N₂.
- ✅ **Conversion routes:** Thermochemical (combustion, pyrolysis, gasification, HTL) + Biochemical (AD, fermentation) + Chemical (transesterification).
- ✅ **Biogas composition:** CH₄ (50–70 %) + CO₂ (30–40 %) + trace H₂S; produced via **4-stage AD** (hydrolysis → acidogenesis → acetogenesis → methanogenesis).
- ✅ **Biodiesel** = transesterification of triglycerides with methanol → FAME (biodiesel) + glycerol.
- ✅ **Woody vs Herbaceous:** woody has high lignin (energy-dense); herbaceous has high ash/alkali (slagging risk).
- ✅ Kerala-specific feedstocks: **coconut shell, rice husk, bagasse, areca husk, cashew nut shell liquid (CNSL)**.
- ✅ **Syngas** from gasification: CO (30–50 %), H₂ (20–30 %), CO₂ (10–20 %), CH₄ (5–10 %), N₂ (balance).
- ✅ **Higher VM (70–85 %)** in biomass vs coal (15–35 %) → easier ignition, more tar in gasification.
- ✅ **Energy content hierarchy:** Wood < Bagasse < Coal < Heavy oil < Natural gas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wood and Charcoal: Classification and Rank of Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wood and Charcoal: Classification and Rank of Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Wood as a Solid Fuel

**Wood** is the most ancient and naturally occurring solid fuel derived from biomass, primarily composed of **cellulose (40–50%)**, **hemicellulose (20–30%)**, **lignin (20–30%)**, and extractives/resins. It is classified as a renewable fuel with a moderate calorific value of approximately **3500–4500 kcal/kg** (oven-dry basis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Wood is a fibrous, hygroscopic, lignocellulosic biomass fuel formed through photosynthesis. Its effective heating value is highly dependent on its **moisture content**, which makes direct combustion efficiency strongly tied to the drying stage.

### Conceptual Analogy (The Wet Sponge Metaphor)
Think of freshly cut wood exactly like a **wet kitchen sponge**. When you try to "burn" a wet sponge (wood), most of your heat energy (fire) is wasted in simply evaporating the trapped water (moisture) before the actual combustion can occur. This is precisely why seasoned firewood (moisture < 20%) burns dramatically hotter and more efficiently than freshly cut wood (moisture > 50%). The hidden water is the silent thief of calorific value.

---

## 1.2 Charcoal — The Carbonized Form of Wood

**Charcoal** is a black, porous, carbonaceous residue produced by the **destructive distillation (pyrolysis/carbonization) of wood in the complete or near-absence of air**. It represents a concentrated, smoke-free, high-grade solid fuel.

**Typical Composition of Charcoal:**
- Fixed Carbon: **70–80%**
- Volatile Matter: **10–20%**
- Ash: **1–5%**
- Moisture: **1–3%**

**Calorific Value of Charcoal:** Approximately **6500–7500 kcal/kg**, which is roughly **1.7 times** that of the original wood on a dry basis because the volatile, water-rich components have been driven off, leaving behind concentrated elemental carbon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Charcoal vs. Coke — A Key Distinction:**
> - **Charcoal** = Wood pyrolyzed (low density ~0.4 g/cm³, highly porous).
> - **Coke** = Coal pyrolyzed (high density ~0.8–1.0 g/cm³, less porous).
> Both are produced by carbonization, but from different parent feedstocks.

---

## 1.3 Coal — The Fossil Solid Fuel

**Coal** is a combustible, sedimentary, organic rock formed over millions of years from the buried, compressed, and thermally altered remains of plant matter (a process called **coalification**). It is a **non-renewable**, **heterogeneous**, **carbon-rich** solid fuel.

**Coalification (The Geological Press):**
Plant matter → **Peat** → **Lignite** → **Sub-bituminous** → **Bituminous** → **Anthracite**

This is a one-way thermochemical journey under increasing **temperature (50–200 °C → 200+ °C)**, **pressure**, and **time** (millions of years).

### Conceptual Analogy (The Toasted Bread Metaphor)
Imagine leaving a slice of bread on a slow, very low toaster for 5 minutes, then 1 hour, then 24 hours, then a week. Each stage of "cooking" produces a darker, drier, harder, more carbon-concentrated product. **Coal is essentially Earth's "bread" — plant matter that has been slowly "toasted" by geological forces over millions of years.** Anthracite is the well-done toast; lignite is barely warm bread.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Coal — Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Two Principal Classification Schemes:**
> 1. **Classification by RANK** — Based on the degree of metamorphism (carbon content, calorific value, volatile matter).
> 2. **Classification by GRADE** — Based on purity (ash content, sulfur content, moisture content).
>
> The **RANK** refers to the *inherent* geological maturity, while **GRADE** reflects *commercial* quality after mining/preparation.

---

## 1.5 Rank of Coal — The Maturity Meter

The **Rank** of coal is a measure of its **degree of coalification** — i.e., how far the original plant material has progressed along the metamorphic journey from peat to anthracite. Higher rank = older, harder, drier, more carbon-rich, and higher calorific value.

**The Four Master Parameters of Rank:**

1. **Fixed Carbon (FC)** — Increases with rank ↑ (from ~30% to >92%)
2. **Volatile Matter (VM)** — Decreases with rank ↓ (from ~60% to <10%)
3. **Calorific Value (CV)** — Increases then stabilizes (peaks in bituminous range)
4. **Moisture Content** — Decreases sharply with rank ↓ (from >90% in peat to <5% in anthracite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid — "FC↑ VM↓ CV↑ Moisture↓"** as you move from Peat → Anthracite.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Wood — Detailed Fuel Analysis

### 2.1.1 Composition of Dry Wood
The three structural polymers of wood are:
- **Cellulose (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ** — Linear glucose polymer providing structural strength.
- **Hemicellulose** — Branched, amorphous polymer of various sugars (xylose, mannose).
- **Lignin** — Highly cross-linked aromatic polymer; responsible for the brown color and rigidity.

The empirical formula of dry wood approximates to **C₆H₉O₄** with an average carbon content of **~50%**.

### 2.1.2 Proximate Analysis of Wood (Typical)
- Moisture: **30–60%** (green wood), **10–20%** (air-dried)
- Volatile Matter: **70–80%**
- Fixed Carbon: **15–20%**
- Ash: **0.5–2%**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why wood smokes:** The high volatile matter (~70%) escapes during heating as combustible gases (CH₄, CO, H₂, tars). These gases, when not fully oxidized, condense as visible white smoke — the hallmark of wood combustion.

---

## 2.2 Charcoal — Carbonization Chemistry

### 2.2.1 The Carbonization Process (Destructive Distillation)

Carbonization is conducted in **kilns, retorts, or pits** under limited air supply at temperatures between **400–700 °C**. The process yields three product streams:

| Product Stream | Components | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid Residue (Charcoal)** | C, ash | 25–35 |
| **Liquid Condensate (Pyroligneous acid)** | Water, acetic acid, methanol, wood tar | 40–50 |
| **Non-condensable Gases** | CO, CO₂, CH₄, H₂, C₂H₄ | 20–30 |

### 2.2.2 Temperature-Regime Stages of Carbonization

- **Below 200 °C:** Moisture and some non-combustible gases (CO₂) released. No significant chemical change.
- **200–280 °C:** Decomposition begins; CO₂, H₂O, acetic acid, and some CO released.
- **280–380 °C:** Vigorous decomposition; large quantities of condensable vapors (tar, methanol), CO, CO₂.
- **380–500 °C:** Peak devolatilization; release of H₂ and light hydrocarbons.
- **Above 500 °C:** Residual char undergoes slow graphitization; final shrinkage.

### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Mechanism (How Charcoal Forms)

**Step 1:** Wood is heated slowly in the absence of air.
**Step 2:** Volatile combustible gases are evolved and partially escape.
**Step 3:** Liquid distillate (tar + pyroligneous acid) condenses out.
**Step 4:** Solid residue — now charcoal — is cooled in inert atmosphere to prevent re-ignition.
**Step 5:** The remaining solid is **70–80% fixed carbon** with a porous honeycomb structure (cellular voids where volatiles escaped).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Charcoal is used extensively in metallurgy (as a reductant), barbecue fuel (smokeless combustion), art (activated carbon for filtration), and historical blast furnace operations before coke became dominant.

---

## 2.3 Coal — Detailed Rank Classification

### 2.3.1 The Five Ranks of Coal (In Order of Increasing Maturity)

| Rank | Fixed Carbon (%) | Volatile Matter (%) | Moisture (%) | Calorific Value (kcal/kg) | Color |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Peat** | <30 | >60 | >75 | 1500–2500 | Brown, fibrous |
| **Lignite** | 30–40 | 40–55 | 30–50 | 2500–4000 | Brown, woody |
| **Sub-bituminous** | 40–55 | 35–45 | 15–30 | 4000–5500 | Dark brown/black |
| **Bituminous** | 55–85 | 15–35 | 2–10 | 5500–8500 | Black, banded |
| **Anthracite** | >85 | <15 | <5 | 8000–9000 | Black, lustrous |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anthracite is the "King of Coals"** — highest carbon, highest calorific value, lowest volatile matter, almost smokeless, slow-burning, and highly prized for domestic heating and metallurgy.

### 2.3.2 Proximate Analysis — The Foundation of Rank

**Proximate Analysis determines four parameters:**

1. **Moisture (M)** — Loss in weight when heated to **110 °C** for 1 hour.
2. **Volatile Matter (VM)** — Loss in weight when heated to **950 °C ± 20 °C** for 7 minutes (excluding moisture).
3. **Ash (A)** — Residue left after complete combustion at **750 °C ± 20 °C**.
4. **Fixed Carbon (FC)** — Calculated by difference.

### 2.3.3 Ultimate Analysis — The Elemental Breakdown

- **Carbon (C):** Increases with rank (50% → 95%).
- **Hydrogen (H):** Decreases with rank (6% → 2%).
- **Oxygen (O):** Decreases sharply with rank (40% → 1%).
- **Nitrogen (N):** Roughly 1–2% throughout.
- **Sulfur (S):** Variable (0.5–5%); environmental pollutant.

### 2.3.4 Classification by Grade

| Grade | Ash Content (%) |
|---|---|
| Premium / Select | <7 |
| Standard / Premium | 7–10 |
| Standard | 10–15 |
| Utility / Reject | 15–20 |
| Reject / Refuse | >20 |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Formulas for Coal/Wood/Charcoal Calculations:**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fixed Carbon (by difference) | $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$ | % (dry basis) | Most-used relation |
| 2 | Dulong's Formula (Gross CV) | $GCV = 8080 C + 34500 \left( H - \dfrac{O}{8} \right) + 2240 S$ | kcal/kg | Empirical; uses ultimate analysis |
| 3 | Dulong's Modified Formula | $GCV = 81 C + 342.5 \left( H - \dfrac{O}{8} \right) + 22.4 S$ | kcal/kg | Value in **kcal/kg** directly |
| 4 | NCV ↔ GCV relation | $NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587$ | kcal/kg | Latent heat of vaporization |
| 5 | Hydrogen from GCV–NCV | $H = \dfrac{GCV - NCV}{0.09 \times 587}$ | % | Inverse rearrangement |
| 6 | Air Required (theoretical) | $O_{2\,req} = \dfrac{32}{12}C + \dfrac{32}{4}H + \dfrac{32}{32}S$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | Stoichiometric O₂ demand |
| 7 | Air Required (with N₂) | $Air = \dfrac{O_{2\,req}}{0.232}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Air is 23.2% O₂ by mass |
| 8 | Stoichiometric CO₂ yield | $CO_{2\,max} = \dfrac{44}{12}C = 3.667\,C$ | kg/kg fuel | Max possible from carbon |
| 9 | Moisture in flue gas | $H_{2}O_{flue} = \dfrac{9H}{100} + M_{fuel}$ | kg/kg fuel | Combustion water from H + fuel moisture |
| 10 | Carbonization yield | $Yield_{charcoal} = \dfrac{W_{charcoal}}{W_{wood}} \times 100$ | % | Mass balance |
| 11 | Charcoal fixed carbon | $FC_{charcoal} = \dfrac{W_{carbon}}{W_{charcoal}} \times 100$ | % | Quality indicator |
| 12 | Rank indicator (FC/VM) | $Fuel\,Ratio = \dfrac{FC}{VM}$ | dimensionless | Lignite < 1; Anthracite > 7 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Ratio Criterion (Used by ASTM to Differentiate Bituminous vs. Anthracite):**
> - If **FC/VM > 7** → Anthracite
> - If **FC/VM = 1.1 to 7** → Bituminous
> - If **FC/VM < 1.1** → Lignite / Sub-bituminous

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Power Plants:** Rank determines combustion technology (anthracite → pulverized; lignite → fluidized bed due to high moisture).
- **Coke Making:** Only **medium-volatile bituminous** with low ash/sulfur is coking-grade.
- **Iron/Steel Industry:** Anthracite and coke are essential reductants.
- **Domestic Heating:** Anthracite preferred (smokeless, high CV, slow-burning).
- **Carbon Capture:** Lower-rank coals (lignite) release more CO₂ per unit energy — challenging for emissions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Fixed Carbon from Proximate Analysis

**Starting Principle:** In proximate analysis, a fuel sample of 100 g is conceptually divided into four parts that sum to 100%:

$$M + VM + FC + A = 100\%$$

**Rearranging for FC:**

$$FC + (M + VM + A) = 100$$

$$FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$$

> This is the **fundamental mass balance identity** used in every coal analysis problem at the KTU level.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Dulong's Formula (Empirical Origin)

**Historical Basis:** The French chemist **Marcellin Berthelot** and later refined by **P.F. Dulong** (late 19th century), based on the principle that the calorific value of a fuel is the **additive sum of the heats of combustion of its constituent combustible elements**, minus the energy required to break the oxygen–hydrogen bond already present in the fuel as water.

**Starting components (per 1 kg fuel):**
- Carbon contributes: $8080 \, C$ kcal/kg (heat of combustion of C)
- Hydrogen contributes: $34500 \, H$ kcal/kg (heat of combustion of H)
- **Correction for pre-existing O in fuel:** Some hydrogen is already bound to oxygen as H₂O. The "available" hydrogen for combustion is therefore $\left( H - \dfrac{O}{8} \right)$ because **8 parts of oxygen lock up 1 part of hydrogen** as water.
- Sulfur contributes: $2240 \, S$ kcal/kg (heat of combustion of S)

**Assembling the terms:**

$$GCV = 8080\,C + 34500\,H_{available} + 2240\,S$$

**Substituting** $H_{available} = \left( H - \dfrac{O}{8} \right)$:

$$GCV = 8080\,C + 34500\left( H - \dfrac{O}{8} \right) + 2240\,S \quad \text{(kcal/kg)}$$

> **Simplified form** (when GCV expressed in kcal/kg without 8080 factor, using 81, 342.5, 22.4):

$$GCV = 81\,C + 342.5\left( H - \dfrac{O}{8} \right) + 22.4\,S \quad \text{(kcal/kg)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: NCV from GCV

**Physical Principle:** When combustion occurs, the hydrogen in the fuel forms water. In a **gross calorific value (GCV)** test, this water is condensed to liquid at 25 °C, releasing the **latent heat of vaporization**. In real boilers, however, this water leaves as **steam**, taking away that latent energy.

**Energy lost per kg fuel due to vaporization:**
The water formed from hydrogen in 1 kg fuel = $\dfrac{9 H}{100}$ kg (since 1 kg H₂ = 9 kg H₂O).

Latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C = **587 kcal/kg**.

**Therefore, the heat lost in NCV is:**

$$\Delta H = \dfrac{9 H}{100} \times 587 = 0.09 \times H \times 587 \quad \text{kcal/kg}$$

**Final relation:**

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587$$

**Rewritten as:**

$$NCV = GCV - 52.83\,H$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Required

**Step 1:** Write the elemental combustion reactions:

$$C + O_{2} \to CO_{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 \text{ kg C requires } \dfrac{32}{12} \text{ kg O}_2 = 2.667 \text{ kg O}_2$$

$$H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \to H_{2}O \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 \text{ kg H}_2 \text{ requires } \dfrac{16}{2} = 8 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg H}_2$$
Wait — for 1 kg of **hydrogen element H** (atomic), the ratio is:

$$1 \text{ kg H} \to \dfrac{8}{1} \text{ kg O}_2 \quad \text{per kg H}$$

$$S + O_{2} \to SO_{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 \text{ kg S requires } \dfrac{32}{32} = 1 \text{ kg O}_2$$

**Step 2:** Sum the demands per kg of fuel containing C%, H%, S%, O%:

$$O_{2\,req} = \dfrac{32}{12}C + 8H + S - \dfrac{32}{32}O$$

The oxygen already in the fuel is itself a partial oxidant, so it is **subtracted**:

$$O_{2\,req} = 2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O$$

**Step 3:** Air = O₂ supply from air. By mass, air contains 23.2% O₂ (and 76.8% N₂). So:

$$Air_{req} = \dfrac{O_{2\,req}}{0.232} \quad \text{kg air per kg fuel}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Example — Complete Coal Analysis

**Problem:** A coal sample has the following composition (as received): C = 65%, H = 4.5%, O = 12%, S = 1%, N = 1.5%, Moisture = 3%, Ash = 13%. Calculate (i) GCV using Dulong's formula, (ii) NCV, (iii) Fixed Carbon from a given proximate VM = 32%.

### Solution

**Given:**
C = 65, H = 4.5, O = 12, S = 1, M = 3, A = 13, VM = 32

**(i) GCV by Dulong's Formula:**

$$GCV = 81\,C + 342.5\left( H - \dfrac{O}{8} \right) + 22.4\,S$$

Substituting the values:

$$GCV = 81 \times 65 + 342.5 \times \left( 4.5 - \dfrac{12}{8} \right) + 22.4 \times 1$$

$$\dfrac{O}{8} = \dfrac{12}{8} = 1.5$$

$$GCV = 5265 + 342.5 \times (4.5 - 1.5) + 22.4$$

$$GCV = 5265 + 342.5 \times 3.0 + 22.4$$

$$GCV = 5265 + 1027.5 + 22.4$$

$$\boxed{GCV = 6314.9 \, \text{kcal/kg}}$$

**(ii) NCV:**

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587$$

$$NCV = 6314.9 - 0.09 \times 4.5 \times 587$$

$$NCV = 6314.9 - 0.405 \times 587$$

$$NCV = 6314.9 - 237.735$$

$$\boxed{NCV = 6077.165 \, \text{kcal/kg}}$$

**(iii) Fixed Carbon (FC):**

$$FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$$

$$FC = 100 - (3 + 32 + 13)$$

$$\boxed{FC = 52\%}$$

**Fuel Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{FC}{VM} = \dfrac{52}{32} = 1.625$$

Since FC/VM is between 1.1 and 7, this is a **Bituminous Coal** (most likely **medium-volatile bituminous**).

> **Verification:** Ultimate sum check: 65 + 4.5 + 12 + 1 + 1.5 = 84%. The remaining 16% includes moisture (3%) + ash (13%) = 16%. ✓

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Charcoal Yield Calculation

**Problem:** 500 kg of wood with 15% moisture is carbonized. The charcoal product weighs 165 kg (oven-dry basis). Calculate the charcoal yield and fixed carbon content, given that the charcoal analyzes as 75% FC, 5% VM, 3% Ash, and 2% Moisture.

### Solution

**Charcoal yield:**

$$Yield = \dfrac{165}{500} \times 100 = 33\%$$

**Fixed Carbon in 165 kg charcoal:**

$$FC_{abs} = 165 \times 0.75 = 123.75 \text{ kg}$$

**Carbon retained from wood:** assuming wood has ~50% C (dry basis), 500 × 0.85 × 0.50 = 212.5 kg C in dry wood.

**Carbonization efficiency:**

$$\eta_{carbon} = \dfrac{123.75}{212.5} \times 100 = 58.24\%$$

This means ~42% of the carbon in the wood escaped as volatile tar/gases during carbonization.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Coalification Journey (Rank Progression)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Plant Matter Biomass]:::stage0 --> B0[Peat Brown Fibrous Moisture 75 CV Low]:::stage1
    B0 --> C0[Lignite Brown Woody Moisture 40 CV 2500 4000]:::stage2
    C0 --> D0[Sub bituminous Dark Brown CV 4000 5500]:::stage3
    D0 --> E0[Bituminous Black Banded CV 5500 8500]:::stage4
    E0 --> F0[Anthracite Black Lustrous CV 8500 CV High]:::stage5

    A0 -.Age plus Pressure plus Heat.-> B0
    B0 -.Coalification Stage 1.-> C0
    C0 -.Coalification Stage 2.-> D0
    D0 -.Coalification Stage 3.-> E0
    E0 -.Coalification Stage 4.-> F0

    classDef stage0 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage1 fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#8b5a2b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage2 fill:#f4a261,stroke:#7a3e09,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage3 fill:#e76f51,stroke:#5b1f0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage4 fill:#264653,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef stage5 fill:#0d1b2a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Charcoal Production Process (Carbonization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1[Wood Logs Air Dried]:::raw --> Kiln[Carbonization Kiln Retort 400 to 700 C Inert or Limited Air]:::unit
    Kiln --> Char[Charcoal Solid 70 to 80 FC]:::prod
    Kiln --> Pyrol[Pyroligneous Acid Liquid Tar plus Methanol plus Acetic]:::byprod
    Kiln --> Gas[Non Condensable Gases CO CH4 H2 CO2]:::gas

    Char --> Stage1[Cooling in Inert Atmosphere]:::step
    Stage1 --> FinalChar[Smokeless Solid Fuel for BBQ Metallurgy]:::finalprod

    classDef raw fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef unit fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef prod fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef byprod fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef gas fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    classDef finalprod fill:#212529,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Coal Sample]:::start --> Prox[Proximate Analysis M VM A]:::test
    Prox --> FC[Calculate FC equals 100 minus M plus VM plus A]:::calc
    FC --> Ratio{Fuel Ratio FC by VM}:::decision
    Ratio -->|Ratio greater than 7| Anth[Anthracite Highest Rank]:::high
    Ratio -->|Ratio 1.1 to 7| Bit[Bituminous Most Common]:::mid
    Ratio -->|Ratio less than 1.1| Low[Sub bituminous Lignite Peat]:::low
    Anth --> UseA[Use Metallurgy Domestic Heating]
    Bit --> UseB[Use Power Plants Coke Making]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

**"Differentiate between the calorific value of wood and charcoal. Why is charcoal considered a superior fuel despite being derived from the same parent material?"**

#### Model Answer (3-Mark Key Points):

1. **Wood's CV:** 3500–4500 kcal/kg (oven-dry basis); **Charcoal's CV:** 6500–7500 kcal/kg.
2. **Wood contains 70–80% volatile matter** and high moisture (20–60% as received), while **charcoal contains 70–80% fixed carbon** with negligible moisture and low VM.
3. **Charcoal is superior** because: (a) higher energy density per unit mass, (b) smokeless combustion (volatiles already driven off), (c) higher ignition temperature, (d) easier storage (no biological degradation), (e) better for metallurgy as a reductant.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for the numerical range, 1 mark for composition distinction, 1 mark for the superiority justification.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

**"What is meant by 'rank' of coal? Name the five ranks of coal in increasing order of metamorphism with one characteristic property of each."**

#### Model Answer (3-Mark Key Points):

**Rank** is the degree of coalification (geological maturity) of coal, reflecting how far the parent plant material has progressed under heat and pressure.

| Rank | Characteristic Property |
|---|---|
| **Peat** | Very high moisture (>75%), brown, fibrous |
| **Lignite** | Brown, woody texture, CV ≈ 2500–4000 kcal/kg |
| **Sub-bituminous** | Dark brown, CV ≈ 4000–5500 kcal/kg |
| **Bituminous** | Black, banded, coking potential, CV ≈ 5500–8500 kcal/kg |
| **Anthracite** | Lustrous black, FC > 85%, smokeless, highest CV |

> **Mnemonic:** "**P**lease **L**eave **S**ome **B**eautiful **A**nthracite" (Peat → Lignite → Sub-bituminous → Bituminous → Anthracite)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternative full questions are provided; student attempts **ONE**.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, Module 1, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** *With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain the classification of coal by rank. Discuss the proximate and ultimate analysis methods, and explain how rank influences fixed carbon, volatile matter, moisture, and calorific value.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A coal sample has the following composition: C = 70%, H = 5%, O = 12%, N = 1%, S = 1.5%, Moisture = 4%, Ash = 6.5%. The proximate analysis shows volatile matter = 30%. Calculate: (i) Gross Calorific Value by Dulong's formula, (ii) Net Calorific Value, (iii) Fixed Carbon, and (iv) Fuel Ratio. Identify the rank of the coal.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Introduce classification of coal by rank — five major ranks in order of metamorphism: Peat → Lignite → Sub-bituminous → Bituminous → Anthracite.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Proximate analysis determines **Moisture (M)**, **Volatile Matter (VM)**, **Ash (A)**, and **Fixed Carbon (FC = 100 − M − VM − A)**. Ultimate analysis determines **C, H, O, N, S** percentages.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Rank-influenced trends:

| Property | Peat → Anthracite Trend |
|---|---|
| Fixed Carbon | Increases (30% → 92%) |
| Volatile Matter | Decreases (60% → 8%) |
| Moisture | Decreases (75% → 3%) |
| Calorific Value | Increases then stabilizes (peaks in bituminous) |

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Mention the **Fuel Ratio (FC/VM)** as a numerical rank indicator:
- > 7 → Anthracite
- 1.1–7 → Bituminous
- < 1.1 → Lignite

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Neat flow diagram (already provided in SECTION 4.1) showing peat-to-anthracite progression with stage-wise property values.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:

**Given:**
C = 70, H = 5, O = 12, N = 1, S = 1.5, M = 4, A = 6.5, VM = 30

**Step 1 (2 marks): GCV by Dulong's formula:**

$$GCV = 81\,C + 342.5\left( H - \dfrac{O}{8} \right) + 22.4\,S$$

$$\dfrac{O}{8} = \dfrac{12}{8} = 1.5$$

$$GCV = 81 \times 70 + 342.5 \times (5 - 1.5) + 22.4 \times 1.5$$

$$GCV = 5670 + 342.5 \times 3.5 + 33.6$$

$$GCV = 5670 + 1198.75 + 33.6$$

$$\boxed{GCV = 6902.35 \text{ kcal/kg}}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): NCV:**

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587$$

$$NCV = 6902.35 - 0.09 \times 5 \times 587$$

$$NCV = 6902.35 - 0.45 \times 587$$

$$NCV = 6902.35 - 264.15$$

$$\boxed{NCV = 6638.20 \text{ kcal/kg}}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Fixed Carbon:**

$$FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$$

$$FC = 100 - (4 + 30 + 6.5)$$

$$\boxed{FC = 59.5\%}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): Fuel Ratio and Rank Identification:**

$$Fuel\,Ratio = \dfrac{FC}{VM} = \dfrac{59.5}{30} = 1.983$$

Since 1.1 < 1.983 < 7, this is a **Bituminous Coal** (specifically **medium-volatile bituminous**, since VM = 30% lies in 15–35% range).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract** $\dfrac{O}{8}$ inside the parentheses of Dulong's formula. Always show $\left( H - \dfrac{O}{8} \right)$.
> 2. **Confusing GCV units** — using 8080/34500/2240 gives kcal/kg directly; using 81/342.5/22.4 also gives kcal/kg. Don't mix.
> 3. **Neglecting ash** in the FC sum. The relation is FC = 100 − (M + VM + A), not FC = 100 − (M + VM).
> 4. **Not identifying the rank** — calculation alone without naming the coal type loses 0.5–1 mark.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, Module 1, Alternative Slot]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the process of charcoal manufacture by carbonization of wood. Discuss the temperature stages, products obtained, and the comparative properties of wood vs. charcoal as a fuel.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *500 kg of wood with 18% moisture is carbonized in a kiln, yielding 150 kg of charcoal (as-received) with the following composition: Fixed Carbon = 72%, Volatile Matter = 18%, Ash = 5%, Moisture = 5%. Calculate: (i) Mass of fixed carbon in charcoal, (ii) Charcoal yield on dry wood basis, (iii) Fixed carbon recovery from wood (assuming wood contains 50% C on dry basis), and (iv) Discuss why charcoal gives a cleaner combustion than wood.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Carbonization Process — Heating wood in the **absence (or limited supply) of air** in kilns/retorts to **400–700 °C**. The process is also called **destructive distillation**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Temperature stages:

| Stage | Temperature | Products |
|---|---|---|
| Drying | <120 °C | Moisture removed |
| Pre-carbonization | 200–280 °C | CO₂, CO, acetic acid |
| Carbonization | 280–500 °C | Tar, methanol, CH₄, H₂ |
| Calcination | >500 °C | Charcoal stabilization |

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Three product streams — Solid (charcoal 25–35%), Liquid (pyroligneous acid 40–50%), Non-condensable gases (20–30%).

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Wood vs. Charcoal:

| Property | Wood | Charcoal |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture (%) | 20–60 | <3 |
| Volatile Matter (%) | 70–80 | 10–20 |
| Fixed Carbon (%) | 15–20 | 70–80 |
| CV (kcal/kg) | 3500–4500 | 6500–7500 |
| Smoke | Heavy | Smokeless |
| Storage | Decays | Stable |

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 (1.5 marks): Mass of Fixed Carbon in 150 kg charcoal:**

$$FC_{mass} = 150 \times 0.72 = 108 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Charcoal yield on dry wood basis:**

Dry wood mass:

$$W_{dry\,wood} = 500 \times (1 - 0.18) = 500 \times 0.82 = 410 \text{ kg}$$

Charcoal yield (dry basis):

$$Yield = \dfrac{150}{410} \times 100 = 36.59\%$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Fixed carbon recovery:**

Carbon in dry wood:

$$C_{wood} = 410 \times 0.50 = 205 \text{ kg}$$

Fixed carbon recovery:

$$\eta = \dfrac{108}{205} \times 100 = 52.68\%$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Cleaner combustion rationale — charcoal has low volatile matter (18%) and negligible moisture, so combustion is essentially **fixed carbon burning with minimal smoke/soot formation**. Wood's high volatile matter (70–80%) leads to incomplete combustion, releasing tar, CO, soot, and visible smoke. Charcoal combustion is more **stoichiometric**, releasing primarily CO₂.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Mixing dry and as-received bases** — moisture correction must be applied to wood before yield calculation.
> 2. **Misapplying** 50% C content to wet wood — must use dry wood mass first.
> 3. **Confusing "fixed carbon recovery"** with "mass yield." They are entirely different quantities.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (always specify kg or %).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Twice Before Every Exam:**

- **Wood** is a lignocellulosic biomass fuel (CV ≈ 3500–4500 kcal/kg) — affected strongly by moisture.
- **Charcoal** is wood pyrolyzed in air-starved conditions at 400–700 °C (CV ≈ 6500–7500 kcal/kg).
- **Carbonization** yields three streams: solid charcoal, liquid pyroligneous acid, non-condensable gases.
- **Coal** is a sedimentary organic rock formed from buried plant matter over millions of years.
- **Coalification** progresses: Plant → Peat → Lignite → Sub-bituminous → Bituminous → Anthracite.
- **Rank** = degree of metamorphism. **Grade** = purity (ash/sulfur/moisture).
- **Proximate analysis:** Moisture, Volatile Matter, Ash, Fixed Carbon (by difference).
- **Ultimate analysis:** C, H, O, N, S percentages.
- **Key FC relation:** $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$.
- **Dulong's GCV formula:** $GCV = 81 C + 342.5 \left( H - \dfrac{O}{8} \right) + 22.4 S$ (in kcal/kg).
- **NCV formula:** $NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587$.
- **Fuel Ratio = FC/VM** classifies rank: >7 anthracite, 1.1–7 bituminous, <1.1 lignite.
- **Anthracite** = highest FC (>85%), highest CV, smokeless, best for domestic/metallurgy.
- **Bituminous** = most common, coking grade in 15–35% VM range.
- **Lignite** = high moisture, low CV, used in mine-mouth power plants.
- **Peat** = precursor, not a true coal; used as domestic fuel and soil conditioner.
- **Always correct for moisture** when computing yields, efficiencies, or heating values.
- **Anthracite and coke** are the kings of solid fuels for iron making; **lignite and peat** are the bottom of the rank ladder.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Peat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Peat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Peat — The Origin of the Coal Family

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Peat is the first and lowest-rank member of the coal series, formed by the partial decomposition and accumulation of vegetable matter (mosses, reeds, sedges, and woody plants) under anaerobic, waterlogged conditions in bogs, marshes, and swamps. It represents an intermediate stage between unconsolidated plant debris and true coal (lignite).*

In the strict KTU Fuel Engineering context, peat is classified as a **solid fossil fuel** but is **NOT considered true coal** because it has not undergone significant coalification (bacterial, physical, and geochemical alteration beyond peatification).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **wet kitchen sponge** that has been left in a sealed plastic bag for thousands of years in a swamp. The sponge (plant fibers) cannot dry out or rot completely because there is no oxygen (anaerobic conditions). Slowly, the trapped material darkens, compacts, and partially "carbonizes." That is essentially **peat**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geological Age of Peat:** *Recent (Quaternary period — less than ~10,000 years old).* Peat bogs are still actively forming peat today in countries like **Ireland, Finland, Russia, Scotland, and Indonesia.**

## 1.3 Position in the Coalification Series

The coalification series (rank progression) is:

$$\text{Peat} \longrightarrow \text{Lignite} \longrightarrow \text{Bituminous Coal} \longrightarrow \text{Anthracite}$$

| Rank | Carbon (% daf) | Age (approx.) | Energy Density |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peat | **55–60 %** | < 10,000 yrs | Very Low |
| Lignite | 60–70 % | ~10⁶ yrs | Low |
| Bituminous | 75–90 % | ~10⁸ yrs | High |
| Anthracite | > 90 % | ~3×10⁸ yrs | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Peat Formation, Structure & Properties

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Peat Formation (Peatification)

The transformation of plant debris into peat proceeds in **three overlapping biochemical stages**:

1. **Aerobic Stage (Surface Layer)** — Plant cells, after dying, are initially attacked by aerobic bacteria and fungi. Oxygen and cellulose/hemicellulose are consumed, releasing **CO₂** and **H₂O**.
2. **Anaerobic Stage (Below Water Table)** — Once the water table rises and oxygen is excluded, anaerobic bacteria dominate. Cellulose is slowly broken down into **humic acids, fulvic acids, methane (CH₄)**, and **CO₂**. Lignin is largely preserved.
3. **Geochemical Stage (Compaction)** — Overburden pressure squeezes out water and volatiles; porosity decreases; carbon content slowly rises. This continues for centuries until the deposit is harvested as peat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Condition:** *Anaerobic (oxygen-free) environment is non-negotiable.* Without it, complete bacterial decay occurs and peat never forms.

## 2.2 Physical & Chemical Properties

### (a) Physical Properties
- **Color:** Yellowish-brown when fresh → Dark brown to black when dry
- **Texture:** Fibrous, spongy, earthy; visible plant remains
- **Density (bulk):** ~0.4–0.8 g/cm³ (very low)
- **Hardness:** Friable, crumbles easily
- **Smell:** Characteristic earthy/musty odor (due to humic acid)
- **Moisture in situ:** **80–90 %** (as dug)

### (b) Proximate Analysis (Air-Dried Basis)

| Constituent | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | **8–15 %** | High — reduces CV |
| Volatile Matter | **50–65 %** | Very high — burns with long smoky flame |
| Ash | **3–15 %** | Variable; high in mineral-rich bogs |
| Fixed Carbon | **20–30 %** | Low — not yet coalified |

### (c) Ultimate Analysis (Dry, Ash-Free Basis)

| Element | Mass % |
|---|---|
| Carbon (C) | **55–60 %** |
| Hydrogen (H) | **5–6 %** |
| Oxygen (O) | **30–35 %** |
| Nitrogen (N) | **1–2 %** |
| Sulfur (S) | **0.3–1.0 %** |

### (d) Calorific Value (GCV)

$$\text{GCV of air-dried peat} \approx 1500 \text{ to } 3000 \;\text{kcal/kg}\;\;(6.3 \text{ to } 12.6 \;\text{MJ/kg})$$

For comparison, bituminous coal ranges **6500–8500 kcal/kg**. Hence peat is roughly **¼ the energy density** of good coal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Goutal's Approximation):** The GCV of a solid fuel can be estimated from its ultimate analysis as:
> $$\text{GCV} = 81.4\,C + 345\,(H - \tfrac{O}{8}) + 25\,S \quad \text{kcal/kg (daf)}$$
> where C, H, O, S are mass percentages on dry, ash-free basis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Peat

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture content (wet basis) | $M_w = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{wet\;total}} \times 100$ | % |
| Calorific value (rough) | $GCV = 81.4\,C + 345\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 25\,S$ | kcal/kg |
| Dulong's formula | $GCV = \dfrac{8080\,C + 34500\,H - 2850\,O}{100}$ (as alternative) | kcal/kg |
| Energy density ratio | $R = \dfrac{GCV_{peat}}{GCV_{bituminous}} \approx 0.20 - 0.30$ | dimensionless |
| Porosity of peat | $n = 70 - 90 \;\%$ | % |

## 2.4 Engineering Relevance

- **Power Generation:** Finland and Ireland burn peat in dedicated power stations (e.g., Edenderry, Ireland).
- **Domestic Fuel:** Cut into briquettes and burned in stoves.
- **Agriculture/Horticulture:** As a **soil conditioner** (peat moss), it improves water retention and acidity.
- **Source of Humic Acid:** Used as a soil supplement and in environmental remediation.
- **Carbon Sink:** Peat bogs store ~30 % of all soil carbon globally — ecologically critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Worked Derivations & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Moisture Conversion (Wet vs. Dry Basis)

**Question:** A sample of raw peat as dug from a bog contains **85 % moisture** (wet basis). If it is air-dried to a moisture of **12 %**, what mass of air-dried peat is obtained from **1000 kg** of wet peat?

### Step-by-Step Solution

Let:
- $m_1 = 1000$ kg = initial mass of wet peat
- $M_1 = 85\%$ = moisture content (wet basis)
- $M_2 = 12\%$ = moisture content of air-dried peat
- $m_2 = ?$ = mass of air-dried peat

**Step 1 — Compute dry (bone-dry) solid mass in original peat**

The dry solid mass remains constant during drying:

$$m_{dry} = m_1 \left(1 - \dfrac{M_1}{100}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$m_{dry} = 1000 \left(1 - \dfrac{85}{100}\right) = 1000 \times 0.15$$

$$m_{dry} = 150 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Express dry mass in terms of air-dried peat mass**

For air-dried peat:

$$m_{dry} = m_2 \left(1 - \dfrac{M_2}{100}\right)$$

Equating:

$$150 = m_2 \left(1 - \dfrac{0.12}{1}\right) = m_2 \times 0.88$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $m_2$**

$$m_2 = \dfrac{150}{0.88}$$

$$\boxed{m_2 \approx 170.45 \;\text{kg}}$$

**Interpretation:** Only ~170 kg of usable fuel is recovered from every 1000 kg of bog peat — a massive **~83 % mass loss** due to water removal. This is why **drying cost** is the biggest economic factor in peat utilization.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Calorific Value by Goutal's Formula

**Question:** The ultimate analysis (daf basis) of a peat sample is:

- C = 58 %
- H = 5.5 %
- O = 33 %
- S = 0.8 %
- N = 1.5 % (neglected in Goutal's formula)

Calculate the GCV using Goutal's approximation.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Write the formula**

$$\text{GCV} = 81.4\,C + 345\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 25\,S$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values**

$$\text{GCV} = 81.4(58) + 345\left(5.5 - \dfrac{33}{8}\right) + 25(0.8)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term**

Term 1 (Carbon contribution):

$$81.4 \times 58 = 4721.2 \;\text{kcal/kg}$$

Term 2 (Hydrogen contribution, corrected for bound oxygen):

$$\dfrac{33}{8} = 4.125$$

$$H - \dfrac{O}{8} = 5.5 - 4.125 = 1.375$$

$$345 \times 1.375 = 474.375 \;\text{kcal/kg}$$

Term 3 (Sulfur contribution):

$$25 \times 0.8 = 20.0 \;\text{kcal/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Sum all terms**

$$\text{GCV} = 4721.2 + 474.375 + 20.0$$

$$\boxed{\text{GCV} \approx 5215.6 \;\text{kcal/kg (daf basis)}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to air-dried basis (with 12 % moisture and 8 % ash)**

Daf basis value must be corrected using the formula:

$$\text{GCV}_{ar} = \text{GCV}_{daf} \times \dfrac{100 - M - A}{100}$$

$$\text{GCV}_{ar} = 5215.6 \times \dfrac{100 - 12 - 8}{100} = 5215.6 \times 0.80$$

$$\boxed{\text{GCV}_{ar} \approx 4172.5 \;\text{kcal/kg}}$$

This result is consistent with the empirical range for air-dried peat (**1500–4500 kcal/kg**).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Peat vs. Lignite vs. Bituminous Coal

| Property | Peat | Lignite | Bituminous Coal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon (% daf) | 55–60 | 60–70 | 75–90 |
| Moisture (% ar) | 8–15 | 20–40 | 2–10 |
| Volatile Matter (% daf) | 50–65 | 40–55 | 15–40 |
| GCV (kcal/kg) | 1500–4500 | 4000–6500 | 6500–8500 |
| Color | Brown/Black | Brown/Black | Black |
| Spontaneous Combustion | High | High | Low–Moderate |
| Smoke on Burning | Heavy | Moderate | Low |
| Geological Age | Recent | ~10⁶ yrs | ~10⁸ yrs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Schematic & Process Flow Diagrams

## 4.1 Peat Formation (Peatification) — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Debris: Moss, Reeds, Wood] --> B[Aerobic Bacterial Decay]
    B --> C[Loss of CO2 and H2O]
    C --> D[Submergence Below Water Table]
    D --> E[Anaerobic Bacterial Activity]
    E --> F[Formation of Humic Acid and Fulvic Acid]
    F --> G[Compaction Under Overburden]
    G --> H[Peat Deposit - Fibrous, Brown]
    H --> I[Optional: Further Burial and Compression]
    I --> J[Lignite - Next Rank in Coalification]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Peat Utilization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RawPeat[Raw Bog Peat 85 percent Moisture] --> Dewater[Dewatering and Air Drying]
    Dewater --> DriedPeat[Air Dried Peat 12 percent Moisture]
    DriedPeat --> Briquette[Briquette Pressing]
    DriedPeat --> PowerPlant[Power Plant Boiler]
    DriedPeat --> Domestic[Domestic Stove Fuel]
    Briquette --> Domestic
    PowerPlant --> Electricity[Electricity Generation]
    Domestic --> Heat[Space Heating]
    DriedPeat --> Agri[Soil Conditioner and Humic Acid]
```

## 4.3 Peat-to-Coal Rank Progression

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Peat C 55 to 60 percent] --> P2[Lignite C 60 to 70 percent]
    P2 --> P3[Sub-Bituminous C 70 to 75 percent]
    P3 --> P4[Bituminous C 75 to 90 percent]
    P4 --> P5[Anthracite C greater than 90 percent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
*Define peat. Mention any three characteristic properties.*

**Model Answer:**
Peat is the lowest-rank solid fossil fuel formed by the partial decomposition and compaction of vegetable matter (mosses, reeds, and wood fragments) in waterlogged, anaerobic conditions such as bogs and swamps.

Three characteristic properties:
1. Very high moisture content in situ (**80–90 %**).
2. Fibrous, earthy texture with visible plant remains.
3. Low calorific value (**1500–4500 kcal/kg**) and high volatile matter (**50–65 %**).

*(3 marks)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
*Why is peat classified as a low-rank fuel?*

**Model Answer:**
Peat is classified as a low-rank fuel because:
1. It has a **low carbon content** (~55–60 % daf).
2. It contains **high moisture and oxygen**, which reduce its heating value.
3. It has undergone **only partial coalification**, retaining much of the original plant structure and volatile matter.

*(3 marks)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Coalification Series & Peat Properties*

**(a)** Explain the coalification series. Discuss the position of peat in this series with its carbon content. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the formation of peat from vegetable matter. List four disadvantages of using peat as a fuel. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **coalification series** describes the progressive transformation of plant matter into coal under the combined effects of **pressure, temperature, and time**, with a corresponding increase in carbon content and decrease in moisture and volatile matter.

The series proceeds as follows:

$$\text{Peat} \to \text{Lignite} \to \text{Bituminous Coal} \to \text{Anthracite}$$

| Rank | Carbon (% daf) |
|---|---|
| Peat | 55–60 |
| Lignite | 60–70 |
| Bituminous | 75–90 |
| Anthracite | > 90 |

**Position of Peat:** Peat occupies the **first (lowest) rank** in the series. It has a carbon content of **55–60 % (daf)** and is essentially an *immature coal* — geologically recent and not yet subjected to the deep burial that produces true coal. Peat is sometimes excluded from the strict coal classification because coalification proper begins at the lignite stage.

*(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Formation (refer to flow diagram in Section 4.1):**

1. Plant debris (mosses, reeds, woody material) accumulates in waterlogged areas — bogs, swamps, and marshes.
2. In the upper aerobic zone, bacteria and fungi decompose cellulose, releasing CO₂ and H₂O.
3. As material sinks below the water table, oxygen is excluded, and **anaerobic bacteria** take over, slowly breaking down cellulose into humic and fulvic acids.
4. Overburden pressure compacts the deposit, expelling water and volatiles.
5. The result is a dark brown, fibrous, carbonaceous mass — **peat**.

**Four Disadvantages of Peat as a Fuel:**
1. **Very high moisture content (80–90 % as dug)** — drying is expensive and energy-intensive.
2. **Low calorific value** (1500–4500 kcal/kg) — large quantities required per unit heat.
3. **Tendency to spontaneous combustion** during storage due to residual microbial activity.
4. **Heavy smoke and air pollution** on burning, due to high volatile matter.

*(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Peat Analysis & Calorific Value Calculation*

**(a)** A peat sample has the following ultimate analysis (daf basis): C = 57 %, H = 6 %, O = 34 %, S = 1 %, N = 2 %. Estimate the GCV using Goutal's formula. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the proximate analysis of peat. Compare its properties with bituminous coal. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- C = 57 %, H = 6 %, O = 34 %, S = 1 %, N = 2 %

**Formula (Goutal's Approximation):**

$$\text{GCV} = 81.4\,C + 345\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 25\,S \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]$$

**Step 1 — Substitute**

$$\text{GCV} = 81.4(57) + 345\left(6 - \dfrac{34}{8}\right) + 25(1)$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term**

$$81.4 \times 57 = 4639.8$$

$$\dfrac{34}{8} = 4.25$$

$$6 - 4.25 = 1.75$$

$$345 \times 1.75 = 603.75$$

$$25 \times 1 = 25$$

**Step 3 — Total**

$$\text{GCV} = 4639.8 + 603.75 + 25$$

$$\boxed{\text{GCV} \approx 5268.55 \;\text{kcal/kg (daf)}}$$

*(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Proximate Analysis of Peat (Air-Dried Basis):**

| Constituent | Range |
|---|---|
| Moisture | 8–15 % |
| Volatile Matter | 50–65 % |
| Ash | 3–15 % |
| Fixed Carbon | 20–30 % |

**Comparison with Bituminous Coal:**

| Property | Peat | Bituminous Coal |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | High (8–15 %) | Low (2–10 %) |
| Volatile Matter | Very High (50–65 %) | Moderate (15–40 %) |
| Fixed Carbon | Low (20–30 %) | High (45–65 %) |
| GCV | Low (~4000 kcal/kg) | High (~7500 kcal/kg) |
| Smoke | Heavy | Low |
| Spontaneous Combustion | High Risk | Low–Moderate |
| Age | Recent (< 10,000 yrs) | ~10⁸ yrs |

*(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing peat with lignite.** Peat is *not* coal. It is a precursor. Examiners deduct marks if you classify peat as a true coal.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert wet-basis moisture to dry-basis** (or vice versa). Always show the conversion explicitly.
> 3. **Sign error in Goutal's formula:** It is $\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right)$, NOT $\left(H + \dfrac{O}{8}\right)$.
> 4. **Using daf-basis GCV without correcting** to as-received or air-dried basis. Always state and apply the correction factor when asked for "as-fired" values.
> 5. **Neglecting anaerobic condition** when explaining peat formation — this is the most frequently missed critical point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Points to Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Peat is the FIRST (lowest) rank** in the coalification series — not a true coal.
- **Formed under anaerobic, waterlogged conditions** in bogs, swamps, and marshes.
- **Carbon content: 55–60 % (daf)**; GCV: **1500–4500 kcal/kg**.
- **Moisture in situ: 80–90 %**; air-dried: **8–15 %**.
- **High volatile matter (50–65 %)** → burns with **long, smoky flame**.
- **Main gaseous products of decay:** CO₂, CH₄, H₂O.
- **Acid formed:** Humic acid (signature of peat).
- **Goutal's formula** is the standard quick-calculation tool for GCV.
- **Dulong's formula** is the rigorous alternative.
- **Disadvantages:** high moisture, low CV, spontaneous combustion, smoky, polluting.
- **Uses:** domestic fuel (briquettes), small-scale power (Finland, Ireland), soil conditioner.
- **Peat bogs act as massive carbon sinks** — ~30 % of global soil carbon.
- **Country leaders in peat use:** Ireland, Finland, Russia, Scotland, Sweden.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lignite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lignite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lignite**, commonly referred to as *brown coal*, is a soft, low-rank sedimentary rock of the carbon series formed from naturally compressed peat. It is characterized by a high moisture content (typically **30–60%**), a relatively low carbon content (**25–35%** on a dry basis**), and a calorific value that generally lies between **6,700–17,000 kJ/kg (1,600–4,100 kcal/kg)**. Geologically, lignite represents an intermediate stage in the coalification series between peat and sub-bituminous coal, making it the youngest and least carbonized form of naturally occurring coal.

According to the **ASTM D388** classification standard, lignite is defined as a non-agglomerating coal having a gross calorific value (moist, mineral-matter-free basis) of **less than 8,300 Btu/lb (19,310 kJ/kg)**. The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus on Fuel Engineering classifies lignite as a critical *primary solid fuel* owing to its abundance and its strategic role as a transitional energy resource.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Lignite is treated as an independent solid fuel category. Students must distinguish it clearly from bituminous coal, peat, and sub-bituminous coal based on calorific value, moisture, and fixed carbon content.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a sandwich left forgotten on a kitchen counter for several weeks. The bread (the original plant matter) begins to dry out, compress, and harden under its own weight. Lignite is nature's own version of this slow "compression process" — but instead of weeks, it takes **millions of years** under heat and pressure inside the Earth's crust.

- The **fresh vegetable matter (peat)** corresponds to the freshly baked sandwich — full of water, soft, and uncompressed.
- The **lignite** is the slightly stale, partially dried, and lightly compressed version.
- Continuing to apply pressure and heat yields **sub-bituminous → bituminous → anthracite** — the fully hardened, energy-dense "toast" of the carbon world.

This analogy helps students remember that lignite is essentially "young coal" — it has *not yet cooked long enough* underground to become a high-energy fuel.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Typical Range (Lignite) |
|----------|--------------------------|
| Moisture Content | **30–60%** (as-received) |
| Volatile Matter (dry) | **45–60%** |
| Fixed Carbon (dry) | **25–35%** |
| Ash Content | **4–15%** |
| Gross Calorific Value (as-received) | **6,700–17,000 kJ/kg** |
| Density | **~640–860 kg/m³** |
| Color | Brown to dark brown |
| Texture | Woody, earthy, friable |

> [!NOTE]
> The exceptionally **high moisture content** is the single most defining attribute of lignite and the principal reason it is rarely transported long distances — most lignite is burned in *mine-mouth power plants*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin and Coalification Series

Lignite is formed through the process of **coalification**, the progressive physical and chemical transformation of plant biomass (peat) into coal under the combined influence of:

1. **Pressure** from overlying sedimentary strata.
2. **Heat** from the Earth's geothermal gradient (~30°C/km).
3. **Time** spanning millions of years.
4. **Anaerobic conditions** preventing complete oxidation.

The complete coalification series in chronological order is:

$$\text{Peat} \longrightarrow \text{Lignite} \longrightarrow \text{Sub-bituminous Coal} \longrightarrow \text{Bituminous Coal} \longrightarrow \text{Anthracite} \longrightarrow \text{Graphite}$$

At each stage, the following systematic changes occur:
- **Moisture** decreases (from ~90% in peat to ~1% in anthracite).
- **Carbon content** increases (from ~50% in peat to ~95% in anthracite).
- **Calorific value** rises and then stabilizes.
- **Volatile matter** decreases.
- **Hardness and density** increase.

### 2.2 Chemical Composition of Lignite

On a **dry, mineral-matter-free (dmmf)** basis, lignite typically contains:

| Element | Composition Range |
|---------|-------------------|
| Carbon (C) | **60–75%** |
| Hydrogen (H) | **5–7%** |
| Oxygen (O) | **15–25%** |
| Nitrogen (N) | **0.5–1.5%** |
| Sulfur (S) | **0.5–3.0%** |

The relatively **high oxygen content** (compared to bituminous coal) is responsible for lignite's *hygroscopic nature* — it readily absorbs atmospheric moisture, which is a major engineering drawback.

### 2.3 Why Lignite has Lower Calorific Value — The "Why" Explanation

The low calorific value of lignite is fundamentally attributable to three factors:
- **High moisture content (M):** Water absorbs heat during vaporization (latent heat of vaporization ≈ 2,260 kJ/kg), drastically reducing net usable energy.
- **High oxygen content (O):** Oxygen is already chemically bound to carbon, so less heat is released during combustion (the C–O bonds have already consumed part of the energy).
- **Low fixed carbon (FC):** Less combustible solid residue remains after volatile matter is driven off.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formulas for Lignite Calculations**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units |
|---|--------------|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | Dulong's Formula (GCV) | $\text{GCV} = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]\ \text{kcal/kg}$ | C, H, O, S in mass % |
| 2 | Calorific Value on dry basis | $\text{GCV}_{\text{dry}} = \dfrac{\text{GCV}_{\text{wet}}}{1 - M}$ | M = moisture fraction |
| 3 | NCV (Net CV) | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9\,H \times \text{latent heat}$ | H in %; latent = 587 kcal/kg |
| 4 | Air Requirement (stoichiometric) | $\text{Air} = \dfrac{11.6\,C + 34.8\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S}{100}\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$ | C, H, O, S in % |
| 5 | Proximate Analysis Sum Check | $M + VM + FC + A = 100\%$ | All in mass % |
| 6 | Ultimate Analysis Sum | $C + H + O + N + S + A + M = 100\%$ | All in mass % |
| 7 | Hydrogen Correction | $H_{\text{actual}} = H_{\text{fuel}} - \dfrac{M}{9}$ | Moisture M in % |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mine-mouth power generation:** Lignite powers large-scale thermal stations located adjacent to mining sites (e.g., *Neyveli Lignite Corporation* in Tamil Nadu, India).
- **Synthetic Natural Gas (SNG):** Lignite is gasified to produce substitute natural gas.
- **Urea and Fertilizer Production:** The ammonia–urea industry utilizes lignite-derived synthesis gas.
- **Activated carbon and humic acid production:** Lignite is a feedstock for soil conditioners and specialty chemicals.
- **Drying and Briquetting:** Lignite is upgraded into *briquettes* after dewatering, raising its calorific value to ~20,000 kJ/kg.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — GCV of Lignite from Dulong's Formula

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A sample of lignite has the following ultimate analysis (as-received basis):

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 40.5\%, \quad H = 3.2\%, \quad O = 22.8\%, \\
N &= 0.8\%, \quad S = 0.5\%, \quad \text{Moisture} = 28.0\%, \quad \text{Ash} = 4.2\%
\end{aligned}$$

Calculate the **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** using Dulong's formula. State whether this value is consistent with lignite classification.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify the analysis sums to 100%.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= C + H + O + N + S + M + A \\
&= 40.5 + 3.2 + 22.8 + 0.8 + 0.5 + 28.0 + 4.2 \\
&= 100.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

The analysis is self-consistent. ✓

**Step 2 — Substitute values into Dulong's formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right] \\
\text{GCV} &= \frac{1}{100}\left[8080(40.5) + 34500\left(3.2 - \frac{22.8}{8}\right) + 2240(0.5)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Oxygen correction term O/8.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{O}{8} &= \frac{22.8}{8} = 2.85
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bracketed term inside H.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H - \frac{O}{8} &= 3.2 - 2.85 = 0.35
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the high oxygen content of lignite drastically reduces the effective hydrogen available for combustion. This is the principal chemical reason behind lignite's low GCV.

**Step 5 — Evaluate each term of Dulong's formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
8080 \times C &= 8080 \times 40.5 = 327{,}240 \\
34500 \times \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) &= 34500 \times 0.35 = 12{,}075 \\
2240 \times S &= 2240 \times 0.5 = 1{,}120
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Sum the three terms.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= \frac{1}{100}\left[327{,}240 + 12{,}075 + 1{,}120\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{100}\left[340{,}435\right] \\
&= 3{,}404.35\ \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to SI units (kJ/kg) for international conformity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= 3{,}404.35 \times 4.186 \\
&\approx 14{,}250\ \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Interpret the result.**

$$\text{GCV}_{\text{as-received}} = 3{,}404.35\ \text{kcal/kg} \approx 14{,}250\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

This value falls **squarely within the lignite range (6,700–17,000 kJ/kg)**, confirming the classification. ✓

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Converting GCV to Dry Basis

**Problem Statement:**
