For the lignite in Problem 3.1, compute the GCV on a **dry basis** and the **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** on an as-received basis.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the dry-basis conversion.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV}_{\text{dry}} &= \frac{\text{GCV}_{\text{as-received}}}{1 - M} \\
&= \frac{3404.35}{1 - 0.28} \\
&= \frac{3404.35}{0.72} \\
&= 4{,}728.26\ \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the maximum theoretical calorific value once all moisture is removed.

**Step 2 — Apply the NCV formula.**

The standard latent heat of vaporization of water at 100°C is **587 kcal/kg**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= \text{GCV} - 9 \times H \times 587\ \text{kcal/kg} \\
&= 3404.35 - 9 \times 3.2 \times 587 \\
&= 3404.35 - 16{,}905.6 \times 0.001 \\
&\text{(Correction in kcal/kg)} \\
\text{NCV} &= 3404.35 - 16.91 \\
&= 3{,}387.44\ \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always subtract the latent heat correction using the *fuel hydrogen* content, **not** the total hydrogen in the fuel including the hydrogen bound in moisture.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Lignite vs. Other Coal Ranks

| Parameter | Peat | **Lignite** | Sub-bituminous | Bituminous | Anthracite |
|-----------|------|-------------|----------------|------------|------------|
| Moisture (as-received) | > 75% | **30–60%** | 15–30% | 5–15% | < 5% |
| Carbon (dmmf) | 50–60% | **60–75%** | 75–85% | 85–90% | > 90% |
| GCV (kcal/kg, AR) | < 2,500 | **1,600–4,100** | 4,100–7,000 | 7,000–8,800 | > 8,800 |
| Volatile Matter (dry) | > 60% | **45–60%** | 35–45% | 15–35% | < 10% |
| Caking Property | None | **None** | None | Variable | None |
| Color | Brown | **Brown** | Dull black | Black | Black, lustrous |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Coalification Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["PEAT\nFresh plant debris\nHigh moisture, low C"]
    nodeB["LIGNITE\nBrown coal\n30-60 percent moisture"]
    nodeC["SUB-BITUMINOUS\nDull black coal\nLower moisture"]
    nodeD["BITUMINOUS\nHard black coal\nHigh energy density"]
    nodeE["ANTHRACITE\nHighest rank coal\n90 plus percent C"]
    nodeF["GRAPHITE\nMetamorphic end product"]

    nodeA -->|"Burial +\nHeat + Pressure"| nodeB
    nodeB -->|"Continued\nmetamorphism"| nodeC
    nodeC -->|"Increased\ncompression"| nodeD
    nodeD -->|"Maximum\nthermal alteration"| nodeE
    nodeE -->|"Prolonged\ngeologic time"| nodeF

    style nodeA fill:#A8C686
    style nodeB fill:#C49A6C
    style nodeC fill:#555555
    style nodeD fill:#1A1A1A
    style nodeE fill:#0D0D0D
    style nodeF fill:#404040
```

### 4.2 Lignite Analysis Pathway (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawLignite["Raw Lignite Sample"]
    proximate["Proximate Analysis"]
    ultimate["Ultimate Analysis"]
    calorimetry["Bomb Calorimetry"]

    rawLignite --> proximate
    rawLignite --> ultimate
    rawLignite --> calorimetry

    proximate --> pM["Moisture M"]
    proximate --> pVM["Volatile Matter VM"]
    proximate --> pFC["Fixed Carbon FC"]
    proximate --> pA["Ash A"]

    ultimate --> uC["Carbon C"]
    ultimate --> uH["Hydrogen H"]
    ultimate --> uO["Oxygen O"]
    ultimate --> uN["Nitrogen N"]
    ultimate --> uS["Sulfur S"]

    calorimetry --> gcv["Gross Calorific Value GCV"]
    gcv --> ncv["Net Calorific Value NCV"]

    pM --> calc["Energy Calculations"]
    pVM --> calc
    pFC --> calc
    pA --> calc
    uC --> calc
    uH --> calc
    uO --> calc
    uS --> calc
    gcv --> calc

    calc --> qualityCheck["Fuel Quality Classification\nASTM D388 / KTU Standards"]
```

### 4.3 Lignite End-Use Applications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL["Lignite Resource"]
    nodeDry["Drying and\nUpgradation Plant"]
    nodeBriq["Briquette\nProduction"]
    nodePower["Mine-mouth\nPower Plant"]
    nodeGas["Gasification Unit"]
    nodeChem["Chemical Feedstock"]

    nodeL --> nodeDry
    nodeDry --> nodeBriq
    nodeDry --> nodePower
    nodeL --> nodeGas
    nodeGas --> nodeChem
    nodeL --> nodePower

    style nodeL fill:#C49A6C
    style nodePower fill:#FFCC00
    style nodeBriq fill:#88AA66
    style nodeGas fill:#6688CC
    style nodeChem fill:#AA6688
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The dominant end-use pathway for lignite in India and globally is **mine-mouth power generation**, owing to the prohibitive cost of transporting high-moisture fuel over long distances.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Define lignite. List any four characteristic properties that distinguish it from bituminous coal.

**Model Answer:**
Lignite, also known as **brown coal**, is a low-rank, soft, sedimentary coal formed by the partial coalification of peat. It is the youngest form of naturally occurring coal.

Four distinguishing properties compared to bituminous coal:

1. **Higher moisture content (30–60%)** vs. bituminous (5–15%).
2. **Lower calorific value (1,600–4,100 kcal/kg)** vs. bituminous (7,000–8,800 kcal/kg).
3. **Lower fixed carbon (25–35% dry)** vs. bituminous (45–65%).
4. **No caking or coking property** — lignite does not soften or agglomerate on heating.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** What are the major drawbacks of using lignite as a fuel in thermal power plants?

**Model Answer:**
Major drawbacks:
1. **High moisture content** — reduces net calorific value and requires extensive pre-drying.
2. **Spontaneous combustion** — high VM and reactive nature make storage hazardous.
3. **High ash and sulfur in some deposits** — cause slagging and environmental pollution.
4. **Transportation cost** — cannot be economically shipped; hence requires mine-mouth plants.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 1 Internal Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **formation, classification, and physico-chemical properties of lignite**. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Formation:** Lignite is formed from peat through the process of **coalification**. Dead plant matter accumulates in swamps and bogs where anaerobic conditions slow decomposition. Over millions of years, it is buried under successive layers of sediment, undergoing progressive compaction, dewatering, and geochemical maturation. The geothermal gradient (~30°C/km) provides the heat necessary to drive off oxygenated functional groups, gradually enriching the residue in carbon.

**Classification (ASTM D388):**
Lignite is a non-agglomerating coal with GCV (moist, mineral-matter-free) < **8,300 Btu/lb (19,310 kJ/kg)**. Indian classification (IS: 770) sub-divides lignite into:
- **High-ash lignite** (A > 15%)
- **Normal lignite** (A = 4–15%)

**Physico-chemical Properties:**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Moisture | 30–60% |
| Ash | 4–15% |
| VM (dry) | 45–60% |
| FC (dry) | 25–35% |
| C (dmmf) | 60–75% |
| H (dmmf) | 5–7% |
| O (dmmf) | 15–25% |
| GCV | 6,700–17,000 kJ/kg |
| Color | Brown to dark brown |
| Texture | Woody, friable |

**(b)** A lignite sample has the following ultimate analysis:

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 38.0\%, \quad H = 3.0\%, \quad O = 20.0\%, \\
N &= 1.0\%, \quad S = 0.5\%, \quad M = 30.0\%, \quad A = 7.5\%
\end{aligned}$$

Calculate the **GCV (as-received)** and the **NCV** using Dulong's formula. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sum-check.**

$$\begin{aligned}
38.0 + 3.0 + 20.0 + 1.0 + 0.5 + 30.0 + 7.5 = 100.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute H − O/8.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H - \frac{O}{8} &= 3.0 - \frac{20.0}{8} = 3.0 - 2.5 = 0.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Dulong's formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= \frac{1}{100}\left[8080 \times 38.0 + 34500 \times 0.5 + 2240 \times 0.5\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{100}\left[307{,}040 + 17{,}250 + 1{,}120\right] \\
&= \frac{325{,}410}{100} \\
&= 3{,}254.10\ \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to SI.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= 3254.10 \times 4.186 \\
&= 13{,}621\ \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute NCV.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= \text{GCV} - 9 \times H \times 587 \\
&= 3254.10 - 9 \times 3.0 \times 587 \\
&= 3254.10 - 15{,}849 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 3254.10 - 15.85 \\
&= 3{,}238.25\ \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{GCV} = 3{,}254.10\ \text{kcal/kg} \quad;\quad \text{NCV} = 3{,}238.25\ \text{kcal/kg}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **proximate and ultimate analysis** of lignite. Why is proximate analysis alone insufficient for accurate calorific value estimation? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Proximate Analysis** determines four empirical parameters:
- **Moisture (M)** — mass loss on heating at ~110°C.
- **Volatile Matter (VM)** — mass loss on heating at 925°C in absence of air.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC)** — residual combustible matter (by difference: 100 − M − VM − Ash).
- **Ash (A)** — non-combustible residue after complete combustion.

**Ultimate Analysis** determines the elemental composition:
- **Carbon, Hydrogen, Nitrogen, Sulfur** (by direct analytical methods).
- **Oxygen** (typically by difference: 100 − C − H − N − S − Ash).

**Why proximate alone is insufficient:**
Proximate analysis is empirical and does not reveal the actual chemical composition. Two lignites with identical proximate analyses can have substantially different GCVs because the *elemental* composition (especially oxygen and hydrogen ratios) governs the heat of combustion. Ultimate analysis, when combined with Dulong's formula, allows accurate GCV estimation. Therefore, ultimate analysis is essential for precise calorific value calculation.

**(b)** Compare lignite with **bituminous coal and peat** on the basis of (i) moisture, (ii) fixed carbon, (iii) GCV, and (iv) caking property. State two major industrial applications of lignite. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Tabular comparison.**

| Property | Peat | **Lignite** | Bituminous Coal |
|----------|------|-------------|------------------|
| Moisture (AR) | > 75% | **30–60%** | 5–15% |
| Fixed Carbon (dry) | < 25% | **25–35%** | 50–70% |
| GCV (kcal/kg, AR) | < 2,500 | **1,600–4,100** | 7,000–8,800 |
| Caking Property | Absent | **Absent** | Strong |

**Step 2 — Industrial applications.**

1. **Mine-mouth thermal power generation** — Lignite powers large captive power stations (e.g., Neyveli, Rajasthan lignite projects).
2. **Gasification for chemical feedstock** — Lignite is gasified to produce **synthesis gas (CO + H₂)** used in ammonia/urea manufacture.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently confuse** the moisture content of lignite with that of sub-bituminous coal. State the *numerical range* explicitly.
> - In Dulong's formula, students often **omit the O/8 correction** for oxygen. Always show the corrected hydrogen term.
> - For NCV, students **incorrectly** use total hydrogen (including moisture-bound H) instead of the *dry-fuel hydrogen*. This loses 1–2 marks.
> - When asked to "classify," do **not** answer only "it is a coal." Specify **rank, ASTM band, and calorific value**.
> - Always include **units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless numerical results.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Lignite = brown coal**, low-rank sedimentary fuel.
- 🔹 **GCV range:** 1,600–4,100 kcal/kg (6,700–17,000 kJ/kg).
- 🔹 **Moisture content (30–60%)** is the single most defining property.
- 🔹 **High oxygen (15–25% dmmf)** causes hygroscopicity and low heating value.
- 🔹 **No caking property** — cannot be used in metallurgical coke production.
- 🔹 **Dulong's formula:** $\text{GCV} = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]\ \text{kcal/kg}$
- 🔹 **NCV correction:** $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \times H \times 587\ \text{kcal/kg}$
- 🔹 **ASTM D388 boundary:** GCV (moist, mmf) < **8,300 Btu/lb**.
- 🔹 **End use:** predominantly **mine-mouth power generation**; secondary use in gasification.
- 🔹 **Indian context:** Major deposits in **Neyveli (Tamil Nadu), Rajasthan, Gujarat, and Kerala.**
- 🔹 **Coalification order:** Peat → Lignite → Sub-bituminous → Bituminous → Anthracite → Graphite.
- 🔹 Always **check sum = 100%** before applying Dulong's formula.
- 🔹 Always **include units** in final numerical answers (kcal/kg or kJ/kg).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub-Bituminous Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sub-Bituminous Coal # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Sub-Bituminous Coal** is a black, sedimentary solid fossil fuel that occupies an intermediate position in the coalification series between **lignite (brown coal)** and **bituminous coal**. According to the **ASTM D388-23** classification standard (the most widely adopted global standard for coal rank classification), sub-bituminous coal is defined on the basis of its **agglomerating character, gross calorific value (moist, mineral-matter-free basis), and fixed carbon content**. Formally stated: > Sub-bituminous coal is a non-agglomerating coal with a gross calorific value (on a moist, mineral-matter-free basis) ranging from **8,300 Btu/lb (19.3 MJ/kg) to 11,500 Btu/lb (26.7 MJ/kg)** and a fixed carbon content (dry, mineral-matter-free basis) less than **69%**. It is sub-divided into three sub-ranks: - **Sub-bituminous C coal** — 8,300–9,500 Btu/lb - **Sub-bituminous B coal** — 9,500–10,500 Btu/lb - **Sub-bituminous A coal** — 10,500–11,500 Btu/lb > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Sub-bituminous coal is essential knowledge for Module 1 because it represents the dominant rank currently mined for thermal power generation globally and in India, accounting for approximately **30–35% of world coal production**. Understanding its properties is foundational to the syllabus units on solid fuels and combustion. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Toasted Bread" Model of Coalification Think of a loaf of bread. The original wheat (raw plant material/peat) is soft, wet, and light. As you toast it: - First level (lignite): the bread becomes warm-brown, still soft, high moisture. - Second level (sub-bituminous): medium-brown, drier, firmer — this is our topic. - Third level (bituminous): dark-brown to black, hard, dense, lots of carbon. - Fourth level (anthracite): charred black, brittle, mostly carbon. **Sub-bituminous coal is "well-toasted bread"** — it has lost much of its water, gained appreciable carbon, but has not yet become the dense, hard, lustrous "charcoal" of bituminous or anthracite rank. > [!NOTE] > **Physical State Snapshot:** If you held a lump of sub-bituminous coal in your hand, you would notice: (1) black color (occasionally dark brown), (2) dull-to-submetallic luster, (3) relatively soft (Mohs hardness ~2), (4) noticeable heft but lighter than bituminous coal, (5) tendency to break in roughly rectangular pieces (conchoidal–cuboidal fracture). ## 1.3 Position in the Coal Rank Hierarchy $$ \text{Peat} \rightarrow \text{Lignite} \rightarrow \textbf{Sub-Bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Anthracite} $$ | Property | Lignite | **Sub-Bituminous** | Bituminous | Anthracite | |----------|---------|-------------------|------------|------------| | Carbon (daf, % w/w) | 25–35 | **35–45** | 45–86 | 86–98 | | Volatile Matter (daf, %) | 50–65 | **40–55** | 14–50 | 2–14 | | Moisture (as-received, %) | 30–60 | **15–30** | 2–15 | 1–5 | | GCV (MJ/kg) | 10–17 | **17–24** | 24–35 | 35–37 | *daf = dry, ash-free basis* --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway — Sub-Bituminous Coal in One Line:** > It is the *transitional, non-coking, black solid fuel* with **moderate carbon (35–45% daf), high moisture (15–30%), moderate calorific value (17–24 MJ/kg)**, primarily used in **pulverized coal-fired thermal power plants**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin and Coalification Pathway Sub-bituminous coal originated during the **Cretaceous to Tertiary geological period (approximately 65–145 million years ago)** through progressive biochemical and geochemical alteration (coalification) of accumulated plant debris in **sub-bituminous coal-forming swamps** — typically in low-lying, waterlogged, lacustrine or deltaic environments. The transformation involves: 1. **Biochemical stage** — anaerobic bacterial decay converting cellulose/lignin to peat. 2. **Geochemical stage** — overburden pressure and geothermal heat (60–120 °C) driving dehydration, decarboxylation, and de-methanation reactions. The dominant chemical change from lignite → sub-bituminous is: - **Loss of oxygen** (as CO₂ and H₂O) - **Increase in carbon content** - **Decrease in moisture content** ## 2.2 Chemical Composition — Two Complementary Analytical Frameworks ### 2.2.1 Proximate Analysis (Practical, Industrial) | Component | Range (%, as-received) | KTU-Relevant Range | |-----------|------------------------|---------------------| | Moisture | 15–30 | **20–28** | | Volatile Matter (VM) | 30–50 | **35–45** | | Fixed Carbon (FC) | 30–55 | **32–45** | | Ash | 3–12 | **5–9** | The governing relationship (always true for any solid fuel): $$ \text{Moisture} + \text{VM} + \text{FC} + \text{Ash} = 100\% $$ ### 2.2.2 Ultimate Analysis (Elemental) | Element | Range (%, dry basis) | |---------|----------------------| | Carbon (C) | 60–75 | | Hydrogen (H) | 4.0–6.0 | | Oxygen (O) | 15–25 | | Nitrogen (N) | 0.5–2.0 | | Sulfur (S) | 0.3–1.5 | Always: $$ \text{C} + \text{H} + \text{O} + \text{N} + \text{S} + \text{Ash} = 100\% $$ ## 2.3 Physical Characteristics (Engineered Specification) - **Color:** black to dark brown - **Luster:** dull to sub-metallic (vitreous when freshly broken) - **Density:** 1.20–1.45 g/cm³ (apparent bulk density lower) - **Hardness:** Mohs 2–2.5 - **Fracture:** irregular, subconchoidal - **Weathering tendency:** moderate (slacks on prolonged air exposure; loses integrity due to moisture loss) - **Spontaneous combustion:** HIGH RISK in storage due to reactive macerals (especially vitrinite and exinite) and retained moisture generating exothermic oxidation heat. ## 2.4 Why Sub-Bituminous Coal Matters in Engineering | Engineering Aspect | Significance | |---------------------|-------------| | **Power Generation** | Single largest use — pulverized-coal (PC) and circulating fluidized-bed combustion (CFBC) plants in India, USA (Powder River Basin), Indonesia | | **Emission Profile** | Lower sulfur (≤ 1.5%) than many bituminous coals → favorable for SO₂ control | | **CO₂ Intensity** | ~ 90–95 kg CO₂/GJ (higher per MJ than bituminous due to lower CV) | | **Handling** | Prone to spontaneous combustion; storage piles must be compacted and rotated | | **Transport Economics** | High moisture (15–30%) inflates transport cost per delivered MJ | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save This Block — These Equations Appear in 80% of KTU Coal Calculations** | # | Formula | Purpose | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 H \cdot 587$ | Net vs Gross Calorific Value correction for water of combustion | H = hydrogen % by mass; result in kJ/kg | | 2 | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 H \cdot 587$ | Alternate form (0.09 = 9/100, when H in %) | kJ/kg | | 3 | $\text{GCV}_{\text{daf}} = \dfrac{\text{GCV}_{\text{ar}}}{100 - M - A} \times 100$ | Convert as-received GCV to dry-ash-free basis | M, A in % | | 4 | $\text{FC} = 100 - (M + \text{VM} + A)$ | Fixed carbon from proximate analysis | % by mass | | 5 | Dulong's: $\text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$ | Theoretical GCV from ultimate analysis | kJ/kg; C, H, O, S in mass fractions or % (factor adjusted) | | 6 | $\text{O}_{\min} = \dfrac{32}{12}C + \dfrac{32}{4}\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + \dfrac{32}{32}S$ | Theoretical O₂ for combustion | kg O₂/kg fuel | | 7 | $\text{Air}_{\min} = \dfrac{100}{21}\,\text{O}_{\min}$ | Stoichiometric air (mass basis) | kg air/kg fuel | | 8 | $\% \text{ excess air} = \dfrac{\text{Air actual} - \text{Air}_{\min}}{\text{Air}_{\min}} \times 100$ | Combustion excess | % | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Dulong's Formula:** **C-H-O-S → 8080–34500–2240.** Write down the constant (8080), then for every unit mass of *combustible hydrogen* ($\mathrm{H_{net} = H - O/8}$) multiply by 34500, and for sulfur add 2240. This is a guaranteed 3–4 mark recovery in KTU Part B problems. ## 2.6 Industrial Application — Indian Power Sector Context India's installed thermal capacity (~ 220 GW as of 2024) is dominated by sub-bituminous and bituminous coal. **Indian sub-bituminous reserves** are concentrated in: - **Jharkhand** (East Bokaro, Karanpura) - **West Bengal** (Raniganj) - **Madhya Pradesh** (Singrauli, Sohagpur) - **Maharashtra** (Wardha Valley) - **Assam & Meghalaya** (Makum) These coals typically have **GCV = 17–22 MJ/kg** (lower than Indonesian sub-bituminous), making them economically transportable to coastal power plants for blending with imported coal. --- > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall — Dulong's Formula Units:** Students frequently write Dulong's equation with constants in cal/g but substitute C, H, O, S in mass %. If C, H, O, S are in mass %, multiply each constant by 100 (or divide C, H, O, S by 100). Always state units explicitly to avoid losing 1–2 marks. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical — Proximate-to-Fixed Carbon Balance > **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A sub-bituminous coal sample has the following proximate analysis (as-received basis): Moisture = 22%, Volatile Matter = 36%, Ash = 7%. Determine: (a) Fixed Carbon by difference, (b) GCV using Dulong's formula, given ultimate analysis: C = 64%, H = 4.8%, O = 17.5%, S = 0.6% (all on dry basis). ### Step 1 — Fix-Carbon by Difference The proximate analysis components must sum to 100%: $$ \begin{aligned} M + \text{VM} + \text{FC} + A &= 100\% \\ 22 + 36 + \text{FC} + 7 &= 100 \\ \text{FC} &= 100 - 65 \\ \text{FC} &= 35\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Convert Ultimate Analysis from Dry to As-Received Basis Ultimate analysis is conventionally given on dry basis. To convert to as-received basis, multiply by $(1 - M/100)$ where M = 0.22. $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{ar}} &= 64 \times (1 - 0.22) = 64 \times 0.78 = 49.92\% \\ H_{\text{ar}} &= 4.8 \times 0.78 = 3.744\% \\ O_{\text{ar}} &= 17.5 \times 0.78 = 13.65\% \\ S_{\text{ar}} &= 0.6 \times 0.78 = 0.468\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Dulong's Formula (as-received basis, kJ/kg) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GCV} &= 8080 \cdot C_{\text{ar}} + 34500 \cdot \left(H_{\text{ar}} - \dfrac{O_{\text{ar}}}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S_{\text{ar}} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting numerical values: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GCV} &= 8080 \times 49.92 + 34500 \times \left(3.744 - \dfrac{13.65}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.468 \\ &= 8080 \times 49.92 + 34500 \times (3.744 - 1.70625) + 2240 \times 0.468 \\ &= 403,353.6 + 34500 \times 2.03775 + 1,048.32 \\ &= 403,353.6 + 70,302.375 + 1,048.32 \\ &= 474,704.3\ \text{kJ/kg (as-received)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute NCV (Net Calorific Value) Using $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \cdot H_{\text{ar}} \cdot 587 / 100$ (with H in %, 9 × 587 / 100 = 52.83): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NCV} &= 474{,}704.3 - 9 \times 3.744 \times 587 \\ &= 474{,}704.3 - 19{,}778.8 \\ &= 454{,}925.5\ \text{kJ/kg (as-received)} \\ &\approx 455\ \text{MJ/kg (as-received)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The numerical GCV (~ 17.5 MJ/kg dry, ~ 13.7 MJ/kg as-received) is in the typical sub-bituminous range, confirming the rank identification. The huge gap between GCV_ar (17.5 MJ/kg on dry-equivalent) and NCV is dominated by the evaporation of the 22% inherent moisture. ## 3.2 Stoichiometric Air Calculation for Sub-Bituminous Coal Given the dry ultimate analysis above, compute the minimum O₂ required per kg of dry coal: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 & \text{O}_2 \text{ needed} &= \dfrac{32}{12} \times 64 = 170.67\ \text{kg} \\ \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} & \text{O}_2 \text{ needed} &= \dfrac{32}{4} \times 4.8 = 38.40\ \text{kg} \\ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{SO}_2 & \text{O}_2 \text{ needed} &= \dfrac{32}{32} \times 0.6 = 0.60\ \text{kg} \\ \text{O}_2 \text{ already in coal} &= \dfrac{32}{32} \times 17.5 = 17.50\ \text{kg (subtract)} \\ \hline \text{Net O}_2 \text{ required} &= 170.67 + 38.40 + 0.60 - 17.50 = 192.17\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{100 kg coal} \end{aligned} $$ Per kg coal: $$ \text{O}_{2,\min} = 1.922\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg coal} $$ Stoichiometric (theoretical) air: $$ \text{Air}_{\min} = \dfrac{1.922}{0.232} = 8.28\ \text{kg air/kg coal (dry)} $$ (Using 23.2% O₂ by mass in air, instead of 21% by volume converted to mass basis.) For 25% excess air (typical PC boiler): $$ \text{Air}_{\text{actual}} = 8.28 \times 1.25 = 10.35\ \text{kg/kg coal} $$ ## 3.3 Rank-Check Using Parr's Formula Parr's formula classifies coal rank using the **unit coal** (dry, mineral-matter-free) basis: $$ \text{Unit Coal C} = \dfrac{\text{FC}_{\text{daf}}}{\text{VM}_{\text{daf}} + \text{FC}_{\text{daf}}} \times 100 = \%\ \text{C in unit coal} $$ For our coal: $\text{FC}_{\text{daf}} = 35/(100-22-7) \times 100 = 35/71 \times 100 = 49.30\%$; $\text{VM}_{\text{daf}} = 36/71 \times 100 = 50.70\%$. $$ \text{Parr Index} = \dfrac{49.30}{50.70 + 49.30} \times 100 = 49.30\% \ \text{C in unit coal} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result Interpretation:** Parr Index of 49.30% places the coal firmly in the **sub-bituminous rank (range 46–52%)**. If the value were < 46%, it would classify as lignite; if > 52%, it would enter the bituminous range. This single calculation is worth 2–3 marks in KTU valuation. ## 3.4 Conversion Between Bases — Summary $$ \text{C}_{\text{daf}} = \dfrac{\text{C}_{\text{ar}}}{100 - M_{\text{ar}} - A_{\text{ar}}} \times 100 $$ For our sample: $$ \text{C}_{\text{daf}} = \dfrac{64}{100 - 22 - 7} \times 100 = \dfrac{64}{71} \times 100 = 90.14\% $$ This value is the carbon fraction on the "pure-coal" basis, used for rank identification. The empirical rule: $$ \text{Sub-bituminous:} \quad 75\% < \text{C}_{\text{daf}} < 85\% \quad \text{(depending on sub-rank A, B, or C)} $$ A $C_{daf}$ of 90% would be unusually high for sub-bituminous, suggesting a low-rank bituminous classification instead. KTU expects students to cross-check both CV and $C_{daf}$ before final classification. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Coal Rank Ladder with Sub-Bituminous Highlight ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[PEAT] --> nodeB[LIGNITE] nodeB --> nodeC["SUB-BITUMINOUS
SubC SubB SubA"] nodeC --> nodeD[BITUMINOUS] nodeD --> nodeE[ANTHRACITE] nodeA -.->|"C: 50% daf"| nodeB nodeB -.->|"C: 65-75% daf"| nodeC nodeC -.->|"C: 75-85% daf"| nodeD nodeD -.->|"C: 85-92% daf"| nodeE nodeE --> nodeF[META-ANTHRACITE] style nodeC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:4px,color:#000 style nodeA fill:#8B4513,color:#fff style nodeB fill:#A0522D,color:#fff style nodeD fill:#2F4F4F,color:#fff style nodeE fill:#000000,color:#fff style nodeF fill:#1C1C1C,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The highlighted yellow node marks the focus of this topic — sub-bituminous coal sits between brown lignite and black bituminous, with carbon content (daf) climbing from ~ 65% at the lignite boundary to ~ 85% at the bituminous boundary. ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Complete Coal-to-Power Workflow for Sub-Bituminous Coal ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SRC["RAW COAL SOURCE"] A1["Mined Sub-Bituminous Coal
GCV 17-22 MJ/kg
Moisture 20-28%"] end subgraph PROC["PREPARATION PLANT"] B1["Crushing and Sizing"] B2["Drying optional
if moisture greater than 25%"] B3["Pulverizing to 75 micron"] B4["Storage Silos
risk of spontaneous combustion"] end subgraph BOIL["COMBUSTION UNIT"] C1["Pulverized Coal Boiler
or CFBC Furnace"] C2["Air supply 25 percent excess"] C3["Ash and Slag removal"] end subgraph POST["EMISSION AND ENERGY RECOVERY"] D1["ESP for Particulate Control"] D2["Flue Gas Desulfurization
FGD for SO2"] D3["Steam to Turbine"] D4["Generator to Grid"] end A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C1 --> D1 --> D2 C1 --> D3 --> D4 style A1 fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style C1 fill:#FF6347,color:#fff style D4 fill:#228B22,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical Analysis Flow — Proximate and Ultimate ```mermaid graph TD subgraph INPUT["Coal Sample Input"] S1["Air-dried Coal
Top size minus 212 micron"] end subgraph PROX["Proximate Analysis Block"] P1["Moisture by Oven Drying
105 to 110 C 1 hour"] P2["Volatile Matter
950 C 7 min in covered crucible"] P3["Ash by Combustion
815 C to constant mass"] P4["Fixed Carbon
by Difference 100 minus M VM A"] end subgraph ULT["Ultimate Analysis Block"] U1["Carbon by CHN Analyzer"] U2["Hydrogen by CHN Analyzer"] U3["Nitrogen by Kjeldahl or Dumas"] U4["Sulfur by Eschka method"] U5["Oxygen by Difference"] end subgraph OUT["Derived Outputs"] O1["GCV by Bomb Calorimeter"] O2["NCV by Calculation"] O3["Air Fuel Ratio"] O4["Flue Gas Composition"] end S1 --> P1 & P2 & P3 P1 & P2 & P3 --> P4 S1 --> U1 & U2 & U3 & U4 U1 & U2 & U3 & U4 --> U5 P4 --> O1 U5 --> O2 U5 --> O3 U5 --> O4 style P4 fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style O1 fill:#90EE90,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Storage and Handling Risk Map for Sub-Bituminous Coal ```mermaid graph LR nodeR["Sub-Bituminous Coal
Arrives with 22 percent moisture"] nodeR --> nodeR1["Stockyard
compacted in layers"] nodeR --> nodeR2["Silo Storage
first in first out"] nodeR1 --> nodeR3["Risk: Spontaneous Combustion
due to reactive macerals"] nodeR1 --> nodeR4["Risk: Slacking
fines generation"] nodeR2 --> nodeR5["Risk: Hang-up
moisture bridge"] nodeR2 --> nodeR6["Mitigation: Mechanical Activation
vibration, air cannons"] nodeR3 --> nodeR7["Mitigation: Temperature Monitoring
infrared scanners, gas sampling"] style nodeR fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style nodeR3 fill:#FF6347,color:#fff style nodeR7 fill:#228B22,color:#fff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Sub-bituminous coal is uniquely vulnerable to *spontaneous combustion* during storage because the moisture (15–30%) and reactive oxygen-containing functional groups in the coal matrix (–COOH, –OH, –C=O) exothermically react with O₂ from air. Indian coal handling manuals recommend **temperature monitoring every 6 hours** and **stockpile rotation within 30 days**. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **Define sub-bituminous coal. List its typical proximate analysis range.** **Model Answer:** Sub-bituminous coal is a black, sedimentary solid fossil fuel that occupies an intermediate rank in the coalification series between lignite and bituminous coal. According to ASTM D388, it is a **non-agglomerating coal** with a gross calorific value (moist, mineral-matter-free) between **8,300 and 11,500 Btu/lb (19.3–26.7 MJ/kg)** and fixed carbon (dry, mineral-matter-free) less than 69%. Typical proximate analysis range (as-received basis): | Component | Range | |-----------|-------| | Moisture | 15–30% | | Volatile Matter | 30–50% | | Fixed Carbon | 30–55% | | Ash | 3–12% | It is sub-classified into **Sub-bituminous A, B, and C** based on increasing calorific value. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` **State two key differences between sub-bituminous and bituminous coal.** **Model Answer:** | Property | Sub-Bituminous Coal | Bituminous Coal | |----------|--------------------|-----------------| | Caking/coking property | **Non-coking** (non-agglomerating) | **Coking** (agglomerating; softens on heating) | | Moisture (as-received) | 15–30% | 2–15% | | Carbon content (daf) | 35–45% | 45–86% | | Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | 17–24 | 24–35 | | Volatile Matter (daf) | 40–55% | 14–50% | > The absence of caking property is the most decisive test — sub-bituminous coal does **not fuse or form coke** when heated in the absence of air, while bituminous coal does. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]` **(a)** *Explain with neat sketches the formation and classification of sub-bituminous coal. Discuss its physical and chemical properties in detail.* **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer Outline (Mark Distribution):** **(i) Formation [2 marks]** - Accumulation of plant debris in swamps → biochemical decay → peat → lignite → progressive burial and geothermal heating → sub-bituminous rank. - Conditions: depth 1–4 km, temperature 60–120 °C, age ~ 65–145 million years (Cretaceous–Tertiary). - Schematic: simple flow diagram peat → lignite → sub-bituminous → bituminous → anthracite. **(ii) Classification [2 marks]** - ASTM D388 classifies sub-bituminous into three sub-ranks: C, B, A (based on moist, mineral-matter-free heating value). - Compare with international (ISO 11760) and Indian (BIS 1350) classifications. **(iii) Physical Properties [1.5 marks]** - Color: black to dark brown; Luster: dull to sub-metallic; Hardness: Mohs ~2; Density: 1.20–1.45 g/cm³; Fracture: subconchoidal. **(iv) Chemical Properties [1.5 marks]** - Proximate and ultimate analysis ranges (as above). - Non-coking behavior; tendency to slack and spontaneously combust. --- **(b)** *A sub-bituminous coal has the following composition by mass (as-received basis): C = 49.5%, H = 3.6%, O = 13.4%, N = 0.8%, S = 0.5%, Moisture = 24%, Ash = 8.2%. Calculate: (i) Gross Calorific Value using Dulong's formula, (ii) Net Calorific Value, (iii) Minimum air required for complete combustion per kg of coal.* **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Gross Calorific Value [3 marks]** Dulong's formula: $$ \text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S \quad \text{kJ/kg} $$ Substituting (as-received basis): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GCV} &= 8080 \times 49.5 + 34500 \times \left(3.6 - \dfrac{13.4}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.5 \\ &= 8080 \times 49.5 + 34500 \times (3.6 - 1.675) + 2240 \times 0.5 \\ &= 399{,}960 + 34500 \times 1.925 + 1{,}120 \\ &= 399{,}960 + 66{,}412.5 + 1{,}120 \\ &= 467{,}492.5\ \text{kJ/kg} \\ &\approx 467.5\ \text{MJ/kg (as-received)} \end{aligned} $$ > *Note: As-received GCV is dominated by the high moisture. On dry basis, GCV ≈ 467.5 / 0.758 = 616.6 MJ/kg — i.e., ~ 24.6 MJ/kg dry, consistent with sub-bituminous rank.* **(ii) Net Calorific Value [2 marks]** Using $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9H \cdot 587 / 100$ with H in %: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NCV} &= 467{,}492.5 - \dfrac{9 \times 3.6 \times 587}{100} \\ &= 467{,}492.5 - 190.19 \\ &= 467{,}302.3\ \text{kJ/kg} \\ &\approx 467.3\ \text{MJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ > *(If you used the relation $NCV = GCV - (9H + M) \times 587 / 100$ accounting for moisture, NCV ≈ 467.5 − (32.4 + 24) × 587/100 ≈ 467.5 − 331 ≈ 136.5 kJ/kg difference.)* **(iii) Minimum Air Required [2 marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{O}_2^{\min} &= \dfrac{32}{12}C + \dfrac{32}{4}H_{\text{net}} + \dfrac{32}{32}S \\ &= \dfrac{32}{12} \times 49.5 + 8 \times 1.925 + 1 \times 0.5 \\ &= 132.0 + 15.4 + 0.5 \\ &= 147.9\ \text{kg O}_2 / \text{100 kg coal} \\ &= 1.479\ \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg coal} \end{aligned} $$ Stoichiometric air (using 23.2% O₂ by mass in air): $$ \text{Air}_{\min} = \dfrac{1.479}{0.232} = 6.375\ \text{kg air/kg coal} $$ --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]` **(a)** *Discuss the Indian and global reserves of sub-bituminous coal. What are the major challenges in storing and transporting sub-bituminous coal?* **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer Outline (Mark Distribution):** **(i) Indian Reserves [2 marks]** - India holds ~ 10% of world coal reserves; sub-bituminous contributes ~ 30% of Indian coal output. - Major belts: Jharkhand (East Bokaro, North Karanpura), West Bengal (Raniganj), Madhya Pradesh (Singrauli), Chhattisgarh (Korba), Maharashtra, Assam (Makum). **(ii) Global Reserves [2 marks]** - USA (Powder River Basin, Wyoming — largest sub-bituminous source in the world). - Indonesia (Kalimantan — major exporter to India for blending). - Australia, Colombia, South Africa. **(iii) Storage Challenges [1.5 marks]** - Spontaneous combustion due to reactive macerals + moisture + exothermic oxidation. - Slacking → fines generation → dust + explosion hazard. - Self-heating monitoring with infrared scanners and gas sampling. **(iv) Transport Challenges [1.5 marks]** - High moisture (15–30%) inflates per-tonne transport cost; effectively carrying water to the boiler. - Fines generation during rail/sea transport causes conveyor/feeder blockages. - Weathering degrades CV; thermal oxidation reduces combustion efficiency. --- **(b)** *A 500 MW thermal power plant burns sub-bituminous coal of the following analysis (dry basis): C = 65%, H = 4.5%, O = 18%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.8%, Ash = 10.5%. The boiler operates with 20% excess air. Calculate: (i) Mass of air supplied per kg of coal, (ii) Mass of dry flue gas per kg of coal, (iii) Composition of dry flue gas by volume.* **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Mass of Air Supplied per kg Coal [2 marks]** Theoretical O₂: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{O}_2^{\min} &= \dfrac{32}{12} \times 65 + \dfrac{32}{4} \times 4.5 + \dfrac{32}{32} \times 0.8 - \dfrac{32}{32} \times 18 \\ &= 173.33 + 36.0 + 0.8 - 18.0 \\ &= 192.13\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{100 kg coal} \\ &= 1.9213\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg coal} \end{aligned} $$ Theoretical air: $$ \text{Air}_{\min} = \dfrac{1.9213}{0.232} = 8.282\ \text{kg/kg coal} $$ Actual air with 20% excess: $$ \text{Air}_{\text{actual}} = 8.282 \times 1.20 = 9.938\ \text{kg/kg coal} \approx 9.94\ \text{kg/kg} $$ **(ii) Mass of Dry Flue Gas per kg Coal [3 marks]** Dry flue gas components per kg coal: - $\text{CO}_2$ from C: $(44/12) \times 0.65 = 2.383$ kg - $\text{SO}_2$ from S: $(64/32) \times 0.008 = 0.016$ kg - $\text{N}_2$ from fuel N: $(28/28) \times 0.012 = 0.012$ kg - $\text{N}_2$ from air (79% of 9.94 kg = 7.8526 kg) - $\text{O}_2$ in flue gas = 20% × 1.9213 = 0.3843 kg Total dry flue gas: $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{fg,dry}} &= 2.383 + 0.016 + 0.012 + 7.8526 + 0.3843 \\ &= 10.648\ \text{kg/kg coal} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Composition of Dry Flue Gas by Volume [2 marks]** Convert masses to kmol: - $\text{CO}_2$: $2.383 / 44 = 0.05416$ kmol - $\text{SO}_2$: $0.016 / 64 = 0.00025$ kmol - $\text{N}_2$: $(0.012 + 7.8526) / 28 = 7.8646 / 28 = 0.2809$ kmol - $\text{O}_2$: $0.3843 / 32 = 0.01201$ kmol Total dry flue gas: $0.05416 + 0.00025 + 0.2809 + 0.01201 = 0.3473$ kmol | Component | Volume % | |-----------|----------| | $\text{CO}_2$ | 15.60% | | $\text{SO}_2$ | 0.07% | | $\text{N}_2$ | 80.88% | | $\text{O}_2$ | 3.46% | > Note: $\text{SO}_2$ is negligible in volume terms compared to $\text{CO}_2$ due to low sulfur in sub-bituminous coal. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Sub-Bituminous Coal Problems:** > 1. **Basis confusion (ar vs dry vs daf):** Dulong's formula demands consistency — if you use dry-basis C/H/O/S, you get dry-basis GCV; convert to NCV using *dry-basis H*. Don't mix and match. Lose 1–2 marks if unmarked. > 2. **Moisture subtraction in NCV:** The 9H correction handles ONLY combustion water. For as-received coal, some authors add an additional $M \cdot 587 / 100$ correction for latent heat of vaporizing fuel moisture. Read the question carefully. > 3. **Coking / agglomerating test:** Always state explicitly whether the coal is coking or non-coking when classifying. Sub-bituminous is ALWAYS non-coking. > 4. **Rank classification:** Students often quote just GCV without stating the basis (moist, mineral-matter-free) — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark. > 5. **Excess air in boiler problems:** Default 25% in modern CFBC; 15–20% in PC boilers. Always state assumed % in the solution. > 6. **Unit consistency:** Calorific value units (kJ/kg vs MJ/kg vs kcal/kg) — KTU prefers kJ/kg. Multiply cal/g by 4.184 to get kJ/kg. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sub-Bituminous Coal** - ✅ **Definition:** Non-coking, black, intermediate-rank coal; ASTM GCV (moist, mmf) **8,300–11,500 Btu/lb** = **19.3–26.7 MJ/kg**. - ✅ **Sub-ranks:** Sub-bituminous **C < B < A** (increasing GCV). - ✅ **Position:** Between lignite and bituminous in coalification series. - ✅ **Proximate Analysis (ar):** Moisture 15–30%, VM 30–50%, FC 30–55%, Ash 3–12%. - ✅ **Ultimate Analysis (dry):** C 60–75%, H 4–6%, O 15–25%, N 0.5–2%, S 0.3–1.5%. - ✅ **Calorific Value (dry, daf):** ~ 24–28 MJ/kg; on as-received basis often only 17–22 MJ/kg due to moisture penalty. - ✅ **Caking property:** NEGATIVE — does not coke → unsuitable for metallurgical coke; used for **combustion only**. - ✅ **Primary use:** Pulverized coal (PC) and CFBC power generation. - ✅ **Storage risk:** High spontaneous combustion tendency; moisture monitoring essential. - ✅ **Dulong's formula:** $\text{GCV} = 8080 C + 34500 (H - O/8) + 2240 S$ kJ/kg (use as-received or dry consistently). - ✅ **NCV formula:** $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9H \times 587 / 100$ (kJ/kg with H in %). - ✅ **Stoichiometric air:** $\text{Air}_{\min} = \text{O}_2^{\min} / 0.232$ kg air / kg coal. - ✅ **Indian belts:** Jharkhand, West Bengal, MP, Chhattisgarh, Maharashtra, Assam. - ✅ **Global giant:** Powder River Basin (Wyoming, USA); Indonesia (major exporter). - ✅ **ASTM D388** is the universally accepted ranking standard — cite it for full marks. - ✅ **Parr Index = FC/(FC + VM) × 100** classifies rank in the range 46–52% for sub-bituminous. - ✅ **Lower SO₂ than bituminous** in many cases → easier FGD sizing.

topics.title: Bituminous Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bituminous Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bituminous Coal** is a dense, black, sedimentary rock classified as a **medium-to-high rank coal** within the coalification series. It contains a fixed carbon content ranging between **54% and 86%** (dry, mineral-matter-free basis), with a gross calorific value typically between **24 MJ/kg and 35 MJ/kg** (≈ 5,700 – 8,300 kcal/kg). It is the most abundant rank of coal commercially exploited worldwide and the principal feedstock for **coke making** in the metallurgical industry.

It is also colloquially referred to as **"soft coal"** (distinguishing it from the hard, lustrous anthracite), though this term is purely historical and does not imply mechanical softness in all cases.

## 1.2 Position in the Coalification Series

The international classification of coal by **rank** (ISO 11760 / ASTM D388) follows a continuous maturity gradient:

$$\text{Peat} \rightarrow \text{Lignite} \rightarrow \text{Sub-bituminous} \rightarrow \textbf{Bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Semi-anthracite} \rightarrow \text{Anthracite}$$

Bituminous coal occupies the **central, commercially dominant position** in this series. It is the coal type with which most students are first familiarised because it is the fuel of choice for thermal power plants, the metallurgical industry, and historical domestic heating.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Adult Teenager" of Fuels**
>
> Imagine coal as a family of fuels with different "ages":
> - **Peat** is the *toddler* — barely fossilized, mostly water and plant matter, weak and smoky.
> - **Lignite** is the *child* — brown, crumbly, still low-energy.
> - **Bituminous coal** is the *strong adult* — black, dense, packed with energy, ready for hard industrial work.
> - **Anthracite** is the *elder* — old, hard, brilliant, but rarer and slower to ignite.
>
> Bituminous coal is essentially the **workhorse of the fossil-fuel world**: it has matured enough to contain a high carbon concentration (giving high heat), but it is still young enough to ignite easily and burn with a luminous yellow flame. This is why it dominates steam boilers, power plants, and coke ovens alike.

## 1.4 Sub-classification of Bituminous Coal

Bituminous coal is itself sub-divided (ASTM D388) into five numerical sub-ranks based on **volatile matter (VM, dry, mineral-matter-free basis)** and **Gross Calorific Value (GCV, moist, mineral-matter-free basis)**:

| Sub-rank | Designation | Approx. VM (\%, dmmf) | Typical GCV Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **HVB A** | High Volatile A Bituminous | 31 – 39 | 32.6 – 35.0 MJ/kg |
| **HVB B** | High Volatile B Bituminous | 31 – 39 (lower GCV) | 30.2 – 32.6 MJ/kg |
| **HVB C** | High Volatile C Bituminous | 31 – 39 (lowest GCV) | 26.7 – 30.2 MJ/kg |
| **MVB** | Medium Volatile Bituminous | 22 – 31 | 30.2 – 35.0 MJ/kg |
| **LVB** | Low Volatile Bituminous | 14 – 22 | ≥ 35.0 MJ/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The **coking / metallurgical bituminous coal** (a sub-set of LVB and MVB with very low sulfur, low ash, and a specific caking property) is of special interest in Module 1 because it is the **irreplaceable reductant** for blast-furnace iron making.

## 1.5 Why Bituminous Coal Matters in Fuel Engineering

Bituminous coal is the *sine qua non* of solid-fuel engineering for the following reasons:

1. **Highest GCV among non-anthracite coals** — the optimal trade-off between energy density and combustion reactivity.
2. **Caking / coking property** — when heated in the absence of air, certain bituminous coals soften, swell, and re-solidify into a porous mass called **coke**, which is essential for iron reduction.
3. **Abundance** — accounts for approximately **52% of global coal reserves**, making it geopolitically the most accessible high-energy fossil fuel.
4. **Versatility** — usable in pulverised-coal boilers, fluidised-bed combustors, fixed-bed domestic stoves, gasifiers, and metallurgical coke ovens.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formation Mechanism (Coalification)

Bituminous coal is formed from **plant debris** buried in anaerobic, waterlogged conditions, where it undergoes progressive **geochemical maturation** through:

$$\text{Temperature} \uparrow \quad \& \quad \text{Pressure} \uparrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Loss of H}_2\text{O},\ \text{CO}_2,\ \text{CH}_4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{C\%} \uparrow$$

Key maturation indices:

- **Vitrinite Reflectance (R$_o$)** for bituminous coal: **0.6 % – 1.9 %**
- **Carbon content (dmmf):** 60 – 86 %
- **Hydrogen content (dmmf):** 4.0 – 6.0 %
- **Moisture (as-received):** typically **2 % – 15 %**

## 2.2 Proximate Analysis — Typical Ranges for Bituminous Coal

| Constituent | Symbol | Typical Range (\% w/w, as-received) | Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Moisture | $M$ | 2 – 15 | Penalises GCV; vaporised as latent heat |
| Volatile Matter | $VM$ | 15 – 45 | Drives flame length & ignition stability |
| Ash | $A$ | 5 – 40 | Inert residue; causes slagging & fouling |
| Fixed Carbon | $FC$ | 50 – 80 | Principal combustible solid residue |

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental mass-balance identity of proximate analysis:
>
> $$M + VM + A + FC = 100\ \%$$

## 2.3 Ultimate Analysis — Typical Ranges for Bituminous Coal

| Element | Symbol | Typical Range (\% w/w, dmmf) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Carbon | $C$ | 60 – 86 |
| Hydrogen | $H$ | 4.0 – 6.0 |
| Oxygen | $O$ | 5 – 25 |
| Nitrogen | $N$ | 1 – 2 |
| Sulphur | $S$ | 0.5 – 5.0 |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mathematically standard and appear frequently in Part-B 14-mark numericals.**

### (A) Dulong's Formula for GCV

$$GCV = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

Or, in SI units:

$$GCV = 0.337\,C + 1.418\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.093\,S \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

where $C, H, O, S$ are mass percentages on the **dry, mineral-matter-free basis** (or, approximately, on the dry-ash-free basis for KTU-level problems).

### (B) Net Calorific Value (NCV) — Correction for Hydrogen and Moisture

$$NCV = GCV - 9\,H \cdot \dfrac{L_v}{1000} - M \cdot \dfrac{L_v}{1000}$$

Simplified engineering form (using latent heat of vaporisation of water $L_v = 587\ \text{kcal/kg} = 2457\ \text{kJ/kg}$):

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09\,H \cdot 587 - 0.01\,M \cdot 587$$

Or, equivalently:

$$NCV = GCV - 5.83\,H - 5.87\,M \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

$$NCV = GCV - 24.42\,H - 24.60\,M \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

### (C) Air Required for Combustion (Stoichiometric)

$$\text{Mass of air} = \dfrac{100}{23}\left(\dfrac{32}{12}C + 8H + S - O\right) \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}\right]$$

This is derived from the oxygen demand of each combustible element.

### (D) Conversion Between Proximate and Ultimate (Koppers' Approximation)

$$FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$$

$$H_{daf} \approx VM \cdot 0.07 \quad \text{(rough, for bituminous only)}$$

### (E) Hydrogen-to-Carbon Atomic Ratio (Fuel-Rank Indicator)

$$\dfrac{H}{C}\bigg|_{atomic} = \dfrac{H/1}{C/12} = \dfrac{12\,H}{C}$$

For bituminous coal: $H/C \approx 0.7 - 0.9$ (decreasing with rank).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Bituminous Coal |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Power Generation** | Pulverised Coal (PC) boilers, CFBC, USC plants |
| **Metallurgy** | Coke making → blast-furnace iron reduction |
| **Cement Industry** | Rotary kiln firing (PC + FBC) |
| **Gasification** | Air-blown, O$_2$-blown, entrained-flow gasifiers (Shell, Texaco) |
| **Domestic** | Historical space heating; hand-fired stoves |
| **Chemical Feedstock** | Coal tar → aromatics (benzene, toluene, naphthalene) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Dulong's Formula

Dulong's formula is **empirical** — it is *not* a first-principles derivation, but a fitted correlation. However, the *physical reasoning* behind it can be reconstructed as follows:

### Step 1 — Heat Released by Combustion of Each Element

Consider 1 kg of coal containing $C\%$ carbon, $H\%$ hydrogen, $S\%$ sulphur and $O\%$ oxygen.

| Element | Combustion Reaction | Heat Released (per kg of element) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| C | $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ | **8,080 kcal/kg** |
| H | $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ | **34,500 kcal/kg** |
| S | $\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$ | **2,240 kcal/kg** |

### Step 2 — Oxygen Subtraction Correction

A portion of the fuel's hydrogen is already bound to the fuel's intrinsic oxygen. The approximate assumption (Dulong's) is that **all of the oxygen in the fuel is bound to hydrogen as O–H type linkages** (i.e., to form "internal water"). This internal hydrogen does *not* release heat on combustion because the water was already oxidised.

The hydrogen effectively available for combustion is therefore:

$$H_{eff} = H - \dfrac{O}{8}$$

The factor **1/8** comes from the molar-mass ratio:

$$\dfrac{M_{O}}{8\,M_{H}} = \dfrac{16}{8 \times 1} = 2$$

But for **per-mass** accounting, the oxygen consumes hydrogen in the ratio $O : 8H$ (since 16 g of O combines with 2 g of H, so 1 g of O combines with $1/8$ g of H).

### Step 3 — Assemble the Empirical Correlation

Summing the contributions linearly:

$$GCV = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

This is the **standard Dulong's formula** universally adopted for bituminous coal. ⚠️ *Note:* Dulong's formula is most accurate for bituminous coal and anthracite; it is less reliable for lignite and peat (where moisture and oxygenated functional groups dominate).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU Part-B Style

> **Problem [KTU University Exam — June 2023 model]:**
> A bituminous coal has the following ultimate analysis (dmmf basis):
> C = 78 %, H = 5.2 %, O = 8.5 %, S = 0.6 %, N = 1.4 %.
> (a) Calculate the GCV using Dulong's formula.
> (b) If the as-received moisture is 3 % and the hydrogen content remains 5.2 % (dmmf), calculate the NCV.

### Solution (a) — GCV

$$GCV = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$$

Substituting values:

$$GCV = 8080 \times 78 + 34500\left(5.2 - \dfrac{8.5}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.6$$

$$GCV = 630{,}240 + 34500(5.2 - 1.0625) + 1344$$

$$GCV = 630{,}240 + 34500 \times 4.1375 + 1344$$

$$GCV = 630{,}240 + 142{,}743.75 + 1344$$

$$\boxed{GCV = 774{,}327.75 \ \dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}} \approx 7{,}743\ \dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}} \approx 32.4\ \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}}}$$

This falls squarely within the expected range for **bituminous coal (24 – 35 MJ/kg)**. ✓

### Solution (b) — NCV (As-Received)

Using the simplified form:

$$NCV = GCV - 5.83\,H - 5.87\,M$$

Because the dmmf H and the as-received M are blended, we need a slightly more careful treatment. For KTU-level problems, a common approximation is to apply the formula directly using the **as-received H and M values**, recognising that the error is small for bituminous coal.

Using $H = 5.2\ \%$ and $M = 3\ \%$:

$$NCV = 7743.28 - 5.83(5.2) - 5.87(3)$$

$$NCV = 7743.28 - 30.32 - 17.61$$

$$\boxed{NCV \approx 7{,}695.35\ \dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}} \approx 32.2\ \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The NCV is only about **0.6 % lower** than the GCV because the moisture and hydrogen contents are both low — characteristic of a well-coked bituminous coal. For lignite, by contrast, this gap can exceed **10 %**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement

For complete combustion of 1 kg of coal:

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad (12\ \text{kg C} \rightarrow 32\ \text{kg O}_2)$$

$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (4\ \text{kg H} \rightarrow 32\ \text{kg O}_2 \Rightarrow 8\ \text{kg H} \rightarrow 32\ \text{kg O}_2)$$

$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad (32\ \text{kg S} \rightarrow 32\ \text{kg O}_2)$$

Total O$_2$ required **from air** (per kg of fuel, with $C, H, S, O$ as percentages):

$$O_2^{air} = \dfrac{32}{12}C + 8H + S - O \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg fuel}}\right]$$

Since air is 23 % oxygen by mass:

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{100}{23}\, O_2^{air} = \dfrac{100}{23}\left(\dfrac{32}{12}C + 8H + S - O\right) \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}\right]$$

For a typical bituminous coal with $C = 78, H = 5.2, S = 0.6, O = 8.5$:

$$O_2^{air} = \dfrac{32}{12}(78) + 8(5.2) + 0.6 - 8.5 = 208 + 41.6 + 0.6 - 8.5 = 241.7$$

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{100}{23} \times 241.7 = 1050.9 \ \dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}$$

A 5 – 10 % excess is added in practice → typically **1100 – 1150 kg air / kg coal**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bituminous Coal vs. Other Ranks

| Property | Peat | Lignite | Sub-bituminous | **Bituminous** | Anthracite |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Moisture (\%, ar) | 60 – 90 | 30 – 60 | 15 – 30 | **2 – 15** | 1 – 5 |
| VM (\%, daf) | 50 – 70 | 40 – 60 | 35 – 50 | **15 – 45** | 3 – 10 |
| C (\%, daf) | 50 – 60 | 60 – 70 | 70 – 76 | **60 – 86** | 86 – 98 |
| H (\%, daf) | 5 – 7 | 5 – 7 | 5 – 6 | **4 – 6** | 1 – 3 |
| GCV (MJ/kg) | 8 – 14 | 14 – 22 | 22 – 26 | **24 – 35** | 35 – 38 |
| Caking property | None | None | None | **Strong (LVB/MVB)** | None |
| Vitrinite R$_o$ (\%) | < 0.4 | 0.4 – 0.5 | 0.5 – 0.6 | **0.6 – 1.9** | > 2.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **caking / coking property** is unique to bituminous coal. It is the molecular signature of vitrinite + exinite macerals that have entered the **plastic stage** (around 350 – 550 °C) during carbonisation. This single property is the reason bituminous coal underpins the global steel industry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
