# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define bituminous coal. State any two of its distinguishing properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Bituminous coal is a medium-to-high rank coal containing **60 % – 86 % carbon** (dmmf) with a gross calorific value of **24 – 35 MJ/kg**. It is the most abundant rank of coal and the principal feedstock for coke making.

**Distinguishing properties (any two):**
1. It has a strong **caking / coking property** — on heating in the absence of air it softens, swells, and re-solidifies into porous coke. *(1.5 marks)*
2. It is **black, dense, and banded**, with a greasy lustre; it burns with a long, smoky yellow flame owing to its relatively high volatile-matter content (15 – 45 %). *(1.5 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between bituminous coal and anthracite in any three respects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, 1 mark each):**

| S.No. | Property | Bituminous Coal | Anthracite |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Carbon content (daf) | 60 – 86 % | 86 – 98 % |
| 2 | Volatile matter (daf) | 15 – 45 % | 3 – 10 % |
| 3 | Calorific value | 24 – 35 MJ/kg | 35 – 38 MJ/kg |
| 4 | Caking property | **Present** (coking grade) | **Absent** |
| 5 | Moisture | 2 – 15 % | 1 – 5 % |
| 6 | Combustion | Easy to ignite, smoky flame | Difficult to ignite, smokeless |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **classification of coal by rank** with a neat sketch. Where does bituminous coal fit in this classification, and what is its **significance** in the metallurgical industry?

**(b) [7 marks]** A bituminous coal sample has the following ultimate analysis on a dry, mineral-matter-free basis: C = 80 %, H = 5.0 %, O = 9.0 %, S = 1.0 %, N = 1.5 %, ash = 3.5 % (as-received). The as-received moisture is 4 %. Calculate:
1. The GCV using Dulong's formula.
2. The NCV.
3. The stoichiometric air required per kg of coal.

#### Model Solution (a) — Classification by Rank (7 marks)

**Rank classification (4 marks):** Coals are classified by rank according to their **degree of coalification** (maturation), based on parameters such as **fixed carbon, volatile matter, gross calorific value, and vitrinite reflectance**. The standard ranks, in order of increasing maturity, are:

$$\text{Peat} \rightarrow \text{Lignite} \rightarrow \text{Sub-bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Semi-anthracite} \rightarrow \text{Anthracite}$$

Key rank-defining parameters:

| Rank | C (\%, daf) | VM (\%, daf) | GCV (MJ/kg) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Lignite | 60 – 70 | 40 – 60 | < 22 |
| Sub-bituminous | 70 – 76 | 35 – 50 | 22 – 26 |
| **Bituminous** | **60 – 86** | **15 – 45** | **24 – 35** |
| Anthracite | 86 – 98 | 3 – 10 | > 35 |

**Position of bituminous coal (1 mark):** It lies **between sub-bituminous and semi-anthracite**, occupying the central, commercially dominant position. It is the only rank that exhibits **caking / coking behaviour**.

**Significance in metallurgical industry (2 marks):** Bituminous coal of the **low-volatile / medium-volatile grade** (with low ash < 10 %, low sulfur < 1 %, and high caking index) is the **only coal suitable for metallurgical coke making**. The coke produced is a porous, strong, carbon-rich solid that serves as the **fuel, reductant, and permeability-supporting matrix** in the blast furnace. Without coking bituminous coal, primary steel making via the blast-furnace route would be impossible.

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert ultimate analysis to dry basis for GCV calculation.**

Since the given C, H, O, S, N values are stated as dmmf, they are used directly in Dulong's formula. The ash and moisture are reported separately and are *not* used in Dulong's GCV formula.

**Step 2 — GCV by Dulong's formula (2 marks).**

$$GCV = 8080(80) + 34500\left(5.0 - \dfrac{9.0}{8}\right) + 2240(1.0)$$

$$GCV = 646{,}400 + 34500(5.0 - 1.125) + 2240$$

$$GCV = 646{,}400 + 34500(3.875) + 2240$$

$$GCV = 646{,}400 + 133{,}687.5 + 2240$$

$$\boxed{GCV = 782{,}327.5\ \dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}} \approx 7823\ \dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}} \approx 32.75\ \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}}}$$

**Step 3 — NCV (2 marks).**

$$NCV = GCV - 5.83\,H - 5.87\,M$$

With H on dmmf basis ≈ 5.0 % and M (as-received) = 4 %:

$$NCV = 7823.28 - 5.83(5.0) - 5.87(4)$$

$$NCV = 7823.28 - 29.15 - 23.48$$

$$\boxed{NCV \approx 7{,}770.65\ \dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}} \approx 32.53\ \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}}}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric air required (3 marks).**

$$O_2^{air} = \dfrac{32}{12}(80) + 8(5.0) + 1.0 - 9.0$$

$$O_2^{air} = 213.33 + 40 + 1 - 9 = 245.33\ \dfrac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg fuel}}$$

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{100}{23} \times 245.33$$

$$\boxed{m_{air} \approx 1066.7\ \dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}}$$

With ~25 % excess air (typical for industrial practice), actual air supplied ≈ **1330 kg air / kg fuel**.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — ALTERNATIVE

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **byproduct coke-making process** starting from bituminous coal. List the major products obtained and their uses.

**(b) [7 marks]** The proximate analysis of a bituminous coal (as-received) is: Moisture = 3 %, VM = 30 %, Ash = 12 %, FC = 55 %. The ultimate analysis on a **dry, ash-free basis** is: C = 82 %, H = 5.5 %, O = 8.0 %, S = 1.2 %, N = 3.3 %. Estimate the GCV and the air required.

#### Model Solution (a) — Coke Making Process (7 marks)

**Process description (4 marks):** Coking is the **destructive distillation of bituminous coal** in the absence of air at **1100 – 1200 °C** for 16 – 20 hours in sealed silica-brick ovens. The classic byproduct recovery system comprises:

1. **Coal charging** — Crushed bituminous coal (≈ 75 % LVB/MVB, < 3 mm fines) is charged from the top into pre-heated ovens.
2. **Carbonisation** — In the plastic zone (350 – 550 °C), the coal melts, swells, and resolidifies into semi-coke, which then shrinks to form hard, porous **metallurgical coke** at 1100 °C.
3. **Gas collection** — Volatile products leave the oven, pass through the ascension pipe to a **primary cooler**, and then to a **byproduct plant**.
4. **Byproduct recovery** — In sequence: tar and liquor separation → ammonia scrubbing with H$_2$SO$_4$ → light oil (BTX) scrubbing with wash oil → desulphurisation → gas storage.

**Products and uses (3 marks, 0.5 each):**

| Product | Use |
| :--- | :--- |
| Metallurgical coke | Blast-furnace iron reduction; foundry cupolas |
| Coke-oven gas (COG) | Fuel gas; H$_2$ source for ammonia/methanol |
| Coal tar | Source of BTX (benzene, toluene, xylene), naphthalene, phenols |
| Ammonium sulphate | Nitrogenous fertiliser |
| Light oil (crude benzol) | Refined into pure aromatics |
| Pitch | Electrode binder, road tar, waterproofing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Required figure:** Draw a block diagram of the byproduct coke oven with arrows from the oven to: coke, gas → cooler → tar/liquor/ammonia/BTX recovery, with final products labelled. (KTU expects a labelled sketch; without it, deduct 1 – 2 marks.)

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert ultimate analysis from daf to dry, mineral-matter-free basis.**

Since the ultimate analysis is already given on a **daf** basis, the GCV formula can be applied **directly without adjustment** (Dulong's formula assumes dmmf, but daf ≈ dmmf for KTU problems where ash minerals are unspecified).

**Step 2 — GCV by Dulong (3 marks).**

$$GCV = 8080(82) + 34500\left(5.5 - \dfrac{8.0}{8}\right) + 2240(1.2)$$

$$GCV = 662{,}560 + 34500(5.5 - 1.0) + 2688$$

$$GCV = 662{,}560 + 34500(4.5) + 2688$$

$$GCV = 662{,}560 + 155{,}250 + 2688$$

$$\boxed{GCV = 820{,}498\ \dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}} \approx 8205\ \dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}} \approx 34.3\ \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}}}$$

This is at the **upper end of bituminous coal**, indicating a **low-volatile bituminous** grade (likely suitable for metallurgical coke making).

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric air (4 marks).**

$$O_2^{air} = \dfrac{32}{12}(82) + 8(5.5) + 1.2 - 8.0$$

$$O_2^{air} = 218.67 + 44 + 1.2 - 8 = 255.87\ \dfrac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg fuel}}$$

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{100}{23} \times 255.87$$

$$\boxed{m_{air} \approx 1112.5\ \dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Dulong's formula unit confusion:** Many students wrongly mix units — for example, applying kcal/kg coefficients to percentages expressed in MJ/kg. Always cross-check the units before writing the final answer.
> 2. **daaf vs daf vs dmmf confusion:** Dulong's formula is strictly for **dmmf**. Using "daf" values when ash is high can lead to **5 – 8 % error**, which costs 1 – 2 marks in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract O/8 from H:** This is the most common single mistake; the corrected hydrogen is $H - O/8$, **not** $H$ alone.
> 4. **NCV correction:** KTU expects the *hydrogen and moisture latent-heat correction*. Skipping this step loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Stoichiometric air:** Forgetting the *23 % oxygen in air* factor is a frequent error. The factor is **100/23**, not **100/21** (the latter is volumetric).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bituminous Coal in 90 Seconds**

- **Definition:** Medium-to-high rank coal; C = 60 – 86 % (dmmf); GCV = 24 – 35 MJ/kg.
- **Position in coalification:** Lies between sub-bituminous and semi-anthracite.
- **Sub-ranks (ASTM D388):** HVB-A, HVB-B, HVB-C, MVB, LVB.
- **Proximate analysis (typical, ar basis):** M = 2 – 15 %, VM = 15 – 45 %, Ash = 5 – 40 %, FC = 50 – 80 %.
- **Ultimate analysis (typical, daf basis):** C = 60 – 86 %, H = 4 – 6 %, O = 5 – 25 %, N = 1 – 2 %, S = 0.5 – 5 %.
- **Vitrinite reflectance:** $R_o$ = 0.6 % – 1.9 %.
- **Unique property:** **Caking / coking** — vitrinite + exinite macerals go through a plastic stage (350 – 550 °C) during carbonisation.
- **Two commercial pillars:**
  1. **Power generation** (PC, CFBC, USC boilers) — uses all sub-ranks.
  2. **Metallurgical coke** (blast furnace) — uses only **LVB / MVB** with low ash and low sulfur.
- **Dulong's formula (KCU-kcal/kg):**
  $$GCV = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$$
- **NCV correction:**
  $$NCV = GCV - 5.83\,H - 5.87\,M \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$
- **Stoichiometric air:**
  $$m_{air} = \dfrac{100}{23}\left(\dfrac{32}{12}C + 8H + S - O\right) \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}\right]$$
- **Atomic H/C ratio for bituminous coal:** ≈ 0.7 – 0.9 (decreases with rank).
- **End uses:** Power, coke, syngas, tar/BTX, cement kilns, historical domestic heating.
- **Exam traps:** Unit inconsistency in Dulong's, O/8 omission, daf vs dmmf confusion, ignoring moisture + hydrogen correction in NCV, and using 100/21 instead of 100/23 in air calculation.
- **Global significance:** Bituminous coal accounts for ~**52 % of global coal reserves** and is the **only rank** that can yield metallurgical coke — making it geopolitically and industrially irreplaceable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anthracite Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anthracite Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anthracite Coal** is the highest rank of coal in the coalification series, characterized by a **carbon content of 92–98 %**, very low volatile matter (**2–12 %**), high density (**1.3–1.7 g/cm³**), and a semi-metallic to submetallic luster. It is a hard, black, compact, non-coking variety of coal that originated from progressive metamorphism of bituminous coal under high pressure and temperature over geological timescales.

The American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) classifies anthracite by fixed carbon and volatile matter content on a dry, mineral-matter-free basis:

> [!NOTE]
> **ASTM Classification of Anthracite (D388):**
> - **Meta-anthracite:** FC > 98 %, VM < 2 %
> - **Anthracite:** FC 92–98 %, VM 2–8 %
> - **Semi-anthracite:** FC 86–92 %, VM 8–14 %

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **coalification series** like the **ripening of a fruit** or the **curing of a cheese** — but over **millions of years**:

1. **Peat** (wet, fibrous, soil-like) → young, unprocessed material
2. **Lignite** (brown, crumbly) → partially "cooked"
3. **Bituminous** (black, soft, smoky) → fully "cooked"
4. **Anthracite** (jet black, hard, shiny) → "over-cooked" and compressed

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine squeezing and heating a wet sponge (peat) for millions of years under Earth's crust. As water, oxygen, and hydrogen are slowly squeezed out, what remains is denser, harder, and almost pure carbon — that is **anthracite**. It is the **"diamond's cousin"** in the coal world: pure, dense, and energy-rich.

### 1.3 Position in the Coalification Series

Anthracite represents the **terminal stage of coal metamorphism**, formed when bituminous coal is subjected to elevated temperatures (150–250 °C) and tectonic pressures over 250–400 million years.

**Key Standard Metrics (Anthracite, Dry Basis):**

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Fixed Carbon | **92 – 98 %** |
| Volatile Matter | **2 – 12 %** |
| Moisture | **1 – 5 %** |
| Ash | **5 – 15 %** |
| Sulphur | **0.5 – 1.5 %** |
| Gross Calorific Value | **30 – 35 MJ/kg** (7200 – 8400 kcal/kg) |
| Density | **1.3 – 1.7 g/cm³** |
| Hardness (Mohs) | **2.2 – 3.8** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Chemistry of Anthracite

Anthracite's structure is dominated by **polycondensed aromatic hydrocarbon units** (graphene-like lamellae) arranged in a partially ordered microcrystalline network.

- **Carbon skeleton:** 90 %+ of atoms are in aromatic ring structures (C=C, C–C).
- **Hydrogen:** Primarily located at the periphery of aromatic clusters, decreasing with rank.
- **Side chains:** Almost completely eliminated compared to bituminous coal.
- **Porosity:** Low specific surface area (~1–5 m²/g) compared to lignite (>200 m²/g).

> [!NOTE]
> As coal rank progresses from lignite → anthracite, the **aromaticity (fa)** — the fraction of carbon in aromatic rings — rises from ~0.5 to **>0.95**. This explains anthracite's near-graphitic behavior and electrical conductivity.

### 2.2 Proximate and Ultimate Analysis Significance

**Proximate Analysis** quantifies four operational fractions:
1. **Moisture (M):** Residual water; anthracite is intrinsically dry.
2. **Volatile Matter (VM):** Gases released on heating to 950 °C; very low in anthracite.
3. **Ash (A):** Non-combustible residue; affects slag formation.
4. **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Calculated by difference: **FC = 100 – (M + VM + A)**.

**Ultimate Analysis** gives elemental composition (C, H, N, S, O), critical for **Dulong's calorific value formula**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are board-exam essentials. Memorize the units carefully.

| Formula | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fixed Carbon (by difference) | $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$ | % | Proximate analysis |
| Carbon balance | $C + H + O + N + S + A + M = 100$ | % | Ultimate analysis |
| Dulong's Formula (GCV) | $GCV = \dfrac{8080 \cdot C}{100} + \dfrac{34500 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right)}{100} + \dfrac{2240 \cdot S}{100}$ | kcal/kg | Approximate GCV from ultimate analysis |
| Dulong's Formula (SI units) | $GCV = 0.338 \cdot C + 1.428 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095 \cdot S$ | MJ/kg | SI equivalent |
| Net Calorific Value (NCV) | $NCV = GCV - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot L - 0.01 \cdot M \cdot L$ | kcal/kg | Actual usable heat |
| Latent heat of vaporization | $L = 587$ (at 25 °C) | kcal/kg | Water from H combustion |
| Hydrogen-to-water ratio | $H_2O$ formed = $\dfrac{9 \cdot H}{100}$ | kg/kg fuel | Combustion stoichiometry |
| Air requirement (theoretical) | $A_{th} = \dfrac{11.6 \cdot C + 34.8 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35 \cdot S}{100}$ | kg/kg fuel | Combustion calc |
| Stoichiometric O₂ | $O_2 = \dfrac{32}{12}C + \dfrac{32}{4}H + \dfrac{32}{32}S - O$ | kg/kg | Theoretical O₂ |
| Energy density | $E_d = \rho \cdot GCV$ | MJ/m³ | Storage / transport |

> **Note:** $\vert x \vert$ notation in exam scripts is written as `\vert x \vert` in markdown to avoid table syntax conflicts.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Generation:** Anthracite's high carbon density makes it ideal for **thermal power plants** in regions like Pennsylvania (USA), Wales (UK), and parts of China, where a long, sustained heat release is desired.
- **Metallurgical Use:** Acts as a **carbon raiser** in steelmaking and as a **reductant** in ferroalloy production.
- **Domestic Heating:** Smokeless fuel briquettes (e.g., **Phurnacite**, **Ancit**) in urban UK/Ireland — anthracite's low VM eliminates smoke pollution.
- **Filtration Media:** Crushed anthracite is used in **dual-media water filters** above sand beds because of its angular shape and high specific gravity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relation Between GCV and NCV for Anthracite

**Starting Premise:** When 1 kg of fuel is burned:
- The hydrogen in the fuel is oxidised to liquid water: $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$
- The moisture already present in the fuel is vaporised.

Both effects **absorb latent heat**, reducing the heat available to the boiler (i.e., NCV < GCV).

**Step 1:** Hydrogen present in 1 kg of fuel = $\dfrac{H}{100}$ kg.
Oxygen needed: $\dfrac{8}{100}$ kg (to form H₂O, ratio 8:1 by mass for H).

**Step 2:** Mass of water formed from H combustion:

$$m_{w,1} = \dfrac{9 \cdot H}{100} \text{ kg per kg fuel}$$

Reason: 2 kg H + 16 kg O → 18 kg H₂O, hence 1 kg H → 9 kg H₂O.

**Step 3:** Moisture already in the fuel is converted to steam:

$$m_{w,2} = \dfrac{M}{100} \text{ kg per kg fuel}$$

**Step 4:** Total water to be vaporised per kg of fuel:

$$m_w = \dfrac{9 \cdot H}{100} + \dfrac{M}{100} = \dfrac{9H + M}{100}$$

**Step 5:** Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 25 °C is $L = 587$ kcal/kg (or 2457 kJ/kg).

**Step 6:** Total latent heat lost (per kg fuel):

$$\Delta H = L \cdot m_w = 587 \cdot \left(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Expanding the bracket:

$$\Delta H = \dfrac{0.09 \cdot H \cdot 587 + 0.01 \cdot M \cdot 587}{1} = 52.83\,H + 5.87\,M$$

**Step 8:** Therefore, the **Net Calorific Value** is:

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot 587 - 0.01 \cdot M \cdot 587$$

$$\boxed{NCV = GCV - 52.83\,H - 5.87\,M} \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]$$

In SI units (with $L = 2457$ kJ/kg):

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09\,H(2457) - 0.01\,M(2457) = GCV - 221.13\,H - 24.57\,M \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dulong's Formula (CGS / kcal basis)

**Step 1:** Combustion enthalpies (at constant volume, 25 °C):
- Carbon → CO₂: $\Delta H_C = 8080$ kcal/kg C
- Hydrogen → H₂O(l): $\Delta H_H = 34500$ kcal/kg H
- Sulphur → SO₂: $\Delta H_S = 2240$ kcal/kg S

**Step 2:** Oxygen already combined with hydrogen in the fuel **reduces the effective H** because it has already been "pre-oxidised." Net available hydrogen = $H - \tfrac{O}{8}$ (kg per kg fuel).

**Step 3:** Summing weighted contributions:

$$GCV = \left(\dfrac{C}{100}\right)(8080) + \left(\dfrac{H - O/8}{100}\right)(34500) + \left(\dfrac{S}{100}\right)(2240)$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging into the standard compact form:

$$\boxed{GCV = \dfrac{8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S}{100}} \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** An anthracite coal has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis): C = 92 %, H = 2.5 %, O = 2.0 %, N = 0.8 %, S = 1.0 %, Ash = 1.7 %. Moisture in the fuel as fired = 2.0 %. Calculate:
1. GCV using Dulong's formula
2. NCV on "as fired" basis
3. Theoretical air required for complete combustion

**Solution Step 1 — GCV:**

$$GCV = \dfrac{8080 \times 92 + 34500 \times \left(2.5 - \dfrac{2.0}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 1.0}{100}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{743360 + 34500 \times (2.5 - 0.25) + 2240}{100}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{743360 + 34500 \times 2.25 + 2240}{100} = \dfrac{743360 + 77625 + 2240}{100}$$

$$\boxed{GCV = \dfrac{823225}{100} = 8232.25 \text{ kcal/kg}}$$

**Solution Step 2 — NCV (as fired, with M = 2.0 %):**
Note: H must be re-based to "as fired": $H_{af} = H_{db} \times \dfrac{100 - M}{100} = 2.5 \times 0.98 = 2.45$ %.

$$NCV = GCV - 52.83\,H_{af} - 5.87\,M$$

$$NCV = 8232.25 - 52.83(2.45) - 5.87(2.0)$$

$$NCV = 8232.25 - 129.43 - 11.74 = \boxed{8091.08 \text{ kcal/kg}}$$

**Solution Step 3 — Theoretical Air:**

$$A_{th} = \dfrac{11.6 \cdot C_{af} + 34.8 \cdot \left(H_{af} - \dfrac{O_{af}}{8}\right) + 4.35 \cdot S_{af}}{100}$$

With $C_{af} = 92 \times 0.98 = 90.16$ %, $O_{af} = 2.0 \times 0.98 = 1.96$ %, $S_{af} = 1.0 \times 0.98 = 0.98$ %:

$$A_{th} = \dfrac{11.6(90.16) + 34.8 \times (2.45 - 0.245) + 4.35(0.98)}{100}$$

$$A_{th} = \dfrac{1045.86 + 34.8 \times 2.205 + 4.26}{100} = \dfrac{1045.86 + 76.73 + 4.26}{100}$$

$$\boxed{A_{th} = 11.27 \text{ kg air per kg fuel}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Anthracite vs. Other Coals

| Property | Peat | Lignite | Bituminous | **Anthracite** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon (%, daf) | 50–60 | 60–70 | 75–90 | **92–98** |
| Volatile Matter (%) | 50–65 | 40–55 | 15–40 | **2–12** |
| Moisture (%) | 70–90 | 30–50 | 5–15 | **1–5** |
| GCV (kcal/kg) | 2500–3500 | 4000–5500 | 6500–8500 | **7200–8400** |
| Coking Property | None | None | Strong (coking) | **None** |
| Smoke on Burning | Heavy | Moderate | Smoky | **Smokeless** |
| Hardness (Mohs) | ~1.0 | ~1.5 | 2.0–2.5 | **2.2–3.8** |
| Luster | Dull | Dull | Sub-metallic | **Bright to Metallic** |
| Geological Age (Myr) | <1 | 1–60 | 100–300 | **250–400** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Coalification Series (Peat → Anthracite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[PEAT] -->|Bacterial & Burial Action| A2[LIGNITE]
    A2 -->|Heat and Pressure Increase| A3[SUB BITUMINOUS]
    A3 -->|Further Metamorphism| A4[BITUMINOUS COAL]
    A4 -->|High Rank Metamorphism| A5[ANTHRACITE COAL]
    A5 -->|Extreme Metamorphism| A6[META ANTHRACITE]

    A1 -. C: 50 to 60 PCNT .-> A1
    A2 -. C: 60 to 70 PCNT .-> A2
    A3 -. C: 70 to 78 PCNT .-> A3
    A4 -. C: 78 to 90 PCNT .-> A4
    A5 -. C: 92 to 98 PCNT .-> A5
    A6 -. C: GT 98 PCNT .-> A6
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of Anthracite Combustion System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[RAW ANTHRACITE FEED] --> B2[CRUSHER UNIT]
    B2 --> B3[SCREENING AND CLASSIFICATION]
    B3 --> B4[STORAGE SILO]
    B4 --> B5[STOKER OR PULVERIZER]
    B5 --> B6[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    B6 -->|Flue Gases CO2 H2O SO2| B7[HEAT EXCHANGER BOILER]
    B6 -->|Clinker and Slag| B8[ASH DISPOSAL]
    B7 -->|Steam| B9[TURBINE GENERATOR]
    B7 -->|Exhaust| B10[POLLUTION CONTROL ESP UNIT]
    B10 -->|Clean Gas| B11[STACK EMISSION]

    B1 -. HIGH FIXED CARBON 92 to 98 PCNT .-> B1
    B6 -. LOW VM YIELDS SMOKELESS FLAME .-> B6
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Property Radar Summary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[HIGH FIXED CARBON] --> C5[ANTHRACITE IDENTITY]
    C2[LOW VOLATILE MATTER] --> C5
    C3[LOW MOISTURE] --> C5
    C4[HIGH CALORIFIC VALUE] --> C5
    C6[BRIGHT METALLIC LUSTER] --> C5
    C7[NON COKING] --> C5
    C8[HARD AND COMPACT] --> C5
    C9[SMOKELESS FLAME] --> C5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Anthracite coal. List any four characteristic properties that distinguish it from bituminous coal.**

**Model Answer:**

Anthracite coal is the **highest rank of coal** in the coalification series, containing **92–98 % fixed carbon** on a dry, ash-free basis, formed by intense metamorphism of bituminous coal under high pressure and temperature.

**Four distinguishing properties vs. bituminous coal:**

1. **Higher fixed carbon (92–98 %)** vs. bituminous (75–90 %).
2. **Lower volatile matter (2–12 %)** vs. bituminous (15–40 %) — hence smokeless combustion.
3. **Higher calorific value (30–35 MJ/kg)** vs. bituminous (25–32 MJ/kg).
4. **Semi-metallic/sub-metallic luster** and **higher hardness (Mohs 2.2–3.8)** vs. duller bituminous coal.
5. (Bonus) **Non-coking** nature — anthracite does not soften or fuse on heating.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain why anthracite coal burns with a short, blue, smokeless flame.**

**Model Answer:**

Anthracite's **volatile matter is extremely low (2–12 %)**. During combustion:
- The **devolatilisation stage** releases very little combustible gas, so there is no envelope of hydrocarbon gas around the particle to produce a **long, luminous, yellow flame**.
- With minimal unburnt hydrocarbons and soot precursors, the **radiation from soot particles is negligible**, hence the flame is **short, non-luminous, and blue** (due to CH* radical emission at ~431 nm).
- The high carbon content ensures the heat is released mainly during the **heterogeneous char-oxidation stage** (C + O₂ → CO₂) producing a steady, concentrated heat release.
- Low moisture (1–5 %) means **no quenching of the flame** by water vaporisation.

Therefore anthracite is a **premium smokeless fuel**, ideal for urban and indoor use.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **coalification series** and indicate the position of anthracite in it. Discuss how the **carbon content, volatile matter, and calorific value** change as coal rank increases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An anthracite sample has the following **proximate analysis** (as received basis): Moisture = 3 %, VM = 8 %, Ash = 6 %, Fixed Carbon = 83 %. Calculate:
1. The GCV using **Dulong's formula** (assume C: H: O: N: S in the fuel is 90:3:2:1:1 on dry basis).
2. The NCV on as-fired basis.
3. The percentage of **fixed carbon on dry, ash-free (daf) basis**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The coalification series proceeds as:
**Peat → Lignite → Sub-bituminous → Bituminous → Anthracite → (Meta-anthracite)**

As rank increases, the following trends are observed (with anthracite at the apex):

| Property | Peat → Anthracite Trend |
|---|---|
| Carbon (daf) | **Increases** (50 % → 98 %) |
| Hydrogen (daf) | **Decreases** (6 % → 2 %) |
| Oxygen (daf) | **Decreases sharply** (40 % → 1 %) |
| Volatile Matter | **Decreases** (60 % → 5 %) |
| Moisture | **Decreases** (80 % → 2 %) |
| Calorific Value | **Increases** (3000 → 8500 kcal/kg) |
| Aromaticity | **Increases** (0.5 → 0.95) |
| Hardness/Luster | **Increases** (dull → metallic) |

This monotonic trend arises because progressive burial, compaction, and geothermal heating cause **devolatilisation**, **deoxygenation**, and **aromatisation** of the organic matter. Anthracite, being the most metamorphosed, has the highest energy density per unit mass and burns with a clean, short, blue flame.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Re-base to dry basis** (divide all except M by 0.97):
- VM(dry) = 8/0.97 = 8.25 %
- Ash(dry) = 6/0.97 = 6.19 %
- FC(dry) = 83/0.97 = 85.57 %

**Step 2: Convert dry ultimate analysis to as-fired** (multiply by 0.97):
- C = 90 × 0.97 = 87.3 %
- H = 3 × 0.97 = 2.91 %
- O = 2 × 0.97 = 1.94 %
- N = 1 × 0.97 = 0.97 %
- S = 1 × 0.97 = 0.97 %

**Step 3: Apply Dulong's formula:**

$$GCV = \dfrac{8080(87.3) + 34500\left(2.91 - \dfrac{1.94}{8}\right) + 2240(0.97)}{100}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{705384 + 34500 \times 2.6675 + 2172.8}{100}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{705384 + 92028.75 + 2172.8}{100} = \dfrac{799585.55}{100}$$

$$\boxed{GCV = 7995.86 \text{ kcal/kg}}$$

**Step 4: NCV (as fired):**

$$NCV = 7995.86 - 52.83(2.91) - 5.87(3.0) = 7995.86 - 153.74 - 17.61$$

$$\boxed{NCV = 7824.51 \text{ kcal/kg}}$$

**Step 5: FC on daf basis:**

$$FC_{daf} = \dfrac{FC_{dry}}{100 - A_{dry}} \times 100 = \dfrac{85.57}{93.81} \times 100 = \boxed{91.22\%}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Discuss the **physical, chemical, and combustion characteristics** of anthracite coal. Explain why it is preferred as a **domestic fuel** despite its higher cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermal power station uses **1000 tonnes/day** of anthracite with GCV = 8000 kcal/kg and overall plant efficiency of 32 %. Calculate:
1. The **thermal power output** in MW.
2. The **daily coal consumption** if plant efficiency is improved to 38 % for the same output.
3. The **CO₂ emitted per day** (assume 85 % carbon in fuel is converted to CO₂). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Physical Characteristics:**
- Jet-black color with **bright metallic to sub-metallic luster**.
- Hard, compact, brittle; conchoidal fracture.
- High density (1.3–1.7 g/cm³), low porosity.
- Negligible weathering, high resistivity.

**Chemical Characteristics:**
- **C: 92–98 %** (daf), **H: 2–3 %**, **O < 3 %**.
- Low sulphur (0.5–1.5 %) and nitrogen.
- Highly aromatic (>95 % aromaticity); near-graphitic structure.
- Negligible extractable bitumen.

**Combustion Characteristics:**
- **Ignition temperature:** 400–500 °C (high — harder to ignite than bituminous).
- **Short, blue, smokeless flame** (low VM).
- Slow burning rate; long residence time needed.
- High activation energy; slow char oxidation.

**Why preferred as domestic fuel despite higher cost:**
1. **Smokeless combustion** — no indoor pollution; permitted in smoke-control zones (UK Clean Air Act).
2. **High calorific value** — more heat per kg, lower storage volume.
3. **Low ash and sulphur** — clean chimneys, less corrosion, less maintenance.
4. **No caking** — burns uniformly without clinker formation in stoves.
5. **Long, steady burn** — preferred for overnight heating.
6. **Low moisture** — instant ignition, no drying loss.

Therefore the **premium price is offset by cleanliness, efficiency, and convenience**.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Coal feed = 1000 t/day
- GCV = 8000 kcal/kg
- η₁ = 32 % = 0.32

**Step 1: Thermal output (kcal/day):**

$$Q_{in} = 1000 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/day} \times 8000 \text{ kcal/kg} = 8 \times 10^9 \text{ kcal/day}$$

Convert to kWh: 1 kcal = 1.163 × 10⁻³ kWh

$$Q_{in} = 8 \times 10^9 \times 1.163 \times 10^{-3} = 9.304 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/day}$$

Convert to MW (1 day = 24 h):

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{9.304 \times 10^6}{24} = 387{,}667 \text{ kW} = \boxed{387.67 \text{ MW}}$$

**Step 2: New coal feed at η₂ = 38 %:**

$$Q_{in,new} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{\eta_2} = \dfrac{9.304 \times 10^6}{0.38} = 24.484 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/day}$$

$$= 24.484 \times 10^6 / 1.163 \times 10^{-3} = 2.105 \times 10^{10} \text{ kcal/day}$$

$$\text{Coal}_{new} = \dfrac{2.105 \times 10^{10}}{8000} = 2.631 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/day} = \boxed{2631.6 \text{ t/day}}$$

**Step 3: Daily CO₂ emission:**

Mass of C in fuel = 85 % of 2,631,600 kg = 2,236,860 kg C/day.
Reaction: C + O₂ → CO₂; 12 kg C → 44 kg CO₂.

$$m_{CO_2} = 2{,}236{,}860 \times \dfrac{44}{12} = 2{,}236{,}860 \times 3.667$$

$$\boxed{m_{CO_2} = 8{,}202{,}488 \text{ kg/day} \approx 8202.5 \text{ t/day}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** GCV in kcal/kg vs. kJ/kg — Dulong's constant 8080 is for **kcal/kg**. Use 33,800 for kJ/kg, not mixing.
> 2. **Forgetting to re-base:** When asked for "as-fired" NCV, do **not** use dry-basis H value — rebase via $H_{af} = H_{db}(1 - M/100)$.
> 3. **Sign of latent heat:** NCV is **always less** than GCV. A negative answer is an instant red flag.
> 4. **Dulong's hydrogen correction:** Students often write $(H + O/8)$ instead of $(H - O/8)$. The minus sign is critical.
> 5. **Forgetting the moisture loss term:** $0.01\,M\,L$ in NCV formula is frequently dropped.
> 6. **CO₂ stoichiometry:** 12 g C → 44 g CO₂, not 32 or 22. Use the molar masses correctly.
> 7. **Plant efficiency application:** Apply efficiency to **heat input** to get electrical output, not the reverse.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Anthracite Coal**

- **Definition:** Highest rank coal; C = 92–98 % (daf); VM = 2–12 %; GCV = 30–35 MJ/kg.
- **Origin:** Bituminous coal subjected to 150–250 °C + tectonic pressure over 250–400 Myr.
- **Classification (ASTM D388):** Meta-anthracite, Anthracite, Semi-anthracite (based on FC and VM).
- **Structure:** Highly aromatic (>95 %); near-graphitic; low porosity; high density.
- **Appearance:** Jet-black, bright metallic/sub-metallic luster, conchoidal fracture.
- **Flame:** Short, blue, **smokeless** (low VM).
- **Ignition temperature:** 400–500 °C (relatively high).
- **Coking property:** **None** — non-coking.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$
  - Dulong: $GCV = \dfrac{8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S}{100}$ kcal/kg
  - $NCV = GCV - 52.83\,H - 5.87\,M$ kcal/kg
  - $A_{th} = \dfrac{11.6C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35S}{100}$ kg/kg fuel
  - $E_d = \rho \cdot GCV$ (energy density)
- **Uses:** Smokeless domestic fuel, thermal power generation, carbon raiser in steel, water filtration.
- **Global Deposits:** USA (Pennsylvania), UK (Wales), Russia, China, Vietnam, South Africa.
- **Indian Deposits:** **Limited** — small pockets in **Rajasthan (Dholpur)** and **Jammu & Kashmir (Kalakot)**; India is **anthracite-deficient**.
- **Compare:** Anthracite vs. Bituminous: Anthracite has higher C, lower VM, lower moisture, higher GCV, but is **harder to ignite** and **does not coke**.
- **Examiner traps:** Unit mismatches (kcal vs. kJ); wrong sign in Dulong's H correction; forgetting moisture re-basing for as-fired calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cannel and Boghead Coal, Physical Properties of Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cannel and Boghead Coal, Physical Properties of Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cannel Coal — Formal Definition

**Cannel coal** (also historically called **"candle coal"** or *parrot coal*) is a hard, compact, homogeneous, sapropelic variety of **bituminous coal** that ignites easily and burns with a long, luminous, smoky, candle-like flame. It is derived from the accumulation of **spores, pollen grains, resin globules, and finely divided plant debris** (collectively termed *spore-rich sapropel*) deposited under still, anaerobic, sub-aquatic conditions such as lagoons, swamps, and sheltered lake basins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cannel coal is classified as a **Sapropelic Coal** — one of the two principal categories of coal (the other being *Humic Coal*). Cannel is essentially a *spore-rich* sapropelic coal.

**Key Identifying Parameters (Cannel Coal):**
- **Specific Gravity:** $\approx \mathbf{1.20 - 1.50}$
- **Volatile Matter (d.a.f.):** $\approx \mathbf{40 - 60\%}$ (significantly higher than humic bituminous)
- **Hydrogen Content (d.a.f.):** $\approx \mathbf{5.5 - 6.5\%}$
- **Luster:** Dull to slightly greasy, with a *flat* appearance (no pronounced banding)
- **Fracture:** Distinctly **conchoidal** (shell-like)
- **Soils Fingers:** No (unlike lignite)

### 1.2 Boghead Coal — Formal Definition

**Boghead coal** is another member of the **sapropelic coal** family, named after the village of **Boghead in Lanarkshire, Scotland**, where it was first industrially mined. It is a tough, compact, brownish-black coal whose origin is dominated by **colonial and unicellular algae** (principally the genera *Pila* and *Reinschia*), which accumulated as an algal ooze in freshwater lakes. It possesses an even **higher hydrogen and volatile matter content** than cannel coal, making it an excellent feedstock for **oil extraction** (low-temperature carbonization or hydrogenation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Cannel:** While both are sapropelic, the microscopic "mother material" (maceral) differs. **Cannel = spore/pollen-rich (microspores); Boghead = algal-rich (alginite).** Boghead is the more *oil-prone* of the two.

**Key Identifying Parameters (Boghead Coal):**
- **Specific Gravity:** $\approx \mathbf{1.15 - 1.40}$
- **Volatile Matter (d.a.f.):** $\approx \mathbf{55 - 80\%}$ (the highest among naturally occurring coals)
- **Hydrogen Content (d.a.f.):** $\approx \mathbf{6.0 - 8.0\%}$
- **Oil Yield (Fischer Assay):** Can exceed **300 litres per tonne** of dry coal
- **Luster:** Sub-dull to dull, often brownish

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Lake-Bottom "Sapropel" Story

Imagine a **shallow tropical lake** that has been cut off from a river for thousands of years. On its surface, oily algae bloom (in *boghead*) and wind-blown spores from surrounding fern forests (in *cannel*) settle. The lake bottom becomes a thick, **oxygen-free, mucky sludge called *sapropel*** — basically "biological ooze." Over millions of years, this ooze is buried, compressed, and heated, transforming into a coal that **inherits the original hydrogen-rich, waxy chemistry of its biological precursors.** This is why both cannel and boghead coals yield **candle-like flames** and abundant **liquid oils** when heated — they are "memory banks" of their oily biological origins, unlike ordinary humic coal which is derived from woody, cellulose-rich peat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Importance:** During World War I and II, cannel and boghead coals were **strategic feedstocks for producing synthetic gasoline** in Germany (Bergius process) and the UK, because their exceptionally high hydrogen content made hydrogenation to liquid fuels far easier than with humic coal.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Logic — Where Cannel and Boghead Fit

The two genetic families of coal are:

| Family | Origin (Parent Material) | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Humic Coal** | Woody tissues (cellulose, lignin), bark, leaves | Peat → Lignite → Bituminous → Anthracite |
| **Sapropelic Coal** | Spores, pollen, algae, resin, fine plant debris | **Cannel Coal**, **Boghead Coal**, *Torbanite* |

Within the sapropelic group, the gradation is:
**Boghead (algal) → Cannel (spore-rich) → Torbanite (highly algal, canneloid type)**

### 2.2 Physical Properties of Coal — A Comprehensive Framework

Physical properties govern **handling, storage, beneficiation, combustion, gasification, and carbonization behavior** of coal. They are also crucial proxies for **rank** (degree of coalification) and **type** (genetic origin).

#### 2.2.1 Color
- **Brown** → Lignite (low rank)
- **Dark Brown to Black** → Bituminous (mid rank)
- **Black with metallic / sub-metallic luster** → Anthracite (high rank)
- **Brownish-Black, dull** → Sapropelic (Cannel/Boghead)

#### 2.2.2 Luster (Optical Reflectivity of Macerals)
- **Bright Luster:** High vitrain (humic bituminous)
- **Sub-Bright:** Mixed durain/vitrain
- **Dull:** Fusain, sapropelic coals
- Quantitative measure: **Vitrinite Reflectance ($R_o$, in %)** — increases monotonically with rank.

#### 2.2.3 Fracture
- **Conchoidal:** Smooth, curved, shell-like (typical of *Cannel* and *Boghead* — amorphous, isotropic structure)
- **Cubical:** Anthracite
- **Irregular / Prismatic:** Bituminous (along bedding planes)
- **Slaty:** Higher-rank anthracites

#### 2.2.4 Hardness
- **Mohs Hardness:** Generally **2 to 2.5** for most bituminous coals
- Anthracite can reach **3.5 – 4.0**
- Measured practically by the **Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI)** — see Section 2.3 below.

#### 2.2.5 Specific Gravity (True / Apparent)
- **Lignite:** $\approx 1.10 - 1.25$
- **Bituminous:** $\approx 1.25 - 1.40$
- **Anthracite:** $\approx 1.40 - 1.80$
- **Cannel:** $\approx 1.20 - 1.50$
- **Boghead:** $\approx 1.15 - 1.40$

> [!NOTE]
> Higher specific gravity corresponds to higher carbon content, lower porosity, and higher rank.

#### 2.2.6 Porosity
Coal porosity ranges from **2 % (anthracite)** up to **20 % (low-rank bituminous)**. Pores are classified as:
- **Micropores** (< 2 nm)
- **Mesopores** (2 – 50 nm)
- **Macropores** (> 50 nm)

High porosity favors **spontaneous combustion risk** during storage.

#### 2.2.7 Friability
Defined as the **tendency of coal to break into smaller pieces** under impact or abrasion. Highly friable coals (low-rank, high-vitrinite) generate dust, fines, and pose dust-explosion hazards.

#### 2.2.8 Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI)
- **Range:** 30 (very hard) to 110 (very soft)
- **Standard Reference:** Coal sample is ground in a ball-mill-type apparatus under standard load (64 N) for 60 revolutions; the index is computed from the fraction passing a 75 µm sieve.
- **High HGI (soft)** = Lower pulverizer energy cost.

#### 2.2.9 Calorific Value (CV / GCV / NCV)
- **Gross Calorific Value (GCV):** Heat released by complete combustion with products $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ in liquid form.
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV):** Heat released when $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ leaves as vapor.
- **Bituminous:** 24 – 35 MJ/kg
- **Anthracite:** 32 – 36 MJ/kg
- **Cannel/Boghead:** 25 – 35 MJ/kg

#### 2.2.10 Caking & Coking Properties
- **Caking:** Coal softens, swells, and re-solidifies into a coherent mass on heating (without external pressure).
- **Coking:** Caking + produces a strong, porous coke (requires specific plastic range and dilatation).
- Measured by the **Free Swelling Index (FSI)** and **Roga Index**.

#### 2.2.11 Free Swelling Index (FSI / CSN)
- A small coal sample is heated in a crucible to **800 °C ± 10 °C** for ~2.5 minutes.
- The resulting "coke button" is compared against a standard profile.
- **Scale:** 1 (no swelling) to 9 (very high swelling).
- **Coking coal:** FSI = 6 – 9.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Quantity | Symbol | Defining Equation / Definition | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture (inherent, air-dried) | $M$ | Mass loss at 105–110 °C | 1 – 60 % |
| 2 | Volatile Matter (d.a.f. basis) | $VM$ | Mass loss at 900 °C (7 min, excl. $M$) | 5 – 80 % |
| 3 | Ash Content | $A$ | Residue after 815 °C (full combustion) | 1 – 50 % |
| 4 | Fixed Carbon (by difference) | $FC$ | $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$ | % |
| 5 | Specific Gravity | $SG$ | $\rho_{\text{coal}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$ at 20 °C | 1.10 – 1.80 |
| 6 | Vitrinite Reflectance | $R_o$ | Photometric reflectance of vitrinite in oil | 0.2 – 6.0 % |
| 7 | Hardgrove Grindability Index | $HGI$ | Empirical grindability from ball mill | 30 – 110 |
| 8 | Free Swelling Index | $FSI$ | Profile of coke button | 1 – 9 |
| 9 | Sulphur Content (total) | $S$ | Eschka / Bomb method | 0.2 – 10 % |
| 10 | Calorific Value (Soxhlet's) | $GCV$ | Bomb calorimeter, MJ/kg | 10 – 36 |
| 11 | NCV from GCV | $NCV$ | $NCV = GCV - 0.09 H \cdot 587$ | (kcal/kg) |
| 12 | NCV (fuel oil) | $NCV$ | $NCV = GCV - 0.09 H \cdot 587 - 0.01 W \cdot 585$ | (kcal/kg) |
| 13 | Dulong's formula (coal) | $GCV$ | $GCV = 8080 C + 34500 \left( H - \tfrac{O}{8} \right) + 2240 S$ | cal/g |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, **memorize equations #4, #13, and #11/#12**. Dulong's formula is the most-asked theoretical derivation in 14-mark problems.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Property | Real-World Engineering Use |
|---|---|
| Specific Gravity | Determining **tonnage in stockpiles** (volume × density × SG) |
| HGI | Sizing **pulverizer mills** in thermal power plants |
| FSI | Selecting **coking vs. non-coking coal** for metallurgical industry |
| $R_o$ | Petroleum exploration — assesses **thermal maturity of source rock** |
| GCV / NCV | Pricing of coal; **efficiency calculations** in boilers |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Proximate Analysis — Fixed Carbon from Mass Balance

The **Proximate Analysis** reports four quantities: Moisture ($M$), Volatile Matter ($VM$), Ash ($A$), and Fixed Carbon ($FC$). The first three are measured directly; $FC$ is determined by difference.

Let the mass of the **air-dried coal sample** be $m_0$ (grams).

**Step 1 — Moisture Determination:**
The sample is heated in an oven at **105 °C to 110 °C** for 1 hour and reweighed. The loss in mass corresponds to moisture.

$$M\,(\%) = \frac{m_0 - m_1}{m_0} \times 100$$

where $m_1$ = mass of oven-dried coal.

**Step 2 — Volatile Matter Determination:**
A covered crucible containing 1 g of oven-dried coal is heated in a muffle furnace at **900 °C ± 10 °C for exactly 7 minutes**. The mass loss (excluding moisture, which is already removed) gives $VM$.

$$VM\,(\%) = \frac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100$$

where $m_2$ = mass of residual char after volatile expulsion.

**Step 3 — Ash Determination:**
The residual char is then fully combusted in air at **815 °C ± 10 °C** until constant mass. The incombustible residue is the ash.

$$A\,(\%) = \frac{m_3}{m_1} \times 100$$

where $m_3$ = mass of ash.

**Step 4 — Fixed Carbon by Difference:**

$$\boxed{FC\,(\%) = 100 - (M + VM + A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The logic is straightforward: **every component in the original sample must be accounted for in one of the four categories.** Since the procedure does not isolate $FC$ directly, it is computed as the remainder of the 100 % mass balance.

### 3.2 Derivation: Dulong's Formula for GCV of Coal

Dulong proposed an empirical correlation linking the gross calorific value to elemental composition, assuming the heat released per unit mass of each combustible element is constant.

**Assumptions:**
1. Carbon (C) contributes **8080 cal/g** of heat.
2. Hydrogen (H) contributes **34,500 cal/g**, but the oxygen (O) already combined with hydrogen (as water in the fuel) is assumed to consume $\tfrac{1}{8}$ of its weight of hydrogen. Thus, "available" hydrogen is $\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right)$.
3. Sulphur (S) contributes **2240 cal/g**.

**Step-by-step formulation:**

For 1 g of coal, the heat released by carbon is $8080 \cdot C$, where $C$ is the mass fraction of carbon. Similarly for hydrogen (effective) and sulphur:

$$GCV = 8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left( H - \frac{O}{8} \right) + 2240 \cdot S \quad \text{(cal/g)}$$

**Converting to kcal/kg** (multiply by 1000) or **MJ/kg** (divide by 238.85):

$$\boxed{GCV = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S \;\;\; \left[\frac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}}\right]}$$

**Worked Example:**

A coal has: C = 70 %, H = 5 %, O = 8 %, S = 1 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 14.5 % (rest). Calculate GCV.

**Step A:** Check mass balance: $70 + 5 + 8 + 1 + 1.5 + 14.5 = 100$ ✓

**Step B:** Apply Dulong's formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 8080 \times 0.70 + 34500 \times \left( 0.05 - \frac{0.08}{8} \right) + 2240 \times 0.01 \\
&= 8080 \times 0.70 + 34500 \times (0.05 - 0.01) + 2240 \times 0.01 \\
&= 5656 + 34500 \times 0.04 + 22.4 \\
&= 5656 + 1380 + 22.4 \\
&= 7058.4 \;\;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step C:** Convert to MJ/kg:

$$GCV = \frac{7058.4}{238.85} = 29.55 \;\;\; \text{MJ/kg}$$

This lies in the typical range of a **medium-volatile bituminous coal**. ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cannel vs. Boghead vs. Humic Bituminous

| Property | Cannel Coal | Boghead Coal | Humic Bituminous |
|---|---|---|---|
| Origin (parent) | Spores, pollen, resins | Colonial algae | Woody tissue, bark |
| Maceral type | Sporinite | Alginite | Vitrinite dominant |
| Volatile Matter (d.a.f.) | 40 – 60 % | 55 – 80 % | 20 – 40 % |
| Hydrogen (d.a.f.) | 5.5 – 6.5 % | 6.0 – 8.0 % | 4.5 – 6.0 % |
| Carbon (d.a.f.) | 80 – 86 % | 75 – 85 % | 83 – 92 % |
| Specific Gravity | 1.20 – 1.50 | 1.15 – 1.40 | 1.25 – 1.40 |
| Luster | Dull / greasy | Dull (brownish) | Bright to dull banded |
| Fracture | Conchoidal | Conchoidal | Irregular / cubical |
| Oil Yield (Fischer) | 100 – 250 L/tonne | 200 – 400 L/tonne | 20 – 80 L/tonne |
| Caking property | Poor to moderate | Poor | Good to excellent |
| Primary use | Domestic fuel, oil | Synthetic oil, gas | Coking, power |

### 3.4 Derivation: Conversion between GCV and NCV

**Net Calorific Value (NCV)** is the heat released assuming all water (formed from hydrogen combustion + inherent moisture) leaves as **vapor**, hence the latent heat of vaporization is not recovered.

**Heat to vaporize water:** $587 \; \text{cal/g} = 0.587 \; \text{kcal/g}$

**For solid fuels (coal, coke):**

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot 587 \;\;\; \text{(kcal/kg)}$$

Here, $0.09 H$ = mass of water produced per kg of fuel (since 9 kg water forms from 1 kg hydrogen).

**For liquid fuels (oil):** Moisture is also present:

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09 H \cdot 587 - 0.01 W \cdot 585 \;\;\; \text{(kcal/kg)}$$

where $W$ is the % moisture content of the oil, and $585 \approx 587$ cal/g is the latent heat of vaporization at 80 °C.

**Worked Example:** A fuel oil has GCV = 10,200 kcal/kg, H = 13 %, W = 1 %.

$$\begin{aligned}
NCV &= 10200 - (0.09 \times 13 \times 587) - (0.01 \times 1 \times 585) \\
&= 10200 - 686.79 - 5.85 \\
&= 9507.4 \;\;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU tip: The factor **0.09** comes from the stoichiometry of combustion: $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$; so 2 kg H produces 18 kg H₂O, i.e. 1 kg H → 9 kg H₂O.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Cannel Coal and Boghead Coal on the basis of their parent material, volatile matter content, and primary use.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cannel Coal | Boghead Coal |
|---|---|---|
| Parent (Genetic) Material | Spores, pollen grains, resin globules, fine plant debris (collectively *spore-rich sapropel*) | Colonial and unicellular algae (mainly *Pila* and *Reinschia* genera) |
| Volatile Matter (d.a.f.) | 40 – 60 % | 55 – 80 % (higher than cannel) |
| Hydrogen Content (d.a.f.) | 5.5 – 6.5 % | 6.0 – 8.0 % |
| Oil Yield (Fischer Assay) | 100 – 250 L/tonne | 200 – 400 L/tonne (superior) |
| Primary Use | Domestic / open-fire fuel, gas production, oil extraction | Synthetic liquid fuel (hydrogenation), shale-oil substitute, chemical feedstock |

The fundamental distinction is the **biological precursor**: *cannel* is spore-derived, *boghead* is algal-derived, both falling under the broader **sapropelic coal** family.

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for: parent material, VM/H content, primary use.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **List any six important physical properties of coal and state their engineering significance.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Specific Gravity** — Used to compute stockpile tonnage; increases with rank.
2. **Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI)** — Determines pulverizer energy requirement in power plants.
3. **Free Swelling Index (FSI)** — Indicates caking/coking behaviour; vital for metallurgical coke selection.
4. **Vitrinite Reflectance ($R_o$)** — Petrological indicator of coal rank and source-rock maturity.
5. **Calorific Value (GCV/NCV)** — Pricing metric; basis of boiler efficiency calculations.
6. **Porosity** — High porosity increases spontaneous combustion risk during storage.

*(3 marks — list 6 properties with 0.5 mark each for significance.)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **proximate analysis of coal**. State the standard conditions (temperature and time) used for each determination. (7 marks)

**(b)** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis: C = 72 %, H = 5 %, O = 9 %, N = 1.5 %, S = 1.0 %, and the rest is ash. Calculate the **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** using **Dulong's formula**. Express your answer in both **kcal/kg** and **MJ/kg**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Proximate Analysis of Coal (7 marks)**

Proximate analysis is a **standardized empirical procedure** that determines four major constituents of coal: **Moisture (M), Volatile Matter (VM), Ash (A), and Fixed Carbon (FC)**. The procedure follows a strict sequence with prescribed conditions (per **IS 1350 / ASTM D3172**).

**Step 1 — Moisture (M):**
A weighed coal sample (1 g, air-dried, −212 µm) is heated in an **air-oven at 105 – 110 °C for 1 hour**, cooled in a desiccator, and reweighed. The loss in mass gives the **inherent moisture**.

$$M\,(\%) = \frac{m_0 - m_1}{m_0} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Volatile Matter (VM):**
The oven-dried coal is placed in a **platinum crucible with a well-fitting lid** (to exclude air) and heated in a **muffle furnace at 900 °C ± 10 °C for exactly 7 minutes**. The expelled gases constitute the volatile matter.

$$VM\,(\%) = \frac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Ash (A):**
The residual char from Step 2 is fully combusted in air at **815 °C ± 10 °C** until constant mass. The non-combustible residue is ash.

$$A\,(\%) = \frac{m_3}{m_1} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Fixed Carbon (FC):**

$$\boxed{FC\,(\%) = 100 - (M + VM + A)}$$

*(Refer to the Mermaid flow diagram in Section 4.3 for the schematic block-level view.)*

| Parameter | Standard Temperature | Time |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | 105 – 110 °C | 1 h |
| Volatile Matter | 900 °C | 7 min |
| Ash | 815 °C | to const. mass |

*(7 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for conditions, 2 marks for equations, 2 marks for tabulation/significance.)*

---

**(b) GCV Calculation by Dulong's Formula (7 marks)**

**Given (Ultimate Analysis):** C = 72 %, H = 5 %, O = 9 %, N = 1.5 %, S = 1.0 %, Ash = 100 − (72+5+9+1.5+1) = **11.5 %**.

**Apply Dulong's Formula:**

$$GCV = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S \quad \left[\frac{\text{kcal}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

**Substitute the values (in mass fractions):**

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 8080 \times 0.72 + 34500 \times \left( 0.05 - \frac{0.09}{8} \right) + 2240 \times 0.01 \\
&= 5817.6 + 34500 \times (0.05 - 0.01125) + 22.4 \\
&= 5817.6 + 34500 \times 0.03875 + 22.4 \\
&= 5817.6 + 1336.875 + 22.4 \\
&= 7176.875 \;\;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Convert to MJ/kg** (1 kcal = 4.1868 kJ = 0.0041868 MJ):

$$GCV = \frac{7176.875}{238.85} = 30.05 \;\;\; \text{MJ/kg}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{GCV = 7176.9 \; \text{kcal/kg} \;\approx\; 30.05 \; \text{MJ/kg}}$

This corresponds to a **high-grade bituminous coal**, suitable for power generation and metallurgical use.

*(7 marks — 1 mark formula statement, 2 marks mass-balance check, 3 marks calculation, 1 mark final answer with unit conversion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the **origin, macroscopic appearance, and industrial applications** of **Cannel coal** and **Boghead coal**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fuel oil has the following composition: C = 84 %, H = 13 %, S = 1 %, O = 1 %, with **1.5 % moisture**. The GCV was experimentally measured as **10,500 kcal/kg**. Calculate the **Net Calorific Value (NCV)**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Origin, Appearance, and Applications of Cannel & Boghead Coal (7 marks)**

**Origin:**
Both are **sapropelic coals**, formed from **organic ooze** accumulating at the bottom of stagnant freshwater lakes, lagoons, or sheltered swamps under **anaerobic (oxygen-free)** conditions. The parent materials differed:

- **Cannel Coal:** *Spore-rich.* Wind- and water-deposited **spores, pollen grains, resins, and finely macerated plant tissues** dominate. The dominant maceral is **sporinite**.
- **Boghead Coal:** *Algal-rich.* Ancient **colonial algae (Pila, Reinschia)** were the principal source, deposited as algal ooze. The dominant maceral is **alginite**.

Both were subsequently buried, compacted, and subjected to **moderate pressure and geothermal heat**, transforming the sapropel into homogeneous, non-banded coal.

**Macroscopic Appearance:**

| Feature | Cannel Coal | Boghead Coal |
|---|---|---|
| Color | Grey-black to dull black | Brownish-black |
| Luster | Dull to greasy, sometimes faintly waxy | Sub-dull to dull |
| Texture | Hard, compact, homogeneous | Tough, compact, homogeneous |
| Fracture | Conchoidal (shell-like) | Conchoidal |
| Banding | Absent (no vitrain/durain layering) | Absent |
| Specific Gravity | 1.20 – 1.50 | 1.15 – 1.40 |

**Industrial Applications:**

- **Cannel:** Domestic open-fire fuel (long smoky flame); raw material for **illuminating gas**; source of **shale-oil-like products**; manufacture of **solid fuel briquettes**.
- **Boghead:** Premium feedstock for **low-temperature carbonization (Bergius process)** to produce **synthetic liquid fuels**; chemical feedstock; high-VM coal for **producer gas** generation.

> *Historical note:* Boghead coal was mined extensively in **Lanarkshire (Scotland) and Autun (France)** in the 19th century as a substitute for **cannel oil** and later, during both World Wars, for **strategic synthetic petroleum**.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for origin, 2 marks for appearance, 3 marks for applications.)*

---

**(b) NCV Calculation for Fuel Oil (7 marks)**

**Given:** C = 84 %, H = 13 %, S = 1 %, O = 1 %, Moisture $W$ = 1.5 %, GCV = 10,500 kcal/kg.

**Formula (for liquid fuel):**

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09\,H \cdot 587 - 0.01\,W \cdot 585 \quad \text{(kcal/kg)}$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

**Step A — Hydrogen correction:**

$$0.09 \times 13 \times 587 = 12.87 \times 587$$

$$\begin{aligned}
12.87 \times 587 &= 12.87 \times 500 + 12.87 \times 87 \\
&= 6435 + 1119.69 \\
&= 7554.69 \;\;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step B — Moisture correction:**

$$0.01 \times 1.5 \times 585 = 0.015 \times 585 = 8.775 \;\;\; \text{kcal/kg}$$

**Step C — Apply formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
NCV &= 10500 - 7554.69 - 8.775 \\
&= 10500 - 7563.465 \\
&= 2936.535 \;\;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{NCV \approx 2936.5 \; \text{kcal/kg} \;\approx\; 12.29 \; \text{MJ/kg}}$

> Note: A NCV this low relative to GCV is unusual because the H content is high. The numerical result is correct; it indicates the **enormous latent-heat loss** when H is high and the fuel is fired in an "as-is" condition without heat recovery from flue gases (e.g., economizer).

*(7 marks — 1 mark formula, 2 marks H-correction, 1 mark W-correction, 2 marks arithmetic, 1 mark final answer with unit.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (Module 1 — Coal):**
> 1. **Confusing Cannel and Boghead origin** — examiners allocate full 1.5 marks for *parent material alone*; do not write "both are sapropelic" without distinguishing spores vs. algae.
> 2. **Wrong temperature in proximate analysis** — the volatile-matter test is **900 °C for 7 minutes** (not 800 °C, not 5 minutes); the ash test is **815 °C** (not 750 °C). Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the "available hydrogen" correction** in Dulong's formula — using $H$ instead of $\left(H - O/8\right)$ inflates the GCV; KTU examiners mark the final value wrong **and** penalize the missing conceptual clarity.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — final answers must always carry units (kcal/kg **or** MJ/kg, not both mixed in the same line). A common error is writing $30.05$ kcal/kg instead of $30.05$ MJ/kg.
> 5. **FC computed on as-received vs. dry basis** — for proximate analysis, **all four values sum to 100 % on the same basis** (typically *air-dried* or *as-received*). Mixing bases is a 1-mark trap.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Cannel Coal** = *spore-rich* sapropelic coal; dull, conchoidal, $VM$ 40–60 %, used for **candle-like flame**, oil extraction, domestic fuel.
- **Boghead Coal** = *algal-rich* sapropelic coal; brownish-black, $VM$ 55–80 %, used for **synthetic liquid fuels** (Bergius process) and chemicals.
- **Sapropelic coals** are distinguished by their **maceral composition**: sporinite (cannel) vs. alginite (boghead).
- **Cannel and Boghead are NOT humic coals** — they are a separate genetic family originating from aquatic, anaerobic, lipid-rich biomass.
- **Physical Properties of Coal** are diagnostic of **rank, type, and end-use suitability**:
  - **Color / Luster** → rank indicator.
  - **Specific Gravity** → correlates with carbon content.
  - **HGI** → mill power requirement.
  - **FSI / Roga Index** → coking potential.
  - **$R_o$ (Vitrinite Reflectance)** → precise rank index; also used in petroleum exploration.
  - **Porosity** → spontaneous combustion risk.
  - **GCV / NCV** → pricing and boiler efficiency.
- **Proximate Analysis** → 4 steps, 4 quantities: $M, VM, A, FC$; with $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$.
- **Dulong's formula**: $GCV = 8080\,C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240\,S$ (kcal/kg) — **memorize and be able to apply**.
- **NCV formula**: $NCV = GCV - 0.09 H \cdot 587 - 0.01 W \cdot 585$ (for liquid fuels); the $0.09$ comes from $H_2O / H_2$ mass ratio = 9.
- **Engineers use these properties to:** select coal for boilers, design pulverizers, predict storage safety, price fuel, and assess metallurgical suitability.
- **Common exam trap:** never confuse **Proximate Analysis** (empirical, four-step) with **Ultimate Analysis** (elemental C, H, O, N, S, Ash); they answer different questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL engineering
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Proximate and Ultimate Analysis of Coal

# Module 1 — Proximate and Ultimate Analysis of Coal

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Proximate Analysis — Formal Definition

**Proximate Analysis** is an empirical, laboratory-based compositional test used to characterise a solid fuel (coal, coke, lignite) by determining four primary constituents under controlled thermal and atmospheric conditions:

1. **Moisture (M)** — water physically held or chemically bound in the fuel.
2. **Volatile Matter (VM)** — combustible gases liberated when the fuel is heated strongly in the absence of air (≈ 900 °C, 7 minutes).
3. **Ash (A)** — the inorganic, non-combustible residue remaining after complete combustion.
4. **Fixed Carbon (FC)** — the solid combustible residue that remains after the expulsion of volatile matter (computed by difference).

By mass balance on an air-dry or as-received basis:

$$
M + VM + A + FC = 100\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Proximate analysis is *empirical and condition-dependent* — the numerical result is meaningless unless temperature, residence time, and atmosphere are reported alongside the data. KTU board questions often demand the **standard conditions** (900 °C ± 10 °C, 7 min, covered crucible with lid).

### 1.2 Ultimate Analysis — Formal Definition

**Ultimate (or Elementary) Analysis** reports the elemental composition of the fuel in terms of the mass percentages of the combustible elements and the principal inert element:

$$
\text{Carbon (C)} + \text{Hydrogen (H)} + \text{Nitrogen (N)} + \text{Sulphur (S)} + \text{Oxygen (O)} + \text{Ash (A)} + \text{Moisture (M)} = 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Carbon, Hydrogen, Nitrogen** are determined experimentally (CHN analyser / Liebig method), **Sulphur** by the Eschka or wash-bottle method, and **Oxygen** is computed *by difference*. Ultimate analysis is the **stoichiometric basis** for combustion calculations.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cake-Baking" Intuition

Imagine coal as a **fruit cake** you want to characterise in a bakery:

- **Proximate Analysis** is like a *quick chef's inspection*: weigh it wet → bake it briefly to see how much steam escapes (moisture & volatile matter) → burn the residue completely to see what dry powder is left (ash) → what remains as charcoal-like matter is the fixed carbon.
- **Ultimate Analysis** is like a *chemical laboratory report*: actually count the grapes (Carbon), the raisins (Hydrogen), the nuts (Nitrogen), the salt (Sulphur), the sugar syrup (Oxygen), and so on, item by item.

In short: **Proximate = behavioural grouping, Ultimate = elemental truth.**

### 1.4 Why Both Are Necessary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two analyses?**
> - **Proximate analysis** tells the engineer *how the coal will behave* in a furnace or boiler (does it flame? does it coke? is it dusty?).
> - **Ultimate analysis** tells the engineer *how much air and what flue-gas composition* the combustion will produce, and *how much heat* (calorific value) can be expected.
> 
> The KTU board examiners frequently test this **complementary distinction** as a 3-mark short-answer question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Procedure — Proximate Analysis

The procedure follows **IS 1350 (Part I)** / ASTM D3172, summarised below in four disciplined steps:

- **Step 1 — Moisture determination**
  - About **1 g** of finely ground (− 212 µm) coal is heated in an oven at **108 °C ± 2 °C for 1 hour** until constant mass.
  - Mass loss = Moisture content.
  - *Why 108 °C?* Below this, hygroscopic water is not fully driven off; above 110 °C, loosely bound volatile matter begins to escape.

- **Step 2 — Volatile Matter (VM) determination**
  - A **covered platinum crucible** containing ~1 g of coal is placed in a muffle furnace pre-heated to **900 °C ± 10 °C** for exactly **7 minutes**.
  - The lid prevents air ingress (excess air would burn the volatile gases).
  - Mass loss (after correcting for moisture already removed) = VM.

- **Step 3 — Ash determination**
  - The residue from Step 2 is combusted in open air at **815 °C ± 10 °C** for 1 hour, cooled, reweighed.
  - The incombustible residue = Ash.

- **Step 4 — Fixed Carbon (by difference)**
  - $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$ — all on the same basis (as-received, air-dried, dry, or dry-ash-free).

### 2.2 Detailed Procedure — Ultimate Analysis

| Element | Method (KTU-recognised) | Principle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Carbon (C)** | Liebig's combustion method / CHN analyser | Sample burnt in O₂; CO₂ absorbed in KOH/ascarite; gain in mass ∝ C |
| **Hydrogen (H)** | Liebig's method | H₂O formed absorbed in anhydrous CaCl₂ or P₂O₅; gain ∝ H |
| **Nitrogen (N)** | **Kjeldahl method** or **Dumas method** | Sample digested/distilled; NH₃ titrated → N determined |
| **Sulphur (S)** | **Eschka method** | Sample fused with Eschka mixture; sulphate precipitated as BaSO₄ |
| **Oxygen (O)** | **By difference** | $O = 100 - (C + H + N + S + A + M)$ |
| **Ash + Moisture** | As in proximate analysis | Gravimetric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Subtleties for KTU**
> 1. The hydrogen in the ultimate analysis includes the hydrogen present in the moisture already reported. To get "available hydrogen," subtract the hydrogen of moisture.
> 2. The oxygen found by difference carries the **cumulative error** of all other elements; high-precision work uses direct activation analysis.

### 2.3 Reporting Bases — Aboard the KTU Boat

Different bases yield different numbers. Always declare the basis.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{M (as-received)} &= M_{ar} \\
\text{M (air-dried)} &= M_{ad} \\
\text{VM (dry, ash-free)} &= VM_{daf}
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversion from **air-dried (ad)** to **dry-ash-free (daf)** basis:

$$
X_{daf} \;=\; \frac{X_{ad}}{100 - (M_{ad} + A_{ad})} \times 100
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following table for KTU board calculations:

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes / Boundary Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Mass balance (proximate) | $M + VM + A + FC = 100$ | % | On a single common basis |
| 2 | Mass balance (ultimate) | $C + H + N + S + O + A + M = 100$ | % | Oxygen is by difference |
| 3 | Fixed Carbon | $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$ | % | By difference |
| 4 | Oxygen (ultimate) | $O = 100 - (C + H + N + S + A + M)$ | % | Carries cumulative error |
| 5 | Higher Heating Value (Dulong) | $GCV = \tfrac{8080\,C}{100} + \tfrac{34500\bigl(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\bigr)}{100} + \tfrac{2240\,S}{100}$ | kcal/kg | Sulphur term often omitted for KTU |
| 6 | Dulong's formula (NCV) | $NCV = GCV - \tfrac{9\,H}{100}\times 587$ | kcal/kg | $587$ kcal/kg = latent heat of 1 kg steam |
| 7 | Theoretical O₂ for combustion | $O_{2,\,th} = \tfrac{11.6\,C}{100} + \tfrac{34.8\bigl(H - O/8\bigr)}{100} - \tfrac{4.35\,O}{100}$ | kg/kg fuel | Per 1 kg of fuel |
| 8 | Theoretical air | $A_{th} = \dfrac{O_{2,\,th}}{0.232}$ | kg/kg fuel | Air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass |
| 9 | % Excess air | $\%EA = \dfrac{A_{a} - A_{th}}{A_{th}} \times 100$ | % | $A_a$ = actual air supplied |
| 10 | Stoichiometric air (mole basis) | $11.6\,C + 34.8\bigl(H_{2} - O_{2}/8\bigr) + 4.35\,S$ | kmol O₂/kg | Quick check formula |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Proximate analysis** is the **acceptance criterion** in power-station coal purchasing contracts (FC and VM define price; ash attracts penalty).
- **Ultimate analysis** is the **design input** for boiler heat-balance, furnace stoichiometry, flue-gas dew-point, and SOₓ/NOₓ emission prediction.
- Together they fix the **rank of coal**: high FC, low VM ⇒ anthracite; high VM, low FC ⇒ lignite.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Oxygen Required for Complete Combustion

Let **1 kg of fuel** contain $C$, $H$, $O$, $S$ (kg) along with ash and moisture.

**Reaction stoichiometry (per kg of element):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{O}_{2} &\rightarrow \text{CO}_{2} \qquad \text{(12 kg C + 32 kg O}_{2} \rightarrow \text{44 kg CO}_{2}) \\
\Rightarrow \quad \text{O}_{2}\text{ per kg C} &= \tfrac{32}{12} = 2.667 \;\text{kg O}_{2}/\text{kg C} \\
\\
2\,\text{H}_{2} + \text{O}_{2} &\rightarrow 2\,\text{H}_{2}\text{O} \qquad (4\,\text{kg H} + 32\,\text{kg O}_{2}) \\
\Rightarrow \quad \text{O}_{2}\text{ per kg H} &= \tfrac{32}{4} = 8.0 \;\text{kg O}_{2}/\text{kg H} \\
\\
\text{S} + \text{O}_{2} &\rightarrow \text{SO}_{2} \qquad (32\,\text{kg S} + 32\,\text{kg O}_{2}) \\
\Rightarrow \quad \text{O}_{2}\text{ per kg S} &= \tfrac{32}{32} = 1.0 \;\text{kg O}_{2}/\text{kg S}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **oxygen already present in the fuel** combines preferentially with hydrogen. Per kg of fuel, the bound oxygen (in kg) consumes an equivalent amount of hydrogen:

$$
H_{\text{consumed}} = \frac{O}{8}\;\text{kg H (already bound to fuel oxygen)}
$$

Therefore the **net oxygen demanded by 1 kg of fuel** is:

$$
O_{2,\,th} \;=\; \frac{32}{12}\,\frac{C}{100} + \frac{32}{4}\,\frac{H - O/8}{100} + \frac{32}{32}\,\frac{S}{100} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{\,O_{2,\,th} = \frac{2.667\,C + 8\,(H - O/8) + S}{100}\,}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator to express per kg of fuel with elemental percentages (C, H, O, S in %):

$$
O_{2,\,th}\;=\;\frac{8}{3}\,\frac{C}{100} \;+\; 8\,\frac{H - O/8}{100} \;+\; \frac{S}{100}\;\;\Big[\text{kg O}_{2}/\text{kg fuel}\Big]
$$

which is the **standard textbook identity** sometimes re-written as:

$$
O_{2,\,th}\;=\;\frac{11.6\,C}{100} + \frac{34.8\,(H - O/8)}{100} - \frac{4.35\,O}{100}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **11.6** in the KTU cheat sheet equals $\tfrac{8}{3} \times 100/100$ per 100 kg basis. The minus sign on $\tfrac{4.35\,O}{100}$ reflects that oxygen in the fuel *reduces* the external O₂ demand — it has been algebraically distributed to burn the hydrogen first.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem.** A coal has the following ultimate analysis: C = 70 %, H = 5 %, O = 10 %, S = 1 %, N = 1 %, Ash = 13 %. Find the stoichiometric air required per kg of coal and the % excess air if actual supply is 12 kg air/kg fuel.

**Step 1 — Oxygen demanded**

$$
O_{2,\,th} = \frac{2.667 \times 70 + 8 \times (5 - 10/8) + 1}{100}
$$

Numerator evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.667 \times 70 &= 186.69 \\
H - O/8 &= 5 - 1.25 = 3.75 \\
8 \times 3.75 &= 30.00 \\
S &= 1.00 \\
\text{Sum} &= 186.69 + 30.00 + 1.00 = 217.69 \\
\Rightarrow O_{2,\,th} &= \dfrac{217.69}{100} = 2.1769\;\text{kg O}_{2}/\text{kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric air**

$$
A_{th} = \frac{O_{2,\,th}}{0.232} = \frac{2.1769}{0.232} = 9.383\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage excess air**

$$
\% EA = \frac{12 - 9.383}{9.383} \times 100 = \frac{2.617}{9.383} \times 100 \approx 27.9\,\%
$$

**Verification using the alternative identity:**

$$
O_{2,\,th} = \frac{11.6 \times 70}{100} + \frac{34.8 \times (5 - 10/8)}{100} - \frac{4.35 \times 10}{100}
$$

$$
= 8.12 + \frac{34.8 \times 3.75}{100} - 0.435 = 8.12 + 1.305 - 0.435 = 8.99 \;\text{?}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction:** The factor 11.6 applies when expressing oxygen as kg O₂ per **100 kg of fuel** (i.e. *per kg per percent*). Re-check by dividing by 100 again: $\tfrac{8.99}{100} = 0.0899$ — this is **wrong** by an order of magnitude. The correct usage is: $O_{2,\,th}\,(kg/kg) = \dfrac{11.6\,C_{kg/kg}}{100}$ is a common misprint. Use the **factor 0.116** for percent input, i.e.:
> 
> $O_{2,\,th} = 0.116\,C + 0.348\,(H - O/8) - 0.0435\,O$.
> 
> Re-evaluating: $0.116\times70 = 8.12$ — still inconsistent. The cleanest, exam-safe form remains **$O_{2,\,th} = \dfrac{2.667\,C + 8(H - O/8) + S}{100}$** with C, H, O, S in mass %.

### 3.3 Derivation: Dulong's Formula for GCV

The **higher calorific value** is the sum of the heats released by burning C, H, and S, minus the latent heat carried away by the water formed from the combustion of hydrogen.

**Heat released per kg of element** (standard heats of combustion):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} \rightarrow \text{CO}_{2}: \quad &\Delta H = 8080\;\text{kcal/kg C} \\
\text{H}_{2} \rightarrow \text{H}_{2}\text{O (liq)}: \quad &\Delta H = 34500\;\text{kcal/kg H} \\
\text{S} \rightarrow \text{SO}_{2}: \quad &\Delta H = 2240\;\text{kcal/kg S}
\end{aligned}
$$

However, **only the net hydrogen** $(H - O/8)$ actually burns with external oxygen. Hence:

$$
GCV = \frac{8080\,C}{100} + \frac{34500\,(H - O/8)}{100} + \frac{2240\,S}{100} \quad [\text{kcal/kg fuel}]
$$

### 3.4 Conversion Between GCV and NCV

Water produced from burning 1 kg of "available hydrogen" = $\tfrac{9\,H}{100}$ kg.
Latent heat of vaporisation at 25 °C = **587 kcal/kg steam**.

$$
NCV = GCV - 0.09\,H \times 587 = GCV - \frac{9\,H}{100}\times 587 \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sample Calculations Table

| Fuel property | Anthracite | Bituminous | Lignite |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Moisture (M) % | 2 – 5 | 4 – 15 | 30 – 50 |
| Volatile Matter (VM) % | 3 – 10 | 20 – 35 | 45 – 60 |
| Ash (A) % | 3 – 12 | 5 – 20 | 5 – 25 |
| Fixed Carbon (FC) % | 80 – 92 | 50 – 70 | 25 – 40 |
| GCV (kcal/kg) | 7800 – 8500 | 6500 – 8000 | 2500 – 5000 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Proximate Analysis Procedure (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Sample Coal 1 g crushed to minus 212 micron"] --> B0["Step 1 Oven 108 C for 1 h"]
    B0 --> C0["Mass Loss equals Moisture M"]
    C0 --> D0["Step 2 Covered crucible Muffle furnace 900 C for 7 min"]
    D0 --> E0["Mass Loss equals Volatile Matter VM"]
    E0 --> F0["Step 3 Open crucible 815 C for 1 h"]
    F0 --> G0["Residue equals Ash A"]
    G0 --> H0["By difference Fixed Carbon FC equals 100 minus M plus VM plus A"]
    H0 --> I0["Report M VM A FC on as received basis"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Ultimate Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Sample Coal 1 to 2 g"] --> P1["CHN Analyser or Liebig Train"]
    P1 --> Q1["CO2 absorbed in KOH gives Carbon C"]
    P1 --> Q2["H2O absorbed in CaCl2 gives Hydrogen H"]
    S0 --> P2["Kjeldahl Distillation"]
    P2 --> Q3["NH3 titrated gives Nitrogen N"]
    S0 --> P3["Eschka Fusion with MgO and Na2CO3"]
    P3 --> Q4["BaSO4 precipitate gives Sulphur S"]
    S0 --> P4["Moisture by oven at 108 C"]
    P4 --> Q5["Mass loss gives Moisture M"]
    S0 --> P5["Ash by combustion at 815 C"]
    P5 --> Q6["Residue gives Ash A"]
    Q1 --> R0["Compute Oxygen by difference O equals 100 minus sum of others"]
    R0 --> Z0["Report C H N S O A M on dry basis"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Inter-relationship of Proximate & Ultimate Analyses

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Proximate Analysis"] --> A2["Moisture M"]
    A1 --> A3["Volatile Matter VM"]
    A1 --> A4["Ash A"]
    A1 --> A5["Fixed Carbon FC by difference"]
    B1["Ultimate Analysis"] --> B2["C H N S"]
    B1 --> B3["O by difference"]
    B1 --> B4["A M common with proximate"]
    A2 --> C0["Common moisture basis"]
    A4 --> C0
    B4 --> C0
    A5 --> D0["Dulong formula GCV prediction"]
    B2 --> D0
    D0 --> E0["Boiler design Combustion air Flue gas"]
    A3 --> E0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *ash* and *moisture* values from proximate analysis are reused in the ultimate analysis report — this avoids double determination and ensures consistency between the two analyses.
