## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between proximate analysis and ultimate analysis of coal. Why are both analyses required for complete fuel characterisation?*

**Model Answer (≈ 130 words):**

> **Proximate Analysis** is empirical and determines four mass fractions — **Moisture, Volatile Matter, Ash, and Fixed Carbon (by difference)** — by controlled heating under standard conditions (108 °C for M, 900 °C for VM, 815 °C for A). It reveals the **thermal behaviour** of the coal in a furnace — its ease of ignition, flame length, and coke-forming tendency.
>
> **Ultimate Analysis** is stoichiometric and reports the **elemental composition** — C, H, N, S, O, A, M — with O determined by difference. It is the basis for **combustion calculations, air requirement, flue-gas composition, and calorific value prediction (Dulong's formula)**.
>
> Both are required because proximate analysis alone cannot predict combustion air or calorific value, while ultimate analysis alone cannot predict the flame characteristics or coking behaviour — they are **complementary engineering inputs**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why is volatile matter determined in a covered crucible? What is the significance of the 7-minute residence time at 900 °C in the proximate analysis of coal?*

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

> The **covered crucible** prevents the ingress of external air so that the volatile gases evolved during heating are *not oxidised* and lost as additional mass loss — only the *devolatilisation* mass loss is measured. This isolates the **true volatile matter** from any combustion of volatile gases.
>
> The **7-minute duration at 900 °C** is a standardised empirical compromise:
> - Shorter times ⇒ incomplete release of volatiles ⇒ **under-reporting** of VM.
> - Longer times ⇒ secondary decomposition of fixed carbon and coke shrinkage ⇒ **over-reporting** of VM.
>
> Hence the IS 1350 / ASTM D3172 standard specifies **900 °C ± 10 °C for exactly 7 min** to obtain reproducible, comparable VM values across laboratories worldwide.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, internal choice)

> **ESE Module 1 Internal Choice Instruction (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Answer any one of the following:*

---

#### **Question A — 14 marks** *(Compute-driven descriptive)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 model paper]**

**(a)** A coal sample has the following proximate analysis on an air-dried basis: Moisture 4 %, Volatile Matter 28 %, Ash 18 %. Calculate the Fixed Carbon and convert all four constituents to the **dry, ash-free (daf)** basis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The ultimate analysis of a different coal is: C = 72 %, H = 5 %, N = 1.4 %, S = 0.6 %, O = 8 %, Ash = 9 %, Moisture = 4 %. Determine (i) the **GCV using Dulong's formula**, (ii) the **NCV**, and (iii) the **theoretical minimum air required** for complete combustion per kg of fuel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

$$
FC_{ad} = 100 - (M + VM + A) = 100 - (4 + 28 + 18) = 50\,\%
$$

Conversion to **daf basis** — denominator:

$$
100 - (M_{ad} + A_{ad}) = 100 - (4 + 18) = 78
$$

Each constituent on daf:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{daf} &= \frac{4}{78}\times100 = 5.13\,\% \\
VM_{daf} &= \frac{28}{78}\times100 = 35.90\,\% \\
A_{daf} &= \frac{18}{78}\times100 = 23.08\,\% \\
FC_{daf} &= \frac{50}{78}\times100 = 64.10\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $5.13 + 35.90 + 0 + 64.10 = 105.13$ — Wait, **ash becomes zero on daf basis** because ash is mathematically removed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake — Pitfall 1:** On the **dry, ash-free basis**, the moisture and ash are *both* zero by definition. Re-check: $(M + VM + FC)_{daf} = 5.13 + 35.90 + 64.10 = 105.13$ — but on daf basis *only* M, VM, FC exist (ash = 0). The correct formulation: $X_{daf} = \dfrac{X_{ad}}{100 - (M_{ad}+A_{ad})}\times100$, applied to **M, VM, FC only**, gives $5.13 + 35.90 + 64.10 = 105.13$. The 5 % over-shoot is because the denominator $(78)$ does not include ash, but we are now summing without ash; the *correct* relationship: $M_{ad}+VM_{ad}+FC_{ad} = 100 - A_{ad} = 82$ and dividing each by 82 would give $4.88 + 34.15 + 60.98 = 100$. The formula in Section 2.3 above states $X_{daf} = \dfrac{X_{ad}}{100 - (M_{ad}+A_{ad})}\times100$. Apply this to **M, VM** only, and compute FC$_{daf}$ = $100 - M_{daf} - VM_{daf}$:
> 
> $$
> M_{daf} = \frac{4}{78}\times100 = 5.13\,\%, \quad VM_{daf} = \frac{28}{78}\times100 = 35.90\,\%, \quad FC_{daf} = 100 - 5.13 - 35.90 = 58.97\,\%
> $$
> 
> Verify: $5.13 + 35.90 + 58.97 = 100.00\;\checkmark$

**Model Solution — (b):**

**(i) GCV by Dulong's formula:**

$$
GCV = \frac{8080\,C}{100} + \frac{34500\,(H - O/8)}{100} + \frac{2240\,S}{100}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{8080 \times 72}{100} &= 5817.60\;\text{kcal/kg} \\
H - O/8 &= 5 - 8/8 = 5 - 1 = 4.0 \\
\frac{34500 \times 4}{100} &= 1380.00\;\text{kcal/kg} \\
\frac{2240 \times 0.6}{100} &= 13.44\;\text{kcal/kg} \\
\boxed{GCV &= 5817.60 + 1380.00 + 13.44 = 7211.04\;\text{kcal/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) NCV:**

$$
NCV = GCV - \frac{9\,H}{100}\times 587 = 7211.04 - \frac{9\times 5}{100}\times 587 = 7211.04 - 264.15
$$

$$
\boxed{NCV = 6946.89\;\text{kcal/kg}}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical air:**

$$
O_{2,\,th} = \frac{2.667\,C + 8\,(H - O/8) + S}{100} = \frac{2.667\times72 + 8\times4 + 0.6}{100}
$$

$$
= \frac{192.024 + 32 + 0.6}{100} = \frac{224.624}{100} = 2.246\;\text{kg O}_{2}/\text{kg fuel}
$$

$$
A_{th} = \frac{2.246}{0.232} = \boxed{9.68\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}}
$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 marks** *(Alternative choice — Descriptive + conceptual)*

**(a)** Explain, with a neat flowchart, the **proximate analysis** of coal. State the standard temperature and time conditions for each determination and clearly mention the role of the crucible lid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coal has the following ultimate analysis: C = 65 %, H = 4 %, O = 12 %, S = 0.5 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 12 %, Moisture = 5 %. Calculate:
   (i) **GCV** using Dulong's formula,
   (ii) **NCV**,
   (iii) **Stoichiometric air** required per kg of coal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

Refer to the **Block Diagram in Section 4.1** above. The flowchart structure is:

- **Sample preparation** → 1 g of coal ground to − 212 µm.
- **Moisture** → oven at **108 °C ± 2 °C for 1 h**, cooled in desiccator, reweighed. Mass loss = M.
- **Volatile Matter** → covered platinum crucible in muffle furnace at **900 °C ± 10 °C for exactly 7 min**. **Crucible lid prevents air ingress** so that volatile gases escape without burning; the resulting mass loss = VM (corrected for moisture).
- **Ash** → uncovered crucible at **815 °C ± 10 °C for 1 h** until constant mass. Residue = A.
- **Fixed Carbon** → by difference: $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$.

**Role of the lid:** The crucible lid ensures that the *primary volatile gases* leave the sample without oxidising in air (which would otherwise yield higher mass loss and contaminate the result). It standardises the test so that the VM value is a *property of the coal*, not of the test conditions.

**Model Solution — (b):**

**(i) GCV by Dulong's formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{8080 \times 65}{100} &= 5252.00\;\text{kcal/kg} \\
H - O/8 &= 4 - 12/8 = 4 - 1.5 = 2.5 \\
\frac{34500 \times 2.5}{100} &= 862.50\;\text{kcal/kg} \\
\frac{2240 \times 0.5}{100} &= 11.20\;\text{kcal/kg} \\
\boxed{GCV &= 5252.00 + 862.50 + 11.20 = 6125.70\;\text{kcal/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) NCV:**

$$
NCV = GCV - \frac{9\,H}{100}\times 587 = 6125.70 - \frac{9\times 4}{100}\times 587 = 6125.70 - 211.32
$$

$$
\boxed{NCV = 5914.38\;\text{kcal/kg}}
$$

**(iii) Stoichiometric air:**

$$
O_{2,\,th} = \frac{2.667\times65 + 8\times2.5 + 0.5}{100} = \frac{173.355 + 20 + 0.5}{100} = 1.939\;\text{kg O}_{2}/\text{kg fuel}
$$

$$
A_{th} = \frac{1.939}{0.232} = \boxed{8.36\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Marks Are Lost**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract O/8** from H in Dulong's formula — costs **2 marks** typically.
> 2. **Mixing reporting bases** (as-received vs air-dried vs daf) within the same answer — always re-state the basis before each numerical column.
> 3. **Oxygen in fuel not credited** when computing air requirement — the net hydrogen $(H - O/8)$ is the *only* hydrogen that demands external oxygen.
> 4. **Covered vs uncovered crucible** confusion in VM determination — examiners repeatedly deduct marks for omitting the word *"covered"*.
> 5. **Ash non-zero on daf basis** — by definition, ash = 0 on dry-ash-free basis; do not convert ash to daf.
> 6. **NCV sign error** — NCV is *always* lower than GCV (latent heat loss); a numerically higher NCV means an arithmetic error.
> 7. **Latent heat constant** — use **587 kcal/kg** (not 540 or 600); examiners check the constant.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Proximate analysis** reports **M, VM, A, FC** (FC by difference); empirical; standard conditions: **108 °C / 900 °C / 815 °C**.
- **Ultimate analysis** reports **C, H, N, S, O, A, M** (O by difference); stoichiometric basis for design.
- **Crucible must be covered** during VM determination to exclude air.
- **VM standard time = 7 min** at 900 °C; deviations distort rank classification.
- **Net hydrogen** for Dulong and air-calculation = $(H - O/8)$.
- **Dulong's formula:** $GCV = \tfrac{8080\,C}{100} + \tfrac{34500(H-O/8)}{100} + \tfrac{2240\,S}{100}$ kcal/kg.
- **NCV = GCV − 0.09 H × 587** kcal/kg.
- **Theoretical O₂** per kg fuel = $\tfrac{2.667\,C + 8(H-O/8) + S}{100}$ kg; **air = O₂/0.232**.
- **Reporting bases:** as-received (ar), air-dried (ad), dry (d), dry-ash-free (daf) — always declare.
- **daF basis:** Ash = 0; Moisture = 0; VM + FC = 100.
- **Rank progression:** Peat → Lignite → Bituminous → Anthracite (increasing FC, decreasing VM, increasing GCV).
- **Oxygen-by-difference** carries cumulative experimental error — flag it in viva.
- **Ash and Moisture** values are common to both analyses — don't re-determine.
- **KTU-must-memorise constants:** $8080$, $34500$, $2240$, $587$, $0.232$, $900$ °C, $7$ min.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cleaning, Washing and Storage of Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cleaning, Washing and Storage of Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cleaning of Coal — Formal Definition

**Cleaning of Coal** is the physical, chemical, or biological separation of undesirable mineral matter (ash-forming constituents), sulfur-bearing compounds, and moisture from raw run-of-mine (ROM) coal to upgrade its heating value, improve combustion efficiency, and reduce environmental pollutants during utilization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Coal cleaning (also called coal beneficiation or coal preparation)* refers to the treatment of raw coal to **reject a portion of inherent mineral matter and sulfur**, producing a clean coal product of consistent quality that meets the specifications of the end-user (utility boilers, cement kilns, metallurgical coke ovens, or gasifiers).

### Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **ROM Coal** | Run-of-Mine coal — the raw coal as it comes from the mine |
| **Clean Coal** | Coal after removal of impurities (low ash, low sulfur, low moisture) |
| **Yield** | Mass ratio of clean coal obtained from ROM feed |
| **Near-Grid Material** | Coal particles with density close to separating medium; difficult to separate |
| **Refuse / Tailings** | Rejected high-ash, high-density fraction |
| **Middlings** | Intermediate product containing locked-in ash particles |
| **Cut-point Density** | Specific gravity at which 50% separation is achieved |

---

## 1.2 Washing of Coal — Formal Definition

**Washing of Coal** is the specific *physical* cleaning process in which coal particles are separated from mineral impurities based on **differences in density (specific gravity)** using a liquid medium — typically water, magnetite suspensions, or organic dense liquids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Washing" is a subset of "Cleaning":**
> All washing is cleaning, but cleaning includes non-aqueous methods (e.g., dry dedusting, electrostatic separation, chemical leaching). Washing strictly involves liquid-media separations.

---

## 1.3 Storage of Coal — Formal Definition

**Storage of Coal** refers to the engineered methods employed to stockpile coal at mines, transit points, or consumer sites in a manner that:
1. Preserves its **calorific value**,
2. Prevents **spontaneous combustion**,
3. Minimizes **weathering** (oxidation by air, rain, sun),
4. Allows **blending** for consistent quality, and
5. Provides **operational buffer** against supply disruptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:** A typical 500 MW thermal power plant consumes **~12,000–15,000 tonnes of coal per day**. Storage strategies must guarantee at least **15–30 days** of buffer inventory.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **Analogy: The Rice Cleaning Story** 🍚
>
> Imagine you buy a sack of raw rice from a farmer. Inside the sack are:
> - Whole rice grains (your **clean coal** — useful fuel),
> - Tiny stones of the same color as rice (your **shale and clays**),
> - Yellow/broken grains (your **pyritic sulfur and middlings**),
> - Fine dust and husks (your **moisture and surface clays**).
>
> **Cleaning** is the overall process of removing everything bad from the rice.
> **Washing** specifically mimics rinsing rice in water — the good grains sink, the dust and chaff float (a *float-and-sink* separation).
> **Storage** is like keeping the cleaned rice in an airtight steel container away from sunlight and moisture to keep it fresh — just like coal in a covered silo with inert gas blanketing.
>
> In both cases, the goal is identical: **maximum fuel/grain, minimum contaminant, stable quality until use.**

---

## 1.5 Why Cleaning, Washing & Storage Matter — The Driving Forces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Favourite: Reasons for Coal Cleaning**

1. **Environmental Compliance:** Reduces SO₂ emissions (by up to **40–50%**), NOx, and particulate load.
2. **Boiler Efficiency:** Every 1% ash reduction improves boiler efficiency by ~**0.5–1%**.
3. **Reduced Slagging & Fouling:** Less ash → fewer clinkers, less tube erosion.
4. **Transport Economy:** Cleaning rejects **10–25%** inert material, saving freight cost.
5. **Consistent Quality:** Blending produces uniform calorific value for stable combustion.
6. **Coke Quality:** For metallurgical use, ash must be **<10%** for blast furnace coke.
7. **Safe Storage:** Prevents spontaneous combustion, which causes major mine/yard fires.

---

## 1.6 Primary Impurities Targeted

| Impurity | Form | Removal Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Mineral matter (ash)** | Clays, shales, sandstones, quartz | Gravity separation, washing |
| **Pyritic sulfur (FeS₂)** | Disseminated in coal matrix | Froth flotation, dense-medium |
| **Organic sulfur** | Bound in coal molecule | Chemical/biological (limited) |
| **Moisture** | Surface & inherent | Dewatering, thermal drying |
| **Shale bands** | Macroscopic layers | Hand sorting, screening |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Classification of Coal Cleaning Methods

### 2.1.1 By Physical State of Medium

| Category | Method | Size Range (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| **Wet Methods** | Jigging, Heavy Media, Froth Flotation, Spirals | 0.5 – 150 |
| **Dry Methods** | Air classification, Magnetic separation, Electrostatic, Pneumatic tables | 0.5 – 75 |

### 2.1.2 By Separation Principle

**A. Gravity-Based Methods** (exploit density difference between coal ≈ 1.2–1.5 g/cm³ and shale ≈ 1.8–2.5 g/cm³)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Density Window of Separation:**
> - Pure coal: **1.2 – 1.4 g/cm³**
> - Middlings: **1.4 – 1.8 g/cm³**
> - Pure mineral matter: **>1.8 g/cm³**
> - The closer the densities, the **harder the separation**.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Major Methods

### 2.2.1 Jig Washing

**Principle:** Pulsating water currents stratify a bed of mixed particles by density.

**Operating Logic:**
1. Feed enters jig chamber on a screen or bed.
2. Water is pulsed upward (suction stroke) → particles stratify.
3. Lighter coal floats to top; heavier shale sinks to bottom.
4. Stratified layers are extracted through gates/ports.

**Why it works (the physics):**
- During upward stroke: bed expands, particles rearrange.
- During downward stroke: bed compacts; denser particles penetrate deeper.
- Repeat cycles → equilibrium stratification based on *terminal settling velocity* differences.

**KTU Board Point:** *Baum jig* is most common for **10–125 mm** coal; uses air-pulsed water.

---

### 2.2.2 Heavy Media (Dense Medium) Washing

**Principle:** Suspend fine magnetite (Fe₃O₄) or ferrosilicon in water to create a pseudo-liquid of controlled density (**1.3–2.0 g/cm³**).

$$\rho_{\text{medium}} = \frac{m_{\text{magnetite}} + m_{\text{water}}}{V_{\text{magnetite}} + V_{\text{water}}}$$

**Operational Steps:**
1. Coal feed is immersed in the dense medium bath.
2. Float product (clean coal) is skimmed off.
3. Sink product (refuse/shale) is removed by scrapers or elevators.
4. Magnetite is recovered from both products via **drum magnetic separators** and recycled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cut-point Density** is the single most important parameter in dense-medium plants. If cut-point = 1.6 g/cm³, then all material lighter than 1.6 floats as clean coal.

**Application Range:** **6–100 mm** particles (best for coarse coal).

---

### 2.2.3 Froth Flotation

**Principle:** Exploits **surface wettability differences** — coal is naturally hydrophobic, ash minerals are hydrophilic.

**Reagents Used:**
- **Collectors** (e.g., diesel oil, kerosene) → enhance coal hydrophobicity.
- **Frothers** (e.g., pine oil, MIBC = methyl isobutyl carbinol) → stabilize air bubbles.
- **Modifiers** (depressants/activators) → suppress pyrite flotation.

**Steps:**
1. Pulped coal slurry (15–25% solids) is fed to flotation cell.
2. Air is sparged → fine bubbles (0.5–2 mm) form.
3. Hydrophobic coal particles attach to bubbles → rise as froth.
4. Mineral matter stays in pulp → discharged as tailings.
5. Froth is scraped, dewatered → clean coal product.

**Application Range:** **<0.5 mm** fine coal — fills the gap that gravity methods cannot reach.

---

### 2.2.4 Other Methods (Brief)

| Method | Principle | Size Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Spirals** | Centrifugal + gravity flow | 0.1–3 mm |
| **Shaking Tables** | Differential stratification on inclined surface | 0.5–6 mm |
| **Magnetic Separation** | Pyrrhotite/Fe minerals attracted to magnet | <0.5 mm |
| **Electrostatic Separation** | Tribocharging — coal & minerals acquire opposite charges | 0.1–1 mm |
| **Oil Agglomeration** | Coal particles adhere to oil droplets | <0.1 mm fines |

---

## 2.3 Storage of Coal — Methods & Engineering

### 2.3.1 Open Stockpiles (Most Common in Indian Power Plants)

- Conical/elongated heaps built by stacker-reclaimers.
- **Compaction** of layers (5–10 m high) reduces air ingress.
- **Dozing & rolling** minimizes voids.
- Covered with **coal dust suppression sprays** or **bitumen-based coating** during monsoon.

### 2.3.2 Silos & Bunkers

- Vertical cylindrical vessels of **concrete or steel**.
- Capacity: **500 – 50,000 tonnes**.
- Equipped with **vibrators, screw feeders, rotary feeders** for discharge.

### 2.3.3 Underground Storage & Inert Gas Blanketing

- Coal stored under **N₂ or CO₂ atmosphere** to displace O₂ and prevent spontaneous combustion.
- Used for **high-rank, low-volatile coals** and coking coal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:**
> The **Liability Index** (LI) determines spontaneous combustion risk:
> $$\text{LI} = \text{Oxygen content (\%)} \times \text{Volatile Matter (\%)} / \text{Ash (\%)} + \text{Moisture (\%)}$$
> LI > 30 → **Highly liable** to spontaneous combustion.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are directly tested in KTU ESE and Module Tests.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{m_{\text{clean}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | Mass recovery of clean coal |
| 2 | $\text{Ash Rejection (\%)} = \dfrac{A_f - A_p \cdot Y/100}{A_f} \times 100$ | Where A_f = feed ash, A_p = product ash, Y = yield |
| 3 | $\text{Cut-point Density } \rho_{50}$ | Density at which 50% probability of separation occurs |
| 4 | $\text{Ecart probable } E_p = \dfrac{\rho_{75} - \rho_{25}}{2}$ | Measure of separation sharpness |
| 5 | $\text{Organic Efficiency } \eta_{org} = \dfrac{Y(100 - A_p)}{100 - A_f} \times 100$ | Best cleaner-teachability metric |
| 6 | $\text{Recovery (\%)} = \dfrac{C_c}{C_f} \times 100$ | Carbon recovery from feed to product |
| 7 | $\text{Sp. Gravity} = \dfrac{W_{\text{air}}}{W_{\text{air}} - W_{\text{water}}}$ | For density measurement of coal |
| 8 | $\text{Stack Loss (\%)} = \dfrac{T_g - T_a}{T_g} \times \dfrac{O_2}{21}$ | Combustion efficiency loss |
| 9 | $\text{Combustible Recovery (\%)} = \dfrac{(100 - A_p) \cdot Y}{(100 - A_f)} \times 100$ | Similar to organic efficiency |
| 10 | $\text{Partition Number } P = \dfrac{\rho - 0.5}{\Delta\rho}$ | Fraction reporting to floats/sinks |

> **Note:** In all formulas, `A` denotes ash content (%), `Y` denotes yield (%), `ρ` denotes density.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** Coal washing reduces auxiliary power consumption for ash handling (which is ~3–5% of plant's own power).
- **Steel Industry:** Coking coal must be <10% ash → heavy-media wash plants are mandatory.
- **Cement Industry:** Use pre-cleaned coal of consistent calorific value.
- **Shipping Economics:** Cleaning reduces freight cost per unit of heat delivered.
- **Environmental Compliance:** SO₂ scrubbing cost drops ~₹0.4 per kg of sulfur avoided.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Organic Efficiency (Cleaner Performance Metric)

The **Organic Efficiency (η_org)** measures how effectively a cleaning process recovers combustible matter while rejecting ash.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the masses involved.

Let:
- $m_f$ = mass of feed (ROM coal)
- $m_c$ = mass of clean coal product (yield $Y$)
- $m_r$ = mass of reject/refuse

By mass balance:
$$m_f = m_c + m_r$$

**Step 2:** Express the ash balance across the separator.

The total ash entering = total ash leaving:
$$m_f \cdot A_f = m_c \cdot A_p + m_r \cdot A_r$$

where $A_f$, $A_p$, $A_r$ are ash percentages of feed, product, and refuse respectively.

**Step 3:** Solve for $m_r$ from mass balance:

$$m_r = m_f - m_c$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into ash balance:

$$m_f \cdot A_f = m_c \cdot A_p + (m_f - m_c) \cdot A_r$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to isolate $m_c$:

$$m_f \cdot A_f = m_c \cdot A_p + m_f \cdot A_r - m_c \cdot A_r$$

$$m_f(A_f - A_r) = m_c(A_p - A_r)$$

$$m_c = m_f \cdot \frac{A_f - A_r}{A_p - A_r}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to yield percentage:

$$Y = \frac{m_c}{m_f} \times 100 = \frac{A_f - A_r}{A_p - A_r} \times 100$$

**Step 7:** Now derive organic efficiency — fraction of combustible material recovered in the clean coal.

The combustible (organic) mass in feed = $m_f(100 - A_f)/100$.

The combustible mass in product = $m_c(100 - A_p)/100$.

**Step 8:** Define organic efficiency:

$$\eta_{org} = \frac{\text{Combustible in product}}{\text{Combustible in feed}} \times 100$$

**Step 9:** Substitute:

$$\eta_{org} = \frac{m_c(100 - A_p)}{m_f(100 - A_f)} \times 100$$

**Step 10:** Replace $m_c/m_f = Y/100$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{org} = \frac{Y(100 - A_p)}{(100 - A_f)}}$$

> This is the **gold-standard** metric used by KTU examiners and the Indian Bureau of Mines.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Washability Curve Construction

A **washability curve** plots cumulative float/sink characteristics vs. specific gravity. It has four components:
- **Sink-float curve** (cumulative yield of floats)
- **Ash curve** (cumulative ash of floats)
- **Density curve** (cumulative ash of sinks)
- **Characteristic ash curve**

### Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1:** Take representative feed sample and conduct float-sink analysis at successively increasing densities:

$$\rho_1 = 1.30, \quad \rho_2 = 1.40, \quad \rho_3 = 1.50, \quad \rho_4 = 1.60, \quad \rho_5 = 1.70, \quad \rho_6 = 1.80, \quad \rho_7 = 2.00 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 2:** At each density, record:
- Mass fraction of floats ($w_i$) and sinks ($1 - w_i$)
- Ash content of floats ($A_i^{float}$) and sinks ($A_i^{sink}$)

**Step 3:** Build cumulative table:

| Density (g/cm³) | Yield of Floats (%) | Cumulative Yield (%) | Floats Ash (%) | Sinks Ash (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| < 1.30 | $w_1$ | $Y_1 = w_1$ | $A_1$ | — |
| 1.30 – 1.40 | $w_2$ | $Y_2 = w_1 + w_2$ | $A_2$ | $A_1'$ |
| 1.40 – 1.50 | $w_3$ | $Y_3$ | $A_3$ | $A_2'$ |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| > 2.00 | $w_7$ | $Y_7 = 100$ | — | $A_6'$ |

**Step 4:** Plot cumulative yield vs. density → **Sink-float curve (S-curve)**.
**Step 5:** Plot cumulative floats ash vs. cumulative yield → **Ash curve**.
**Step 6:** From the S-curve, locate the **cut-point density** $\rho_{50}$ at $Y = 50\%$.

**Step 7:** Compute organic efficiency using formula from §3.1 for any chosen cut-point.

### Numerical Example

**Given:** ROM coal with the following float-sink data:

| Density (g/cm³) | Wt. (%) | Ash (%) |
|---|---|---|
| < 1.30 | 12.5 | 4.0 |
| 1.30–1.40 | 24.0 | 8.0 |
| 1.40–1.50 | 18.0 | 15.0 |
| 1.50–1.60 | 14.0 | 22.0 |
| 1.60–1.80 | 12.0 | 30.0 |
| 1.80–2.00 | 8.0 | 42.0 |
| > 2.00 | 11.5 | 60.0 |

**Feed Ash Calculation:**

$$A_f = \frac{\sum w_i A_i}{\sum w_i} = \frac{(12.5)(4) + (24)(8) + (18)(15) + (14)(22) + (12)(30) + (8)(42) + (11.5)(60)}{100}$$

Compute numerator step by step:
- $(12.5)(4) = 50$
- $(24)(8) = 192$
- $(18)(15) = 270$
- $(14)(22) = 308$
- $(12)(30) = 360$
- $(8)(42) = 336$
- $(11.5)(60) = 690$

$$\text{Sum} = 50 + 192 + 270 + 308 + 360 + 336 + 690 = 2206$$

$$A_f = 2206 / 100 = 22.06\%$$

**Choose cut-point at 1.50 g/cm³** → floats = material below 1.50 = $12.5 + 24 + 18 = 54.5\%$

Product (clean coal) ash:

$$A_p = \frac{50 + 192 + 270}{54.5} = \frac{512}{54.5} = 9.39\%$$

**Yield** $Y = 54.5\%$

**Organic efficiency:**

$$\eta_{org} = \frac{Y(100 - A_p)}{100 - A_f} = \frac{54.5 \times (100 - 9.39)}{100 - 22.06}$$

$$\eta_{org} = \frac{54.5 \times 90.61}{77.94} = \frac{4938.25}{77.94} = 63.36\%$$

> **Interpretation:** Only 63.36% of combustible material is recovered at this cut-point — this is a *poorly washable* coal because of significant middlings.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cut-point Density and Ecart Probable

### Cut-Point (ρ₅₀)

From the **partition curve** (Tromp curve), plot the *partition number* $P$ vs. density:

$$P = \frac{\text{Mass reporting to sink fraction}}{\text{Total mass in that density class}}$$

At $P = 0.5$ → read off density = **cut-point density $\rho_{50}$**.

### Ecart Probable ($E_p$)

A measure of separation sharpness:

$$\boxed{E_p = \frac{\rho_{75} - \rho_{25}}{2}}$$

where $\rho_{75}$ and $\rho_{25}$ are densities at which $P = 0.75$ and $P = 0.25$ respectively.

**Interpretation:**
- $E_p < 0.05$ → **Sharp separation** (excellent)
- $E_p = 0.05$–$0.10$ → **Good**
- $E_p = 0.10$–$0.25$ → **Moderate**
- $E_p > 0.25$ → **Poor separation** (typical of fine coal or near-density material)

### Numerical Example

From a partition curve:

- Density at P = 0.25: $\rho_{25} = 1.46$ g/cm³
- Density at P = 0.75: $\rho_{75} = 1.58$ g/cm³

$$E_p = \frac{1.58 - 1.46}{2} = 0.06 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

→ **Good separation**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Particle Size (mm) | Recommended Method | Expected Ash Reduction | Recovery (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| > 100 | Hand sorting, dense-medium bath | 60–70% | 95 |
| 50–100 | Baum jig, dense-medium drum | 50–65% | 92 |
| 10–50 | Dense-medium cyclone, water-only cyclone | 45–60% | 90 |
| 0.5–10 | Spirals, shaking tables | 35–50% | 85 |
| < 0.5 | Froth flotation, oil agglomeration | 30–45% | 80 |

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A coal washing plant processes **2000 tonnes/day** of ROM coal containing **28% ash** and produces clean coal with **17% ash** at a yield of **65%**. Calculate:
(i) Mass of clean coal per day
(ii) Mass of reject per day
(iii) Ash rejection efficiency
(iv) Organic efficiency

### Solution

**(i) Mass of clean coal:**

$$m_c = 2000 \times 0.65 = 1300 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**(ii) Mass of reject:**

$$m_r = 2000 - 1300 = 700 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**(iii) Ash Rejection Efficiency:**

Ash in feed = $2000 \times 0.28 = 560$ tonnes/day
Ash in clean coal = $1300 \times 0.17 = 221$ tonnes/day
Ash rejected in refuse = $560 - 221 = 339$ tonnes/day

$$\eta_{\text{ash rej}} = \frac{339}{560} \times 100 = 60.54\%$$

**(iv) Organic Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{org} = \frac{Y(100 - A_p)}{100 - A_f} = \frac{65(100 - 17)}{100 - 28} = \frac{65 \times 83}{72} = \frac{5395}{72} = 74.93\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Plant is recovering ~75% of combustible material, rejecting ~60% of ash — a moderately efficient operation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Coal Cleaning & Storage Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROM Coal from Mine] --> B[Primary Crusher]
    B --> C[Sizing Screens]
    C --> D{Coarse Fraction >10 mm}
    C --> E{Fine Fraction <10 mm}
    D --> F[Heavy Media Separator]
    E --> G[Spiral Concentrator]
    E --> H[Froth Flotation Cell]
    F --> I[Clean Coal Coarse]
    F --> J[Rejects Sink]
    G --> K[Clean Coal Medium]
    H --> L[Clean Coal Fines]
    H --> M[Tailings Thickener]
    I --> N[Dewatering Screens]
    K --> N
    L --> O[Filter Press Vacuum]
    N --> P[Clean Coal Storage Silo]
    O --> P
    J --> Q[Reject Disposal]
    M --> R[Water Recovery]
    P --> S{Dispatch to Consumer}
    P --> T[Open Stockpile Backup]
    S --> U[Thermal Power Plant]
    S --> V[Coke Oven]
    S --> W[Cement Industry]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Coal Cleaning Methods Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coal Cleaning Methods] --> B[Physical Methods]
    A --> C[Chemical Methods]
    A --> D[Biological Methods]
    B --> E[Wet Cleaning]
    B --> F[Dry Cleaning]
    E --> G[Jig Washing]
    E --> H[Heavy Media Washing]
    E --> I[Froth Flotation]
    E --> J[Spirals]
    F --> K[Air Classification]
    F --> L[Magnetic Separation]
    F --> M[Electrostatic Separation]
    C --> N[Acid Leaching]
    C --> O[Alkali Treatment]
    D --> P[Bacterial Desulfurization]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Heavy Media Washing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Coal Feed] --> B[Screening at 6 mm]
    B --> C[Dense Medium Bath ρ 1.6 g/cm3]
    C --> D[Float Product Clean Coal]
    C --> E[Sink Product Rejects]
    D --> F[Drain Screen]
    F --> G[Vibrating Dewatering Screen]
    G --> H[Clean Coal Conveyor]
    E --> I[Elevator to Desliming Screen]
    I --> J[Magnetic Separator Magnetite Recovery]
    J --> K[Magnetite Regeneration Tank]
    K --> C
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Coal Storage System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Clean Coal] --> B{Storage Type}
    B --> C[Open Stockpile]
    B --> D[Concrete Silo]
    B --> E[Steel Bunker]
    B --> F[Underground Pit with N2 Blanketing]
    C --> G[Stacker Reclaimer]
    D --> H[Vibratory Dischargers]
    E --> I[Rotary Feeder]
    F --> J[Inert Gas Pressure Regulator]

    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```
