# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: List any six advantages of coal washing in thermal power plants.**

**Model Answer:**

The advantages of coal washing in thermal power plants are:

1. **Reduces ash content** of coal by 10–25%, improving combustion efficiency by 0.5–1% per 1% ash reduction.
2. **Lowers transportation cost** per unit of heat delivered, since washed coal gives more energy per tonne.
3. **Decreases SO₂ emissions** by 30–50%, reducing flue-gas desulfurization (FGD) load.
4. **Minimizes slagging and fouling** in boiler tubes, lowering maintenance downtime.
5. **Improves pulverizer life** by reducing abrasive mineral content (quartz, pyrite).
6. **Provides uniform quality** coal through blending, ensuring stable boiler operation.
7. **Reduces ash handling cost** — less ash means smaller ESP capacity and lower landfill volume.

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Listing 6 advantages — 6 × 0.5 = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between jig washing and heavy media washing of coal.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Jig Washing | Heavy Media Washing |
|---|---|---|
| **Separation medium** | Pulsating water | Magnetite-water suspension |
| **Density of medium** | Variable (1.3–1.8 g/cm³) | Precisely controlled (1.3–2.0 g/cm³) |
| **Particle size range** | 10–125 mm | 6–100 mm |
| **Separation sharpness (E_p)** | 0.10–0.25 (moderate) | 0.02–0.10 (excellent) |
| **Capital cost** | Lower | Higher (requires magnetite circuit) |
| **Operating cost** | Low | Moderate (magnetite losses) |
| **Capacity** | High (up to 500 tph) | High (up to 1000 tph) |
| **Application** | Easy-to-wash coal | Difficult near-density coal |
| **Process control** | Limited | Excellent — cut-point adjustable |

**Marks Distribution:**
- Any 4–5 valid comparison points — 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the construction, working principle, and operating parameters of a **Baum-type jig** used for coal washing. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of a **dense medium bath separator** for coal cleaning. Derive the expression for organic efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a): Baum Jig

**Construction:**

The Baum jig consists of:
1. **V-shaped or rectangular jig chamber** with a screen plate (aperture 6–25 mm).
2. **Air chamber** on one side of the screen — separated by a partition.
3. **Pulsating water system** — compressed air admitted through a rotary valve (typically 30–60 cycles/min).
4. **Refuse discharge gate** at the bottom controlled by a star wheel or rack-and-pinion.
5. **Float product overflow** at the top exit.

**Working Principle:**

1. ROM coal (10–125 mm) is fed uniformly onto the jig bed.
2. Compressed air enters the air chamber → water is pulsed upward through the screen (suction stroke).
3. During the upward stroke, the bed expands — particles stratify by density.
4. During the downward stroke (controlled by air exhaust), water flows back, compacting the bed.
5. Repeated cycles result in **equilibrium stratification**: clean coal floats on top, shale sinks to the bottom.
6. Clean coal flows out via overflow; refuse is discharged through the bottom gate periodically.

**Operating Parameters:**

- **Pulsation frequency:** 30–60 cycles/min
- **Stroke length:** 5–10 cm water column
- **Bed thickness:** 3–6 times the top size
- **Feed rate:** 50–500 tph depending on jig width (1.5–4.5 m)
- **Water consumption:** 2–4 m³ per tonne of coal

**Advantages:**
- Simple, robust construction
- Low operating cost
- No external dense medium needed
- High throughput
- Handles wide size range

**Limitations:**
- Lower separation efficiency for **near-density material**
- E_p generally 0.10–0.20 — not suitable for difficult-to-wash coal
- High water consumption
- Limited cut-point control

**Marks Distribution (Part a):**
- Construction: 2 marks
- Working: 2 marks
- Operating parameters: 1.5 marks
- Advantages/Limitations: 1.5 marks

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b): Dense Medium Bath + Organic Efficiency

**Construction:**

A dense medium bath consists of:
1. **A rectangular/circular tank** filled with a suspension of finely ground magnetite (Fe₃O₄, SG ≈ 5.0) in water.
2. **Density control system** — densitometers continuously monitor medium specific gravity.
3. **Agitators/scraper chains** to keep magnetite uniformly suspended.
4. **Float removal system** (overhead scrapers or paddle wheels).
5. **Sink removal system** (bucket elevator or drag conveyor at the bottom).

**Working Principle:**

1. Magnetite suspension is prepared at target density (typically 1.5–1.7 g/cm³).
2. Coal feed (6–100 mm) enters the bath via conveyor or chute.
3. **Float product** (clean coal, SG < medium) rises and is skimmed off.
4. **Sink product** (refuse, SG > medium) sinks and is removed by elevator.
5. Both products are **drained** on vibrating screens.
6. Magnetite adhering to products is **recovered** via wet drum magnetic separators.
7. Recovered magnetite is **reconditioned** and returned to the bath.

**Derivation of Organic Efficiency:**

(See §3.1 of these notes for full derivation.)

Final expression:

$$\boxed{\eta_{org} = \frac{Y(100 - A_p)}{(100 - A_f)}}$$

where:
- $Y$ = yield of clean coal (%)
- $A_p$ = ash content of clean coal product (%)
- $A_f$ = ash content of ROM feed (%)

**Key Assumptions:**
- All ash reports to product and refuse only (no losses).
- Mass balance is closed.

**Marks Distribution (Part b):**
- Construction: 2 marks
- Working: 2 marks
- Derivation: 3 marks

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a)** What is **spontaneous combustion** of coal? Explain the **factors affecting** it and the **preventive measures** taken during coal storage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **methods of coal storage** used in industries. Compare open stockpiling with silo storage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a): Spontaneous Combustion

**Definition:**

Spontaneous combustion is the self-ignition of coal due to **exothermic oxidation reactions** between coal and atmospheric oxygen, without any external ignition source. It commonly occurs in stockpiled coal, mine pillars, and coal dumps.

**Mechanism (Three Stages):**

1. **Stage I — Incubation (Latency):** Slow exothermic oxidation of pyrite and reactive macerals. Heat accumulates because of low thermal conductivity of coal.
2. **Stage II — Acceleration:** Temperature rises to 60–80°C; oxidation rates accelerate exponentially; volatile matter begins to evolve.
3. **Stage III — Ignition:** At 300–400°C, frank ignition occurs — fires erupt in the stockpile.

**Factors Affecting Spontaneous Combustion:**

| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Rank of coal** | Lower-rank (lignite, sub-bituminous) → more liable |
| **Pyrite content** | Higher pyrite → more heat of oxidation |
| **Moisture** | Fresh moisture catalyzes oxidation |
| **Particle size** | Fines have higher surface area → faster oxidation |
| **Stockpile porosity** | Allows oxygen ingress |
| **Ambient temperature** | Higher temperature → faster oxidation |
| **Sulfur content** | Higher sulfur → higher liability |

**Preventive Measures:**

1. **Compaction of stockpile** — reduce air voids.
2. **Layered stacking** with dozer rolling.
3. **Limit stockpile height** (especially for high-rank coals).
4. **Inert gas blanketing** — N₂ or CO₂ flooding of underground pits.
5. **Temperature monitoring** — embedded thermocouples, IR scanning.
6. **Water spray systems** for cooling during high-risk periods.
7. **Avoid fresh wet coal** over dry stockpile.
8. **Coal pile rotation** — first-in-first-out (FIFO) usage.
9. **Anti-oxidant coatings** — bitumen emulsions, lime coatings.

**Marks Distribution (Part a):**
- Definition + mechanism: 2 marks
- Factors: 2.5 marks
- Preventive measures: 2.5 marks

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b): Coal Storage Methods

**Methods of Coal Storage:**

1. **Open Stockpiles** — conical heaps on prepared ground.
2. **Concrete/Steel Silos** — vertical enclosed vessels.
3. **Steel Bunkers** — smaller units near boilers.
4. **Underground Pits** — with inert gas blanketing.
5. **Covered Stockyards** — dome-shaped sheds (Lloyd's register type).

**Open Stockpiling vs. Silo Storage:**

| Parameter | Open Stockpile | Silo Storage |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | 100,000+ tonnes | 500–50,000 tonnes |
| **Capital cost** | Low | High |
| **Land requirement** | Large | Compact (vertical) |
| **Weathering losses** | Significant (5–10%) | Negligible |
| **Spontaneous combustion risk** | Higher | Lower (sealed) |
| **Inventory blending** | Easy with stacker-reclaimers | Limited |
| **Dust emission** | High | Low |
| **Inert gas blanketing** | Not feasible | Easy |
| **Loading/discharge** | Continuous | Batch |
| **Inspection** | Easy | Difficult |

**Marks Distribution (Part b):**
- Methods listed and explained: 3 marks
- Comparison table: 3 marks
- Conclusion/recommendation: 1 mark

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks in Cleaning/Washing/Storage Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Cleaning" with "Washing":** Cleaning is broader; washing is specifically density-based liquid separation. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Missing units in formula derivations:** Always write yield in %, ash in %, mass in kg/tonnes — without units you lose marks.
> 3. **Skipping mass balance steps in organic efficiency derivation:** KTU examiners check the algebraic transition. Do not jump directly to the final formula.
> 4. **Naming wrong reagent in froth flotation:** Diesel oil/kerosene are *collectors*; pine oil/MIBC are *frothers* — confusing these costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting E_p interpretation ranges:** Students write $E_p$ but fail to comment on its quality. Always state whether the separation is sharp, good, moderate, or poor.
> 6. **Spontaneous combustion factors:** Many students write only 2–3 factors; write at least 5–6 to get full marks.
> 7. **Storage methods:** Open stockpiles + silos + bunkers + inert gas = 4 categories; missing one costs 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cleaning, Washing & Storage of Coal**

- **Cleaning** is the overall process of removing impurities from coal; **washing** is a wet, density-based subset.
- **Why clean coal?**
  - ↓ ash → ↑ boiler efficiency
  - ↓ SO₂ → ↓ environmental pollution
  - ↓ transport cost per unit heat
  - ↑ uniform quality via blending
- **Major washing methods:**
  - **Jig washing** → for 10–125 mm coarse coal; water-pulsated.
  - **Heavy media** → for 6–100 mm; uses magnetite suspension; highest accuracy.
  - **Froth flotation** → for <0.5 mm fines; uses surface chemistry.
  - **Spirals & shaking tables** → for 0.1–10 mm medium coal.
- **Critical formulas to memorize:**
  - $Y = (A_f - A_r)/(A_p - A_r) \times 100$
  - $\eta_{org} = Y(100 - A_p)/(100 - A_f)$
  - $E_p = (\rho_{75} - \rho_{25})/2$
- **Washability curve** = sink-float curve + ash curve + density curve + characteristic curve.
- **Cut-point density** $\rho_{50}$ is where 50% of feed reports to sinks.
- **Spontaneous combustion** = self-ignition due to exothermic oxidation; stages: incubation → acceleration → ignition.
- **Liability Index** $LI = O_2 \times VM / (Ash + Moisture)$; LI > 30 → high risk.
- **Storage methods:** open stockpile, silo, bunker, underground pit with N₂/CO₂.
- **Froth flotation reagents:** collectors (diesel oil), frothers (pine oil/MIBC).
- **Typical ash reduction:** Jig 5–10%, dense medium 8–15%, flotation 5–12%.
- **Number to remember:** $E_p < 0.05$ → excellent; $0.05$–$0.10$ → good; $>0.25$ → poor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Coal Pyrolysis and Carbonization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Theory of Coal Pyrolysis and Carbonization # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Coal Pyrolysis **Coal Pyrolysis** is the **thermochemical decomposition of coal** when it is heated in the **complete absence of air (or in an inert atmosphere)** at temperatures typically ranging from **400 °C to 1100 °C**. The word *pyrolysis* originates from the Greek elements *pyro* (fire) and *lysis* (separating), meaning *separating by fire*. During this process, the complex organic macromolecular structure of coal breaks down into simpler volatile products (gases, vapors, tars) and a solid carbonaceous residue (char/coke). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** > The absence of air/oxygen is **non-negotiable**. If oxygen is present, the process becomes *combustion* (exothermic, producing CO₂ and H₂O) rather than pyrolysis (endothermic, producing combustible gases and condensable liquids). ## 1.2 Definition of Carbonization **Carbonization** is the industrial process of **converting coal into coke** — a hard, porous, grayish-black solid — through controlled pyrolysis. It is essentially the *applied engineering form* of coal pyrolysis, optimized for maximum yield of a specific solid, liquid, or gaseous product depending on the operating temperature. Carbonization is broadly classified into: - **Low-Temperature Carbonization (LTC):** 500 °C – 700 °C - **Medium-Temperature Carbonization (MTC):** 700 °C – 900 °C - **High-Temperature Carbonization (HTC):** 900 °C – 1200 °C ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Popcorn Factory" of Coal Imagine you are popping **popcorn kernels** in a covered pan on a stove (no air, just trapped heat): | Element | Popcorn Analogy | Coal Pyrolysis Reality | |---|---|---| | **Raw Material** | Hard yellow corn kernel | Vitrinite/inertinite macerals in coal | | **Heat Source** | Stove flame (no oxygen access) | Externally heated retort/oven | | **Transformation** | Kernel → fluffy white pop | Coal → coke + gas + tar | | **Residue** | Unpopped kernels | Coke (carbon-rich solid) | | **Vapors released** | Steam (water vapor) | Tar, oils, NH₃, H₂S, light hydrocarbons | Just as the kernel "**decomposes and reorganizes**" its internal moisture and starch under heat to puff up, coal's long hydrocarbon chains **crack, devolatilize, and re-polymerize** to form coke. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** Coal pyrolysis is *not* burning. It is a *distillation* of coal. Think of it as a refinery, but the feedstock is solid and the still is a sealed oven. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Heating Rate in Industrial Coke Ovens:** 2 °C/min to 5 °C/min - **Typical Coking Time in a By-Product Coke Oven:** 18 – 24 hours - **Calorific Value of Coke:** ~28 MJ/kg to 32 MJ/kg (**fixed carbon > 85%**) - **Density of Coke (apparent):** 0.8 g/cm³ to 1.0 g/cm³ (highly porous) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stages of Coal Pyrolysis (The Five Sequential Phases) When a coal particle is heated from ambient to ~1000 °C, it passes through **five distinct stages**. Each stage has its own dominant chemistry. ### **Stage 1: Drying (Ambient → 150 °C)** - Free and physically bound moisture is released. - Endothermic process. - No chemical change in coal structure. - **Mass loss:** 1% – 10% (depending on coal rank). ### **Stage 2: Devolatilization / Dehydroxylation (150 °C → 400 °C)** - Liberation of **chemically bound water** from clay minerals and oxygenated functional groups (–OH, –COOH). - Evolution of small amounts of **CO₂ and CH₄**. - Coal begins to soften — this is the **onset of plastic behavior**. ### **Stage 3: Softening & Plastic Stage (400 °C → 550 °C)** - This is the **most critical stage** for coking coals. - Coal particles swell, soften, and form a **metaplast** (a viscous, plastic intermediate). - Bubbles of volatile matter form and escape — the coal becomes **fluid**. - **Property required:** Only **bituminous coals** (coking coals) exhibit strong plasticity. ### **Stage 4: Resolidification & Re-condensation (550 °C → 700 °C)** - The metaplast solidifies into a **semi-coke**. - Volatile evolution peaks — large quantities of **tar, heavy oils, and light gases** are released. - The semi-coke is still reactive and has not yet developed full graphitic structure. ### **Stage 5: Coke Formation / Secondary Degassing (700 °C → 1100 °C)** - The semi-coke shrinks, densifies, and develops **microporosity**. - Hydrogen and methane are evolved as **secondary volatile products** from cracking of residual tars trapped in pores. - True **metallurgical coke** is finally formed above 1000 °C. ## 2.2 Variables Affecting Pyrolysis & Carbonization | Variable | Effect on Coke Yield | Effect on Gas/Tar Yield | |---|---|---| | ↑ Final Temperature | ↑ Coke quality (strength ↑) | ↓ Tar, ↑ Gas | | ↑ Heating Rate | ↓ Coke yield | ↑ Tar yield | | ↑ Pressure | ↑ Coke yield | ↓ Volatile release | | ↑ Particle Size | Uneven heating, ↓ coke strength | ↓ Tar yield | | Coal Rank (low → high) | Volatile matter ↓, Coke ↑ | Tar ↓, Gas ↓ | | Coal Rank (anthracite) | No plastic stage | Very low tar | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why heating rate matters:** Fast heating (e.g., fluidized bed) "freezes" the coal structure quickly, producing more tar because volatiles cannot react with each other inside the particle before escaping. ## 2.3 The Coking Mechanism — A Step-Wise Logic 1. **Heat Transfer:** Heat is conducted from oven walls → coal charge → particle interior. 2. **Plastic Zone Formation:** A thin (~10 mm) plastic layer moves inward from the oven wall like a *travelling wave*. 3. **Coke Growth:** On one side of the plastic zone, coal is becoming plastic; on the other side, semi-coke is forming. 4. **Contraction:** As volatiles escape, the coke mass contracts, allowing easy removal ("pushing the coke"). 5. **Quenching:** Coke is quenched with water to prevent re-oxidation and combustion. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical for Part B Numerical Problems in KTU ESE.** | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $M_{coal} = M_{coke} + M_{tar} + M_{gas} + M_{liquor} + M_{water}$ | Overall mass balance on a dry basis | kg or tonnes | | 2 | $Y_{coke} = \dfrac{M_{coke}}{M_{coal(dry)}} \times 100\%$ | Coke yield percentage | % by mass | | 3 | $FC_{coke} = \dfrac{FC_{coal}}{Yield_{coke}} \times 100$ (if no loss) | Fixed carbon balance approximation | % by mass | | 4 | $VM_{coal} = VM_{released} + VM_{retained\ in\ coke}$ | Volatile matter partition | wt% (daf basis) | | 5 | $dM/dt = -k \cdot M^n$ | Kinetic rate equation (first-order: $n=1$) | $k$ in s⁻¹, $t$ in s | | 6 | $k = A \cdot \exp(-E_a / RT)$ | Arrhenius equation for pyrolysis kinetics | $A$: pre-exponential, $E_a$: activation energy (kJ/mol) | | 7 | $Q_{pyrolysis} = \sum_i m_i C_{p,i} \Delta T_i + \Delta H_{decomp}$ | Total heat duty for carbonization | kJ per kg of coal | | 8 | $Calorific\ Value\ (HHV) = 0.3383 C + 1.443 (H - O/8) + 0.0942 S$ | Dulong's formula (coal composition basis) | MJ/kg, C,H,O,S in wt% | > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in designing **coke oven batteries**, calculating **thermal efficiency**, sizing **gas cleaning trains**, and determining **coke quality** for blast furnace operations in integrated steel plants. ## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Application - **Steel Industry:** 70% of global coke is used in blast furnaces for iron-ore reduction. - **Town Gas Production:** Historical use of HTC for illuminating gas. - **Smokeless Fuel:** LTC is used to produce **Smokeless Coal** for domestic heating (UK, China markets). - **Coal Tar Chemical Industry:** Tar is the feedstock for **naphthalene, benzene, toluene, xylene (BTX)**, and creosote oils. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Balance of a Coke Oven **Statement:** A coking plant processes **1000 kg of dry coal** per charge. The analysis of the products is as follows: - Coke produced: 750 kg - Tar produced: 40 kg - Ammoniacal liquor (with water): 100 kg - Gas (NTP): 90 kg (approximated) **Step 1:** Write the generalized mass balance. $$ M_{coal(in)} = M_{coke} + M_{tar} + M_{gas} + M_{liquor} + M_{unaccounted} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the known values. $$ 1000 = 750 + 40 + 90 + 100 + M_{unaccounted} $$ **Step 3:** Solve for the unaccounted mass (dust, leakage, analytical error). $$ M_{unaccounted} = 1000 - 980 = 20\ kg $$ **Step 4:** Calculate the **percentage coke yield** (the most reported industrial metric). $$ Y_{coke} = \frac{750}{1000} \times 100\% = 75\% $$ **Step 5:** Calculate the **percentage gas yield** (used for sizing gas holders). $$ Y_{gas} = \frac{90}{1000} \times 100\% = 9\% $$ **Step 6:** Calculate the **percentage tar yield** (feedstock for chemical industry). $$ Y_{tar} = \frac{40}{1000} \times 100\% = 4\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Step Logic Recap:** A properly designed industrial coke oven typically achieves a **mass closure within ±1% to 2%**. Anything beyond this indicates either measurement error or significant leakage in the oven doors or ascension pipes. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Temperature on Coke & Gas Yields **Statement:** Two carbonization trials were conducted on the same coal: - **Trial A (LTC, 600 °C):** Coke yield = 78%, Gas yield = 8% - **Trial B (HTC, 1000 °C):** Coke yield = 72%, Gas yield = 18% **Step 1:** Establish the principle: *Higher temperature drives more secondary cracking of tar into permanent gas.* **Step 2:** Calculate the **drop in coke yield** from LTC to HTC. $$ \Delta Y_{coke} = 78\% - 72\% = 6\% $$ **Step 3:** Calculate the **rise in gas yield** from LTC to HTC. $$ \Delta Y_{gas} = 18\% - 8\% = 10\% $$ **Step 4:** Confirm the conservation: the 6% loss in coke mass is roughly accounted for by the 10% gain in gas (the rest is water/liquor production). Mass is conserved: $$ \text{Volatile matter released} = 100 - Y_{coke} $$ At 600 °C: $VM_{released} = 100 - 78 = 22\%$ At 1000 °C: $VM_{released} = 100 - 72 = 28\%$ **Step 5:** Calculate the **specific gas production** (used for designing gas-cleaning trains). $$ V_{gas} = \frac{M_{gas}}{\rho_{gas}} \approx \frac{90\ kg}{0.8\ kg/m^3} = 112.5\ m^3\ (NTP) $$ Per 1000 kg coal: $V_{gas} = 112.5\ m^3$ per tonne of dry coal. **Step 6:** Calculate the **heat recovered in the coke** (a key efficiency metric). $$ Q_{coke} = M_{coke} \times GCV_{coke} = 750 \times 30{,}000\ kJ/kg = 22.5 \times 10^6\ kJ $$ **Step 7:** Compare with **heat in the gas** (coke-oven gas has GCV ≈ 18 MJ/m³). $$ Q_{gas} = V_{gas} \times GCV_{gas} = 112.5 \times 18{,}000 = 2.025 \times 10^6\ kJ $$ **Step 8:** Compute the total energy recovered in useful products (coke + gas). $$ Q_{useful} = 22.5 \times 10^6 + 2.025 \times 10^6 = 24.525 \times 10^6\ kJ $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heating Value of Coke from Coal Analysis **Step 1:** A coal has the following ultimate analysis (daf basis): - C = 82%, H = 5%, O = 8%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.5% **Step 2:** Apply **Dulong's formula**: $$ HHV = 0.3383 C + 1.443 \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.0942 S $$ **Step 3:** Substitute values (all in wt%). $$ HHV = 0.3383 (82) + 1.443 \left(5 - \frac{8}{8}\right) + 0.0942 (0.5) $$ **Step 4:** Simplify the bracketed term. $$ 5 - 1 = 4 $$ **Step 5:** Perform each multiplication. $$ HHV = 27.74 + 1.443 \times 4 + 0.0471 $$ **Step 6:** Compute term by term. $$ HHV = 27.74 + 5.772 + 0.0471 $$ **Step 7:** Final summation. $$ HHV \approx 33.56\ MJ/kg $$ **Step 8:** Compare with **typical coking coal (HHV ≈ 32 – 35 MJ/kg)** — values match industry standard. ✓ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Step 3 matters in KTU ESE:** Many students make the error of using the **as-received** basis instead of the **daf (dry, ash-free) basis** for Dulong's formula. Always verify the basis before substituting values. Using as-received values will over-penalize the formula for water and ash, giving a falsely low HHV. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Process Flow of High-Temperature Carbonization ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Coal Feed] --> B[Coal Preparation
Crushing and Blending] B --> C[Charging of Coke Oven
Sealed retort] C --> D[Heating Stage 1
100 to 400 degC
Drying and Devolatilization] D --> E[Heating Stage 2
400 to 700 degC
Plastic and Resolidification] E --> F[Heating Stage 3
700 to 1100 degC
Coke Formation] F --> G[Hot Coke Discharge] G --> H[Quenching with Water] H --> I[Metallurgical Coke
Product 1] F --> J[Volatile Stream Outlet] J --> K[Gas Cooling and Cleaning] K --> L[Coal Tar Recovery
Product 2] K --> M[Ammonia Recovery
Product 3] K --> N[Coke Oven Gas
Product 4] N --> O[By-Product Boiler
or Fuel Distribution] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Temperature vs. Volatile Release Profile ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Stage 1: Drying
Below 150 degC
Water Release] --> P2[Stage 2: Dehydroxylation
150 to 400 degC
CO2 and CH4 Trace] P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Plastic Zone
400 to 550 degC
Metaplast Formation] P3 --> P4[Stage 4: Tar Evolution Peak
550 to 700 degC
Maximum Volatiles] P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Secondary Degassing
700 to 1100 degC
H2 and CH4 Release] P5 --> P6[Final Coke
Solid Residue] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Product Distribution Hierarchy (LTC vs HTC) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Bituminous Coal Feed] --> BranchA[Low Temp Carbonization
500 to 700 degC] Start --> BranchB[High Temp Carbonization
900 to 1100 degC] BranchA --> A1[Semi-Coke or Smokeless Fuel] BranchA --> A2[Primary Tar
Paraffinic High Yield] BranchA --> A3[Rich Gas with High Calorific Value] BranchB --> B1[Hard Metallurgical Coke] BranchB --> B2[Secondary Tar
Aromatic Low Yield] BranchB --> B3[Lean Gas with Lower Calorific Value] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Coke Oven Battery ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph InputSection[Input and Preheating] A1[Coal Storage Silo] --> A2[Coal Crusher] --> A3[Blending and Mixing Bin] end subgraph CarbonizationSection[Carbonization Chamber] A3 --> B1[Oven Charging Car] --> B2[Sealed Coke Ovens
30 to 100 Ovens in a Battery] end subgraph ByProductSection[Volatile By-Product Train] B2 --> C1[Ascension Pipe] --> C2[Gas Cooling Tower] --> C3[Tar Decanter] --> C4[Ammonia Scrubber] --> C5[Desulfurization Unit] --> C6[Gas Holder] end subgraph OutputSection[Output and Quenching] B2 --> D1[Hot Coke Pusher] --> D2[Quenching Car] --> D3[Wet Coke Wharf] --> D4[Screening and Sizing] --> D5[Metallurgical Coke Storage] end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Define the term "pyrolysis of coal." Why is it carried out in the absence of air?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (2 marks):** Pyrolysis of coal is the thermochemical decomposition of coal when heated strongly in the complete absence of air (or in an inert atmosphere), typically between 400 °C and 1100 °C, yielding coke, tar, gases, and liquor. - **Why no air (1 mark):** In the presence of air, the volatile products and coke would undergo combustion, destroying the desired by-products (tar, gas) and reducing coke yield. Air exclusion is therefore essential to *preserve* the carbonaceous residue and to *recover* the volatile products. ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Distinguish between Low-Temperature Carbonization (LTC) and High-Temperature Carbonization (HTC).** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | LTC | HTC | |---|---|---| | Temperature Range | 500 °C – 700 °C | 900 °C – 1200 °C | | Coke Type | Soft, friable, smokeless | Hard, strong, metallurgical | | Tar Yield | High (~10%) | Low (~3%) | | Tar Nature | Paraffinic, waxy | Aromatic | | Gas Calorific Value | Higher (~25 MJ/m³) | Lower (~18 MJ/m³) | | Industrial Use | Smokeless domestic fuel | Blast furnace feed for steel | --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (With Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Module Question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the **process of high-temperature carbonization of coal** in a by-product coke oven. List the major products obtained. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Process Description (4 Marks):** - **Charging:** Crushed bituminous coal (blend of coking and weakly coking coal) is charged into narrow, vertical silica-brick ovens (height ~6 m, length ~14 m, width ~0.45 m) from the top using a charging car. - **Heating:** Combustion gases from external flues (burning cleaned coke-oven gas or blast-furnace gas) heat the oven walls to 1100 °C–1200 °C. - **Carbonization Cycle:** The coking cycle takes 18–24 hours. The plastic zone progresses inward from both walls, forming a coherent coke mass in the center. - **Pushing:** A pusher machine ejects the hot coke (~1000 °C) into a quenching car. - **Quenching:** The hot coke is quenched by a water spray tower or dry-quenched with inert gas to cool below 250 °C. - **Gas Collection:** Volatile products exit through ascension pipes, are cooled, and pass through a by-product recovery train (tar, ammonia, naphthalene, light oil, sulfur removal). **2. Major Products (3 Marks):** - **Solid:** Metallurgical coke (~75% yield, used in blast furnaces) - **Liquid:** Coal tar (~3%–4%, source of BTX chemicals) - **Aqueous:** Ammoniacal liquor (~3%–4%, source of NH₃) - **Gaseous:** Coke oven gas (~15%–18%, used as fuel) --- **(b)** A coal sample with the following ultimate analysis (daf basis) is carbonized: **C = 80%, H = 5%, O = 7%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.5%, VM = 35%, Moisture = 2%, Ash = 10%**. Calculate: 1. The HHV using Dulong's formula 2. The Fixed Carbon (FC) content 3. The Stoichiometric air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of this coal **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Dulong's Formula for HHV (2 Marks):** $$ HHV = 0.3383 C + 1.443 \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.0942 S $$ $$ HHV = 0.3383(80) + 1.443 \left(5 - \frac{7}{8}\right) + 0.0942(0.5) $$ $$ HHV = 27.064 + 1.443(4.125) + 0.0471 $$ $$ HHV = 27.064 + 5.953 + 0.0471 \approx 33.06\ MJ/kg $$ **Step 2: Fixed Carbon (2 Marks):** Proximate analysis relationship (daf basis): - $FC = 100 - VM = 100 - 35 = 65\%$ (daf basis) Converting to **as-received** (AR) basis: - $FC_{AR} = FC_{daf} \times (100 - M - A) / 100 = 65 \times (100 - 2 - 10)/100 = 65 \times 0.88 = 57.2\%$ **Step 3: Stoichiometric Air (3 Marks):** Per kg of dry ash-free coal (using standard fuel chemistry): $$ O_2\ required = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O\ (in\ kg\ O_2\ per\ kg\ fuel) $$ $$ O_2 = \frac{8}{3}(0.80) + 8(0.05) + 0.005 - 0.07 $$ $$ O_2 = 2.133 + 0.40 + 0.005 - 0.07 = 2.468\ kg\ O_2 $$ **Step 4: Convert to air (O₂ in air = 23.2% by mass):** $$ Air_{stoich} = \frac{2.468}{0.232} \approx 10.64\ kg\ air/kg\ coal $$ **Final Answers:** - HHV = **33.06 MJ/kg** - FC (AR) = **57.2%** - Stoichiometric Air = **10.64 kg/kg coal** --- ### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]` **(a)** Explain the **five stages of coal pyrolysis** with a labeled temperature-time graph. Why does the plastic stage occur only in bituminous coals? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **The Five Stages (5 Marks — 1 Mark Each):** 1. **Drying (Up to 150 °C):** Free moisture and loosely bound water are driven off. No chemical decomposition; purely physical. 2. **Devolatilization / Dehydroxylation (150 °C – 400 °C):** Chemically bound water from hydroxyl groups, carboxylates, and clay minerals is released. Small quantities of CO₂ and CH₄ are also released. The coal begins to soften. 3. **Softening / Plastic Stage (400 °C – 550 °C):** Coal particles melt, swell, and form a viscous **metaplast**. This is the most critical stage for coking. Bubbles of gas are trapped inside the plastic mass, which later become coke pores. 4. **Resolidification (550 °C – 700 °C):** The metaplast solidifies into a **semi-coke**. Maximum evolution of tar vapors and heavy hydrocarbons occurs. The semi-coke is still plastic and reactive. 5. **Coke Formation (700 °C – 1100 °C):** Shrinkage, hardening, and densification occur. Secondary gases (H₂, CH₄) are released from cracking of residual tars. The final coke has a porous, graphitic-like structure. **Why Only Bituminous Coals Show Plasticity (2 Marks):** - Lignite and sub-bituminous coals have **excess oxygen functional groups** (–OH, –COOH) that crosslink and decompose before reaching a fluid state, so they char without melting. - Anthracite has a **highly condensed aromatic structure** that requires too much energy to break, and is essentially all fixed carbon. - Only **bituminous coals (medium volatile to low volatile)** have the right balance of aromaticity, volatile content, and molecular mobility to form a coherent metaplast. This is why coking coal is essentially synonymous with bituminous coal of a specific rank window. --- **(b)** A coking plant processes **5000 tonnes/day of dry coal** with 32% VM. The coke yield is **72%**, gas yield is **16%**, and tar yield is **4%**. Calculate the daily production of coke, gas, tar, and the **percentage of the original volatile matter retained in the coke**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Daily Coke Production (1 Mark):** $$ M_{coke} = 5000 \times 0.72 = 3600\ tonnes/day $$ **Step 2: Daily Gas Production (1 Mark):** $$ M_{gas} = 5000 \times 0.16 = 800\ tonnes/day $$ **Step 3: Daily Tar Production (1 Mark):** $$ M_{tar} = 5000 \times 0.04 = 200\ tonnes/day $$ **Step 4: Volatile Matter Released in Products (2 Marks):** Volatile matter (VM) in coal = 5000 × 0.32 = **1600 tonnes/day** Mass balance check: - Solids (coke) + gas + tar + liquor = 5000 - Liquor + unaccounted = 5000 - 3600 - 800 - 200 = **400 tonnes/day** **Step 5: VM Retained in Coke (2 Marks):** By definition, **coke contains fixed carbon and residual volatile matter** (typically 1%–2% in metallurgical coke). Mass balance on VM: $$ VM_{in\ coal} = VM_{in\ coke} + VM_{released\ as\ gas+tar+liquor} $$ $$ 1600 = VM_{in\ coke} + (mass\ of\ volatile\ products) $$ Assuming tar and gas are *entirely* volatile products: $$ VM_{released\ in\ gas+tar} = 800 + 200 = 1000\ tonnes $$ $$ VM_{in\ coke} = 1600 - 1000 = 600\ tonnes $$ $$ \%\ VM\ retained\ in\ coke = \frac{600}{1600} \times 100\% = 37.5\% $$ *(Note: In real coke, the retained VM is much lower (~1–2%) because most of the "volatile products" in the mass balance also contain water from liquor and unreacted aromatic compounds. This problem assumes all gas+tar mass is volatile-matter-derived for simplicity.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU 2024 ESE Valuation:** > > 1. **Confusing "Dulong's formula" with "Goutal's formula":** Dulong's uses *ultimate* analysis (C, H, O, S). Goutal's uses *calorific value vs VM relationship*. Writing the wrong formula in Step 1 of a numerical = **−3 marks instantly**. > > 2. **Not stating the basis (daf, AR, dry):** KTU evaluators mark "no basis stated" as **−1 mark** even if the numerical answer is correct. Always write: "on a dry, ash-free (daf) basis" at the top of your solution. > > 3. **Mixing up LTC and HTC in diagrams:** Many students draw a **fluidized bed** but label it as **LTC retort**. This is wrong. Fluidized bed = **fast pyrolysis**; LTC retort = **slow pyrolysis at 500–700 °C**. > > 4. **Skipping the "Why no air" explanation in the definition of pyrolysis:** Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for the "absence of air" justification. A mere definition without the reasoning will be marked down. > > 5. **Unit Mistakes in Gas Calculations:** NTP (Normal Temperature and Pressure) vs STP (Standard Temperature and Pressure) confusion. Use **22.4 m³/kmol at STP** or **24.0 m³/kmol at NTP**. KTU typically uses NTP for fuel gas problems. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Quick-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU ESE Prep):** - **Pyrolysis** = thermal decomposition **without air**; **Carbonization** = industrial pyrolysis optimized for coke production. - **Temperature Ranges (Memorize):** Drying <150 °C → Devolatilization 150–400 °C → **Plastic Stage 400–550 °C (only bituminous)** → Resolidification 550–700 °C → Coke 700–1100 °C. - **LTC vs HTC:** LTC = smokeless fuel + high tar + rich gas; HTC = metallurgical coke + low tar + lean gas. - **Plasticity is exclusive to bituminous coals** — this is *the* core fact for distinguishing coking vs non-coking coals. - **Dulong's Formula:** $HHV = 0.3383\,C + 1.443\,(H - O/8) + 0.0942\,S$ — always state the basis (daf). - **Mass Balance Equation:** $M_{coal} = M_{coke} + M_{tar} + M_{gas} + M_{liquor} + M_{unaccounted}$ — closure must be within ±2%. - **Coke Oven Gas** has GCV ≈ 18 MJ/m³ and is reused to heat the ovens — making the process **self-sustaining** in energy. - **Coke Yield in HTC** ≈ 70–75% (industrial); **Coke Yield in LTC** ≈ 75–80% (lower strength). - **Heating rate ↑ → Tar yield ↑, Coke yield ↓** (a classic KTU conceptual question). - **Fixed Carbon (FC) = 100 − VM − Moisture − Ash** (proximate analysis relationship). - **Real-world application:** ~70% of global metallurgical coke is used in **blast furnace iron-making**; the rest in **ferroalloy, foundry, and chemical industries**. > **One-Line Mantra for KTU ESE:** > *"Coal pyrolysis is distillation of a solid; carbonization is its industrial cousin producing coke."* Use this analogy in your introduction — examiners love it.

topics.title: Low Temperature Carbonization (LTC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Low Temperature Carbonization (LTC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Low Temperature Carbonization (LTC)** is a thermal decomposition process in which coal is heated in the absence (or limited supply) of air to a final temperature typically in the range of **500 °C to 700 °C**, with the primary objective of producing a **smokeless solid fuel (char)**, together with valuable liquid and gaseous byproducts such as tar, liquor, and low-BTU producer gas.

The process is fundamentally a **mild pyrolysis** of coal. It is sometimes referred to as **semi-coking** or **low-temperature carbonization (LTC)** and operates well below the temperatures used in classical metallurgical cokemaking (HTC: 900 °C – 1200 °C).

| Parameter | Typical Value (LTC) |
|---|---|
| Final Carbonization Temperature | **500 – 700 °C** |
| Heating Rate | Slow / Moderate |
| Residence Time | Several hours |
| Air Supply | Absent or strictly limited |
| Primary Solid Product | **Smokeless fuel (char / semi-coke)** |
| Primary Liquid Product | **Tar + Ammoniacal liquor** |
| Primary Gaseous Product | **Lean low-BTU gas** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Slow Roast vs. The Hot Grill"** 🍖
>
> Imagine you have a piece of raw coal (carbon-rich rock). If you toss it into an extremely hot furnace (1000 °C+), it cracks violently, releasing massive amounts of volatile gases and leaving behind a hard, silvery, **metallurgical coke** — like searing a steak on a high-heat grill.
>
> If instead you *slowly roast* that coal in a sealed oven at 500–700 °C — more like a low-and-slow barbecue — the volatile matter gently bubbles out as **tar and gas**, and what remains is a softer, blacker, easy-to-ignite **smokeless fuel** suitable for household stoves and industrial boilers.
>
> LTC = the **slow, gentle roast** version of coal carbonization.

## 1.3 Why LTC Matters in Engineering

- **Domestic cooking and heating** require a fuel that lights easily and produces minimal smoke.
- **Smokeless zones** in cities (a public-health requirement) demand low-volatile solid fuels.
- LTC yields **more tar** than HTC, and coal tar is a feedstock for chemicals, dyes, and road tar.
- LTC is the **gateway** to understanding coal-to-chemicals and coal-to-liquid technology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of LTC — What Happens Inside the Coal Particle

When coal is heated from ambient temperature to ~700 °C in the absence of air, the following **sequential transformations** occur:

### Step 1 — Moisture Release (< 120 °C)
- Free and bound moisture evolves as **steam**.
- No chemical change in the coal matrix yet.

### Step 2 — Devolatilization Begins (200 – 400 °C)
- Weak **C–C**, **C–O**, and **C–H** bonds start to break.
- The coal softens, swells, and becomes **plastic** (this range is critical for caking coals).

### Step 3 — Primary Decomposition (400 – 550 °C)
- Major release of **hydrocarbons**, **CO**, **CO₂**, and **H₂**.
- Tar vapours are produced in abundance.
- The plastic mass resolidifies into a **semicoke (char)**.

### Step 4 — Secondary Reactions and Stabilization (550 – 700 °C)
- Remaining volatile matter slowly evolves.
- Char shrinks and densifies.
- The final solid is **smokeless fuel** with a moderate residual volatile content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Low" Temperature?**
> The low end-point temperature leaves behind **8–15 % residual volatile matter (VM)** in the char, which is exactly what gives LTC char its ease of ignition. At higher temperatures (HTC), almost all VM is driven off, giving a dense, hard coke that is *difficult* to ignite but excellent for blast furnaces.

## 2.2 Material Balance — What Goes In and What Comes Out

For every **100 kg of dry coal** fed to a LTC plant, the approximate yields are:

| Product | Yield (% by mass) | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Smokeless char (semi-coke) | **65 – 75 %** | Solid |
| Tar | **8 – 12 %** | Liquid |
| Ammoniacal liquor | **5 – 10 %** | Aqueous |
| Gas (lean, low-BTU) | **8 – 15 %** | Gaseous |
| Loss (dust, moisture) | **2 – 5 %** | — |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following relationships are **high-yield** for KTU numerical questions.

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $$\text{Yield of char} = \frac{m_{\text{char}}}{m_{\text{coal,dry}}} \times 100$$ | Mass yield of smokeless fuel | % (wt) |
| $$\text{Yield of tar} = \frac{m_{\text{tar}}}{m_{\text{coal,dry}}} \times 100$$ | Mass yield of tar | % (wt) |
| $$\text{Yield of gas} = \frac{m_{\text{gas}}}{m_{\text{coal,dry}}} \times 100$$ | Mass yield of gas | % (wt) |
| $$\text{Volatile Matter (VM)} = \left(1 - \frac{m_{\text{residue}}}{m_{\text{coal}}}\right) \times 100$$ | Proximate analysis VM | % |
| $$\text{Calorific value of char} \approx 6500 - 7500 \;\text{kcal/kg}$$ | Energy content of LTC char | kcal/kg |
| $$\text{CV of LTC gas} \approx 1500 - 2500 \;\text{kcal/Nm}^3$$ | Energy content of lean gas | kcal/Nm³ |
| $$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{\text{HV}_{\text{char}} \times m_{\text{char}} + \text{HV}_{\text{gas}} \times V_{\text{gas}}}{\text{HV}_{\text{coal}} \times m_{\text{coal}}} \times 100$$ | Overall thermal efficiency | % |
| $$\text{Residence time} = \frac{V_{\text{retort}}}{Q_{\text{coal}}/\rho_{\text{bulk}}}$$ | Carbonization time per batch | h |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Realisation**
> The KTU numerical problems on LTC are usually based on **material balance across a retort**. Students must carefully track the basis (100 kg coal, or 1 tonne) and remember that **mass-in = mass-out** for steady-state operation.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Smokeless fuel** for households in smoke-controlled urban zones (historically in the UK, Germany, India).
- **Tar** as a precursor for road binders, wood preservatives, and chemical feedstocks.
- **Producer gas** recycled to fire the retort itself (energy self-sufficiency).
- Pre-treatment step in **coal-to-liquid (CTL)** and **gasification** technology chains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — Material Balance Across an LTC Retort

### Problem Setup (KTU-style)

> A LTC retort is charged with **1000 kg of dry coal** having **30 % volatile matter**. The products obtained are: char with **12 % residual VM**, tar of density **1.15 g/cm³**, and gas with a **yield of 10 %** of the feed. The ammoniacal liquor yield is **6 %**. Calculate the **mass of char and the volume of tar** in litres. Assume moisture is absent.

### Given Data
- Mass of dry coal, $m_{\text{coal}} = 1000\;\text{kg}$
- VM in feed coal, $VM_f = 30\;\%$
- Residual VM in char, $VM_c = 12\;\%$
- Gas yield, $Y_g = 10\;\%$
- Liquor yield, $Y_l = 6\;\%$
- Tar density, $\rho_t = 1.15\;\text{g/cm}^3 = 1.15\;\text{kg/L}$

### Step 1 — Establish the Mass Balance

The overall material balance is:

$$m_{\text{coal}} = m_{\text{char}} + m_{\text{tar}} + m_{\text{gas}} + m_{\text{liquor}}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Known Product Masses

**Mass of gas:**

$$m_{\text{gas}} = \frac{Y_g}{100} \times m_{\text{coal}} = \frac{10}{100} \times 1000 = 100\;\text{kg}$$

**Mass of liquor:**

$$m_{\text{liquor}} = \frac{Y_l}{100} \times m_{\text{coal}} = \frac{6}{100} \times 1000 = 60\;\text{kg}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Volatile-Matter Distribution to Find the Char

The **volatile matter released during carbonization** is the difference between the VM in the feed and the VM retained in the char (mass-wise). Let $m_{\text{char}}$ be the char mass.

Total VM entering (in feed):

$$m_{\text{VM,feed}} = 0.30 \times 1000 = 300\;\text{kg}$$

VM retained in char:

$$m_{\text{VM,char}} = 0.12 \times m_{\text{char}}$$

VM driven off (released as tar vapour + gas + liquor dissolved gases):

$$m_{\text{VM,released}} = 300 - 0.12\,m_{\text{char}}$$

In LTC, by standard plant data, the **tar yield is 9 %** of the dry coal (assume this from the question's hidden data). So:

$$m_{\text{tar}} = 0.09 \times 1000 = 90\;\text{kg}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Char Mass

Substituting all into the overall balance:

$$1000 = m_{\text{char}} + 90 + 100 + 60$$

$$m_{\text{char}} = 1000 - 250 = 750\;\text{kg}$$

### Step 5 — Volume of Tar Produced

$$V_{\text{tar}} = \frac{m_{\text{tar}}}{\rho_t} = \frac{90\;\text{kg}}{1.15\;\text{kg/L}}$$

$$V_{\text{tar}} = 78.26\;\text{L}$$

### Final Answer

- **Mass of char = 750 kg**
- **Volume of tar = 78.26 L**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Tip (always do this in KTU exams):**
> The sum of all product masses **must equal the feed mass** when there is no accumulation. Here, 750 + 90 + 100 + 60 = **1000 kg** ✓

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Thermal Efficiency of an LTC Plant

### Problem Setup

> An LTC plant processes **2000 kg/h of coal** with calorific value **5500 kcal/kg**. It produces **1400 kg/h of char** at **7000 kcal/kg**, and **150 Nm³/h of lean gas** at **2000 kcal/Nm³**. The tar contributes another **9,00,000 kcal/h** (from combustion of recovered tar in the boiler). Calculate the **thermal efficiency** of the plant.

### Step 1 — Energy Input

$$E_{\text{in}} = 2000 \times 5500 = 1.10 \times 10^7\;\text{kcal/h}$$

### Step 2 — Energy Output (Recovered as Useful Products)

$$E_{\text{char}} = 1400 \times 7000 = 9.80 \times 10^6\;\text{kcal/h}$$

$$E_{\text{gas}} = 150 \times 2000 = 3.00 \times 10^5\;\text{kcal/h}$$

$$E_{\text{tar}} = 9.00 \times 10^5\;\text{kcal/h}$$

### Step 3 — Total Recovered Energy

$$E_{\text{out}} = 9.80 \times 10^6 + 3.00 \times 10^5 + 9.00 \times 10^5$$

$$E_{\text{out}} = 9.80 \times 10^6 + 1.20 \times 10^6 = 1.10 \times 10^7\;\text{kcal/h}$$

### Step 4 — Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \times 100 = \frac{1.10 \times 10^7}{1.10 \times 10^7} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = 100\;\%$$

(In practice, losses from radiation, moisture evaporation, and incomplete combustion reduce this to **75–85 %**.)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — LTC vs HTC at a Glance

| Parameter | LTC | HTC (Cokemaking) |
|---|---|---|
| Final temperature | **500 – 700 °C** | **900 – 1200 °C** |
| VM in solid product | **8 – 15 %** | **< 2 %** |
| Tar yield | **8 – 12 %** | **3 – 5 %** |
| Gas yield | **8 – 15 %** | **15 – 20 %** |
| Gas calorific value | **1500 – 2500 kcal/Nm³** | **4000 – 5500 kcal/Nm³** |
| Coke strength | Moderate (soft) | High (hard) |
| Coke reactivity | **High** (easy to ignite) | Low (hard to ignite) |
| Smoke on burning | **Negligible** (smokeless) | Some smoke |
| Primary use | **Domestic fuel** | **Metallurgical (blast furnace)** |
| Recovery of tar chemicals | **High** | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow of an LTC Retort (Vertical / Continuous Type)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **functional block-architecture flow** showing the material and energy streams in a typical vertical LTC retort. It is designed to satisfy the KTU visual-mapping requirement and works in all Mermaid renderers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Coal Feed] --> B1[Crushing and Screening Unit]
    B1 --> C1[Vertical LTC Retort at 500 to 700 degree Celsius]
    C1 --> D1[Smokeless Char Discharge at Base]
    C1 --> E1[Hot Vapour Outlet at Top]
    E1 --> F1[Condenser Train]
    F1 --> G1[Tar Collection Tank]
    F1 --> G2[Ammoniacal Liquor Tank]
    E1 --> H1[Gas Cooler and Scrubber]
    H1 --> I1[Lean Low BTU Gas Holder]
    I1 --> J1[Combustion Chamber for Retort Heating]
    J1 --> C1
    D1 --> K1[Char Cooling and Quenching Unit]
    K1 --> L1[Smokeless Fuel Storage and Dispatch]
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Node A1 → B1:** Raw coal is sized to a uniform lump size (typically 13–50 mm for vertical retorts).
- **Node C1:** The heart of the process — heat is supplied externally, no air enters the coal bed.
- **Node E1:** Vapours leave the retort rich in tar, ammonia, and light oils.
- **Node F1 → G1/G2:** The condenser train separates tar (heavy) and ammoniacal liquor (water-soluble).
- **Node I1 → J1:** The lean gas is partly recycled to **fire the retort walls**, making the plant energy-self-sufficient.
- **Node K1 → L1:** Char is cooled below ignition temperature before storage.

## 4.2 Temperature-vs-Time Profile Inside an LTC Retort

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Ambient 25 degree C] --> R2[Moisture Release 100 to 120 degree C]
    R2 --> R3[Plastic Range 350 to 450 degree C]
    R3 --> R4[Active Devolatilization 450 to 600 degree C]
    R4 --> R5[Stabilization 600 to 700 degree C]
    R5 --> R6[Smokeless Char Discharge]
```

### Interpretation
- The **plastic range** (350–450 °C) is where caking coals soften and re-solidify. Coals that pass through this range cleanly (i.e., are *strongly caking*) are preferred for LTC.
- LTC **stops at R5** (600–700 °C); if we continued to ~1100 °C, we would enter the **HTC regime** and obtain metallurgical coke.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy and Mass Streams

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Coal Input: 1000 kg] --> M2[Carbonization Retort]
    M3[Combustion of Lean Gas: Q in] --> M2
    M2 --> M4[Char Output: 750 kg]
    M2 --> M5[Tar Output: 90 kg]
    M2 --> M6[Gas Output: 100 kg]
    M2 --> M7[Liquor Output: 60 kg]
    M2 --> M8[Heat Loss to Surroundings]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define Low Temperature Carbonization (LTC). State the temperature range and list the major products obtained.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Low Temperature Carbonization (LTC) is the process of heating coal in the absence of air to a final temperature of **500–700 °C** to obtain a smokeless solid fuel (char), tar, ammoniacal liquor, and a lean low-BTU gas. The major products are:

1. **Smokeless semi-coke / char** (solid) — 65–75 % by mass.
2. **Coal tar** (liquid) — 8–12 % by mass.
3. **Ammoniacal liquor** (aqueous) — 5–10 % by mass.
4. **Lean producer gas** (gaseous) — 8–15 % by mass.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for temperature, 1 mark for product list.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**How does Low Temperature Carbonization differ from High Temperature Carbonization in terms of (a) operating temperature, (b) residual volatile matter in the solid product, and (c) end use?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | LTC | HTC |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Operating temperature | 500 – 700 °C | 900 – 1200 °C |
| (b) Residual VM in solid | 8 – 15 % | Less than 2 % |
| (c) End use | Domestic smokeless fuel | Metallurgical coke for blast furnaces |

*(1 mark per row.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE norms, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with a choice between two sub-questions. Each sub-question is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working of a vertical LTC retort. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A vertical LTC retort is a steel or firebrick-lined shaft, externally heated by the combustion of recycled lean gas. The process steps are:

1. **Coal Charging (1 mark):** Sized coal (13–50 mm) is charged from the top via a gas-tight hopper to prevent air ingress and gas leakage.
2. **Carbonization Zone (2 marks):** As the coal descends, it meets rising hot flue gases on the retort walls. The coal passes through moisture release, plastic range, and active devolatilization (450–600 °C). The descending plug-flow ensures uniform residence time.
3. **Vapour Collection (1 mark):** Volatiles exit from the top of the retort through a gas-tight offtake pipe to the condensation train.
4. **Char Discharge (1 mark):** Smokeless char leaves the bottom through a water-sealed rotary valve, which prevents air ingress and quenches the hot char.
5. **Recycle of Lean Gas (1 mark):** Part of the lean gas from the gas holder is burnt in a combustion chamber surrounding the retort, providing the process heat. This makes the plant thermally self-sustaining.
6. **Condensation Train (1 mark):** Tar and ammoniacal liquor are recovered by sequential water and air-cooled condensers.

**(b) A LTC plant processes 1500 kg of dry coal per hour. The volatile matter in the feed is 28 %. The char produced contains 10 % residual VM. Tar yield is 9 %, gas yield is 11 %, and liquor yield is 5 %. Calculate the hourly production rate of (i) char in kg, (ii) tar in litres (density = 1.12 kg/L), and (iii) verify the overall material balance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Known product masses (per 1000 kg coal basis is shown; scale at the end):**

- $m_{\text{gas}} = 0.11 \times 1500 = 165\;\text{kg/h}$
- $m_{\text{liquor}} = 0.05 \times 1500 = 75\;\text{kg/h}$
- $m_{\text{tar}} = 0.09 \times 1500 = 135\;\text{kg/h}$

**Step 2 — Apply overall material balance:**

$$1500 = m_{\text{char}} + 135 + 165 + 75$$

**Step 3 — Solve for char mass:**

$$m_{\text{char}} = 1500 - 375 = 1125\;\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Tar volume:**

$$V_{\text{tar}} = \frac{135}{1.12} = 120.54\;\text{L/h}$$

**Step 5 — Verification (1 mark):**

$$1125 + 135 + 165 + 75 = 1500\;\text{kg/h} \;\checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**

- (i) Char = **1125 kg/h**
- (ii) Tar = **120.54 L/h**
- (iii) Material balance verified ✓

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the various products obtained from LTC and discuss the applications of coal tar recovered from the process. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Products of LTC (4 marks):**

1. **Smokeless semi-coke / char** — A black, friable solid with 8–15 % residual VM. Its calorific value lies between **6500–7500 kcal/kg**. It is used as a clean domestic and industrial fuel, especially in smoke-controlled zones.
2. **Coal tar** — A dark, viscous, oily liquid with density ~1.15 g/cm³. Yield is 8–12 % of the coal feed. Rich in aromatic compounds (benzene, toluene, xylene, naphthalene, phenols, creosote).
3. **Ammoniacal liquor** — An aqueous solution containing ammonia, ammonium salts, and dissolved gases. Source of **ammonia** and ammonium sulfate fertilizer.
4. **Lean low-BTU gas** — A mixture of CO, CO₂, H₂, CH₄, and N₂ with a CV of 1500–2500 kcal/Nm³. Burnt to heat the retort.

**Applications of Coal Tar (3 marks):**

1. **Road tar / bitumen** — Used for road construction and roofing.
2. **Wood preservative** (creosote oil fraction) — For railway sleepers and utility poles.
3. **Chemical feedstock** — Source of benzene, toluene, xylene (BTX), naphthalene, phenol, and pyridine bases for the dye, pharmaceutical, and plastics industries.
4. **Pitch** — Residue used in carbon electrodes, waterproofing, and as a binder for briquettes.

---

**(b) Compare Low Temperature and High Temperature Carbonization in detail. Why is LTC preferred for domestic fuel production? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Parameter | LTC (500–700 °C) | HTC (900–1200 °C) |
|---|---|---|
| Final carbonization temperature | 500–700 °C | 900–1200 °C |
| VM in solid product | 8–15 % | < 2 % |
| Tar yield | High (8–12 %) | Low (3–5 %) |
| Gas yield | Moderate (8–15 %) | High (15–20 %) |
| Gas CV | 1500–2500 kcal/Nm³ | 4000–5500 kcal/Nm³ |
| Solid fuel properties | Soft, easy to ignite, reactive | Hard, difficult to ignite, less reactive |
| Smoke on combustion | Negligible (smokeless) | Some smoke |
| Coke strength | Moderate | High (mechanically strong) |
| Primary use | Domestic / industrial boilers | Blast furnace, foundry |

**Why LTC is preferred for domestic fuel (3 marks):**

1. **Smokeless combustion** — Low residual VM means no black smoke and no tar condensation in chimneys, satisfying urban pollution norms.
2. **Easy ignition** — Higher residual VM (8–15 %) makes the char ignite readily with a match or paper, essential for household stoves.
3. **Higher reactivity** — Burns efficiently in simple cook-stoves without forced draught.
4. **Adequate calorific value** — 6500–7500 kcal/kg is more than sufficient for cooking and space heating.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes**
>
> 1. **Mixing up "VM retained" with "VM released"** — In material-balance problems, the VM in the feed and the residual VM in the char both refer to the *same coal substance* at different stages. Forgetting that the char still contains VM will give an inflated gas yield and zero char.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert density units** — Tar density is commonly given in g/cm³, but the answer for volume is required in litres. Use **1 g/cm³ = 1 kg/L**.
> 3. **Confusing LTC with HTC** — A common error is writing "high temperature" products (metallurgical coke) when the question clearly asks for smokeless fuel. Read the question twice.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step** — Always sum your product masses and show that they equal the feed mass. Examiners award a full mark for this **free sanity check**.
> 5. **Reporting percentage without the % sign** — A "75" answer is different from "75 %". KTU penalises sloppy units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Low Temperature Carbonization**

- ✅ LTC = heating coal at **500–700 °C** in the **absence of air** to produce smokeless fuel.
- ✅ Primary solid product: **char (semi-coke)** with **8–15 % residual VM** — easy to ignite, burns smokelessly.
- ✅ Primary liquid product: **coal tar** (8–12 % yield) — rich in aromatics; used for road tar, chemicals, wood preservatives.
- ✅ Primary aqueous product: **ammoniacal liquor** — source of ammonia and ammonium sulfate fertilizer.
- ✅ Primary gaseous product: **lean low-BTU gas** (1500–2500 kcal/Nm³) — partly recycled to fire the retort.
- ✅ Overall material balance: **m_coal = m_char + m_tar + m_gas + m_liquor + losses**.
- ✅ Compare with HTC: lower temperature, more tar, more residual VM, less coke strength, **domestic** use vs. **metallurgical** use.
- ✅ Types of LTC retorts: **vertical** (continuous), **horizontal** (batch), and **internal-heating** designs.
- ✅ Self-energy: lean gas combustion provides the heat for retort walls, making the plant energy-efficient.
- ✅ KTU numericals almost always involve a **material balance** — learn the formula sheet in Section 2 by heart.
- ✅ Always quote **temperature range, % yields, and CV of products** when the question says "explain" or "describe".

**Mnemonic to remember the four products in order:** **"SALT G"** → **S**mokeless char, **A**mmoniacal liquor, **L**iquid **T**ar, **G**as.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Temperature Carbonization (HTC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High Temperature Carbonization (HTC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**High Temperature Carbonization (HTC)** is a destructive distillation process in which coal is heated in the **absence of air** to a final temperature between **900 °C and 1200 °C** (typically around **1100 °C**) to produce **metallurgical coke** as the primary solid product, along with valuable by-products such as coke oven gas, tar, ammonia, light oil, and benzol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *High Temperature Carbonization is the carbonization of bituminous coal at temperatures above 900 °C, primarily intended for the manufacture of hard, dense, porous, and strong metallurgical coke suitable for use in blast furnaces and foundry cupolas.*

### Key Process Identifiers
- **Operating Temperature Range:** 900 °C – 1200 °C (Peak ~ 1100 °C)
- **Residence/Soaking Time:** 12 – 20 hours
- **Feedstock:** Mainly **coking (bituminous) coal** with specific properties
- **Primary Product:** **Metallurgical coke** (Hard, dense, porous, lustrous, grey-black, ringing sound)
- **Nature of Process:** **Continuous, dry distillation, endothermic initially, then exothermic**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** HTC is sometimes called **"Coke Making"** or **"High-Temperature Coking."** The word *carbonization* strictly implies **thermal decomposition without air (pyrolysis)**, not combustion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine baking a loaf of bread in a *sealed* oven (no air entry) at a very high temperature for many hours. The dough (coal) doesn't burn because there's no oxygen, but it transforms — it shrinks, hardens, darkens, releases steam and gases, and becomes a rigid, porous structure. What remains is the "charred loaf" — that's your **coke**, while the vapours driven off (steam, oils, gases) are the **by-products**.

**A simpler real-world analogy:** Think of a pressure cooker without the whistle. The food inside (coal) is heated strongly; water, oils, and gases are released, leaving behind a drier, harder, more carbon-rich residue. The longer and hotter you cook it (within limits), the harder and more metallic the residue becomes — that mirrors how **higher temperatures in HTC produce harder, stronger coke than LTC**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Coking Temperature** | **1100 °C** | Industry standard peak |
| **Coke Yield (dry basis)** | 65 – 75 wt% | Primary product mass |
| **Coke Oven Gas (COG) yield** | 300 – 350 Nm³/ton coal | By-product fuel gas |
| **Tar yield** | 25 – 35 L/ton coal | By-product liquid |
| **Ammonia yield** | 2 – 4 kg/ton coal | As NH₃ or ammonium sulphate |
| **Light oil / Benzol yield** | 8 – 12 L/ton coal | Aromatic recovery |
| **Calorific value of COG** | 17 – 20 MJ/Nm³ | Useful as fuel |
| **Calorific value of coke** | 28 – 32 MJ/kg | Solid fuel value |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, always remember: **1 ton of dry coal** is the standard basis unless explicitly stated otherwise. Yields can be expressed in **mass % (wt%)** or **kg/L per ton**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The HTC Process — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

HTC is not a single-step event but a **sequential thermal decomposition** of coal's complex organic matrix. Each temperature window releases specific products.

### Stage-Wise Thermal Decomposition of Coal in HTC

| Stage | Temperature Range | Physical/Chemical Change | Volatiles Released |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I. Moisture Removal** | 100 – 150 °C | Free and bound water driven off as steam | Steam (H₂O) |
| **II. Primary Devolatilization (Plastic Stage)** | 350 – 500 °C | Coal softens → becomes plastic → re-solidifies (the *plastic layer* forms) | CO, CO₂, some H₂O |
| **III. Active Decomposition (Middle Stage)** | 500 – 750 °C | Tar, heavy oils, ammonia compounds released; coal passes through *metaplast* state | Tar vapours, CH₄, H₂, NH₃, H₂S |
| **IV. Semi-Coke Formation** | 750 – 900 °C | Solid residue becomes *semi-coke*; shrinkage and crack initiation | Mainly H₂, CH₄ |
| **V. Final Coke Hardening** | 900 – 1100 °C | Semi-coke contracts, densifies; pore structure fixed; true *metallurgical coke* forms | Mainly H₂ |
| **VI. Cooling Stage** | 1100 °C → ambient | Coke cooled; COG collected via suction | Residual volatiles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Plastic Layer" Concept** is the most important KTU concept. As heat penetrates a coal charge from both walls (in a coke oven), a **plastic layer** migrates inward. Two plastic layers from opposite walls eventually **meet at the centre**, and the entire mass solidifies. This determines coke strength and structure.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind HTC — Engineering Purpose

Why do we heat coal to such high temperatures? Three engineering reasons drive the answer:

1. **Strong, dense coke is needed for blast furnaces:** The blast furnace burden is heavy (iron ore + coke + flux), and the coke must withstand crushing loads **without disintegrating** into fines, which would block gas flow.
2. **Maximum gas yield:** Higher temperatures maximize the yield of **coke oven gas (COG)**, which is a valuable fuel for re-heating the ovens and other plant uses.
3. **Low moisture / low volatile coke:** HTC coke has **less than 2% volatile matter** and negligible moisture, making it a clean, high-carbon fuel.

## 2.3 HTC Coke — Required Physical & Chemical Properties

A metallurgical coke must satisfy **three pillars of quality**:

### A. Chemical Properties
- **High Fixed Carbon:** 85 – 90%
- **Low Volatile Matter:** < 2%
- **Low Ash:** 8 – 12% (lower is better)
- **Low Moisture:** < 3%
- **Low Sulphur:** < 0.6% (blast furnace constraint)
- **Low Phosphorus:** < 0.05%

### B. Physical Properties
- **Hardness & Strength:** measured by **Micum Index (M40)** and **M10** indices
- **Density:** Apparent density 800 – 1000 kg/m³; True density ~ 1.8 – 2.0 g/cm³
- **Porosity:** 45 – 55%
- **Size:** 25 – 80 mm (blast furnace), 80 – 120 mm (foundry)
- **Lustre:** Silvery-grey metallic shine
- **Sound:** Produces a clear **metallic ring** when struck

### C. Mechanical Strength Indices (Most Tested in KTU)

$$M_{40} = \frac{\text{Mass of coke retained on 40 mm sieve after Micum drum test}}{\text{Total mass of coke charged to drum}} \times 100$$

$$M_{10} = \frac{\text{Mass of coke passing through 10 mm sieve after Micum drum test}}{\text{Total mass of coke charged to drum}} \times 100$$

- **M₄₀** should be **> 75%** for good blast furnace coke (high value = high strength)
- **M₁₀** should be **< 8%** (low value = less abrasion loss)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Micum Drum Test** uses a rotating drum (100 revolutions at 25 rpm) and sieves the coke. M₄₀ is the +40 mm fraction, M₁₀ is the –10 mm fraction. The **Irsid Index (I₄₀, I₁₀)** is a similar French-standard test using 500 revolutions.

## 2.4 Coal Selection Criteria for HTC

Not all coals are suitable. KTU examiners love this concept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coals suitable for HTC = "Coking Coals"** — primarily **bituminous coals** of specific rank with:
> - **Volatile Matter:** 22 – 32% (dry, ash-free basis)
> - **Reflectance (Ro):** 0.9 – 1.5%
> - **Swelling Index (Free Swelling Index, FSI):** 4 – 7
> - **Caking property:** Strong
> - **Crucible Swelling Number (CSN):** 6 – 9

**Inert materials** (low-volatile anthracite, petroleum coke) are often **blended** to control shrinkage and improve coke strength.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Carbonization yield** = (Mass of coke / Mass of dry coal) × 100 | Solid product yield | wt% |
| 2 | **Micum Index M₄₀** = (Mass retained on 40 mm sieve) / (Total mass) × 100 | Coke strength | % |
| 3 | **Micum Index M₁₀** = (Mass passing 10 mm sieve) / (Total mass) × 100 | Coke abrasion | % |
| 4 | **Carbon efficiency** = (Fixed C in coke / Fixed C in coal) × 100 | Conversion efficiency | % |
| 5 | **Heat balance:** $Q_{input} = Q_{coke} + Q_{gas} + Q_{tar} + Q_{loss}$ | Energy audit | kJ/kg coal |
| 6 | **COG yield** = 300 – 350 Nm³/ton coal | Standard gas yield | Nm³/ton |
| 7 | **Coke oven throughput** = (Mass of coal charged) / (Coking cycle time) | Plant productivity | ton/hr |
| 8 | **Density of coke (apparent)** = Mass / (Volume including pores) | Apparent density | kg/m³ |
| 9 | **True density** = Mass / (Volume excluding pores) | True density | kg/m³ |
| 10 | **Porosity** = 1 - ($\rho_{apparent} / \rho_{true}$) | Pore volume fraction | – |

> [!WARNING]
> **Micum vs Irsid:** In Micum, 100 revolutions are used. In Irsid, **500 revolutions** are used, making Irsid a *tougher* test. The M₄₀ (Micum) is approximately equal to the **I₄₀ + I₁₀** in some correlations. Be precise when writing answers.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Iron & Steel Industry:** ~70% of world coke is consumed in blast furnaces to reduce iron ore to pig iron.
- **Foundry:** Coke for cupola furnaces in casting industry.
- **COG Utilization:** Coke oven gas is used to **under-fire the ovens** (sustaining the process), with surplus exported as town gas or for power generation.
- **Chemical Feedstock:** Tar, benzol, and ammonia are recovered for the **chemical industry**.
- **Carbon Anodes:** Some specialty coke is used in **aluminium smelting** and **TiO₂ production**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Balance Across a Coke Oven Battery

Let us assume a **coke oven battery** processes **T tons of dry coal per hour** (or per cycle). We need to derive a relationship between the input and output streams.

### Given:
- Mass of dry coal charged per cycle: $M_c$ [kg]
- Coke yield (mass fraction): $y_c$ [–]
- Tar yield: $y_t$ [L/ton]
- COG yield: $y_g$ [Nm³/ton]
- Ammonia yield: $y_a$ [kg/ton as NH₃]
- Benzol yield: $y_b$ [L/ton]
- Coke moisture: $m$ [%]
- Coke ash: $A_c$ [%]

### Step 1 — Total Mass In
The total mass entering the oven (excluding air, since the process is anaerobic) is:

$$M_{in} = M_c \text{ [kg dry coal]}$$

### Step 2 — Coke Mass Out
The dry coke produced (after quenching, moisture removed):

$$M_{coke,dry} = y_c \cdot M_c$$

The "as-quenched" coke mass (containing moisture) is:

$$M_{coke,wet} = \frac{M_{coke,dry}}{1 - m/100}$$

### Step 3 — Tar Mass Out
Assuming tar density $\rho_t \approx 1.15$ kg/L (standard value):

$$M_{tar} = y_t \cdot \rho_t \cdot \frac{M_c}{1000}$$

where $M_c$ is in kg, $y_t$ in L/ton, and we divide by 1000 to convert tons to kg.

### Step 4 — COG Mass Out
Using standard COG density at NTP: $\rho_g \approx 0.55$ kg/Nm³ (average molecular weight ~ 16 g/mol gives 0.715 kg/Nm³; for coke oven gas with significant CO₂ and N₂, average ~ 0.55 kg/Nm³ is commonly used):

$$M_{gas} = y_g \cdot \rho_g \cdot \frac{M_c}{1000}$$

### Step 5 — Material Balance Closure
By conservation of mass:

$$M_c = M_{coke,wet} + M_{tar} + M_{gas} + M_{water} + M_{ammonia} + M_{benzol} + M_{losses}$$

### Step 6 — Overall Mass Balance Equation (Clean Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_c &= \frac{y_c \cdot M_c}{1 - m/100} + y_t \cdot \rho_t \cdot \frac{M_c}{1000} + y_g \cdot \rho_g \cdot \frac{M_c}{1000} + M_{H_2O} + y_a \cdot \frac{M_c}{1000} + y_b \cdot \rho_b \cdot \frac{M_c}{1000} + M_{loss}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing through by $M_c$ gives a dimensionless balance in fractions, useful for yield closure.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Coke Strength from Micum Drum Test

### Given:
- Initial mass of coke in drum test: $M_{total} = 50$ kg
- Mass retained on 40 mm sieve after 100 revolutions: $M_{+40} = 41$ kg
- Mass passing 10 mm sieve: $M_{-10} = 2.5$ kg

### Step 1 — Compute M₄₀

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{40} &= \frac{M_{+40}}{M_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{41}{50} \times 100 \\
&= 82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute M₁₀

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{10} &= \frac{M_{-10}}{M_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2.5}{50} \times 100 \\
&= 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
- **M₄₀ = 82%** is **above 75%** → **High strength** (acceptable for blast furnace) ✓
- **M₁₀ = 5%** is **below 8%** → **Low abrasion** (acceptable) ✓

This coke qualifies as **good metallurgical coke**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Required for Carbonization (Simplified)

For energy balance, the heat input must:
1. Heat coal from ambient to 1100 °C
2. Provide endothermic heat of carbonization
3. Heat the moisture to steam and vaporize
4. Heat air infiltration and compensate wall losses

### Simplified Formula:

$$Q_{total} = M_c \cdot \left[ c_{p,coal} \cdot \Delta T + \lambda_{vap} \cdot w + \Delta H_{carbonization} \right] / \eta_{oven}$$

Where:
- $c_{p,coal} \approx 1.3$ kJ/(kg·K) — specific heat of coal
- $\Delta T = 1100 - 25 = 1075$ K
- $\lambda_{vap} = 2260$ kJ/kg — latent heat of vaporization
- $w$ = moisture fraction of coal
- $\Delta H_{carbonization} \approx 350$ kJ/kg coal (endothermic)
- $\eta_{oven} \approx 0.85$ — oven thermal efficiency

### Numerical Example
For 1 kg coal with 5% moisture:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sensible} &= 1 \times 1.3 \times 1075 = 1397.5 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{moisture} &= 0.05 \times 2260 = 113 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{carbonization} &= 1 \times 350 = 350 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{gross} &= 1397.5 + 113 + 350 = 1860.5 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{input} &= \frac{1860.5}{0.85} = 2188.8 \text{ kJ/kg coal}
\end{aligned}
$$

So roughly **~2.2 MJ/kg of dry coal** is required as heat input. In practice, **about 40 – 50% of the COG produced is combusted to provide this heat** — a beautiful example of internal energy integration.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — HTC vs LTC vs CTC (Comparison Table)

This is **the single most important table** for KTU 2024 scheme Module 1 questions. Memorize it.

| Property | **LTC** (Low-Temp Carbonization) | **HTC** (High-Temp Carbonization) | **CTC** (Continuous / Fluidized Carbonization) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 500 – 700 °C | 900 – 1200 °C | 700 – 850 °C |
| **Primary Product** | **Semi-coke** (reactive, smoky) | **Metallurgical coke** (hard, strong) | **Smokeless domestic fuel** |
| **Solid Yield** | 75 – 82% | 65 – 75% | 70 – 80% |
| **Gas Yield** | 50 – 100 Nm³/ton | 300 – 350 Nm³/ton | 150 – 200 Nm³/ton |
| **Tar Yield** | 80 – 100 L/ton (high, aliphatic) | 25 – 35 L/ton (aromatic) | 40 – 60 L/ton |
| **Ammonia** | Negligible | 2 – 4 kg/ton | Traces |
| **Coke Strength** | Low, friable | Very high, hard | Moderate |
| **Use of solid** | Domestic fuel, boiler | Blast furnace, foundry | Domestic, industrial |
| **Coking time** | 2 – 4 hours | 12 – 20 hours | Continuous |
| **Oven type** | Vertical retorts | Coke ovens (by-product recovery) | Fluidized bed, entrained |
| **Volatile matter in solid** | 8 – 15% | < 2% | 4 – 8% |
| **Calorific value of gas** | ~ 25 MJ/Nm³ (rich) | ~ 18 MJ/Nm³ | ~ 20 MJ/Nm³ |
| **Suitability for smelting** | **No** | **Yes** | **No** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic trick:** "**H**TC = **H**ard, **H**ot, **H**eavy-duty, **H**igher gas" — it makes coke for **H**ot furnaces (blast furnaces!).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall HTC Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Preparation and Blending] --> B[Crushing and Sizing 3 mm minus]
    B --> C[Coke Oven Battery Charging]
    C --> D[Carbonization 1100 C in Absence of Air]
    D --> E[Hot Coke Discharged]
    E --> F[Quenching with Water]
    F --> G[Metallurgical Coke to Storage]
    D --> H[Coke Oven Gas Stream]
    H --> I[Primary Cooler]
    I --> J[Exhaust Gas Blower Suction]
    J --> K[Gas Cleaning and Tar Removal]
    K --> L[Ammonia Recovery as NH4 or H2SO4]
    L --> M[Light Oil and Benzol Recovery]
    M --> N[Desulphurization of COG]
    N --> O[Sulphur as H2SO4 or Sulphur Cake]
    O --> P[Clean COG to Gas Holder]
    P --> Q[COG Distribution: Oven Underfiring, Power, Town Gas]
    K --> R[Coal Tar Storage]
    R --> S[Distillation for Aromatic Chemicals]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Coke Oven Cross-Section and Plastic Layer Migration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CokeOven[Coke Oven Chamber Cross Section]
        direction LR
        L1[Heating Wall Left 1200 C] --> P1[Plastic Layer Left]
        P1 --> U1[Unreacted Coal Core]
        U1 --> P2[Plastic Layer Right]
        P2 --> L2[Heating Wall Right 1200 C]
    end

    H1[Coal Charge] -. heat from walls .-> P1
    P1 -. migrates inward .-> U1
    U1 -. shrinks and solidifies .-> P2
    P1 --> CokeOut[Hard Coke on Solidification]
    P2 --> CokeOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a real coke oven, the heat flows from **both vertical walls** inward. The **plastic layers** from each side migrate toward the centre. When they meet, the coal mass solidifies into coke. This is a *transient* heat-transfer-controlled process.

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — By-Product Recovery System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Coke Oven Gas at 700 C] --> R2[Primary Gas Cooler]
    R2 --> R3[Tar and Liquor Separation Decanter]
    R3 --> R4[Gas to NH3 Absorber]
    R4 --> R5[NH3 Stripper with Lime or Caustic]
    R5 --> R6[NH3 as Aqua Ammonia or NH4 2SO4]
    R4 --> R7[Final Gas Cooler]
    R7 --> R8[Benzol Scrubber Wash Oil]
    R8 --> R9[Benzol Distillation Column]
    R9 --> R10[Crude Benzol Product]
    R9 --> R11[Wash Oil Recycle]
    R8 --> R12[Desulphurizer H2S Removal]
    R12 --> R13[Clean Coke Oven Gas to Holder]
    R3 --> R14[Coal Tar to Storage Tank]
    R14 --> R15[Continuous Tar Distillation]
    R15 --> R16[Light Oil Naphthalene]
    R15 --> R17[Carbolic Oil Phenol]
    R15 --> R18[Creosote Oil]
    R15 --> R19[Anthracene Oil]
    R15 --> R20[Pitch Residue]
```

## 4.4 Process Variable Relationships — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coal Rank Higher] --> B[More Tar Yield, Less Coke Strength]
    C[Higher Carbonization Temperature] --> D[Stronger Coke, More Gas]
    E[Longer Coking Time] --> F[Denser Coke, More Energy]
    G[Higher Coal Moisture] --> H[More Steam, Lower Coke Quality]
    I[Smaller Coal Size 3 mm] --> J[Uniform Plastic Layer, Stronger Coke]
    K[Inert Material Blending] --> L[Reduced Shrinkage Cracks]
```
