# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**Define High Temperature Carbonization (HTC). State any two distinguishing features of coke produced by HTC.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** High Temperature Carbonization (HTC) is the process of heating coal in the absence of air to a temperature of **900 – 1200 °C** (typically 1100 °C) to produce hard, dense, porous **metallurgical coke** along with by-products like coke oven gas, tar, ammonia, and benzol.

**Two Distinguishing Features of HTC Coke:**
1. **High strength and hardness** — measured by Micum Index M₄₀ > 75% — withstands the burden in a blast furnace without disintegration.
2. **Low volatile matter content** (< 2%) and high fixed carbon (~ 85 – 90%), giving a clean, smokeless, high-calorific-value solid fuel (28 – 32 MJ/kg).

*(Any two of: porosity 45 – 55%, metallic ring sound, silvery grey lustrous appearance, low moisture, low sulphur.)* — **3 marks**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**Differentiate between High Temperature Carbonization (HTC) and Low Temperature Carbonization (LTC) in terms of operating temperature and primary solid product.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **HTC** | **LTC** |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Temperature** | 900 – 1200 °C | 500 – 700 °C |
| **Primary Solid Product** | Hard, strong **metallurgical coke** (for blast furnace) | Soft, friable **semi-coke** (for domestic/industrial fuel) |
| **Coke volatile matter** | < 2% | 8 – 15% |

The high temperature in HTC drives off nearly all volatiles, leaving a dense, strong coke, whereas LTC preserves more volatiles, giving a reactive but weak semi-coke. — **3 marks**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **process of High Temperature Carbonization of coal**. Explain the role of the **plastic layer** in coke formation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A coke oven battery processes **500 tons/day** of dry coal with a **coke yield of 70%** and **COG yield of 320 Nm³/ton coal**. Calculate the daily production of **(i) coke, (ii) coke oven gas, and (iii) total energy in COG** if its calorific value is **18 MJ/Nm³**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram** *(describe the same flow as Section 4.1 above)*

**Step-by-Step Process Description:**

1. **Coal Preparation:** Bituminous coking coal is crushed to < 3 mm size and blended with appropriate proportions of weakly coking or non-coking coal (inerts) to control shrinkage.

2. **Charging:** The blended coal is charged into the **coke oven chamber** (a narrow, tall refractory-lined slot, ~ 0.45 m wide, 4 – 6 m tall, 12 – 17 m long) through charging holes from the top.

3. **Carbonization:** The coke oven is heated externally by burning part of the COG in the flues between adjacent ovens. Heat flows from both side walls into the coal charge. Temperature rises from ambient to **~ 1100 °C** over **12 – 20 hours**.

4. **Stages of Carbonization** *(in order of temperature rise):*
   - **Below 100 °C:** Moisture removal
   - **350 – 500 °C:** Coal softens, becomes plastic
   - **500 – 750 °C:** Tar, ammonia, and gas evolution
   - **750 – 900 °C:** Semi-coke forms, shrinkage begins
   - **900 – 1100 °C:** Coke densification and hardening

5. **Discharging:** At the end of the cycle, the **pusher machine** pushes the hot coke out of the oven into a **quenching car**.

6. **Quenching:** Coke is rapidly cooled with water (wet quenching) or inert gas (dry quenching — CDQ).

7. **By-Product Recovery:** The COG leaving the oven is cooled, and tar, ammonia, naphthalene, light oil (benzol), and hydrogen sulphide are recovered in sequence.

**Role of the Plastic Layer (4 marks out of 7):**

When heat penetrates the coal charge, a **molten plastic zone** moves inward from each wall. Inside this zone, the coal is in a viscous, semi-fluid state where coal particles fuse together.

- The **plastic layer** acts as a **binding medium** that welds coal particles into a continuous mass.
- **Two plastic layers** (one from each side wall) eventually **meet at the centre** of the charge.
- Beyond this point, the plastic mass **re-solidifies** into a coherent, porous coke structure.
- The behaviour of the plastic layer (its thickness, fluidity, temperature range) directly determines the **strength, density, and porosity** of the final coke.
- If the plastic range is too narrow → poor fusion → weak coke.
- If the plastic range is too wide → excessive contraction → cracks in coke.

*(A labelled diagram showing plastic layer migration is essential for full marks.)* — **7 marks**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Coal processed per day: $M_{coal} = 500$ tons/day
- Coke yield: $y_c = 70\% = 0.70$ (mass fraction)
- COG yield: $y_g = 320$ Nm³/ton coal
- Calorific value of COG: $CV_{COG} = 18$ MJ/Nm³

**(i) Daily Coke Production:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{coke} &= y_c \times M_{coal} \\
&= 0.70 \times 500 \\
&= 350 \text{ tons/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Daily COG Production:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{COG} &= y_g \times M_{coal} \\
&= 320 \times 500 \\
&= 160{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total Energy in COG:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{COG} &= V_{COG} \times CV_{COG} \\
&= 160{,}000 \times 18 \\
&= 2{,}880{,}000 \text{ MJ/day} \\
&= 2.88 \times 10^6 \text{ MJ/day} \\
&= 2.88 \times 10^3 \text{ GJ/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i) Coke = 350 tons/day**
- **(ii) COG = 1,60,000 Nm³/day**
- **(iii) Energy in COG = 2.88 × 10⁶ MJ/day = 800 MWh/day** *(dividing by 3.6 gives MWh)* — **7 marks**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **properties of metallurgical coke** required for use in a **blast furnace**. How is the strength of coke measured? List the limitations of the Micum Index.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Micum drum test on 50 kg of coke gave the following results:
- Mass retained on 40 mm sieve: **41.5 kg**
- Mass passing 10 mm sieve: **2.0 kg**

Calculate the **M₄₀ and M₁₀ indices** and comment on whether this coke is **suitable for blast furnace use**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Required Properties of Metallurgical Coke for Blast Furnace:**

**1. Chemical Properties (3 marks):**
- **High Fixed Carbon:** 85 – 90% (provides heat and reducing agent)
- **Low Ash content:** 8 – 12% (ash is an inert burden that wastes energy)
- **Low Moisture:** < 3% (moisture wastes sensible heat in the blast furnace)
- **Low Sulphur:** < 0.6% (sulphur contaminates iron; must be low)
- **Low Phosphorus:** < 0.05%
- **Low Volatile Matter:** < 2% (indicates complete carbonization)

**2. Physical Properties (2 marks):**
- **High Strength:** must not crush under furnace burden
- **Correct Size:** 25 – 80 mm for blast furnace
- **Porosity:** 45 – 55% (allows gas flow through the bed)
- **Density:** Apparent 800 – 1000 kg/m³
- **Hardness and impact resistance**
- **Silvery grey metallic luster; ringing sound when struck**

**3. Measurement of Coke Strength (1 mark):**

The most common test is the **Micum Drum Test (ISO 556 / BS 1016)**:
- A known mass of coke is placed in a rotating drum (1 m diameter × 1 m length).
- Drum rotates at **25 rpm for 100 revolutions**.
- After rotation, coke is sieved and the **+40 mm fraction (M₄₀)** and **–10 mm fraction (M₁₀)** are measured.
- M₄₀ indicates **resistance to impact and abrasion** (higher is better).
- M₁₀ indicates **abrasion loss** (lower is better).
- A modified test, the **Irsid Index**, uses **500 revolutions** for a more severe evaluation.

**4. Limitations of Micum Index (1 mark):**
- The Micum test uses **only 100 revolutions**, which is **insufficiently severe** for modern large blast furnaces.
- It does not fully simulate the **coke degradation after moisture absorption (CSR – Coke Strength after Reaction)**.
- The Micum test does not account for **thermal stress** during transit through the blast furnace (where coke descends through hot zones and absorbs CO₂).
- It is a **bulk property test** and does not give information on individual coke piece fracture behaviour.

— **7 marks**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Total coke mass tested: $M_{total} = 50$ kg
- Mass retained on 40 mm sieve: $M_{+40} = 41.5$ kg
- Mass passing 10 mm sieve: $M_{-10} = 2.0$ kg

**Step 1 — Calculation of M₄₀**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{40} &= \frac{M_{+40}}{M_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{41.5}{50} \times 100 \\
&= 83.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculation of M₁₀**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{10} &= \frac{M_{-10}}{M_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2.0}{50} \times 100 \\
&= 4.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with Specifications**

| Parameter | Calculated | Required for BF | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| M₄₀ | **83%** | > 75% | ✓ Pass |
| M₁₀ | **4%** | < 8% | ✓ Pass |

**Step 4 — Conclusion**

The coke has **M₄₀ = 83%** (well above the 75% threshold) and **M₁₀ = 4%** (well below the 8% upper limit). This indicates **high impact strength and low abrasion loss**. **The coke is suitable for use in a blast furnace.** ✓

— **7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Errors in Numericals:** Many students write COG yield in **m³/ton** but forget the **"Nm³"** (Normal cubic metres, at 0 °C, 1 atm). Mixing up Nm³ with m³ can cost **1 – 2 marks**.
> 2. **Plastic Layer Confusion:** A common mistake is to say *"the plastic layer cools and forms coke"*. This is **wrong** — the plastic layer **solidifies (re-solidifies)** into coke as temperature continues to rise. Cooling is a separate post-process.
> 3. **Micum vs Irsid:** Do not interchange the two. The **500-revolution Irsid** test gives a **lower M₄₀** and a **higher M₁₀** than the 100-revolution Micum test on the *same* coke.
> 4. **M₄₀ vs M₁₀ Interpretation Reversal:** Many students wrongly conclude that a **higher M₁₀** is better. It is **not** — a high M₁₀ means **more fines**, i.e., **more degradation**.
> 5. **By-Product Yields:** Tar yield is given in **L/ton**, gas in **Nm³/ton**, ammonia in **kg/ton**. Do not assume they are all in the same units.
> 6. **Coke Oven vs Coke:** The **coke oven** is the *equipment*; **coke** is the *product*. Examiners deduct marks for confusing the two.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **HTC = Carbonization at 900 – 1200 °C (peak ~ 1100 °C)** of coking bituminous coal in the **absence of air**.
- **Primary Product = Metallurgical Coke** (hard, dense, porous, strong, M₄₀ > 75%, M₁₀ < 8%).
- **By-Products:** Coke Oven Gas (COG), Coal Tar, Ammonia (NH₃), Crude Benzol, Naphthalene, Sulphur.
- **Process Stages:** Drying → Plastic → Active Decomposition → Semi-coke → Hardened Coke → Cooling.
- **Plastic Layer** is the *binding zone* that welds coal particles; it migrates inward from both walls and solidifies into coke at the centre.
- **Micum Test:** 100 revolutions, M₄₀ = +40 mm %, M₁₀ = –10 mm %.
- **Irsid Test:** 500 revolutions (more severe).
- **Coking Cycle:** 12 – 20 hours per oven; oven wall temperature ~ 1200 °C; charge centre temperature reaches ~ 1000 – 1100 °C.
- **Coal Feedstock:** Bituminous coal, VM 22 – 32% (daf), FSI 4 – 7, reflectance Ro 0.9 – 1.5%.
- **HTC vs LTC:** HTC → metallurgical coke; LTC → semi-coke. Higher temperature ⇒ stronger coke, more gas, less tar (and tar is more aromatic in HTC).
- **COG Composition (typical vol%):** H₂ ~ 55%, CH₄ ~ 25%, CO ~ 6%, N₂ ~ 5%, CO₂ ~ 2%, CₘHₙ ~ 2%, O₂ ~ 0.5%.
- **Calorific Values:** Coke ~ 28 – 32 MJ/kg; COG ~ 17 – 20 MJ/Nm³; Tar ~ 36 – 40 MJ/kg.
- **Approximately 40 – 50% of COG** produced is combusted to provide the heat for coking (oven underfiring).
- **Coke oven is a slot-type refractory-lined chamber**, ~ 0.45 m wide × 4 – 6 m tall × 12 – 17 m long, heated by gas flues in the side walls.
- **Wet vs Dry Quenching:** Wet = water spray (simple, but loses sensible heat); Dry (CDQ) = inert gas, recovers waste heat.
- **Coke moisture specification:** < 3% (high moisture wastes heat in blast furnace).
- **Coke ash specification:** 8 – 12% (lower is better; high ash increases slag volume).
- **Coke Sulphur specification:** < 0.6% (sulphur is undesirable in iron).
- **For KTU numericals:** Always carry out mass balance on **1 ton (or 1000 kg) of dry coal** as the basis; show all units in the final answer.

> **End of Notes — High Temperature Carbonization (HTC) | Module 1 | OECHT832 Fuel Engineering**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crude Oil Exploration** is the systematic, multidisciplinary engineering and geoscientific process of identifying, locating, and evaluating subsurface geological formations that have the potential to contain commercial accumulations of hydrocarbons (crude oil and natural gas). It integrates **geology, geophysics, geochemistry, and reservoir engineering** to reduce geological uncertainty and confirm the presence of a viable petroleum reservoir.

The exploration workflow progresses through a sequential funnel:

$$\text{Basin Analysis} \longrightarrow \text{Seismic Survey} \longrightarrow \text{Wildcat Drilling} \longrightarrow \text{Appraisal} \longrightarrow \text{Reserves Booking}$$

The primary objective is to discover a **petroleum trap** — a geometric configuration of porous, permeable **reservoir rock** sealed by an impermeable **cap rock**, charged by a mature **source rock**, and positioned along a viable **migration pathway**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Components of a Petroleum System (Demaison's Triad):**
> - **Source Rock** – Organic-rich shale/coal that generates hydrocarbons under thermal maturation.
> - **Reservoir Rock** – Porous medium (sandstone, carbonate) capable of storing hydrocarbons.
> - **Cap Rock** – Impermeable seal (shale, salt, anhydrite) preventing hydrocarbon escape.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of crude oil exploration as **finding a buried treasure chest in a vast, opaque desert**.

- The **geologist** acts as a *cartographer*, studying surface rock outcrops and historical data to sketch where treasure *might* be hidden.
- The **geophysicist** is like a *metal detector operator*, sending sound waves (seismic) into the ground and listening for the *echoes* that reveal hidden caverns.
- The **geochemist** plays the role of a *forensic analyst*, sniffing soil and rock samples for hydrocarbon "fingerprints" that seep upward.
- The **drilling engineer** is the *excavator*, finally digging the well to confirm whether the treasure (oil) is genuinely there.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Cost Benchmark:** A single deep-water exploration well can cost between **USD 100 million to USD 200 million**, while a 3D seismic survey over 1,000 km² can cost **USD 1–5 million**. Success rates for wildcat exploration wells historically range from **10–30%**, depending on basin maturity.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics in Crude Oil Exploration

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Seismic wave velocity in sedimentary rock | 2,000 – 5,000 m/s |
| Average density of crustal rocks | 2,500 – 2,900 kg/m³ |
| Standard API gravity of crude oil | 10° – 45° API |
| Minimum porosity for commercial reservoir | > 8 % |
| Minimum permeability for commercial flow | > 1 mD (milli-Darcy) |
| Source rock maturity threshold (Vitrinite Reflectance, Ro) | 0.6 – 1.2 % |
| Typical exploration licence (acreage) duration | 6 – 10 years |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Petroleum System — Sequential Logic

The **Petroleum System** concept (Magoon & Dow, 1994) is the fundamental framework used by KTU examiners. It comprises seven elements that must coexist in time and space:

1. **Source Rock Deposition** — Organic-rich sediments accumulate in anoxic marine or lacustrine environments.
2. **Burial & Compaction** — Overlying sediments progressively bury the source rock, increasing pressure and temperature.
3. **Thermal Maturation (Kerogen → Hydrocarbons)** — At temperatures of **60 °C – 150 °C** (corresponding to depths of **2–5 km**), kerogen thermally cracks into oil and gas. The "**oil window**" is bounded by:
   $$0.6\% \leq R_o \leq 1.2\% \quad (\text{Vitrinite Reflectance})$$
4. **Primary Migration** — Expelled hydrocarbons move from low-permeability source rock into higher-permeability carrier beds.
5. **Secondary Migration** — Buoyancy drives hydrocarbons upward through permeable strata until trapped.
6. **Trap Formation** — A structural or stratigraphic closure forms **before or synchronously** with hydrocarbon migration.
7. **Preservation** — Cap rock integrity prevents leakage over geological time scales (millions of years).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does oil float upward?** Hydrocarbons are less dense than formation water. Buoyancy force per unit volume is given by the difference in densities: $\Delta \rho = \rho_w - \rho_{hc}$. The Darcy velocity of secondary migration is extremely slow — typically **1–10 km per million years**.

## 2.2 Stages of Crude Oil Exploration

### Stage A: Reconnaissance (Desktop Study)
- Basin analysis using satellite imagery, well logs from neighbouring fields, and published geological maps.
- Identification of **leads** (potential prospects lacking confirmed trap geometry).

### Stage B: Detailed Geological Mapping
- Field surveys to map surface structures (anticlines, faults, unconformities).
- Geochemical analysis of soil and rock samples for **seepage indicators** (alkanes, biomarkers like pristane/phytane ratio).

### Stage C: Geophysical Surveys
- **Gravity Survey** — Measures lateral variations in Earth's gravitational field caused by density contrasts (e.g., salt domes vs. sedimentary fill).
- **Magnetic Survey** — Maps magnetic anomalies to identify basement structures and igneous intrusions.
- **Seismic Survey** — The most critical exploration tool, described in detail below.

### Stage D: Exploratory (Wildcat) Drilling
- Drilling of a **wildcat well** in the most geologically favourable location.
- Result categories: **Dry hole, Producer (oil/gas), or Technical discovery**.

### Stage E: Appraisal & Delineation
- Drilling of **appraisal wells** to determine the lateral extent, vertical thickness, and fluid contacts of the discovery.
- Reserves estimation: **P90 (Proved)**, **P50 (Probable)**, **P10 (Possible)** at 90%, 50%, 10% probability of exceedance respectively.

## 2.3 Seismic Survey Methodology — The Core Tool

A seismic survey is performed in three phases:

**1. Acquisition** — A seismic source (vibrator truck, airgun, or dynamite) generates acoustic energy. Receivers (geophones on land, hydrophones at sea) record the reflected waves.

**2. Processing** — Raw data undergo stacking, migration, deconvolution, and filtering to produce a coherent subsurface image.

**3. Interpretation** — Geoscientists identify reflectors, faults, and structural closures. A **time-to-depth conversion** is then performed using:
$$D = \frac{V \times t}{2}$$
where $D$ is depth (m), $V$ is interval velocity (m/s), and $t$ is two-way travel time (s).

> [!NOTE]
> **Seismic Survey Types (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **2D Seismic** — Single profile lines; cheap, low resolution.
> - **3D Seismic** — Dense grid of lines; provides volumetric subsurface image; industry standard since 1990s.
> - **4D Seismic** — 3D surveys repeated over time (months/years) to monitor reservoir changes during production (time-lapse monitoring).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Time-to-Depth Conversion | $D = \dfrac{V \cdot t}{2}$ | Converts seismic two-way time to depth |
| Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot V$ | Product of density and velocity; controls reflection coefficient |
| Reflection Coefficient | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Fraction of energy reflected at interface |
| Porosity (Density Log) | $\phi = \dfrac{\rho_{ma} - \rho_b}{\rho_{ma} - \rho_f}$ | From bulk density $\rho_b$, matrix density $\rho_{ma}$, fluid density $\rho_f$ |
| Hydrocarbon Saturation (Archie) | $S_w = \sqrt{\dfrac{a \cdot R_w}{\phi^m \cdot R_t}}$ | $R_w$ = water resistivity, $R_t$ = true resistivity |
| Darcy's Law | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | Volumetric flow rate through porous medium |
| Buoyancy Force (Migration) | $F_b = \Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot V$ | Force driving hydrocarbon migration |
| Trap Closure | $H_c = z_{spill} - z_{crest}$ | Vertical closure height of a structural trap |
| Recovery Factor (Primary) | $RF = \dfrac{\text{OOIP}_{produced}}{\text{OOIP}_{original}}$ | Typically 10–30% for primary recovery |
| Material Balance (Schilthuis) | $N = \dfrac{N_p \cdot B_o + (W_p - W_i) - G_i \cdot B_g}{B_o - B_{oi} + (R_p - R_{si}) \cdot B_g}$ | Original Oil In Place calculation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked about exploration methods, always structure your answer as: (1) Geological, (2) Geophysical, (3) Geochemical, (4) Drilling. Memorize the seismic reflection coefficient formula — it is a guaranteed 2-mark question in Part A.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Exploration risk reduction** — Multi-disciplinary integration reduces dry-hole probability, saving millions of dollars per well.
- **Field development planning** — Exploration data feeds directly into reservoir simulation models used for production forecasting.
- **Environmental site characterization** — Modern exploration incorporates baseline environmental surveys to comply with **EIA (Environmental Impact Assessment)** regulations under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, India.
- **Energy security policy** — India's **Hydrocarbon Exploration & Licensing Policy (HELP, 2016)** and **Open Acreage Licensing Policy (OALP)** regulate exploration acreage bidding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reflection Coefficient at a Subsurface Interface

**Physical Premise:** When a seismic P-wave travels through a rock layer of acoustic impedance $Z_1 = \rho_1 V_1$ and strikes a second layer with impedance $Z_2 = \rho_2 V_2$, a fraction of the energy is reflected and the remainder is transmitted. The **reflection coefficient** $R$ defines the amplitude ratio of the reflected wave to the incident wave.

**Boundary Conditions (Zoeppritz Equations, simplified for normal incidence):**
- Continuity of particle displacement at the interface.
- Continuity of stress (pressure) at the interface.

**Derivation Steps:**

Step 1: Define the incident, reflected, and transmitted pressure amplitudes.
- $A_i$ = incident amplitude
- $A_r$ = reflected amplitude
- $A_t$ = transmitted amplitude

Step 2: Apply stress continuity (force per unit area must balance on both sides of the interface):
$$\rho_1 V_1 A_i + \rho_1 V_1 (-A_r) = \rho_2 V_2 A_t$$
$$\rho_1 V_1 (A_i - A_r) = \rho_2 V_2 A_t$$

Step 3: Apply displacement continuity (particles cannot separate at the interface):
$$\frac{A_i + A_r}{V_1} = \frac{A_t}{V_2}$$

Step 4: Introduce acoustic impedance $Z = \rho V$ and rewrite Step 2 as:
$$Z_1 (A_i - A_r) = Z_2 A_t$$

Step 5: From Step 3, solve for $A_t$:
$$A_t = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_i + A_r}{V_1}$$

Step 6: Substitute $A_t$ into the impedance equation:
$$Z_1 (A_i - A_r) = Z_2 \cdot V_2 \cdot \frac{A_i + A_r}{V_1}$$

Step 7: Recognize that $Z_2 V_2 / V_1 = Z_2$, so:
$$Z_1 (A_i - A_r) = Z_2 (A_i + A_r)$$

Step 8: Expand and collect terms containing $A_r$:
$$Z_1 A_i - Z_1 A_r = Z_2 A_i + Z_2 A_r$$
$$A_r (Z_1 + Z_2) = A_i (Z_1 - Z_2)$$

Step 9: Solve for the reflection coefficient $R = A_r / A_i$:
$$R = \frac{A_r}{A_i} = \frac{Z_1 - Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- If $Z_2 > Z_1$ (e.g., shale over sandstone, or gas-filled sand below wet sand), $R > 0$ — a "positive" reflection, often appearing as a **peak (black)** on seismic sections.
- If $Z_2 < Z_1$, $R < 0$ — a "negative" reflection, often a **trough (red)** — a classic **bright spot** indicator for gas sands (AVO/amplitude anomalies).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time-to-Depth Conversion (Two-Way Travel Time)

**Premise:** A seismic wave travels downward from the surface, reflects off a subsurface interface, and returns to the surface receiver. The recorded two-way travel time (TWTT) must be converted to one-way depth for drilling decisions.

**Step-by-Step:**

Step 1: Total travel path length $L$ = down-leg + up-leg = $2D$ (vertical path assumed for normal incidence).

Step 2: Apply the kinematic relation $V = L / t$:
$$V = \frac{2D}{t}$$

Step 3: Solve for depth $D$:
$$D = \frac{V \cdot t}{2}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given:
- Interval velocity $V = 3{,}000$ m/s
- Two-way travel time $t = 1.8$ s

Compute:
$$D = \frac{3{,}000 \times 1.8}{2} = \frac{5{,}400}{2} = 2{,}700 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** The reflector lies at a depth of **2,700 metres below the surface**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Porosity from Bulk Density (Density Log Equation)

**Premise:** A density tool emits gamma rays into the formation and measures the back-scattered intensity, which is proportional to the bulk density $\rho_b$ of the rock-fluid system.

**Step 1:** The bulk density is the volumetric average of matrix and fluid densities:
$$\rho_b = (1 - \phi) \rho_{ma} + \phi \rho_f$$

Step 2: Rearrange to isolate $(1 - \phi)$:
$$(1 - \phi) = \frac{\rho_b - \phi \rho_f}{\rho_{ma}}$$

Step 3: Expand and collect $\phi$ terms:
$$\rho_b - \phi \rho_f = (1 - \phi) \rho_{ma}$$
$$\rho_b = \rho_{ma} - \phi \rho_{ma} + \phi \rho_f$$
$$\rho_b - \rho_{ma} = \phi (\rho_f - \rho_{ma})$$

Step 4: Solve for porosity:
$$\phi = \frac{\rho_{ma} - \rho_b}{\rho_{ma} - \rho_f}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given:
- $\rho_{ma} = 2.65$ g/cm³ (sandstone matrix, pure quartz)
- $\rho_b = 2.35$ g/cm³ (from log)
- $\rho_f = 1.00$ g/cm³ (fresh water filtrate)

Compute:
$$\phi = \frac{2.65 - 2.35}{2.65 - 1.00} = \frac{0.30}{1.65} = 0.182 = 18.2\%$$

**Interpretation:** A porosity of 18.2% exceeds the commercial threshold of 8%, indicating a viable reservoir rock.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Archie's Equation for Water Saturation

**Premise:** Hydrocarbon saturation $S_{hc} = 1 - S_w$ is computed from resistivity logs.

**Step 1:** Archie's empirical relation for clean (shale-free) formations:
$$R_t = \frac{a \cdot R_w}{\phi^m \cdot S_w^n}$$

where:
- $R_t$ = true formation resistivity (from deep resistivity log)
- $R_w$ = formation water resistivity (from water analysis or SP log)
- $\phi$ = porosity
- $a$ = tortuosity factor (typically 0.6 – 1.0; for clean sand $a = 0.62$)
- $m$ = cementation exponent (typically 1.8 – 2.0 for sandstones)
- $n$ = saturation exponent (typically $\approx 2.0$)

Step 2: Solve for $S_w$:
$$S_w^n = \frac{a \cdot R_w}{\phi^m \cdot R_t}$$
$$S_w = \sqrt[n]{\frac{a \cdot R_w}{\phi^m \cdot R_t}} = \left(\frac{a \cdot R_w}{\phi^m \cdot R_t}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given:
- $a = 0.62$, $m = 2.0$, $n = 2.0$
- $R_w = 0.05$ ohm·m
- $\phi = 0.20$
- $R_t = 10$ ohm·m (from deep induction log)

Compute:
$$S_w^2 = \frac{0.62 \times 0.05}{(0.20)^{2.0} \times 10} = \frac{0.031}{0.04 \times 10} = \frac{0.031}{0.40} = 0.0775$$
$$S_w = \sqrt{0.0775} = 0.278 = 27.8\%$$
$$S_{hc} = 1 - S_w = 1 - 0.278 = 0.722 = 72.2\%$$

**Interpretation:** A hydrocarbon saturation of **72.2%** confirms a producible oil/gas zone (commercial threshold $\geq 50\%$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram: The Crude Oil Exploration Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete exploration workflow from basin reconnaissance to reserves booking.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Basin Reconnaissance] --> A2[Geological Mapping]
    A2 --> A3[Geochemical Survey]
    A3 --> A4[Gravity and Magnetic Survey]
    A4 --> A5[2D Seismic Survey]
    A5 --> A6{Lead Identified?}
    A6 -- No --> A7[Acreage Relinquished]
    A6 -- Yes --> A8[3D Seismic Survey]
    A8 --> A9[Prospect Generation]
    A9 --> A10[Wildcat Well Drilling]
    A10 --> A11{Commercial Discovery?}
    A11 -- No --> A12[Plug and Abandon]
    A11 -- Yes --> A13[Appraisal Drilling]
    A13 --> A14[Reserves Estimation]
    A14 --> A15[Field Development Plan]
    A15 --> A16[Production Phase]

    style A10 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the interrelationships between the four critical elements of a petroleum system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Rock - Organic Rich Shale] -- Thermal Maturation --> HCM[Hydrocarbon Generation]
    HCM -- Primary Migration --> CAR[Carrier Bed - Permeable Strata]
    CAR -- Secondary Migration --> RES[Reservoir Rock - Porous Sandstone]
    RES -- Trapped Beneath --> CAP[Cap Rock - Impermeable Shale]
    CAP -- Prevents Leakage --> PRES[Preservation Over Geological Time]
    TPC[Trap Configuration - Anticline Fault Salt Dome] --> RES
    TIM[Timing - Trap Forms Before Migration] --> PRES

    style SRC fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q: Define "Petroleum Trap" and list the four major types of structural traps.**

**Model Answer:**

A **petroleum trap** is a geometric configuration of rocks that allows hydrocarbons to accumulate and be sealed against further migration. A valid trap must have (i) a **reservoir rock**, (ii) a **cap rock (seal)**, and (iii) a **closure** to prevent lateral escape of hydrocarbons.

The four major types of structural traps are:
1. **Anticlinal trap** — Upward-arching fold that concentrates hydrocarbons at the crest.
2. **Fault trap** — Impermeable fault plane juxtaposes reservoir against sealing rock.
3. **Salt dome trap** — Piercement salt creates an arched reservoir sealed by surrounding sediments.
4. **Stratigraphic trap** — Lateral facies change (e.g., pinch-out, unconformity) seals the reservoir.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **structural traps** (formed by tectonic deformation) with **stratigraphic traps** (formed by depositional variations). Examiners specifically deduct marks if you list stratigraphic traps under the "structural" category.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q: What is the significance of the "Reflection Coefficient" in seismic exploration? How is a "Bright Spot" related to gas reservoirs?**

**Model Answer:**

The **reflection coefficient** $R$ is the ratio of the amplitude of the reflected seismic wave to the incident wave at an interface between two layers. It is calculated as:
$$R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$
where $Z = \rho V$ is the acoustic impedance.

**Significance:**
- It quantifies the strength of the seismic reflector.
- A higher $\vert R \vert$ value produces a strong, easily identifiable reflection.
- It enables identification of lithology and fluid contacts.

**Bright Spot:**
A "bright spot" is an anomalously high-amplitude reflection on a seismic section, caused by a large negative reflection coefficient at the top of a gas-saturated sand. Gas drastically reduces the rock's acoustic impedance ($V$ drops sharply), producing $R \ll 0$ and a strong reflection. Bright spots are direct hydrocarbon indicators (DHI).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2023 — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete petroleum system concept. Discuss the role of source rock, reservoir rock, cap rock, and trap in hydrocarbon accumulation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, describe the different geophysical methods used in crude oil exploration. Compare the relative effectiveness of gravity, magnetic, and seismic surveys.

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **Petroleum System** is a natural system that encompasses a pod of active source rock and all related oil and gas accumulations. The seven elements are:

1. **Source Rock** — Fine-grained organic-rich sediments (e.g., Type II/III kerogen shales) deposited in anoxic environments. The total organic carbon (TOC) must exceed **2%** for commercial generation. Thermal maturation converts kerogen into liquid/gaseous hydrocarbons at temperatures between **60 °C and 150 °C**, corresponding to vitrinite reflectance $R_o = 0.6$–$1.2\%$.

2. **Reservoir Rock** — Porous and permeable lithologies (sandstone, fractured carbonate, dolomite) that store and transmit hydrocarbons. Minimum commercial porosity is **8%** and permeability is **1 mD**.

3. **Cap Rock (Seal)** — An impermeable layer (e.g., shale, evaporite, tight marl) that prevents vertical migration of hydrocarbons. Capillary entry pressure must exceed the buoyancy pressure of the hydrocarbon column:
$$P_c > \Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot H_c$$

4. **Trap** — A geometric feature that halts hydrocarbon migration. Classified into **structural** (anticline, fault, salt dome) and **stratigraphic** (pinch-out, reef, unconformity) types.

5. **Migration** — Primary (source to carrier bed) and Secondary (carrier bed to trap) migration, driven by buoyancy and capillary forces.

6. **Timing** — The trap must form **before or simultaneously** with hydrocarbon generation and migration; otherwise, hydrocarbons will escape.

7. **Preservation** — Cap rock integrity over geological time is essential.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Geophysical Methods in Crude Oil Exploration:**

1. **Gravity Survey:**
   - **Principle:** Measures lateral variations in Earth's gravitational field caused by density contrasts between rock bodies.
   - **Instrument:** Gravimeter (e.g., LaCoste-Romberg, accuracy $\pm 0.01$ mGal).
   - **Application:** Identification of salt domes (low density, $\rho \approx 2.2$ g/cm³), basement highs, and sedimentary basin geometry.
   - **Limitation:** Low resolution; ambiguous interpretation.

2. **Magnetic Survey:**
   - **Principle:** Measures variations in Earth's magnetic field due to magnetic susceptibility contrasts (especially in igneous rocks).
   - **Instrument:** Proton-precession or fluxgate magnetometer.
   - **Application:** Mapping basement topography, igneous intrusions, and regional structural trends.
   - **Limitation:** Useful mainly for regional reconnaissance.

3. **Seismic Survey (Reflection Method):**
   - **Principle:** Generates acoustic energy at the surface; measures the two-way travel time of reflections from subsurface interfaces.
   - **Resolution:** $\sim \frac{V}{4f}$ (where $V$ = velocity, $f$ = frequency). For $V = 3{,}000$ m/s and $f = 30$ Hz, resolution $\approx 25$ m.
   - **Application:** Detailed structural imaging, stratigraphic interpretation, direct hydrocarbon detection.
   - **Variants:** 2D, 3D, and 4D (time-lapse).

**Comparison:**

| Method | Target Anomaly | Typical Cost | Penetration | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gravity | Density contrast | Low | Deep crust | Low |
| Magnetic | Magnetic susceptibility | Low | Deep crust | Low |
| Seismic | Acoustic impedance | High | Basin-scale | High |

**Conclusion:** Seismic reflection is the most effective and is universally the primary tool for crude oil exploration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** When asked to "compare" methods, many students merely list them sequentially. Examiners expect an **explicit comparison table** or matrix with at least 3 parameters. Failure to compare leads to a deduction of 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the complete sequence of crude oil exploration stages from basin reconnaissance to reserves estimation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A seismic reflector is observed at a two-way travel time of 2.4 seconds. The interval velocity in the overlying layer is 3,500 m/s. (i) Calculate the depth of the reflector. (ii) If the densities of the two layers are 2.20 g/cm³ and 2.40 g/cm³, calculate the reflection coefficient.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Stages of Crude Oil Exploration:**

1. **Acreage Acquisition:** The exploration company obtains exploration rights (licence/lease) from the government (e.g., OALP rounds in India).

2. **Reconnaissance / Desktop Study:** Compilation of existing geological, geophysical, and well data to identify prospective basins.

3. **Geological Mapping:** Field surveys to map outcrop patterns, structural features, and stratigraphy.

4. **Geochemical Survey:** Analysis of soil, water, and rock samples for hydrocarbon seepage indicators (C1–C4 light hydrocarbons, biomarkers).

5. **Gravity and Magnetic Survey:** Regional-scale geophysical surveys to map basement structure and basin configuration.

6. **2D Seismic Survey:** Acquisition of regional seismic lines to identify leads and prospects.

7. **3D Seismic Survey:** High-resolution volumetric imaging of priority prospects.

8. **Prospect Generation and Ranking:** Integration of all data into a "prospect map" with associated geological risking (source, reservoir, trap, timing, preservation).

9. **Wildcat Well Drilling:** Drilling the first exploration well on the prospect.

10. **Well Testing and Logging:** If hydrocarbons are encountered, logging (SP, GR, resistivity, density, neutron, sonic) and drill-stem testing are performed.

11. **Appraisal Drilling:** Drilling of additional wells to delineate the field boundaries.

12. **Reserves Estimation:** Calculation of Original Oil In Place (OOIP) and recoverable reserves using volumetric or material balance methods. Reserves are categorized as **Proved (P90), Probable (P50), and Possible (P10)**.

13. **Field Development Plan (FDP):** Detailed engineering plan for production.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Two-way travel time, $t = 2.4$ s
- Interval velocity, $V = 3{,}500$ m/s
- $\rho_1 = 2.20$ g/cm³, $\rho_2 = 2.40$ g/cm³

**(i) Depth Calculation:**

Using the time-to-depth conversion formula:
$$D = \frac{V \times t}{2} = \frac{3{,}500 \times 2.4}{2} = \frac{8{,}400}{2} = 4{,}200 \text{ m}$$

**The reflector lies at a depth of 4,200 m.**

**(ii) Reflection Coefficient Calculation:**

Step 1: Compute acoustic impedances.
$$Z_1 = \rho_1 \times V_1 = 2{,}200 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 3{,}500 \text{ m/s} = 7{,}700{,}000 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$
$$Z_2 = \rho_2 \times V_2 = 2{,}400 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 3{,}500 \text{ m/s} = 8{,}400{,}000 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$

Step 2: Apply the reflection coefficient formula.
$$R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{8{,}400{,}000 - 7{,}700{,}000}{8{,}400{,}000 + 7{,}700{,}000}$$
$$R = \frac{700{,}000}{16{,}100{,}000} = 0.0435$$

**The reflection coefficient is $+0.0435$ (or approximately $+4.35\%$).**

**Interpretation:** A small positive reflection indicates a weak but detectable impedance increase, typical of a transition from a softer overlying rock (e.g., shale) to a denser underlying rock (e.g., limestone or compacted sandstone).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Petroleum System**: Source + Reservoir + Cap + Trap + Migration + Timing + Preservation.
- **Oil Window**: Vitrinite Reflectance $R_o = 0.6$–$1.2\%$; Temperature **60–150 °C**; Depth **2–5 km**.
- **Traps**: Structural (anticline, fault, salt dome) vs. Stratigraphic (pinch-out, reef, unconformity).
- **Time-to-Depth**: $D = \frac{V \cdot t}{2}$ — must always be divided by 2 (two-way time).
- **Reflection Coefficient**: $R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$; positive $R$ → peak; negative $R$ → trough/bright spot (often gas).
- **Density Log Porosity**: $\phi = \frac{\rho_{ma} - \rho_b}{\rho_{ma} - \rho_f}$; standard values: $\rho_{ma} = 2.65$ g/cm³ (quartz sandstone), $\rho_f = 1.00$ g/cm³ (fresh mud filtrate).
- **Archie's Equation**: $S_w = \left(\frac{a \cdot R_w}{\phi^m \cdot R_t}\right)^{1/n}$; for clean sandstones use $a = 0.62$, $m = 2.0$, $n = 2.0$.
- **Exploration Methods Order (in answer)**: Geological → Geochemical → Geophysical (Gravity, Magnetic, Seismic) → Drilling.
- **Seismic Variants**: 2D (regional), 3D (detailed), 4D (monitoring).
- **Reserves Categories**: P90 (Proved), P50 (Probable), P10 (Possible).
- **Industry Cost Reference**: A deep-water wildcat well can cost **USD 100–200 million**; 3D seismic survey over 1,000 km² costs **USD 1–5 million**.
- **Indian Policy Context**: **HELP (2016)** and **OALP** govern exploration acreage bidding; **PEL/PML** licences issued by DGH (Directorate General of Hydrocarbons).
- **Common KTU exam questions**: Define petroleum trap, list trap types, explain seismic reflection coefficient, calculate depth from TWTT, describe petroleum system.
- **Valuation Favourite**: "Compare geological, geophysical, and geochemical methods" — always use a comparison table.
- **Memorize numerical constants**: 8% minimum porosity, 1 mD minimum permeability, 50% minimum hydrocarbon saturation for commercial production.
- **Avoid mixing units**: Always convert g/cm³ to kg/m³ (multiply by 1,000) before computing acoustic impedance.
- **Don't forget the denominator "2"** in the time-to-depth formula — this is the most common single error in numerical KTU questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Petroleum Refining Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Petroleum Refining Processes # Module 2: Petroleum Refining Processes — Foundational Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Petroleum Refining** is the industrial process sequence by which **crude oil** (a complex, naturally occurring hydrocarbon mixture) is chemically and physically transformed into a slate of commercially useful products such as **liquefied petroleum gas (LPG)**, **gasoline (petrol)**, **kerosene**, **diesel**, **jet fuel**, **fuel oils**, **lubricating oils**, **waxes**, **asphalt/bitumen**, and **petrochemical feedstocks (naphtha)**. The refining operation is broadly divided into **three sequential process stages**: 1. **Separation Processes** — Distillation-based physical separation (Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation). 2. **Conversion Processes** — Chemical restructuring of molecular size/form (Cracking, Reforming, Isomerization, Alkylation, Coking). 3. **Finishing / Treatment Processes** — Removal of impurities and quality upgradation (Hydrotreating, Desulfurization, Sweetening, Blending). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course outcome for this module maps to **CO2 — "Apply the principles of petroleum refining to evaluate product yields and process efficiency."** Students must therefore link every refining unit to *what it produces*, *how much*, and *at what purity*. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a giant **mixed-fruit smoothie (crude petroleum)** arriving at a juice factory (refinery). You cannot drink it as-is because it contains bitter seeds, sour peels, pulp, and water mixed together. | Step in the Factory | Equivalent Refining Process | |---|---| | Straining the smoothie through progressively finer sieves to obtain separate juice streams | **Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation** (separation by boiling point) | | Cooking the leftover pulp to extract more juice | **Cracking / Coking** (breaking heavy molecules into lighter ones) | | Adding sugar, blending flavors, removing bitter compounds | **Reforming, Isomerization, Hydrotreating** (upgrading quality) | | Bottling the final juice variants | **Blending & Product Dispatch** | In short: **a refinery is a chemical kitchen that sorts, cooks, cleans, and packages hydrocarbon molecules into fuels and chemicals.** --- ## 1.3 Key Refinery Performance Metrics (Bold Constants) | Metric | Symbol | Standard Reference Value | |---|---|---| | **Crude API Gravity (benchmark)** | **°API** | **30 – 35 °API** (light sweet crude) | | **Barrel of crude oil** | **bbl** | **1 bbl = 159 litres** | | **Refinery utilization / capacity factor** | **η_ref** | **85 – 95 %** (world-class refinery) | | **Energy Intensity of refining** | **EII** | **≈ 6 – 8 GJ per m³ of crude** | | **Catalytic reforming severity** | **R + O / M + H** | **Research Octane Number of reformate: 95 – 102** | | **FCC conversion per pass** | **X_FCC** | **65 – 80 %** (typical riser reactor) | > [!TIP] > Memorise: **1 barrel = 42 US gallons = 159 litres = 5.61 ft³.** This conversion appears in nearly every KTU refinery numerical problem. # Module 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Refinery Process Train ## 2.1 The Refinery Flow Train (Top-Down Architecture) A modern integrated refinery follows a **hierarchical process flow** in which every downstream unit depends on the product slate of the unit above it. We now dissect each unit into its **purpose, operating principle, and operating window**. --- ### 2.2 Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU / Crude Distillation Unit – CDU) - **Purpose:** Splits desalted crude into **refinery gas, naphtha, kerosene, diesel, and atmospheric residue (AR)** based on boiling range. - **Operating pressure:** **Slightly above atmospheric (≈ 1 – 2 bar gauge)** to push overhead vapours through condensers. - **Tower internals:** Combination of **sieve trays** (above feed) and **structured packing** (below feed, where high liquid loads occur). - **Heat source:** **Two-stage desalter → preheat train (heat exchangers recovering product heat) → fired heater (furnace) ≈ 350 – 400 °C** at the column inlet. - **Side draws:** Naphtha, Kerosene (jet fuel), Light Diesel Oil (LDO), Heavy Diesel Oil (HDO). > [!NOTE] > **Why atmospheric pressure?** Crude partially decomposes (cracks) above ≈ 400 °C. By distilling under vacuum, the same boiling point cut is achieved at a much lower temperature — preventing thermal cracking. --- ### 2.3 Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU) - **Purpose:** Further fractionate **atmospheric residue** into **vacuum gas oil (VGO), heavy gas oil (HGO), lube oil distillates**, and **vacuum residue (VR / short residue)**. - **Operating pressure:** **10 – 50 mmHg absolute** (≈ 0.013 – 0.066 bar). - **Steam ejectors / liquid-ring vacuum pumps** maintain low pressure. - **Feed temperature at heater outlet:** ≈ 420 – 440 °C *(without vacuum, this would crack the feed).* --- ### 2.4 Catalytic Cracking — Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | **Catalyst** | **Zeolite-Y (USY) in a silica-alumina matrix** | | **Reactor** | **Riser reactor** (lift-line, vapor-catalyst contact time ≈ 2 – 4 seconds) | | **Temperature** | **500 – 550 °C** | | **Catalyst : Oil ratio** | **5 – 8 (w/w)** | | **Regenerator temperature** | **680 – 730 °C** (coke burn-off in air) | **Why catalytic cracking?** Cracking raises the **H : C ratio** of liquid products and produces **high-octane gasoline blending stock**. The catalyst breaks long-chain **n-paraffins (low RON)** into **iso-paraffins and aromatics (high RON)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thermal cracking vs Catalytic cracking:** > - *Thermal cracking* → free-radical mechanism → produces olefins, low octane. > - *Catalytic cracking* → carbocation mechanism → produces iso-paraffins + aromatics → **higher RON (≈ 90 – 92)**. --- ### 2.5 Hydrocracking - **Reactor type:** Fixed-bed trickle reactor with **bifunctional catalyst** (noble metal: Pt/Pd + acidic zeolite). - **Hydrogen partial pressure:** **70 – 200 bar**. - **Temperature:** **320 – 420 °C**. - **Key advantage:** Converts **VGO + atmospheric residue → high-quality diesel, jet, naphtha** with **simultaneous desulfurization and denitrogenation**. --- ### 2.6 Catalytic Reforming | Variable | Operating Value | |---|---| | **Catalyst** | **Pt / Re (or Pt/Sn) on chlorided γ-Al₂O₃** | | **Reactor** | **3 – 4 adiabatic fixed beds with inter-stage heating** | | **Pressure** | **3 – 35 bar** (modern CCR = 3 – 5 bar) | | **Temperature** | **480 – 525 °C** | **Reactions occurring:** - Naphthenes → Aromatics (dehydrogenation, endothermic) - Paraffins → Isoparaffins (isomerization, mildly exothermic) - Paraffins → Aromatics (dehydrocyclization, endothermic) - Hydrocracking of C₅⁻ to LPG (exothermic) **Key product:** **Reformate (RON 95 – 102)** — the primary gasoline blending pool contributor. > [!NOTE] > **CCR (Continuous Catalyst Regeneration)** reactors revolutionized reforming. Spent catalyst is withdrawn, regenerated in a separate regenerator, and returned — allowing operation at very low pressure and high selectivity. --- ### 2.7 Alkylation - **Reactants:** Isobutylene (or butenes) + Isobutane → **Alkylate (C₈ isoparaffins)**. - **Catalyst:** **Concentrated H₂SO₄ (98 %)** or **HF** (modern refineries prefer H₂SO₄ due to safety). - **Reaction:** Low temperature (0 – 10 °C), liquid-phase, fast & exothermic. - **Why:** Alkylate has **RON ≈ 92 – 96** and **zero olefins/aromatics** → premium gasoline blendstock. --- ### 2.8 Isomerization - Converts **n-pentane / n-hexane → iso-pentane / iso-hexane** over **Pt / chlorided alumina or Pt / zeolite (ZSM-5)**. - **RON uplift:** from 60 – 70 (n-paraffin) to **82 – 92 (iso-paraffin)**. - Important for meeting modern octane specs without lead or excessive aromatics. --- ### 2.9 Coking (Delayed Coking & Fluid Coking) - **Purpose:** Convert **vacuum residue / asphalt** into **naphtha, diesel, gas oil + petroleum coke**. - **Delayed Coking** — feed heated in a furnace to ≈ 490 °C, then routed to a **drum** where residence time of ≈ 24 hr cracks the feed thermally → coke forms inside drum. - **Fluid Coking** — feed contacts hot fluidized coke particles in a reactor; coke circulates to a burner for reheating. - **Petroleum coke** is used in aluminium smelting (anode grade) and steel making. --- ### 2.10 Hydrotreating / Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) - **Purpose:** Remove **S, N, O, metals** from gasoline, diesel, kerosene, VGO. - **Catalyst:** **Co-Mo / Ni-Mo on γ-Al₂O₃** (sulfided form active). - **H₂ pressure:** **30 – 100 bar**. - **Sulfur removal efficiency:** **90 – 99.9 %** depending on feed. --- ### 2.11 Visbreaking - **Mild thermal cracking** of vacuum residue to reduce viscosity → produces **visbroken residue** + small amounts of **naphtha + gas oil**. - **Operating T:** ≈ 450 – 490 °C, residence time a few minutes. - **Used by refineries** that want to make **fuel oil spec compliance** without full coking. --- ## 2.12 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Petroleum Refining > [!IMPORTANT] > The formulas below are the **high-yield equations** that the KTU examiner expects students to reproduce verbatim in numerical problems. | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---| | $${}^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60^\circ F}} - 131.5$$ | American Petroleum Institute Gravity | dimensionless | | $$SG_{60/60^\circ F} = \dfrac{141.5}{{}^\circ API + 131.5}$$ | Specific gravity from API | dimensionless | | $$K_W = \dfrac{(T_B)^{1/3}}{SG}$$ | Watson Characterization Factor ($T_B$ in Rankine) | dimensionless | | $$X_{conv} = \dfrac{F - U}{F} \times 100$$ | Conversion % of feed (F = feed, U = unconverted) | % | | $$Y_{product} = \dfrac{mass \, of \, product}{mass \, of \, feed} \times 100$$ | Yield of a product | % | | $$\eta_{ref} = \dfrac{actual \, throughput}{nameplate \, capacity} \times 100$$ | Refinery utilization | % | | $$\rho_{oil} = 141.5 \times \dfrac{1000}{{}^\circ API + 131.5} \;\; [kg/m^3]$$ | Density from API | kg/m³ | | $$\eta_{sep} = \dfrac{L_{overhead}}{L_{feed}} \times \dfrac{X_{light}}{X_{heavy,feed}}$$ | Distillation efficiency (Murphree) | fraction | | $$R_{C/O} = \dfrac{m_{cat}}{m_{oil}}$$ | Catalyst-to-oil ratio (FCC) | kg/kg | | $$\Delta P_{tower} = \rho_L g h_{liq}$$ | Static pressure of liquid in column | Pa | > [!WARNING] > **Do not** confuse °API with °Baumé — they use different reference constants. °API uses the constants **141.5 and 131.5** specifically because of its petroleum heritage. --- ## 2.13 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Refinery yield optimization** drives **$1–2 billion EBITDA** decisions in petroleum majors (Reliance Jamnagar, Saudi Aramco, Shell Pernis). - **Carbon footprint reduction** via **electrification of fired heaters**, **blue/green hydrogen in hydrocrackers**, and **carbon capture (CCUS)** is reshaping classical refining into a **hybrid petro-energy hub**. - **Petrochemical integration** — modern refineries are co-located with **steam crackers** to upgrade naphtha into ethylene, propylene → redefining the value chain. # Module 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Demonstrations > [!NOTE] > The following problems are the **canonical KTU problem types** for this module. Work through every step — KTU valuation penalises skipped transitions. --- ## 3.1 Problem Type 1 — API Gravity & Density Conversion **Statement:** A crude oil sample has a specific gravity of 0.876 at 60/60 °F. Compute (a) its API gravity and (b) its density in kg/m³. ### Step 1 — Identify formula $${}^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute SG = 0.876 $$ \begin{aligned} {}^\circ API &= \dfrac{141.5}{0.876} - 131.5 \\ &= 161.5297 - 131.5 \\ &= 30.03 \; {}^\circ API \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Convert API → Density (kg/m³) For petroleum liquids, the standard equation is: $$\rho \; [kg/m^3] = 141.5 \times \dfrac{1000}{{}^\circ API + 131.5}$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute $$ \begin{aligned} \rho &= 141.5 \times \dfrac{1000}{30.03 + 131.5} \\ &= 141.5 \times \dfrac{1000}{161.53} \\ &= 875.97 \; kg/m^3 \;\;\;(\approx 876 \; kg/m^3) \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** The crude is **30.03 °API** with **ρ ≈ 876 kg/m³**. Crude above 31.1 °API is classified as **light crude**. --- ## 3.2 Problem Type 2 — Mass Balance across Atmospheric Distillation **Statement:** A refinery processes **100,000 kg/h of desalted crude**. Yields are: - Naphtha: 22 % - Kerosene: 14 % - Diesel: 26 % - Atmospheric Residue (AR): 36 % - LPG + Refinery Gas: 2 % Compute the **mass flowrate** of each stream and verify the overall material balance closure. ### Step 1 — Mass flow of each stream $$m_i = Y_i \times F$$ $$ \begin{aligned} m_{naphtha} &= 0.22 \times 100{,}000 = 22{,}000 \; kg/h \\ m_{kero} &= 0.14 \times 100{,}000 = 14{,}000 \; kg/h \\ m_{diesel} &= 0.26 \times 100{,}000 = 26{,}000 \; kg/h \\ m_{AR} &= 0.36 \times 100{,}000 = 36{,}000 \; kg/h \\ m_{LPG+gas} &= 0.02 \times 100{,}000 = 2{,}000 \; kg/h \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Closure check $$ \begin{aligned} \sum m_{out} &= 22{,}000 + 14{,}000 + 26{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 2{,}000 \\ &= 100{,}000 \; kg/h = F_{in} \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > **Closure satisfied** — mass balance is exact (loss-free assumption). --- ## 3.3 Problem Type 3 — Conversion % in an FCC Unit **Statement:** The FCC riser is fed with **8000 kg/h of VGO**. The yield slate per hour is: | Stream | kg/h | |---|---| | Dry Gas (C₁–C₂) | 200 | | LPG (C₃–C₄) | 1200 | | Gasoline (C₅ – 220 °C) | 4200 | | LCO (Light Cycle Oil) | 1600 | | Slurry / Clarified Oil | 800 | Compute (a) **Conversion of VGO** and (b) **Gasoline yield on feed**. ### Step 1 — Conversion Definition $$X_{FCC} = \dfrac{F - U}{F} \times 100$$ where **U** = unconverted feed that exits in **products heavier than gasoline** (here, LCO + Slurry). ### Step 2 — Compute Unconverted $$ \begin{aligned} U &= m_{LCO} + m_{slurry} = 1600 + 800 = 2400 \; kg/h \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Conversion $$ \begin{aligned} X_{FCC} &= \dfrac{8000 - 2400}{8000} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{5600}{8000} \times 100 \\ &= 70 \; \% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Gasoline Yield on Feed $$ \begin{aligned} Y_{gasoline} &= \dfrac{m_{gasoline}}{F} \times 100 = \dfrac{4200}{8000} \times 100 = 52.5 \; \% \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** Conversion **70 %** is in the **healthy FCC operating window (65–80 %)** and gasoline yield **52.5 %** is typical for a modern riser reactor. --- ## 3.4 Problem Type 4 — Watson Characterization Factor **Statement:** A refinery stream has a **mean average boiling point (MeABP)** of **450 K** and **SG = 0.82**. Compute its Watson K-factor and identify the stream type. ### Step 1 — Convert K to °R (Rankine) $$ T_B \; [°R] = T_B \; [K] \times \dfrac{9}{5} $$ $$ T_B = 450 \times 1.8 = 810 \; °R $$ ### Step 2 — Apply Watson Equation $$ \begin{aligned} K_W &= \dfrac{(T_B)^{1/3}}{SG} \\ &= \dfrac{(810)^{1/3}}{0.82} \\ &= \dfrac{9.339}{0.82} \\ &\approx 11.39 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Interpretation > [!NOTE] > **Rule of thumb for K-factor:** > - **K_W > 12** → Highly paraffinic (e.g., light naphtha, lube base oil). > - **10 ≤ K_W ≤ 12** → Intermediate / paraffinic-naphthenic (e.g., crude range). > - **K_W < 10** → Naphthenic / aromatic (e.g., heavy cracked feedstocks). **K_W ≈ 11.4** classifies this stream as **intermediate-paraffinic**, consistent with a **gas oil range feedstock**. --- ## 3.5 Problem Type 5 — Refinery Utilization & Economic Throughput **Statement:** A 20 MMTPA (million metric tonnes per annum) refinery operates **340 days** in a year, with an **average capacity utilisation of 92 %**. Compute (a) annual crude throughput in MMT and (b) daily throughput in barrels per day (Mbpd), assuming crude density = 870 kg/m³. ### Step 1 — Actual Annual Throughput $$ \begin{aligned} T_{actual} &= 20 \times 0.92 = 18.4 \; MMTPA \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Daily Throughput (tonnes/day) $$ T_{daily} = \dfrac{18.4 \times 10^6 \, t}{340 \, d} = 54{,}117.6 \; t/d $$ ### Step 3 — Convert tonnes → barrels Volume = mass / density: $$ V_{daily} = \dfrac{54{,}117.6 \times 10^3 \, kg}{870 \, kg/m^3} = 62{,}204 \, m^3/d $$ Convert m³ → barrels (1 m³ = 6.289 bbl): $$ V_{daily} = 62{,}204 \times 6.289 = 391{,}221 \; bbl/d \approx 391.2 \; kbpd $$ > **Result:** A nominally "20 MMTPA" refinery in **reality** processes ≈ **391 kbpd** at 92 % utilization. --- ## 3.6 Problem Type 6 — Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) Mass Balance **Statement:** A diesel hydrotreater is fed with **5000 kg/h** of diesel containing **1.2 wt % sulfur**. Product diesel contains **0.005 wt % S**. Compute (a) mass of sulfur removed per hour, (b) H₂ consumed (assume stoichiometry: for every kg of S removed, 0.0625 kg H₂ is consumed to form H₂S and water). ### Step 1 — S removed $$ \begin{aligned} m_{S,in} &= 0.012 \times 5000 = 60 \; kg/h \\ m_{S,out} &= 0.00005 \times 5000 = 0.25 \; kg/h \\ m_{S,removed} &= 60 - 0.25 = 59.75 \; kg/h \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — H₂ consumed $$ m_{H_2} = 59.75 \times 0.0625 = 3.73 \; kg/h $$ > **Reality check:** This is approximately **41.8 Nm³/h** of H₂ at STP — consistent with industrial hydrotreater make-up hydrogen rates. # Module 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Refinery Process Flow (End-to-End Train) The mermaid diagram below maps the **entire refining sequence** from crude receipt to finished products, with intermediate streams labelled. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Crude Oil Receipt
Storage Tanks] --> B[Desalter Unit
Electrostatic Salt Removal] B --> C[Atmospheric Distillation
Column CDU at 1 to 2 bar] C --> C1[Refinery Gas LPG Naphtha] C --> C2[Kerosene Side Draw] C --> C3[Diesel Side Draw LDO HDO] C --> D[Atmospheric Residue] D --> E[Vacuum Distillation Unit
10 to 50 mmHg] E --> E1[Vacuum Gas Oil VGO] E --> E2[Vacuum Residue VR] E1 --> F[Fluid Catalytic Cracker
FCC Riser at 520 C] E1 --> G[Hydrocracker
100 bar H2] E2 --> H[Delayed Coker
Drum at 490 C] E2 --> I[Visbreaker
470 C] C1 --> J[Light Naphtha Isomerization] C1 --> K[Catalytic Reformer
CCR Pt Re] C2 --> L[Kerosene Hydrotreater] C3 --> M[Diesel Hydrodesulfurization] F --> N[Cracked Naphtha HDS] K --> O[Reformate Gasoline Pool] J --> O N --> O H --> P[Petroleum Coke Dispatch] H --> Q[Coker Naphtha and Diesel] L --> R[Kerosene Pool] M --> S[Diesel Pool] Q --> S O --> T[Gasoline Blending] S --> U[Diesel Dispatch] R --> V[Jet Fuel ATK] P --> W[Industrial Coke Market] I --> X[Visbroken Fuel Oil] ``` --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of FCC Unit The FCC is the **highest-yield gasoline unit** in a refinery. Below is the block-architecture of its key sections: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RiserReactor[Reactor Riser Section] F1[Fresh VGO Feed] --> MIX[Feed Nozzle Mixer] H1[Hot Regenerated Catalyst 720 C] --> MIX MIX --> R[Riser Reactor 520 C Contact Time 3 sec] R --> STR[Stripper Steam Stripping] end STR --> G1[Cracked Vapours to Main Fractionator] STR --> SP[Spent Catalyst to Regenerator] SP --> REG[Regenerator Air Coke Burn off] REG --> H2[Hot Catalyst Recycle to Riser] REG --> FG[Flue Gas to CO Boiler for Power] G1 --> FRAC[Main Fractionator] FRAC --> P1[Dry Gas LPG] FRAC --> P2[Gasoline] FRAC --> P3[LCO] FRAC --> P4[Slurry Oil] ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic — Catalytic Reformer (CCR) Block ```mermaid flowchart LR NAPH[Naphtha Feed] --> HX1[Feed Effluent Heat Exchanger] HX1 --> FURN1[Heater 1] FURN1 --> RX1[Reactor 1 Pt Re Catalyst] RX1 --> FURN2[Heater 2] FURN2 --> RX2[Reactor 2] RX2 --> FURN3[Heater 3] FURN3 --> RX3[Reactor 3] RX3 --> RX4[Reactor 4] RX4 --> HX2[Feed Effluent Heat Exchanger] HX2 --> SEP[High Pressure Separator] SEP --> GAS[H2 Rich Recycle Gas] SEP --> LIQ[Reformate Liquid] GAS --> COMP[Recycle Compressor] COMP --> RX1 LIQ --> STAB[Stabilizer Column] STAB --> REFP[Reformate Product RON 100] STAB --> LPGX[LPG Offgas] ``` --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Distillation Tower Mass Transfer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Tower[Crude Distillation Column CDU] TF[Top Reflux Drum] T1[Tray 1 Overhead Naphtha] T2[Tray 5 Kerosene Draw] T3[Tray 15 Diesel Draw] F[Feed Entry Zone Flash Zone] T4[Steam Stripper Below Feed] T5[Bottom Residue] end TF --> T1 T1 --> T2 T2 --> T3 T3 --> F F --> T4 T4 --> T5 F -- Pump Around and Side Strippers --> T1 F -- Pump Around and Side Strippers --> T2 F -- Pump Around and Side Strippers --> T3 ``` # Module 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions below are modelled on **KTU 2024 Scheme Question Paper patterns** (Part A: 3 marks, Part B: 14 marks with internal choice). Tag IDs indicate question pedigree. --- ## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > Differentiate between **catalytic cracking** and **hydrocracking**. Mention any two distinguishing features. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Catalytic Cracking (FCC) | Hydrocracking | |---|---|---| | **Hydrogen requirement** | **No external H₂** — hydrogen is internal (from heavy feed). | **Requires external high-pressure H₂** (70 – 200 bar). | | **Catalyst** | **Solid acid zeolite (USY)** | **Bifunctional Pt/Pd + zeolite** | | **Operating temperature** | **500 – 550 °C** | **320 – 420 °C** | | **Main product** | **Gasoline (high octane)** | **Middle distillates (jet + diesel) + naphtha** | | **Sulfur removal** | **Not removed** (transferred to products) | **Simultaneous HDS** removes S, N, metals | *(Marks: 1 for difference table entries + 1 for any 2 valid distinctions + 1 for proper terminology.)* --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > A crude oil has specific gravity 0.92. Calculate its °API. **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} {}^\circ API &= \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5 \\ &= \dfrac{141.5}{0.92} - 131.5 \\ &= 153.80 - 131.5 \\ &= 22.30 \; {}^\circ API \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation (½ mark):** The crude is classified as **medium crude** (22 – 31 °API). --- ## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided) > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, divided into sub-parts of 7 + 7. Internal choice means **Q1 OR Q2**, and **Q3 OR Q4** must be answered from a typical module question paper. --- ### Part B Question 1 (Question A — Choose this OR Question B) #### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question Paper]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat labelled flow diagram, describe the **Atmospheric and Vacuum Distillation process** in a petroleum refinery. Discuss the operating pressure, feed temperature, and product slate for each. > **(b) [7 Marks]** A refinery processes 8 MMTPA of crude oil (SG = 0.88). The product yields (wt %) are: LPG 2 %, Naphtha 18 %, Kerosene 12 %, Diesel 28 %, VGO from VDU 22 %, Vacuum Residue 18 %. Compute (i) the mass flow rate (kg/h) of each product, and (ii) the daily volume of diesel produced (assume diesel SG = 0.85). --- ### Model Solution — Part B Question 1 (a) #### Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU / CDU) - **Feed:** Desalted crude (water & salts removed in desalter at 110 – 140 °C, electrostatic field 1 – 2 kV/cm). - **Preheating:** Crude is pre-heated in a **preheat train** by recovering heat from hot product streams → reaches ≈ 220 °C. - **Fired Heater:** Brings the crude to **350 – 400 °C** (entry temperature of distillation column). - **Column Pressure:** **1.0 – 1.5 bar (gauge)** — slightly above atmospheric. - **Tower Internals:** Sieve trays + structured packing. - **Side Draws & Sidestrippers:** - Naphtha (top, IBP – 180 °C) - Kerosene (160 – 250 °C) - Light Diesel Oil (250 – 320 °C) - Heavy Diesel Oil (320 – 370 °C) - **Atmospheric Residue** (bottom, > 370 °C) #### Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU) - **Feed:** Atmospheric residue (AR). - **Operating Pressure:** **10 – 50 mmHg absolute** maintained by steam ejectors. - **Feed Heater Outlet T:** ≈ 420 – 430 °C (lower boiling due to vacuum). - **Product Slate:** - **LVGO** (Light Vacuum Gas Oil) — 350 – 400 °C AET - **HVGO** (Heavy Vacuum Gas Oil) — 400 – 550 °C AET - **Vacuum Residue** (> 550 °C AET) — feedstock for coking, bitumen, lubes. *(Mark distribution: 2 marks flow diagram, 2 marks operating conditions, 2 marks product slate, 1 mark terminology.)* --- ### Model Solution — Part B Question 1 (b) **Given:** - Capacity = 8 MMTPA, SG_crude = 0.88 - Diesel SG = 0.85 **Step 1 — Annual throughput → daily mass:** $$ \text{Daily mass} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^9 \; kg}{365} = 21.92 \times 10^6 \; kg/d $$ **Step 2 — Mass flow rate per second (for kg/h form):** $$ \text{Hourly mass} = 21.92 \times 10^6 \times \dfrac{24}{24} \times \dfrac{1}{24} \times 10^3 = 913{,}243 \; kg/h $$ *(Or directly: 8 × 10⁹ kg/yr ÷ 8760 hr = 913,242 kg/h.)* **Step 3 — Mass flow rates of each product:** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{LPG} &= 0.02 \times 913{,}243 = 18{,}265 \; kg/h \\ m_{naphtha} &= 0.18 \times 913{,}243 = 164{,}384 \; kg/h \\ m_{kero} &= 0.12 \times 913{,}243 = 109{,}589 \; kg/h \\ m_{diesel} &= 0.28 \times 913{,}243 = 255{,}708 \; kg/h \\ m_{VGO} &= 0.22 \times 913{,}243 = 200{,}913 \; kg/h \\ m_{VR} &= 0.18 \times 913{,}243 = 164{,}384 \; kg/h \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Daily mass of diesel:** $$ m_{diesel,daily} = 255{,}708 \times 24 = 6.137 \times 10^6 \; kg/d $$ **Step 5 — Daily volume of diesel:** $$ V_{diesel,daily} = \dfrac{m_{diesel,daily}}{\rho_{diesel}} = \dfrac{6.137 \times 10^6}{850} = 7220 \; m^3/d $$ **Step 6 — Convert m³ → barrels:** $$ V = 7220 \times 6.289 = 45{,}405 \; bbl/d \approx 45.4 \; kbpd $$ *(Mark distribution: 2 marks mass balance logic, 2 marks calculations, 1 mark unit conversion, 1 mark final interpretation, 1 mark check.)* --- ### Part B Question 1 — Alternative Choice (Question B) > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) unit** with a labelled diagram. Discuss the role of the regenerator, catalyst-to-oil ratio, and conversion. > **(b) [7 Marks]** An FCC unit processes 10,000 kg/h of VGO. The product slate is: Dry gas 300 kg/h, LPG 1400 kg/h, Gasoline 4800 kg/h, LCO 2200 kg/h, Slurry 1300 kg/h. Calculate (i) the conversion % of VGO and (ii) the gasoline yield on feed. --- ### Model Solution — Part B Question 1B (a) **Working Principle of FCC:** - **Preheated VGO (≈ 350 °C)** is injected at the **base of the riser**. - **Hot regenerated catalyst (≈ 720 °C)** from the regenerator is fed simultaneously → contact time 2 – 4 seconds, mixture at 500 – 550 °C. - **Cracking reactions** (carbocation mechanism) convert long-chain paraffins → iso-paraffins + olefins + aromatics + gasoline. - **Spent catalyst** carries **coke (≈ 0.5 – 1.0 wt %)** is steam-stripped → sent to regenerator. - **Regenerator:** Air burns off coke at 680 – 730 °C, restoring catalyst activity. **Flue gas** drives a CO boiler (waste-heat recovery). - **Regenerated catalyst** is lifted by air back to the riser base. **Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O):** $$ R_{C/O} = \dfrac{m_{cat}}{m_{oil}} = 5 \; \text{to} \; 8 \; (\text{w/w}) $$ - Higher C/O → higher temperature → higher conversion → lower coke on catalyst. **Conversion:** $$ X_{FCC} = \dfrac{F - (LCO + Slurry)}{F} \times 100 $$ Typical **65 – 80 %** in modern riser design. *(Marks: 3 diagram explanation, 2 role of regenerator, 1 C/O definition, 1 conversion formula.)* --- ### Model Solution — Part B Question 1B (b) **Step 1 — Identify unconverted stream:** $$ U = m_{LCO} + m_{slurry} = 2200 + 1300 = 3500 \; kg/h $$ **Step 2 — Conversion:** $$ \begin{aligned} X_{FCC} &= \dfrac{10{,}000 - 3500}{10{,}000} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{6500}{10{,}000} \times 100 \\ &= 65 \; \% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Gasoline Yield:** $$ Y_{gasoline} = \dfrac{4800}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 48 \; \% $$ *(Marks: 2 unconverted logic, 3 conversion formula & arithmetic, 2 yield calculation.)* --- ### Part B Question 2 (Question A — Choose this OR Question B) > **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **Catalytic Reforming process**. Discuss the reactions occurring, catalyst used, and product quality in terms of RON. > **(b) [7 Marks]** A naphtha feed to a catalytic reformer has a RON of 60. After reforming, the reformate has a RON of 98. If the reformate yield is 85 wt % of feed, calculate (i) the octane-barrel contribution and (ii) the research octane uplift per kg feed. --- ### Model Solution — Part B Question 2 (a) **Catalytic Reforming — Flow & Function:** - **Feed:** Heavy naphtha (C₆ – C₁₁, 80 – 200 °C). - **Catalyst:** **Pt (0.3 – 0.6 wt %) + Re (or Sn) on chlorided γ-Al₂O₃**. - **Reactor:** 3 or 4 adiabatic fixed beds (CCR uses moving bed). - **Pressure:** 3 – 35 bar (CCR: 3 – 5 bar for higher aromatics yield). - **Temperature:** 480 – 525 °C. **Reactions:** | Reaction | Enthalpy | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Naphthenes → Aromatics (dehydrogenation) | Endothermic | **Largest RON contributor** | | Paraffins → Isoparaffins | Mildly exothermic | Octane uplift | | Paraffins → Aromatics (dehydrocyclization) | Endothermic | Octane uplift | | Hydrocracking (C₅⁻ → LPG) | Exothermic | LPG byproduct | **Product:** Reformate with **RON 95 – 102**, primary gasoline blendstock. **Hydrogen byproduct:** **150 – 250 Nm³ H₂ per m³ feed** — recovered for hydrotreaters. *(Marks: 2 flow diagram, 2 reactions, 2 catalyst + RON quality, 1 byproduct.)* --- ### Model Solution — Part B Question 2 (b) **Given:** - $RON_{feed} = 60$, $RON_{reformate} = 98$, Yield $Y = 85 \; wt \%$ **Step 1 — Basis:** Take 100 kg feed. Mass of reformate = 85 kg. **Step 2 — Octane-Barrel Contribution:** Octane-barrel = $RON \times \text{Volume (bbl)}$. Using mass as proxy (since density approx constant): $$ OB_{reformate} = 98 \times 85 = 8330 \; \text{(octane-kg)} $$ Octane-barrel of feed = $60 \times 100 = 6000$. **Octane uplift (on feed basis):** $$ \Delta OB = 8330 - 6000 = 2330 \; \text{octane-kg per 100 kg feed} $$ $$ \Delta OB / \text{kg feed} = 23.3 $$ **Step 3 — RON uplift per kg feed:** $$ \Delta RON_{effective} = \dfrac{2330}{100} = 23.3 \; \text{RON-equivalent units per kg feed} $$ *(Marks: 3 yield logic, 2 octane-barrel definition, 2 final calculation.)* --- ### Part B Question 2 — Alternative (Question B) > **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** of diesel. Discuss catalyst, operating conditions, and reaction stoichiometry for sulfur removal. > **(b) [7 Marks]** A diesel feed contains 800 ppm sulfur (by mass). After HDS, sulfur drops to 15 ppm. For 1,00,000 kg/h feed, calculate (i) mass of sulfur removed per hour, and (ii) volume of H₂S produced per hour at STP (assume S → H₂S stoichiometry, molar masses: S = 32, H₂S = 34, H₂ = 2, density H₂S at STP = 1.539 kg/m³). --- ### Model Solution — Part B Question 2B (a) **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) Process:** - **Purpose:** Remove organic sulfur (thiophenes, benzothiophenes, dibenzothiophenes) from diesel/kerosene/gasoline. - **Catalyst:** **Co-Mo or Ni-Mo on γ-Al₂O₃**, sulfided form (active phase is MoS₂). - **Reactor:** Trickle-bed, downflow, fixed bed. - **Operating Conditions:** - Temperature: 320 – 380 °C - Pressure: 30 – 100 bar - LHSV: 1 – 4 h⁻¹ - H₂ / oil ratio: 100 – 300 Nm³/m³ **Reaction stoichiometry (dibenzothiophene example):** $$ C_{12}H_{8}S + 2 \, H_2 \longrightarrow C_{12}H_{10} + H_2S $$ **Mechanism:** Sulfur atom is **abstracted by H₂** to form H₂S, hydrocarbon is hydrogenated. *(Marks: 2 process description, 2 catalyst + conditions, 2 stoichiometry, 1 mechanism.)* --- ### Model Solution — Part B Question 2B (b) **Given:** - $S_{in} = 800 \; ppm$, $S_{out} = 15 \; ppm$, Feed = 1,00,000 kg/h. **Step 1 — Mass of S in (kg/h):** $$ m_{S,in} = 800 \times 10^{-6} \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 80 \; kg/h $$ **Step 2 — Mass of S out:** $$ m_{S,out} = 15 \times 10^{-6} \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 1.5 \; kg/h $$ **Step 3 — Mass of S removed:** $$ m_{S,removed} = 80 - 1.5 = 78.5 \; kg/h $$ **Step 4 — Moles of H₂S produced = moles of S removed:** $$ n_{H_2S} = \dfrac{78.5}{32} = 2.453 \; kmol/h $$ **Step 5 — Mass of H₂S:** $$ m_{H_2S} = 2.453 \times 34 = 83.4 \; kg/h $$ **Step 6 — Volume of H₂S at STP:** $$ V_{H_2S} = \dfrac{83.4}{1.539} = 54.2 \; m^3/h \; \text{(at STP)} $$ *(Marks: 2 mass calculations, 2 stoichiometry, 2 final volume + unit conversion, 1 interpretation.)* --- ## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students typically lose marks in Module 2:** > > 1. **Confusing °API with °Baumé** — they are NOT interchangeable. °API uses 141.5 / 131.5; °Baumé for liquids lighter than water uses a different pair of constants. (−1 mark deduction.) > > 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to °Rankine** in the Watson K-factor calculation. Always convert to °R, never use °C directly. (−1 mark.) > > 3. **In FCC conversion problems, treating LCO + Slurry as feed** — they are products. Only VGO is feed. The unconverted fraction is (LCO + Slurry). (−2 marks typical.) > > 4. **Using thermal-cracking analogies for catalytic cracking** — catalytic cracking uses **carbocation mechanism**, not free-radical. Mixing the two is a valuation red flag. (−1 mark.) > > 5. **Skipping units in mass-balance closure checks** — KTU examiners specifically check for **kg/h consistency**. Always state units. > > 6. **Forgetting inter-stage H₂ compressor** in catalytic reformer diagrams — it is mandatory for recycle and is a **frequently-asked diagram component.** --- ## 5.2 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density summary for the night-before-the-exam revision.** ### A. Core Process Knowledge - ☐ **ADU (CDU)** operates at **1–2 bar**, separates crude into **LPG, naphtha, kero, diesel, AR**. - ☐ **VDU** operates at **10–50 mmHg**, separates AR into **VGO + VR**. - ☐ **FCC** uses **zeolite catalyst**, riser at **520 °C**, C/O ratio **5–8**, conversion **65–80 %**. - ☐ **Hydrocracker** uses **bifunctional catalyst + H₂ at 100 bar**, produces mid-distillates. - ☐ **Catalytic reformer** uses **Pt-Re/γ-Al₂O₃**, produces **reformate (RON 95–102) + H₂**. - ☐ **Alkylation** uses **H₂SO₄ or HF**, produces **alkylate (RON 92–96)**. - ☐ **Isomerization** upgrades **n-C₅/C₆ → iso-C₅/C₆** (RON uplift 20+ units). - ☐ **Delayed coking** converts VR → **coke + coker naphtha/diesel**. - ☐ **HDS** uses **Co-Mo/Al₂O₃ (sulfided)** under **30–100 bar H₂**. - ☐ **Visbreaking** is mild thermal cracking of VR to lower viscosity. ### B. Critical Formulas to Memorize - ☐ ${}^\circ API = 141.5/SG - 131.5$ - ☐ $K_W = (T_B)^{1/3}/SG$ (T in °R) - ☐ $X = (F-U)/F \times 100$ - ☐ $Y = m_{product}/m_{feed} \times 100$ - ☐ $R_{C/O} = m_{cat}/m_{oil}$ - ☐ $\rho = 141.5 \times 1000/({}^\circ API + 131.5)$ kg/m³ - ☐ 1 bbl = 159 L; 1 m³ = 6.289 bbl ### C. Pitfalls to Avoid - ☐ Don't confuse °API and °Baumé. - ☐ Don't forget °R conversion for Watson K. - ☐ Don't classify LCO + Slurry as feed in FCC. - ☐ Don't say "free-radical" when describing FCC — use "carbocation". ### D. KTU 2024 High-Yield Numerical Templates - ☐ API ↔ SG ↔ Density interconversion. - ☐ Mass balance over ADU/VDU. - ☐ FCC conversion + gasoline yield. - ☐ Watson K-factor calculation. - ☐ Refinery utilization → Mbpd conversion. - ☐ HDS mass balance + H₂S stoichiometry. - ☐ Catalytic reformer octane-barrel uplift. --- > **End of Module 2 — Petroleum Refining Processes** > *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, mapped to OECHT832 — Fuel Engineering, 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned).*

topics.title: Transportation
Storage and Handling of Liquid Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage and Handling of Liquid Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Liquid Fuel Storage and Handling** refers to the comprehensive engineering discipline encompassing the safe containment, preservation, transfer, and dispensing of petroleum-derived liquid fuels (crude oil, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, furnace oil, LDO, HSD, naphtha, and aviation turbine fuel) from the point of refinery production through distribution terminals to the end consumer. This field integrates principles of **mechanical engineering** (tank structural design), **chemical engineering** (corrosion, vapor space chemistry), **safety engineering** (fire/explosion prevention), and **environmental engineering** (vapor recovery, spill containment).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Storage and Handling of Liquid Fuels involves the study of various types of storage tanks (cylindrical, rectangular, underground, above-ground), materials of construction, design codes, fire prevention measures, vapor recovery systems, spillage control, and transportation methodologies required to maintain fuel quality and ensure operational safety throughout the supply chain."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine storing a temperamental liquid at home — like honey. You wouldn't just pour it into any open bucket and leave it outside. You would:
1. **Choose the right container** (glass jar with tight lid, not paper cup).
2. **Place it in a cool, dark place** (to prevent fermentation/oxidation).
3. **Keep it away from flames** (kitchen stove, candle).
4. **Have a backup tray** under the jar (in case it leaks).
5. **Use a funnel when transferring** (no spillage).

**Liquid fuel storage works on the exact same logic — but at industrial scale with hazardous, flammable, volatile liquids.** A tiny spark, leak, or temperature fluctuation can cause explosions, fires, environmental disasters, and economic losses worth crores of rupees. Hence, KTU emphasizes this topic heavily in Module 2.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **Flash Point of Gasoline:** $< 0°C$ (Class A flammable liquid)
- **Flash Point of Kerosene:** $38°C - 72°C$ (Class C combustible liquid)
- **Flash Point of Diesel:** $52°C - 96°C$ (Class D combustible liquid)
- **Flash Point of Furnace Oil:** $> 100°C$ (Class B combustible liquid)
