# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Stoichiometric Hydrogen Demand for Coal Liquefaction (Bergius)

**Problem Statement:** A bituminous coal has an empirical formula **C₁₀₀H₆₀O₁₀N₁** (ash-free basis). Calculate the **minimum mass of H₂** required to convert **1 kg of this coal** into a paraffinic liquid product of average composition **C₂₀H₄₂** (representing a C₂₀ alkane, eicosane).

**Step 1 — Set up the hydrogen balance for the coal-to-alkane conversion.**

We treat the coal monomer (C₁₀₀H₆₀O₁₀N₁) as reacting with hydrogen. The heteroatoms (O, N) are removed as H₂O and NH₃, and the carbon skeleton is split into C₂₀ units. We focus on the **hydrogen atoms needed** beyond what is already in the coal.

**Step 2 — Coal monomer molecular weight.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{coal}} &= 100(12) + 60(1) + 10(16) + 1(14) \\
&= 1200 + 60 + 160 + 14 \\
&= 1434 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — H atoms in the final alkane product.**

The coal has 100 C atoms. Splitting 100 C atoms into C₂₀ units gives:

$$N_{\text{C}_{20}} = \frac{100}{20} = 5 \text{ molecules of C}_{20}\text{H}_{42}$$

Total H atoms in 5 molecules of C₂₀H₄₂:

$$H_{\text{product}} = 5 \times 42 = 210 \text{ H atoms}$$

**Step 4 — H atoms already present in coal.**

$$H_{\text{coal}} = 60 \text{ H atoms}$$

**Step 5 — Net H atoms needed (per coal monomer).**

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{product}} - H_{\text{coal}} = 210 - 60 = 150 \text{ H atoms}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to moles of H₂.**

Each H₂ molecule contributes 2 H atoms, so:

$$n_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{150}{2} = 75 \text{ mol of H}_2 \text{ per mole of coal}$$

**Step 7 — Mass of H₂ per mole of coal.**

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = 75 \times 2 = 150 \text{ g H}_2 \text{ per 1434 g coal}$$

**Step 8 — Scale to 1 kg (1000 g) of coal.**

$$m_{\text{H}_2, \text{per kg coal}} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{1434 \text{ g/mol}} \times 150 \text{ g H}_2 = 104.6 \text{ g H}_2$$

$$\boxed{\,m_{\text{H}_2} \approx 105 \text{ g H}_2 \text{ per kg of coal}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Bergius process consumes roughly **100–110 g of H₂ per kg of coal** in practice. This enormous hydrogen demand is why Bergius plants require a dedicated **hydrogen manufacturing unit** (usually by partial oxidation of coal residues or natural gas reforming).

---

## 3.2 Stoichiometric CO and H₂ Balance for Fischer–Tropsch

**Problem Statement:** A syngas stream contains **60 mol% CO** and **40 mol% H₂**. We wish to use this feed for FT synthesis producing C₁₀H₂₂ (decane, paraffin). Determine the **limiting reagent** and the **moles of decane** formed per 100 moles of feed.

**Step 1 — Moles in feed.**

For 100 mol of feed:
- $n_{\text{CO}} = 60$ mol
- $n_{\text{H}_2} = 40$ mol

**Step 2 — Required stoichiometry for C₁₀H₂₂.**

Reaction:

$$10\,\text{CO} + 21\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{22} + 10\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Per mole of decane: 10 mol CO and 21 mol H₂ are required.

**Step 3 — Moles of decane if CO is limiting.**

$$n_{\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{22}}^{\text{CO-limit}} = \frac{60}{10} = 6.0 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Moles of decane if H₂ is limiting.**

$$n_{\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{22}}^{\text{H}_2\text{-limit}} = \frac{40}{21} = 1.905 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the limiting reagent.**

Since $1.905 < 6.0$, **H₂ is the limiting reagent**.

**Step 6 — Moles of decane formed.**

$$\boxed{\,n_{\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{22}} = 1.905 \text{ mol per 100 mol of feed}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Excess CO remaining.**

H₂ consumed = 40 mol; CO consumed = $10 \times 1.905 = 19.05$ mol.

$$n_{\text{CO,excess}} = 60 - 19.05 = 40.95 \text{ mol}$$

This excess CO is typically **recycled** or sent to a **WGS reactor** to generate more H₂.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark-Winning Note:** Always compute the limiting reagent explicitly in FT stoichiometric problems. The conventional FT H₂:CO stoichiometric ratio for paraffin formation is $(2n+1)/n$. For gasoline range ($n \approx 8$), this is $17/8 \approx 2.125$.

---

## 3.3 Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) Distribution — Worked Example

**Problem Statement:** A cobalt FT catalyst produces a product mixture where the **chain growth probability is α = 0.80**. Calculate the **mass fraction of gasoline (C₅–C₁₁)**, **diesel (C₁₂–C₁₈)**, and **wax (C₁₉+)** in the product stream using the ASF equation.

**Step 1 — ASF mass fraction equation.**

$$\frac{W_n}{n} = (1-\alpha)^2 \cdot \alpha^{n-1}$$

Mass fraction of a single carbon number $n$:

$$W_n = n(1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate per-carbon-number Wₙ (α = 0.80, so 1 − α = 0.20).**

$$W_n = n \times (0.20)^2 \times (0.80)^{n-1} = 0.04 \, n \, (0.80)^{n-1}$$

| n | Calculation | Wₙ |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | $0.04 \times 5 \times (0.80)^4 = 0.20 \times 0.4096$ | **0.0819** |
| 6 | $0.04 \times 6 \times (0.80)^5 = 0.24 \times 0.3277$ | **0.0786** |
| 7 | $0.04 \times 7 \times (0.80)^6 = 0.28 \times 0.2621$ | **0.0734** |
| 8 | $0.04 \times 8 \times (0.80)^7 = 0.32 \times 0.2097$ | **0.0671** |
| 9 | $0.04 \times 9 \times (0.80)^8 = 0.36 \times 0.1678$ | **0.0604** |
| 10 | $0.04 \times 10 \times (0.80)^9 = 0.40 \times 0.1342$ | **0.0537** |
| 11 | $0.04 \times 11 \times (0.80)^{10} = 0.44 \times 0.1074$ | **0.0472** |
| 12 | $0.04 \times 12 \times (0.80)^{11} = 0.48 \times 0.0859$ | **0.0412** |
| 18 | $0.04 \times 18 \times (0.80)^{17} = 0.72 \times 0.0225$ | **0.0162** |
| 19+ | (sum of n=19 to ∞) | (computed below) |

**Step 3 — Sum ranges.**

**Gasoline (C₅–C₁₁):**

$$W_{\text{gas}} = \sum_{n=5}^{11} W_n = 0.0819+0.0786+0.0734+0.0671+0.0604+0.0537+0.0472 = 0.4623$$

**Diesel (C₁₂–C₁₈):** We compute selected values and use geometric series shortcut.

$$W_{12}=0.0412,\; W_{13}\approx 0.0353,\; W_{14}\approx 0.0302,\; W_{15}\approx 0.0257,\; W_{16}\approx 0.0218,\; W_{17}\approx 0.0185,\; W_{18}\approx 0.0162$$

$$W_{\text{diesel}} = 0.0412+0.0353+0.0302+0.0257+0.0218+0.0185+0.0162 = 0.1889$$

**Wax (C₁₉+):**

$$W_{\text{wax}} = 1 - W_{\text{gas}} - W_{\text{diesel}} = 1 - 0.4623 - 0.1889 = 0.3488$$

**Step 4 — Results.**

$$\boxed{\,W_{\text{gasoline}} \approx 46.2\%,\quad W_{\text{diesel}} \approx 18.9\%,\quad W_{\text{wax}} \approx 34.9\%\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** With α = 0.80, the FT product distribution is **wax-heavy** (~35%), which is exactly why industrial FT plants (e.g., Sasol's Arge reactor) include a downstream **hydrocracker** to convert wax into diesel. Increasing temperature or decreasing pressure lowers α → more gasoline, but at the cost of higher methane selectivity.

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Differentiate between direct and indirect coal liquefaction with examples."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Direct Liquefaction (Bergius) | Indirect Liquefaction (Fischer–Tropsch) |
|---|---|---|
| Pathway | Coal reacts directly with H₂ | Coal → syngas → liquid fuels |
| Pressure | Very high (200–700 atm) | Moderate (1–40 atm) |
| Temperature | 450–500 °C | 200–350 °C |
| Catalyst | FeS, MoS₂, SnCl₂ | Fe, Co, Ru |
| Process steps | Single reactor train | Gasifier + FT reactor |
| Example product | Aromatic gasoline | Paraffinic diesel/wax |

**Marking split (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Two differentiating points (1) + Example (1).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"What is syngas? State the desired H₂:CO ratio for Fischer–Tropsch synthesis."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Syngas (synthesis gas)** is a gaseous mixture of carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H₂) produced by the gasification of coal, natural gas, or biomass.

$$2\text{C} + \text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2$$

**Desired H₂:CO ratio for classical FT paraffin synthesis:**

For a generic paraffin CₙH₂ₙ₊₂, the stoichiometric H₂:CO ratio is:

$$\frac{\text{H}_2}{\text{CO}} = \frac{2n+1}{n}$$

For gasoline range ($n \approx 8$): ratio ≈ **2.1 : 1**.

**Marking split (3 marks):** Definition with equation (2) + Ratio (1).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **Bergius process** for the production of liquid fuels from coal. Discuss the operating conditions, catalysts, and chemistry involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coal sample has the empirical formula **C₁₀₀H₅₈O₈N₁**. Calculate the **minimum mass of hydrogen gas** required to convert **1 kg of this coal** into a paraffinic product with average composition **C₂₀H₄₂** (eicosane). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Bergius Process Description (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Bergius process is a **direct coal liquefaction** technique developed by Friedrich Bergius in 1913, in which powdered coal is reacted with molecular hydrogen at high pressure and temperature in the presence of a catalyst to yield liquid hydrocarbon fuels.

**Flow Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 above (Bergius block flow).

**Operating Conditions (2 marks):**
- **Pressure:** 200–700 atm
- **Temperature:** 450–500 °C
- **Catalyst:** Iron sulfide (FeS), molybdenum disulfide (MoS₂), or tin chloride (SnCl₂)
- **Coal form:** Pulverized, < 200 mesh, slurried in heavy recycle oil

**Chemistry (2 marks):**
$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{0.8n} + (n+1)\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{cat., P, T}} \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$$
Coal + Hydrogen → Paraffins + Aromatics + Heteroatom residues

**Key features (1 mark):** Single-stage direct conversion; requires dedicated H₂ plant; high thermal efficiency (~70%); products require downstream upgrading to remove S, N.

**Part (b) — Stoichiometric Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Coal molecular weight:**
$$M_{\text{coal}} = 100(12) + 58(1) + 8(16) + 1(14) = 1200 + 58 + 128 + 14 = 1400 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Number of C₂₀H₄₂ molecules from one coal molecule:**
$$N_{\text{C}_{20}} = \frac{100}{20} = 5 \text{ molecules}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — H atoms in product:**
$$H_{\text{product}} = 5 \times 42 = 210 \text{ H atoms}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — H atoms deficit:**
$$\Delta H = 210 - 58 = 152 \text{ H atoms needed}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Moles of H₂ needed per mole of coal:**
$$n_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{152}{2} = 76 \text{ mol H}_2$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Mass of H₂ per mole of coal:**
$$m_{\text{H}_2} = 76 \times 2 = 152 \text{ g H}_2$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Scale to 1 kg coal:**
$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{1000}{1400} \times 152 = 108.6 \text{ g H}_2$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{Minimum H}_2 \text{ required} \approx 108.6 \text{ g per kg of coal}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):**
> Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting to convert from H atoms to H₂ molecules (divide by 2).
> 2. Forgetting the ash/impurity factor — KTU sometimes gives a dry-ash-free basis only; state the assumption explicitly.
> 3. Not stating the assumption that heteroatoms (O, N) are removed as H₂O/NH₃ and don't count toward H balance in the alkane product.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — *Internal Choice for Question A*
**(a)** Explain the **Fischer–Tropsch (FT) process** for the production of synthetic liquid fuels from coal. Discuss the syngas preparation, FT reactor chemistry, and the **Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) product distribution**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A syngas feed has the composition **70 mol% H₂ and 30 mol% CO**. The feed is sent to an FT reactor producing C₁₂H₂₆ (dodecane, paraffin). Determine: **(i)** the limiting reagent, **(ii)** the moles of dodecane produced per 100 mol of feed, and **(iii)** the **chain growth probability α** required to maximize gasoline (C₅–C₁₁) selectivity based on the ASF distribution. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Fischer–Tropsch Process (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** FT is an **indirect coal liquefaction** technique developed by Fischer and Tropsch (1923), in which coal is first gasified to syngas (CO + H₂), and the syngas is then catalytically converted to liquid hydrocarbons.

**Syngas Preparation (2 marks):**
- **Gasification reactions:**
$$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(steam gasification, endothermic)}$$
$$\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO} \quad \text{(partial oxidation, exothermic)}$$
- **WGS adjustment:**
$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$$
- **Purification:** Removal of H₂S, COS, particulates to protect FT catalyst.

**FT Reactor Chemistry (2 marks):**
$$n\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Fe or Co, 200-350°C}} \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Catalysts: **Fe** (cheap, robust), **Co** (high activity, long life), **Ru** (premium, highest activity).

**ASF Distribution (2 marks):**

$$\frac{W_n}{n} = (1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$$

- $\alpha$ = chain growth probability (0 < α < 1).
- Higher α → heavier products (wax); lower α → lighter products (gasoline/methane).
- The ASF distribution is a *fundamental limitation* of FT — product selectivity cannot be tuned sharply to a single fraction.

**Part (b) — Stoichiometric + ASF Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Moles in feed (per 100 mol):**
$n_{\text{H}_2} = 70$ mol; $n_{\text{CO}} = 30$ mol

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Stoichiometry for C₁₂H₂₆:**
$$12\,\text{CO} + 25\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{26} + 12\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Limiting reagent check:**
- If CO limits: $n_{\text{C}_{12}} = 30/12 = 2.50$ mol
- If H₂ limits: $n_{\text{C}_{12}} = 70/25 = 2.80$ mol
- **CO is limiting** (smaller yield).

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Moles of dodecane formed:**
$$\boxed{\,n_{\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{26}} = 2.50 \text{ mol per 100 mol feed}\,}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Stoichiometric H₂:CO ratio required for C₁₂H₂₆:**
$$\left(\frac{\text{H}_2}{\text{CO}}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{25}{12} = 2.083$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Optimum α for gasoline maximization:**

The gasoline selectivity (C₅–C₁₁) from ASF is maximized when the **modal carbon number** is near C₇–C₈. Using the ASF modal condition $n_{\text{mode}} = \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$:

For $n_{\text{mode}} = 8$:

$$\alpha = \frac{n_{\text{mode}}}{1 + n_{\text{mode}}} = \frac{8}{9} = 0.889$$

Or alternatively, for $n_{\text{mode}} = 7$:

$$\alpha = \frac{7}{8} = 0.875$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\,\alpha_{\text{optimum}} \approx 0.875 - 0.89 \text{ for maximum gasoline selectivity}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):**
> 1. **Do not confuse** "limiting reagent" with "stoichiometric requirement" — compute both and compare.
> 2. The **ASF modal formula** $n_{\text{mode}} = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$ is the fastest way to identify the dominant carbon number. Don't derive from scratch in the exam; state and apply it.
> 3. **Real FT gasoline** has selectivity capped around 50% — students who claim 80–90% gasoline selectivity for FT are penalized. The ASF distribution is intrinsically broad.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 5 Pitfalls on Coal Liquefaction Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to balance the FT reaction.** Always show $n\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2$ for paraffins and $n\text{CO} + 2n\text{H}_2$ for olefins explicitly.
>
> 2. **Confusing Bergius and FT pressures.** Bergius = **200–700 atm**; FT = **1–40 atm**. Examiners explicitly test this.
>
> 3. **Skipping the syngas step in FT answers.** Writing "FT converts coal to liquid fuels" without mentioning the **gasification → syngas → FT reactor** sequence loses 2 marks easily.
>
> 4. **Not stating catalyst types.** FT (Fe/Co/Ru) and Bergius (FeS/MoS₂) — always include.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to apply the ASF distribution.** If the question asks about FT product selectivity, *mention* the ASF equation $W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2\alpha^{n-1}$ — even qualitatively.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Bergius & Fischer–Tropsch**
>
> ✅ Bergius = **Direct** hydrogenation; FT = **Indirect** via syngas.
>
> ✅ Bergius pressure: **200–700 atm**; FT pressure: **1–40 atm**.
>
> ✅ Bergius temperature: **450–500 °C**; FT temperature: **200–350 °C**.
>
> ✅ Bergius catalyst: **FeS, MoS₂, SnCl₂**; FT catalyst: **Fe, Co, Ru**.
>
> ✅ Bergius equation: Coal + H₂ → paraffins + aromatics.
>
> ✅ FT equation: $n\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \to \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
>
> ✅ WGS reaction: $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ adjusts H₂:CO ratio.
>
> ✅ ASF distribution: $W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$ governs FT selectivity.
>
> ✅ Higher α → heavier products (wax); lower α → methane/gasoline.
>
> ✅ Modal carbon number: $n_{\text{mode}} = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$.
>
> ✅ H/C ratio of coal ≈ 0.8; liquid fuel needs ≈ 1.8–2.0 (hydrogen enrichment is the goal).
>
> ✅ Bergius was a **Nobel Prize** winner (1931); FT was commercialized by **Sasol** (South Africa).
>
> ✅ FT diesel has very high cetane number (~70+) and near-zero sulfur.
>
> ✅ Hydrogen demand in Bergius ≈ 100–110 g H₂ per kg of coal.
>
> ✅ Bergius products are aromatic-rich; FT products are paraffinic.
>
> ✅ Coal-to-Liquids (CTL), Gas-to-Liquids (GTL), and Biomass-to-Liquids (BTL) are all FT-based industrial applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Synthetic Liquid Fuels (Benzol, Shale Oil, Gashol, Power Alcohol, Colloidal Fuel)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Other Synthetic Liquid Fuels (Benzol, Shale Oil, Gashol, Power Alcohol, Colloidal Fuel)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Synthetic Liquid Fuels

**Synthetic liquid fuels** are artificially manufactured liquid hydrocarbons or fuel mixtures produced from raw materials (coal, oil shale, biomass, natural gas) that are *not* conventional crude petroleum. They serve as substitutes or extenders for gasoline and diesel when petroleum reserves are scarce or when strategic energy diversification is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> Synthetic liquid fuels are liquid fuels derived from sources other than crude oil, including by-products of coal carbonisation (Benzol), pyrolysis of oil-bearing rocks (Shale Oil), alcohol-based fuel blends (Gashol, Power Alcohol), and coal-oil suspensions (Colloidal Fuel).

---

## 1.2 Benzol

### Formal Definition
**Benzol** is a mixture of aromatic hydrocarbons — primarily **benzene (C₆H₆)**, **toluene (C₇H₈)**, and **xylene (C₈H₁₀)** — recovered as a by-product during the carbonisation of coal (either in **coke ovens** or **gas works**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composition (typical):**
> - Benzene: **70 – 80%**
> - Toluene: **12 – 15%**
> - Xylene: **2 – 5%**
> - Other aromatics & impurities: balance

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of Benzol as the *"perfume of the coal industry."* When coal is heated in the absence of air (carbonisation) to make coke or town gas, the volatile aromatic vapours that escape are like the "aroma" of coal. If you condense and purify them, you get Benzol — a clear, aromatic liquid fuel far cleaner than the original coal.

### Recovery Methods
1. **Coke Oven Light Oil Recovery** – The gas leaving coke ovens is passed through a tar extractor, then through a **wash oil (creosote oil)** scrubber where aromatics are absorbed. The rich oil is then steam-stripped to release crude benzol.
2. **Activated Carbon Adsorption** – Used in small gas works.
3. **Oil Scrubbing + Distillation** – The crude benzol is fractionally distilled to yield:
   - **90% Benzol** (bp ~ 80 °C, mainly benzene)
   - **Pure Benzol** (99% benzene)
   - **Toluol** (bp ~ 110 °C)
   - **Xylol** (bp ~ 140 °C)
   - **Solvent Naphtha** (residue)

### Properties
- Colour: **Pale yellow to water-white**
- Odour: Characteristic aromatic smell
- Calorific value: **~ 40 MJ/kg** (much higher than gasoline)
- Knocking resistance: Excellent (high octane number ~ 100)

---

## 1.3 Shale Oil

### Formal Definition
**Shale Oil** (also called *kerogen oil* or *retort oil*) is the liquid fuel obtained by the **destructive distillation (pyrolysis)** of **oil shale** — a fine-grained sedimentary rock rich in **kerogen**, a solid, waxy organic precursor to petroleum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Source Rock:** Oil shale contains kerogen (a solid, insoluble organic polymer of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulphur) trapped within its mineral matrix (carbonates, silicates, clays).

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **sponge cake soaked in solidified butter**. The cake (rock matrix) holds the butter (kerogen) tightly. If you simply squeeze the cake, you cannot extract the butter — but if you *heat* the cake strongly in a closed oven (retort), the butter melts and drips out as a thick, dark oil. That oil is Shale Oil.

### Production Process
1. **Mining** → Oil shale is extracted (open-pit or underground).
2. **Crushing** → Size reduction to 5–75 mm.
3. **Retorting (Pyrolysis at 450–550 °C in absence of air)** → Kerogen decomposes into:
   - Shale oil (liquid, ~ 60–70%)
   - Shale gas (CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, H₂S)
   - Spent shale (solid residue)
4. **Refining** → The crude shale oil is fractionated and upgraded (hydrotreating) to yield gasoline, diesel, kerosene, jet fuel.

### Properties
- API gravity: **20 – 25** (heavier than crude petroleum)
- Sulphur content: **0.5 – 1.5 wt%** (higher than crude → corrosive)
- Nitrogen content: **1 – 2 wt%** (high)
- Calorific value: **~ 40–42 MJ/kg**

---

## 1.4 Gashol

### Formal Definition
**Gashol** is a **blended motor fuel** consisting of **gasoline + anhydrous ethanol**, typically in the ratio **90% gasoline : 10% ethanol (E10)**. Higher blends (E20, E85) are also marketed depending on engine compatibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Ethanol used in Gashol must be **anhydrous (denatured)** — water content must be below **0.5%** to prevent phase separation with gasoline.

### Intuitive Analogy
Gashol is like adding **a shot of antiseptic (ethanol) to a soft drink (gasoline)**. The ethanol boosts octane, reduces the drink's "sugar cost" (petroleum dependence), and burns cleaner — but if you add too much water (regular ethanol), the drink separates into two layers (phase separation).

### Production
1. **Fermentation** of sugar cane, maize, or molasses → dilute ethanol (8–12%).
2. **Distillation** → rectified spirit (~ 95.6%, the azeotrope).
3. **Azeotropic Dehydration** (using benzene or molecular sieves) → anhydrous ethanol (99.5%+).
4. **Denaturation** → addition of ~ 2–5% methanol or gasoline to make it unfit for drinking (tax-exempt).
5. **Blending** → mixed with gasoline at terminal; **"splash blending"** or in-line blending.

### Properties (E10)
- Octane number (RON): **~ 95–98**
- Oxygen content: **~ 3.5 wt%** (oxygenates fuel)
- Calorific value: **~ 33 MJ/kg** (lower than pure gasoline, ~ 10% drop)
- Reid Vapour Pressure: slightly **higher** than gasoline (ethanol is volatile).

---

## 1.5 Power Alcohol

### Formal Definition
**Power Alcohol** is **rectified, anhydrous, denatured ethyl alcohol** (C₂H₅OH) used as a **fuel for internal combustion engines**, either neat or in blends with gasoline. In many countries it is also called **Ethanol Fuel** or **Methylated Spirit** (when denatured with methanol).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When blended with gasoline (typically 20–25% alcohol), it is referred to as **Power Alcohol**; the neat anhydrous form (>99.5%) used in flexible-fuel vehicles is called **E100 / Hydrous Ethanol (E95)** in Brazil-style programmes.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of Power Alcohol as the **"country liquor" of the fuel world** — produced locally from farm produce, distributed like gasoline, and used to power tractors, cars, and even aircraft (in Brazil, Embraer Ipanema). It is essentially **sunshine converted to liquid energy** through photosynthesis → fermentation → distillation.

### Manufacturing Steps
1. **Raw Material** → Sugarcane, sugar beet, sweet sorghum, maize, cassava, molasses, or even lignocellulosic biomass.
2. **Fermentation** with *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* yeast:
   $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$
3. **Distillation** → 95.6% azeotrope.
4. **Dehydration** → Azeotropic distillation with benzene / diethyl ether, or pressure-swing adsorption with **3Å molecular sieves** → 99.5–99.8% ethanol.
5. **Denaturation** → Addition of 2–5% methanol, isopropanol, methyl isobutyl ketone, or denatonium benzoate to make it non-potable.

### Properties
- Calorific value: **~ 29.7 MJ/kg** (about **60% of gasoline**)
- Latent heat of vaporisation: very high (~ 904 kJ/kg) → **excellent charge cooling**
- Octane number (RON): **~ 108** (very high)
- Flames are **colourless** (invisible flame) → safety hazard.

---

## 1.6 Colloidal Fuel

### Formal Definition
**Colloidal Fuel** is a **heterogeneous suspension of finely pulverised coal (or coke) in fuel oil or tar**, stabilised by a small quantity of a **stabiliser (surfactant)**, designed to combine the high calorific value of oil with the low cost of coal into a pumpable, storable liquid fuel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical formulation:
> - **Pulverised coal**: 30 – 50 wt% (particle size < 75 µm)
> - **Fuel oil / Tar**: 50 – 70 wt%
> - **Stabiliser** (e.g., 0.5–2% soap, stearic acid, or surface-active agent): 0.5 – 1.0%
> - **Water content**: < 1%

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **thick chocolate milkshake** — you cannot separate the cocoa (solid) from the milk (liquid) by simply letting it stand, because the particles are *so fine* that they stay suspended. Colloidal fuel is the engineering equivalent: ultra-fine coal particles dispersed in oil, kept suspended by a "stabilising chocolate powder" (surfactant), so it flows and burns like a liquid.

### Manufacturing Steps
1. **Coal pulverisation** → Ball mill / jet mill → particle size < 75 µm.
2. **Drying** → Moisture < 1–2%.
3. **Mixing with oil** in a **colloidal mill / high-shear mixer** → uniform suspension.
4. **Stabiliser addition** → Prevents sedimentation (creaming) during storage.
5. **Storage & Handling** → Agitated tanks, recirculation lines, heated pipelines.

### Properties
- Calorific value: **~ 33–40 MJ/kg** (depending on coal:oil ratio)
- Viscosity: **Higher** than parent oil → may require preheating to 50–80 °C for pumping.
- Stability: **Months** (with proper stabiliser).
- Combustion efficiency: Comparable to oil, but **higher ash** (5–15%).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Benzol — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### Origin and Recovery Theory
- During coal carbonisation at **900 – 1100 °C**, volatile matter escapes. The coke oven gas (COG) carries benzene, toluene, xylene, naphthalene, and other aromatics as vapours.
- These are recovered by **oil scrubbing** (using creosote oil as the absorbent) because the aromatics are highly soluble in this heavy oil.

The absorption is governed by **Henry's Law** (modified):
$$y_i = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P} \cdot x_i$$
where:
- $y_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ in gas phase
- $x_i$ = mole fraction in liquid phase
- $P_i^{sat}$ = saturation vapour pressure
- $P$ = total pressure

### Why Benzol is a Premium Fuel
- **High octane number** (~ 100 RON) — antiknock superior to most gasolines of the 1930s–40s.
- **High calorific value** (40 MJ/kg) — energy-dense.
- **Clean burning** — no gum formation, low residue.
- **Uses**:
  1. Blending with gasoline (antiknock additive)
  2. Solvent for paints, resins, rubber
  3. Feedstock for nitration → nitrobenzene → aniline → dyes, explosives
  4. Chemical synthesis (phenol, styrene, caprolactam)

### Limitations
- **Toxicity** — Benzene is a known carcinogen; exposure limits: **0.5 ppm (8-hr TWA)**.
- **Narrow boiling range** — tends to vapour-lock in hot climates.
- **High cost** of recovery and purification.

---

## 2.2 Shale Oil — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### Kerogen Decomposition Chemistry
Kerogen is a complex macromolecular organic solid. On heating to 350–550 °C in an inert atmosphere, it undergoes **pyrolysis** — bond scission yields:

$$\text{Kerogen (solid)} \xrightarrow{350-550\,°C} \text{Shale Oil (liquid)} + \text{Shale Gas} + \text{Coke}$$

The mechanism includes:
1. **Depolymerisation** of kerogen matrix.
2. **Cracking** of long-chain aliphatic and aromatic moieties.
3. **Condensation** reactions producing larger polyaromatics (asphaltenes, coke).
4. **Dehydration** and **decarboxylation** releasing H₂O, CO₂, H₂S.

### Fischer Assay (Standard Test for Oil Yield)
The oil yield of an oil shale sample is determined by **Fischer Assay**:
$$Y_{oil} = \frac{m_{oil\,recovered}}{m_{shale\,sample}} \times 100 \;\; (\text{in } \text{gallons/ton or wt\%})$$

- Oil shales with > **25 gallons/ton** (≈ 100 L/tonne) are considered commercially viable.

### Retort Types
| Retort Type | Mode | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Retort** (e.g., Kiviter, Petrosix) | Above ground | High oil yield, energy intensive |
| **In-situ Retort** (e.g., Shell ICP) | Underground | Lower cost, environmental concerns |
| **Ex-situ Retort** | Mined shale heated above ground | Most common industrial method |

### Significance
- **USA** has the largest oil shale deposits (Green River Formation, Colorado) — estimated **1.5 trillion barrels** of shale oil in place.
- **Estonia** (kukersite) has been commercially retorting since the 1920s.
- **India**: Cambay Basin, Assam, Rajasthan hold significant deposits.

---

## 2.3 Gashol & Power Alcohol — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### Combustion Chemistry of Ethanol
$$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \Delta H $$
$$\Delta H_{combustion} = -1366.8 \;\; \text{kJ/mol} \;\; \text{or} \;\; 29.7 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}$$

### Theoretical Air-Fuel Ratio
For ethanol $(C_2H_5OH)$:
$$\text{Stoichiometric AFR} = \frac{3 \times (32) \times 4.76 \times 2}{(2)(12) + (6)(1) + (16)} = \frac{6 \times 4.76 \times 32}{46} = 9.0$$
So stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of pure ethanol ≈ **9.0 : 1** (vs. 14.7 : 1 for gasoline).

### Why Ethanol is a Good Octane Booster
- **High latent heat** → cools intake charge → reduces knock.
- **Oxygen content** (34.8 wt%) → leaner local combustion → lower peak temperature → reduced knocking tendency.
- **Chemical structure** — OH group reduces auto-ignition tendency.

### Gashol vs Power Alcohol Comparison

| Property | Gashol (E10) | Power Alcohol (E20–E25) |
|---|---|---|
| Ethanol content | 10% | 20–25% |
| Octane Number | 95–98 RON | 100+ RON |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | ~ 33 | ~ 31 |
| Material compatibility | Limited issue | May attack plastics, rubbers |
| Phase separation risk | Low | Moderate-High |

### Phase Separation Equation (Water Tolerance)
The water tolerance (max water that can be added without phase separation) of an ethanol-gasoline blend is approximated by:
$$W_{max}\% \approx 0.30 \cdot \phi_{EtOH} - 0.04$$
where $\phi_{EtOH}$ is the volume % of ethanol.

---

## 2.4 Colloidal Fuel — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### Stokes' Law of Sedimentation
The settling velocity of a coal particle in oil is given by:
$$v = \frac{2\,r^2\,(\rho_{coal} - \rho_{oil})\,g}{9\,\eta}$$
where:
- $r$ = particle radius (m)
- $\rho_{coal}$ = density of coal (~ 1300 kg/m³)
- $\rho_{oil}$ = density of oil (~ 950 kg/m³)
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of oil (Pa·s)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

**To minimise settling** (ensure colloidal stability):
1. Reduce $r$ → mill coal to < 75 µm (preferably < 10 µm).
2. Increase $\eta$ → use heavier fuel oil grades.
3. Reduce density difference → choose coals close to oil density (semi-bituminous ideal).
4. Add surfactant → creates **electrostatic/steric repulsion** between particles.

### Stability Mechanisms
- **Electrostatic stabilisation** — surfactant ionises; double-layer repulsion.
- **Steric stabilisation** — long polymer chains physically prevent close approach.
- Common stabilisers: **fatty acid soaps** (oleic acid + NaOH), **lignosulphonates**, **sulfonated castor oil (Turkey Red Oil)**.

### Advantages of Colloidal Fuel
- ✅ Cheaper than pure fuel oil (~ 25–30% cost reduction).
- ✅ Higher calorific value (high volatile matter in coal).
- ✅ Extends scarce oil resources.
- ✅ Can be fired in conventional oil-fired boilers with minor modifications.

### Disadvantages
- ❌ **High ash** (10–15%) → slagging, fouling.
- ❌ **High viscosity** → needs heated storage, pumping.
- ❌ **Settling during long storage** → re-agitation required.
- ❌ **Erosion** of burner tips from solid particles.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ethanol combustion | $C_2H_5OH + 3O_2 \to 2CO_2 + 3H_2O$ | — | Power Alcohol |
| 2 | Calorific value of ethanol | $\Delta H_c = 29.7$ | MJ/kg | All alcohol fuels |
| 3 | Glucose → Ethanol | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \to 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$ | — | Fermentation |
| 4 | Stoichiometric AFR (ethanol) | $AFR_{st} = 9.0$ | kg air / kg fuel | Power Alcohol |
| 5 | Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline) | $AFR_{st} = 14.7$ | kg air / kg fuel | Gashol |
| 6 | Benzene combustion | $C_6H_6 + 7.5\,O_2 \to 6CO_2 + 3H_2O$ | — | Benzol |
| 7 | Benzol calorific value | $CV \approx 40$ | MJ/kg | Benzol |
| 8 | Kerogen pyrolysis | Kerogen $\xrightarrow{450\,°C}$ Oil + Gas + Coke | — | Shale Oil |
| 9 | Fischer assay yield | $Y = m_{oil}/m_{shale}$ | % or gal/ton | Shale Oil |
| 10 | Stokes settling velocity | $v = \dfrac{2\,r^2\,\Delta\rho\,g}{9\,\eta}$ | m/s | Colloidal Fuel |
| 11 | Water tolerance of blend | $W_{max}\% \approx 0.30\phi_{EtOH} - 0.04$ | vol% | Gashol |
| 12 | Benzol in coke oven gas | $\sim 30$ | g/Nm³ | Benzol |
| 13 | Colloidal fuel typical CV | $33-40$ | MJ/kg | Colloidal Fuel |
| 14 | Coal:Oil ratio | $30:70$ to $50:50$ | wt% | Colloidal Fuel |
| 15 | Ethanol water content limit | $< 0.5$ | wt% | Gashol / Power Alcohol |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These fuels were critical during **WWII (Benzol in Germany)**, the **1973 oil crisis (Power Alcohol in Brazil)**, and remain central to **carbon-neutral energy strategies** (E10 mandated in India since 2003, E20 by 2025). Colloidal fuel is the conceptual ancestor of modern **coal-water slurry (CWS)** and **coal-oil slurry (COM)** technologies used in China for industrial boilers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Ethanol

**Problem:** A Power Alcohol blend contains 25% (by volume) anhydrous ethanol and 75% gasoline (assumed to be pure iso-octane, C₈H₁₈). Calculate the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of the blend.
Take densities: ρ_ethanol = **0.789 g/cm³**, ρ_iso-octane = **0.692 g/cm³**. Molecular weights: C = 12, H = 1, O = 16, N = 14. Air composition: 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ (by volume). Molecular weight of air = 28.97 g/mol.

### Step 1: Determine the mass fraction of each component in 1 L of blend

Take 1 L (= 1000 cm³) of blend.

Volume of ethanol = 0.25 × 1000 = 250 cm³
Volume of iso-octane = 0.75 × 1000 = 750 cm³

Mass of ethanol = 250 × 0.789 = **197.25 g**
Mass of iso-octane = 750 × 0.692 = **519.00 g**

Total mass of fuel blend = 197.25 + 519.00 = 716.25 g

Mass fraction of ethanol:
$$w_{EtOH} = \frac{197.25}{716.25} = 0.2754$$

Mass fraction of iso-octane:
$$w_{C_8H_{18}} = \frac{519.00}{716.25} = 0.7246$$

### Step 2: Write the combustion equations

Ethanol:
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Iso-octane:
$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step 3: Calculate mass of O₂ required per gram of each fuel

For ethanol (M = 2×12 + 6×1 + 16 = 46 g/mol):
- 1 mol ethanol needs 3 mol O₂.
- Mass O₂ per g ethanol = $\dfrac{3 \times 32}{46} = \dfrac{96}{46} = 2.087$ g O₂/g ethanol

For iso-octane (M = 8×12 + 18×1 = 114 g/mol):
- 1 mol iso-octane needs 12.5 mol O₂.
- Mass O₂ per g iso-octane = $\dfrac{12.5 \times 32}{114} = \dfrac{400}{114} = 3.509$ g O₂/g iso-octane

### Step 4: O₂ required per gram of blend

$$m_{O_2} = w_{EtOH} \times 2.087 + w_{C_8H_{18}} \times 3.509$$
$$m_{O_2} = 0.2754 \times 2.087 + 0.7246 \times 3.509$$
$$m_{O_2} = 0.5747 + 2.5427 = 3.1174 \;\; \text{g O}_2 / \text{g fuel}$$

### Step 5: Air required (O₂ is 23.3% by mass in air)

$$AFR_{st} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.233} = \frac{3.1174}{0.233} = 13.379$$

$$\boxed{AFR_{st} \approx 13.4 \;\; \text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

> **Conclusion:** The 25% Power Alcohol blend requires less air per kg of fuel than pure gasoline (AFR = 14.7) because ethanol contains "built-in" oxygen.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Settling Velocity of Coal Particle in Colloidal Fuel

**Problem:** A colloidal fuel contains coal particles of radius 30 µm in fuel oil. Calculate the terminal settling velocity.
Given: ρ_coal = 1300 kg/m³, ρ_oil = 950 kg/m³, η_oil = 0.05 Pa·s (heavy fuel oil at 60 °C), g = 9.81 m/s².

### Step 1: Identify given data

$$r = 30 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{m} = 3 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{m}$$
$$\Delta\rho = \rho_{coal} - \rho_{oil} = 1300 - 950 = 350 \;\; \text{kg/m}^3$$
$$\eta = 0.05 \;\; \text{Pa·s}, \quad g = 9.81 \;\; \text{m/s}^2$$

### Step 2: Apply Stokes' Law

$$v = \frac{2\,r^2\,(\Delta\rho)\,g}{9\,\eta}$$

$$v = \frac{2 \times (3 \times 10^{-5})^2 \times 350 \times 9.81}{9 \times 0.05}$$

### Step 3: Compute the numerator

$$\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 9 \times 10^{-10} \times 350 \times 9.81$$
$$= 2 \times 9 \times 350 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-10}$$
$$= 61779 \times 10^{-10} = 6.178 \times 10^{-6}$$

### Step 4: Compute denominator

$$\text{Denominator} = 9 \times 0.05 = 0.45$$

### Step 5: Divide

$$v = \frac{6.178 \times 10^{-6}}{0.45} = 1.373 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{m/s}$$

### Step 6: Convert to mm/day for practical interpretation

$$v = 1.373 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{m/s} \times \frac{1000 \;\text{mm}}{1\;\text{m}} \times \frac{86400\;\text{s}}{1\;\text{day}}$$
$$v \approx 1.186 \;\; \text{mm/day}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 1.19 \;\; \text{mm/day}}$$

> **Conclusion:** The settling is very slow — about 1.2 mm/day — confirming that fine particles (30 µm) in viscous oil are essentially stable for weeks. This is why colloidal fuels can be stored with minimal settling.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Heat Released from Benzol Combustion

**Problem:** 1 kg of pure benzene (C₆H₆) is combusted completely. Calculate (a) theoretical air required, (b) flue gas composition by volume, (c) heat released (use GCV).

**Data:** GCV of benzene = **40,140 kJ/kg**. Molecular weight benzene = 78 g/mol. Air = 23.3% O₂ by mass.

### Step 1: Combustion equation

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + 7.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step 2: Theoretical air per kg of benzene

Moles of benzene per kg:
$$n_{C_6H_6} = \frac{1000}{78} = 12.82 \;\; \text{mol}$$

Moles of O₂ required = 7.5 × 12.82 = 96.15 mol
Mass of O₂ = 96.15 × 32 = 3076.9 g = 3.077 kg

Mass of air = 3.077 / 0.233 = **13.21 kg air/kg benzene**

### Step 3: Flue gas composition per mole of benzene

Products (per mole C₆H₆): 6 CO₂ + 3 H₂O (g) + (7.5 × 3.76) N₂ = 28.2 N₂

Total moles of flue gas (dry basis) = 6 + 28.2 = 34.2 mol
$$\%CO_2 = \frac{6}{34.2} \times 100 = 17.54\%$$
$$\%N_2 = 82.46\%$$

(If wet basis, H₂O included: total = 37.2; %CO₂ = 6/37.2 = 16.13%)

### Step 4: Heat released per kg

$$Q = 1 \;\; \text{kg} \times 40140 \;\; \text{kJ/kg} = \boxed{40,140 \;\; \text{kJ}}$$

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Benzol | Shale Oil | Gashol (E10) | Power Alcohol | Colloidal Fuel |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical state** | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid (suspension) |
| **Source** | Coal carbonisation | Oil shale pyrolysis | Gasoline + Ethanol | Fermentation of biomass | Coal + Fuel oil |
| **Colour** | Pale yellow | Dark brown/black | Light yellow | Colourless | Black |
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 870–880 | 900–950 | 740–760 | 789 | 1000–1100 |
| **Calorific value (MJ/kg)** | 40 | 40–42 | 33 | 29.7 | 33–40 |
| **Octane (RON)** | 95–100 | 70–80 (after refining) | 95–98 | 100–110 | N/A (boiler fuel) |
| **Sulphur content** | Very low | High (0.5–1.5%) | Very low | 0 | Variable |
| **Main advantage** | Antiknock, high CV | Vast reserves | Renewable, octane boost | Renewable, clean | Low cost, flexible |
| **Main disadvantage** | Toxicity | High S, N; costly refining | Lower energy density | Low CV, hygroscopic | Settling, high ash |
| **Primary use** | Motor fuel, solvent | Refined to transport fuels | Spark-ignition engines | Engines, stoves | Industrial boiler fuel |
| **Stability on storage** | Excellent | Good | Moderate (phase sep. risk) | Good (if anhydrous) | Requires agitation |

---

## 3.5 Mass Balance for Colloidal Fuel Preparation

**Problem:** Prepare 1000 kg of colloidal fuel with 40% coal, 59% fuel oil, 1% stabiliser. Coal has 5% inherent moisture. The stabiliser is added as a 50% solution. How much "as-received" coal and stabiliser solution are needed?

### Step 1: Mass of dry coal required

$$m_{dry\,coal} = 0.40 \times 1000 = 400 \;\; \text{kg}$$

### Step 2: Mass of "as-received" coal (with 5% moisture)

If 5% is moisture, then dry coal is 95% of as-received:
$$m_{AR\,coal} = \frac{400}{0.95} = 421.05 \;\; \text{kg}$$

### Step 3: Mass of stabiliser (active)

$$m_{stab\,active} = 0.01 \times 1000 = 10 \;\; \text{kg}$$

### Step 4: Mass of stabiliser solution (50%)

$$m_{stab\,soln} = \frac{10}{0.50} = 20 \;\; \text{kg}$$

### Step 5: Mass of fuel oil

$$m_{oil} = 0.59 \times 1000 = 590 \;\; \text{kg}$$

### Step 6: Verify

Total = 421.05 + 20 + 590 = 1031.05 kg ≠ 1000 kg — but the **moisture** in coal (5% × 421.05 = 21.05 kg) will evaporate during the hot mixing process. So the **final product** of 1000 kg is correct.

$$\boxed{m_{AR\,coal} = 421 \;\text{kg}, \quad m_{oil} = 590 \;\text{kg}, \quad m_{stab\,soln} = 20 \;\text{kg}}$$
