topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Gaseous Fuels: Natural Gases, Methane from Coal Mines, Manufactured Gases, Producer Gas, Water Gas, Biogas, Refinery Gas, LPG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Gaseous Fuels?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**: Gaseous fuels are combustible gases (or mixtures of combustible and non-combustible gases) that are burned with oxygen (or air) to release thermal energy in the form of heat. They are characterized by their **ease of ignition**, **clean combustion**, **high degree of combustion control**, and ability to be **transported through pipelines**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine lighting a campfire using logs versus using a gas stove. With logs, you struggle with smoke, soot, partial combustion, and uneven heat. Now switch to a domestic LPG cylinder connected to your stove: the moment you press the knob, you get instant, clean, adjustable, and complete combustion. That is the magic of **gaseous fuels**. They are essentially fuels that have already been "pre-processed" by nature or by industrial reactors into a state where combustion requires minimal additional energy input (low ignition temperature) and releases maximum energy per unit mass with minimal pollutants.

Gaseous fuels can be broadly classified into three categories based on origin:
1. **Naturally Occurring**: Natural Gas, Coal Bed Methane (CBM)
2. **Biochemically Produced**: Biogas
3. **Industrially Manufactured**: Producer Gas, Water Gas, Refinery Gas, LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas is a byproduct)

---

## 1.2 Natural Gas

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: Natural gas is a naturally occurring hydrocarbon gas mixture consisting primarily of **methane (CH₄ ~ 70–90%)**, along with smaller fractions of ethane (C₂H₆), propane (C₃H₈), butane (C₄H₁₀), and impurities like CO₂, N₂, H₂S, and water vapor.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of natural gas as the "fossil breath" of the Earth. Millions of years ago, dead marine organisms decomposed under immense pressure and heat deep inside the Earth's crust, transforming into crude oil and natural gas. Natural gas often sits **on top of petroleum reservoirs** under impervious rock layers, which is why it is extracted along with crude oil (associated gas) or independently from gas-only fields (non-associated gas).

### Key Characteristics
- **Calorific Value**: **38,000 – 42,000 kJ/m³** (or 850–1000 BTU/scf)
- **Composition**: CH₄ (85–95%), C₂H₆ (3–8%), C₃H₈ + C₄H₁₀ (1–3%), CO₂ + N₂ (1–5%)
- **Specific Gravity**: 0.55 – 0.70 (lighter than air → disperses upward if leaked)
- **Applications**: Domestic cooking, CNG vehicles, power generation, fertilizer feedstock (for H₂ production)

---

## 1.3 Methane from Coal Mines (Coal Bed Methane - CBM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: Coal Bed Methane (CBM) is the **methane gas adsorbed onto the internal surfaces of coal seams**. During the coalification process, methane is generated and stored within the microporous structure of coal under pressure. It is released during coal mining operations and is recovered as a fuel.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of coal as a hard sponge that has been soaked in methane gas for millions of years. As miners dig into coal seams, the pressure drops, and the trapped methane "oozes out" like water from a squeezed sponge. In underground mines, this methane is highly dangerous (causing explosions), so it must be vented out. Cleverly, engineers now capture this vented methane and use it as a clean fuel rather than wasting it.

### Key Characteristics
- **Composition**: **CH₄ (90–95%)**, with traces of N₂, CO₂, and heavier hydrocarbons
- **Calorific Value**: **33,000 – 38,000 kJ/m³**
- **Source**: Coal seams (bituminous and anthracite)
- **Extraction**: Dewatering of coal seams (reducing pressure → releases adsorbed CH₄)

---

## 1.4 Manufactured Gases

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: Manufactured gases (also called **artificial or synthetic gases**) are combustible gases produced from solid or liquid fuels through industrial chemical processes such as gasification, pyrolysis, or partial oxidation.

### Intuitive Analogy
If natural gas is "nature's ready-made fuel," manufactured gases are "human-engineered fuels." Just like we can bake bread from raw wheat, we can convert coal, biomass, or oil into useful gaseous fuels using chemical reactors called **gasifiers**.

Major types of manufactured gases include Producer Gas, Water Gas, and others discussed below.

---

## 1.5 Producer Gas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: Producer gas is a mixture of **combustible gases (CO and H₂)** and **non-combustible gases (CO₂, N₂)** produced by the **partial combustion of solid carbonaceous fuel (coke or coal) with a limited supply of air** in a reactor called a **gas producer**.

### The Governing Reaction
The core reaction in a gas producer is:

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol (exothermic)}$$

The CO₂ then reacts with hot coke to form CO (Boudouard reaction):

$$CO_2 + C \rightarrow 2CO \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol (endothermic)}$$

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a producer gas plant as a "controlled underground fire" that you deliberately starve of oxygen. You burn coal just enough to keep it hot, but the lack of oxygen forces the carbon to grab oxygen from CO₂ (already produced), creating carbon monoxide (CO) — a combustible gas. So instead of letting all the heat escape as flame, you "trap" the chemical energy as CO gas that you can pipe elsewhere and burn later.

### Key Characteristics
- **Composition**: CO (22–30%), H₂ (8–12%), CO₂ (3–6%), N₂ (52–55%), CH₄ (1–3%)
- **Calorific Value**: **4,200 – 7,500 kJ/m³** (LOW calorific value due to high N₂)
- **Uses**: Industrial heating, steel furnaces, power generation, feedstock for chemical synthesis

---

## 1.6 Water Gas (Blue Gas / Synthesis Gas)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: Water gas is a mixture of **carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H₂)** produced by passing **steam over incandescent coke or coal** at temperatures of **1000–1100 °C** in a reactor called a **water gas generator**.

### The Governing Reaction
The primary reaction (endothermic, requires continuous heat input):

$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### Intuitive Analogy
Water gas is produced like this: you have a bed of red-hot coal, and you blow steam over it. The steam (H₂O) is "cracked" by the hot carbon into two combustible gases — hydrogen and carbon monoxide — like squeezing two separate fuels out of water and coal in one clever reaction.

Because the steam-carbon reaction is **endothermic**, the coke bed cools down. To maintain the temperature, the process is alternated with an **air blow** stage (exothermic C + O₂ → CO₂) to reheat the bed. This is called the **"blow-run" cycle** or **"hot blast-cold blast" cycle**.

### Key Characteristics
- **Composition**: CO (40–45%), H₂ (45–50%), CO₂ (3–5%), N₂ (2–5%), CH₄ (traces)
- **Calorific Value**: **10,500 – 12,500 kJ/m³** (higher than producer gas; no N₂ dilution)
- **Uses**: Hydrogen production, ammonia synthesis, Fischer-Tropsch synthesis, illumination

---

## 1.7 Biogas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: Biogas is a renewable gaseous fuel produced by the **anaerobic (oxygen-free) decomposition of organic matter** such as agricultural waste, animal dung, municipal solid waste, sewage, and food waste by **methanogenic bacteria** in a sealed reactor called a **biogas digester (or anaerobic digester)**.

### The Governing Biological Reactions
**Stage 1 — Hydrolysis & Acidogenesis** (by acidogenic bacteria):
Organic matter → Volatile fatty acids, alcohols, H₂, CO₂

**Stage 2 — Methanogenesis** (by methanogenic bacteria):

$$CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$$

$$CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$$

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a sealed compost pit. Inside, bacteria are breaking down kitchen waste without any oxygen — just like how it happens in a cow's stomach (rumen). As they digest the waste, they "exhale" methane, exactly like a cow does! This methane is captured and used as cooking gas. In India, this is implemented through **Gobar Gas Plants** (KVIC model), turning cow dung into clean cooking fuel and organic fertilizer (slurry) simultaneously.

### Key Characteristics
- **Composition**: CH₄ (50–70%), CO₂ (25–45%), N₂ (0–5%), H₂S (traces, must be removed)
- **Calorific Value**: **18,000 – 26,000 kJ/m³**
- **Byproduct**: Digested slurry (high-quality organic fertilizer)
- **Uses**: Rural cooking, electricity generation, vehicle fuel (upgraded biomethane)

---

## 1.8 Refinery Gas

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: Refinery gas is a **mixture of gaseous hydrocarbons (C₁–C₄)** produced as a **byproduct during various petroleum refining processes** such as crude oil distillation, catalytic cracking, hydrocracking, and coking.

### Composition
- **C₁ (Methane)**: CH₄
- **C₂ (Ethane)**: C₂H₆
- **C₃ (Propane)**: C₃H₈
- **C₄ (Butane)**: C₄H₁₀
- Plus small amounts of ethylene, propylene, butylene

### Intuitive Analogy
When you refine crude oil in a refinery, you are essentially "sorting" the hydrocarbons by boiling point. The lightest fractions (with the lowest boiling points) escape as gases. These gases — once considered a waste nuisance — are now precious feedstocks for petrochemicals and fuels.

### Key Characteristics
- **Calorific Value**: Varies widely (typically **20,000 – 50,000 kJ/m³** depending on composition)
- **Uses**: Petrochemical feedstock, hydrogen production (via steam reforming), fuel gas for refinery furnaces, LPG blending

---

## 1.9 LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: LPG is a mixture of **propane (C₃H₈) and butane (C₄H₁₀)** with traces of propylene and butylene, stored and transported in **liquid state under moderate pressure** (around 7–10 bar at ambient temperature) inside pressurized cylinders.

### Intuitive Analogy
LPG is the gas you use in your kitchen cylinder (Indane, HP Gas, Bharat Gas). It is called "liquefied" because although it is a gas at room temperature and atmospheric pressure, under modest pressure (≈ 7 bar), it condenses into a liquid that occupies **1/260th** the volume of its gaseous form. This makes it incredibly convenient — you can carry a huge amount of energy in a small portable cylinder.

### Key Characteristics
- **Composition**: Mainly C₃H₈ (propane) and C₄H₁₀ (butane) in varying ratios (winter → more propane for better vaporization)
- **Calorific Value**: **46,000 – 50,000 kJ/m³** (highest among common gaseous fuels)
- **Density (liquid)**: ~ 0.51 – 0.58 kg/L
- **Uses**: Domestic cooking, industrial heating, autogas (LPG vehicles), aerosol propellant, refrigerant

---

## 1.10 Comparison Snapshot at a Glance

> [!TIP]
> Below is a high-density comparative summary that KTU examiners love to test as a "compare-and-contrast" question. Memorize these ranges well.

| Fuel Type | Primary Combustible | Calorific Value (kJ/m³) | Major Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Natural Gas | CH₄ | 38,000 – 42,000 | CNG, power, domestic |
| CBM | CH₄ | 33,000 – 38,000 | Power, pipeline injection |
| Producer Gas | CO, H₂ | 4,200 – 7,500 | Industrial heating |
| Water Gas | CO, H₂ | 10,500 – 12,500 | H₂ production, synthesis |
| Biogas | CH₄ | 18,000 – 26,000 | Rural cooking, power |
| Refinery Gas | C₁ – C₄ | 20,000 – 50,000 | Petrochemical feedstock |
| LPG | C₃H₈ + C₄H₁₀ | 46,000 – 50,000 | Domestic, autogas |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why Each Fuel Has Its Own Calorific Value

The calorific value of a gaseous fuel is directly proportional to the **heat released per unit volume** when its combustible constituents undergo complete combustion with oxygen. The combustion reactions are:

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O \quad \Delta H = -890 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$CO + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -283 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad \Delta H = -242 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O \quad \Delta H = -2220 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$C_4H_{10} + \frac{13}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 5H_2O \quad \Delta H = -2878 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The calorific value of any gaseous fuel mixture is calculated using:

$$GCV = \sum_i v_i \cdot CV_i$$

where $v_i$ = volume fraction of combustible gas $i$ and $CV_i$ = calorific value of pure gas $i$ per unit volume.

---

## 2.2 Producer Gas — Detailed Process Analysis

A gas producer operates in **two simultaneous zones**:

1. **Combustion Zone (top)**: Coke reacts with air
2. **Reduction Zone (bottom)**: CO₂ + C → 2CO (endothermic)

**Operational steps:**
- Air enters from top through tuyère (water-cooled nozzle)
- C + O₂ → CO₂ (highly exothermic, temperature ~ 1500 °C)
- As CO₂ descends through hot coke, it is reduced to CO
- Ash is removed at the bottom grate
- Steam is sometimes added to enhance H₂ production (producing **semi-water gas**)

**Why calorific value is low**: The high nitrogen content (~55%) from the air dilutes the combustible gases (CO + H₂), drastically reducing energy density.

---

## 2.3 Water Gas — Blow-Run Cycle Deep Dive

Since the steam-carbon reaction is endothermic, the coke bed cools. To maintain continuous operation, the **blow-run cycle** is used:

**Blow Phase (Air Blast)** — 1 minute:
- Air is blown → C + O₂ → CO₂ → heat released → bed temperature rises to ~1100 °C

**Run Phase (Steam Blast)** — 4–5 minutes:
- Steam is blown → C + H₂O → CO + H₂ → water gas produced → bed cools

**Carburetion (optional)**:
- During the run phase, oil/petroleum is sprayed into the hot gas to enrich it with higher hydrocarbons → **carburetted water gas**

---

## 2.4 Biogas — Microbiology and Reactor Design

**Four biological stages**:
1. **Hydrolysis**: Complex organics (proteins, fats, carbohydrates) → monomers
2. **Acidogenesis**: Monomers → volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, CO₂
3. **Acetogenesis**: VFAs → acetic acid + H₂
4. **Methanogenesis**: Acetate + H₂ → CH₄ + CO₂

**Optimal operating conditions**:
- Temperature: **Mesophilic (30–40 °C)** or **Thermophilic (50–60 °C)**
- pH: **6.8 – 7.5** (critical; below 6 inhibits methanogens)
- C/N ratio: **20:1 to 30:1** for optimal digestion
- Retention time: 20–40 days

**Reactor types**: Fixed dome (KVIC), Floating drum, Plug flow, CSTR, UASB

---

## 2.5 LPG — Why It Can Be Liquefied Easily

The critical temperatures and pressures of propane and butane are:
- **Propane**: T_c = 96.7 °C, P_c = 42.5 bar
- **Butane**: T_c = 152 °C, P_c = 37.9 bar

Since ambient temperature (25–35 °C) is **below the critical temperature** of both propane and butane, applying moderate pressure (7–10 bar) is enough to liquefy them. This is why LPG can be stored as a liquid in household cylinders, while natural gas (T_c = -82.6 °C for methane) cannot be liquefied at room temperature without cryogenic cooling — it requires LNG technology at -162 °C.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **This is your rapid-revision arsenal for the examination hall.** These formulas appear in over 80% of KTU Fuel Engineering calculations.

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $GCV_{mix} = \sum v_i \cdot CV_i$ | Calorific value of gas mixture | kJ/m³ |
| $Air_{req} = \frac{100}{21} \left[ CO + \frac{H_2}{2} + CH_4 \cdot 2 + \sum (C_xH_y) \cdot \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) - O_2 \right]$ | Theoretical air required per m³ of fuel gas | m³/m³ |
| $\eta_{combustion} = \frac{GCV}{GCV + \text{Latent heat of water vapor}}$ | Relation between GCV and NCV | dimensionless |
| $C/H \text{ ratio} = \frac{12 \cdot C\%}{1 \cdot H\%}$ | Atomic carbon-to-hydrogen ratio | dimensionless |
| $Sp.Gr. = \frac{M_{fuel}}{28.97}$ (with air M = 28.97) | Specific gravity of fuel gas | dimensionless |
| $V_{gas,liquid} = \frac{RT}{PM}$ | Molar volume equation | m³/mol |
| $Energy_{LPG} = V_{liquid} \cdot \rho_{LPG} \cdot CV_{LPG}$ | Energy stored in LPG cylinder | kJ |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Heating**: Producer gas and water gas are used in steel re-rolling mills, glass factories, and ceramic kilns where precise, controllable, low-cost heat is needed.
- **Distributed Power**: Biogas plants convert rural/agricultural waste into electricity, supporting rural India's energy self-sufficiency.
- **Clean Transport**: CNG (compressed natural gas) and LPG autogas reduce urban air pollution dramatically.
- **Petrochemical Feedstock**: Refinery gases and natural gas are building blocks for plastics, fertilizers (via NH₃ from H₂), and synthetic fuels.
- **Climate Engineering**: Capturing CBM mitigates methane emissions (a greenhouse gas 25× more potent than CO₂).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Air Required for Combustion of a Gaseous Fuel

**Given a dry fuel gas with the following composition by volume**:
- $CO = a\%$, $H_2 = b\%$, $CH_4 = c\%$, $C_2H_6 = d\%$, $O_2 = e\%$, $CO_2 = f\%$, $N_2 = g\%$

We wish to find the **theoretical oxygen required** per m³ of fuel gas.

### Step 1 — Write balanced combustion reactions

Carbon monoxide burns as:

$$CO + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

Hydrogen burns as:

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

Methane burns as:

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

Ethane burns as:

$$C_2H_6 + \frac{7}{2} O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 3H_2O$$

### Step 2 — Count O₂ molecules required per m³ of each combustible component

For each component, the O₂ volume required (per m³ of that component) is:

| Component | O₂ volume required (per m³ of component) |
|---|---|
| CO | 1/2 |
| H₂ | 1/2 |
| CH₄ | 2 |
| C₂H₆ | 7/2 |

### Step 3 — Multiply by volume fractions and sum

The total theoretical oxygen required per m³ of fuel gas is:

$$O_{2,theoretical} = \frac{a}{2} + \frac{b}{2} + 2c + \frac{7d}{2} - e$$

The subtraction of $e$ accounts for the oxygen already present in the fuel gas (which contributes to combustion).

In numerical form:

$$O_{2,theoretical} = 0.5a + 0.5b + 2c + 3.5d - e \quad \text{(in m}^3/\text{m}^3\text{)}$$

### Step 4 — Convert oxygen volume to air volume

Since air contains **21% oxygen by volume** (or 1/4.76 by mole ratio, since oxygen is 21/100 and the balance is 79/100 ≈ 4.76 ratio):

$$Air_{theoretical} = \frac{O_{2,theoretical}}{0.21} = 4.76 \times O_{2,theoretical}$$

So the final formula:

$$Air_{theoretical} = \frac{100}{21}\left[ 0.5a + 0.5b + 2c + 3.5d - e \right] \quad \text{m}^3\text{ air/m}^3\text{ fuel}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

**Given producer gas composition**:
- CO = 25%, H₂ = 10%, CH₄ = 2%, C₂H₆ = 0%, O₂ = 0%, CO₂ = 5%, N₂ = 58%

**Find**: Theoretical air required per m³ of fuel gas.

**Solution**:

$$O_{2,theoretical} = 0.5 \times 25 + 0.5 \times 10 + 2 \times 2 + 3.5 \times 0 - 0$$

Computing each term step by step:

$$= 12.5 + 5 + 4 + 0$$

$$= 21.5 \text{ m}^3 \text{ O}_2 \text{ per 100 m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

$$= 0.215 \text{ m}^3 \text{ O}_2 \text{ per m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

Therefore:

$$Air_{theoretical} = \frac{0.215}{0.21} = 1.024 \text{ m}^3\text{/m}^3\text{ fuel}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Gross Calorific Value of Producer Gas from Composition

**Given composition**:
- CO = 25%, H₂ = 10%, CH₄ = 2%, CO₂ = 5%, N₂ = 58%

**Standard calorific values** (per m³ of pure gas at STP):
- CO: 12,620 kJ/m³
- H₂: 12,620 kJ/m³
- CH₄: 39,750 kJ/m³

### Step 1 — Identify combustible components
Only CO, H₂, and CH₄ are combustible. CO₂ and N₂ are non-combustible diluents.

### Step 2 — Apply weighted sum formula

$$GCV = \sum v_i \cdot CV_i$$

$$GCV = \frac{25}{100}(12620) + \frac{10}{100}(12620) + \frac{2}{100}(39750)$$

Computing each term:

$$= 0.25 \times 12620 + 0.10 \times 12620 + 0.02 \times 39750$$

$$= 3155 + 1262 + 795$$

$$= 5212 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Sanity Check
This value (~5200 kJ/m³) falls within the textbook range for producer gas (4,200 – 7,500 kJ/m³) ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for LPG (Propane)

For **pure propane combustion**:

$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$$

### Step 1 — Molecular masses
- Propane (C₃H₈): $M = 3(12) + 8(1) = 44 \text{ g/mol}$
- Oxygen: $M = 32 \text{ g/mol}$
- Air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass

### Step 2 — Mass of O₂ required
From the balanced equation, 1 mole of propane requires 5 moles of O₂.

$$m_{O_2} = 5 \times 32 = 160 \text{ g O}_2 \text{ per 44 g propane}$$

### Step 3 — Mass of air required
Since air is 23.2% oxygen by mass:

$$m_{air} = \frac{160}{0.232} = 689.66 \text{ g air per 44 g propane}$$

### Step 4 — Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis)

$$A/F = \frac{689.66}{44} = 15.67 \text{ kg air/kg propane}$$

### Step 5 — Air-Fuel Ratio (volume basis at STP)
- Molar volume at STP = 22.4 L/mol
- 5 mol O₂ needs 5/0.21 = 23.81 mol air = 23.81 × 22.4 = 533.3 L air
- 1 mol propane = 22.4 L

$$A/F_{vol} = \frac{533.3}{22.4} = 23.81 \text{ m}^3\text{ air/m}^3\text{ propane}$$

This matches the textbook value of **~24:1** for propane.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Calorific Value Reduction by Nitrogen Dilution

**Concept**: For producer gas, N₂ acts as a thermal ballast. The effective calorific value of producer gas can be related to the calorific value of pure synthesis gas by:

$$GCV_{producer} = GCV_{syngas} \times \left(1 - \frac{\%N_2}{100}\right)$$

**Example**: If water gas has GCV = 11,000 kJ/m³ with 5% N₂, and producer gas has 55% N₂:

$$GCV_{producer} = 11000 \times (1 - 0.55) = 11000 \times 0.45 = 4950 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$

This shows that nitrogen dilution reduces calorific value almost linearly. Reducing N₂ in the producer gas (using oxygen instead of air for gasification → "oxygen gasification") dramatically improves calorific value.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Combustion Equations for All Gaseous Fuels

| Fuel | Combustion Reaction | O₂ Required (mol/mol) | Air Required (mol/mol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Methane (CH₄) | CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O | 2 | 9.52 |
| Ethane (C₂H₆) | C₂H₆ + 3.5O₂ → 2CO₂ + 3H₂O | 3.5 | 16.67 |
| Propane (C₃H₈) | C₃H₈ + 5O₂ → 3CO₂ + 4H₂O | 5 | 23.81 |
| Butane (C₄H₁₀) | C₄H₁₀ + 6.5O₂ → 4CO₂ + 5H₂O | 6.5 | 30.95 |
| Carbon Monoxide (CO) | CO + 0.5O₂ → CO₂ | 0.5 | 2.38 |
| Hydrogen (H₂) | H₂ + 0.5O₂ → H₂O | 0.5 | 2.38 |
| Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S) | H₂S + 1.5O₂ → SO₂ + H₂O | 1.5 | 7.14 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's tip**: For LPG in practical burners, use ~10% excess air above stoichiometric for complete combustion and to prevent CO formation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Producer Gas Plant — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    airIn([Air Blower]) --> tuyere1[Tuyere Injection]
    steamOpt([Optional Steam]) --> tuyere1
    tuyere1 --> combustor[Combustion Zone\nC + O2 to CO2\nExothermic 1500C]
    combustor --> reducer[Reduction Zone\nCO2 + C to 2CO\nEndothermic]
    reducer --> ashHop[Ash Hopper + Grate]
    ashHop --> ashOut([Ash Discharge])
    reducer --> outlet1[Hot Gas Outlet]
    outlet1 --> cooler[Gas Cooler\nHeat Exchanger]
    cooler --> scrubber[Water Scrubber\nTar and Dust Removal]
    scrubber --> cleaned[Clean Producer Gas]
    cleaned --> stock[Gas Storage Holder]
    stock --> dist([Distribution to Furnaces])
```

**Process Logic**:
1. Air + optional steam enter from the bottom via the tuyere
2. In combustion zone: exothermic reaction heats the coke
3. In reduction zone: CO₂ is reduced to CO using hot coke
4. Hot raw gas exits the top, then cooled and cleaned
5. Clean gas stored in holder for distribution

---

## 4.2 Water Gas Generator — Blow-Run Cycle Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cycle [Blow Run Cycle]
        airIn([Air In]) --> valve1[4 Way Valve Position A]
        valve1 --> bed1[Reactor Bed\nAir Blow Stage\nC + O2 to CO2\nHeat Stored]
        bed1 --> flue1([Flue Gas to Stack\nHot Stack Gases])
        bed1 --> mode[Mode Switch\nTemperature Check\nGT 1000C]
        mode --> valve2[4 Way Valve Position B]
        steamIn([Steam In]) --> valve2
        valve2 --> bed2[Reactor Bed\nSteam Run Stage\nC + H2O to CO + H2\nEndothermic Cooling]
        bed2 --> gasOut([Water Gas to Holder\nCO 40 percent H2 45 percent])
    end
```

**Process Logic**: The valve alternates air (exothermic heating) and steam (endothermic gasification) every 4–5 minutes to maintain continuous operation.

---

## 4.3 Biogas Plant — Anaerobic Digester Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1([Animal Dung]) --> mixer[Mixing Tank\nWater Slurry]
    input2([Agricultural Waste]) --> mixer
    input3([Kitchen Waste]) --> mixer
    mixer --> digester[Anaerobic Digester\nSealed Reactor\nMesophilic 35C\nRetention 30 Days]
    digester --> stage1[Stage 1 Hydrolysis\nAcidogenesis]
    stage1 --> stage2[Stage 2 Acetogenesis]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage 3 Methanogenesis]
    stage3 --> gasCollect[Gas Collection Dome\nCH4 60 percent CO2 35 percent]
    stage3 --> slurry[Spent Slurry\nOrganic Fertilizer]
    gasCollect --> desulfur[H2S Scrubber\nIron Oxide Bed]
    desulfur --> storage[Gas Holder / Storage]
    storage --> use1([Cooking Stove])
    storage --> use2([Biogas Engine Generator])
    storage --> use3([Upgraded to Biomethane CNG])
```

**Process Logic**: Sequential microbial digestion in a sealed reactor produces methane-rich biogas and nutrient-rich slurry.

---

## 4.4 Natural Gas Processing Plant — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    well([Wellhead Gas]) --> sep1[Primary Separator\nLiquid Knockout]
    sep1 --> amine[Amine Sweetening\nH2S and CO2 Removal]
    amine --> dehyd[Dehydration Unit\nGlycol Contactor]
    dehyd --> frac[Fractionation Column\nC1 to C4 Separation]
    frac --> methane[Methane Stream\nPipeline Quality]
    frac --> ethane[Ethane Stream]
    frac --> propane[Propane Stream\nLPG Feed]
    frac --> butane[Butane Stream\nLPG Feed]
    propane --> lpgMix[LPG Mixing Station]
    butane --> lpgMix
    lpgMix --> cyl([LPG Cylinder Filling])
    methane --> pipeline([City Gas Distribution])
```

---

## 4.5 LPG Cylinder — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cyl[Toughened Steel Cylinder\nISI Marked\n34 kg Water Capacity\n14.2 kg LPG] --> liquid[Liquid LPG at 7 bar\nPropane plus Butane]
    cyl --> vaporSpace[Vapor Space at Top\nPressurized Gas]
    liquid --> valve[Shut Off Valve\nWith PRD]
    valve --> regulator[Pressure Regulator\nReduces to 0.05 bar]
    regulator --> tube[Rubber Tube to Stove]
    tube --> burner[Burner Nozzle\nVenturi Mixing]
    burner --> flame([Blue Flame Combustion])
```

**Safety Feature**: PRD = Pressure Relief Device — vents gas automatically if pressure exceeds safety limit (overheating protection).

---

## 4.6 Comparative Classification of Gaseous Fuels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Gaseous Fuels] --> nat[Naturally Occurring]
    root --> bio[Biochemical]
    root --> manuf[Manufactured]
    nat --> ng[Natural Gas\nMethane 85 to 95 percent]
    nat --> cbm[Coal Bed Methane\nMethane 90 percent]
    bio --> biogas[Biogas\nMethane 50 to 70 percent]
    manuf --> pg[Producer Gas\nCO plus H2 plus N2]
    manuf --> wg[Water Gas\nCO plus H2]
    manuf --> rg[Refinery Gas\nC1 to C4 Mix]
    manuf --> lpg[LPG\nPropane plus Butane]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**"Differentiate between producer gas and water gas with respect to composition, calorific value, and manufacturing process."**

**Model Answer**:

| Property | Producer Gas | Water Gas |
|---|---|---|
| Manufacturing | Partial combustion of coke with limited air | Passing steam over hot coke |
| Primary Reaction | C + ½O₂ → CO (after C + O₂ → CO₂ and CO₂ + C → 2CO) | C + H₂O → CO + H₂ |
| CO Content (%) | 22 – 30 | 40 – 45 |
| H₂ Content (%) | 8 – 12 | 45 – 50 |
| N₂ Content (%) | 52 – 55 (very high) | 2 – 5 (very low) |
| Calorific Value (kJ/m³) | 4,200 – 7,500 | 10,500 – 12,500 |
| Process Type | Continuous (autothermal) | Cyclic (blow-run) |
| Heat Balance | Exothermic (self-sustaining) | Endothermic (needs blow phase) |

**Key conclusion**: Producer gas has lower calorific value due to high N₂ dilution, while water gas has higher calorific value but requires cyclic operation.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"What is Coal Bed Methane (CBM)? Mention its composition and two environmental advantages."**

**Model Answer**:
Coal Bed Methane (CBM) is the methane gas adsorbed within the microporous matrix of coal seams, released during mining or extracted via wells after dewatering the seam.

**Composition**: CH₄ (90 – 95%), N₂ + CO₂ + heavier hydrocarbons (5 – 10%)

**Environmental Advantages**:
1. **Mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions**: Methane is 25× more potent than CO₂ as a greenhouse gas. Capturing CBM that would otherwise vent to atmosphere reduces global warming impact.
2. **Reduction of mining accidents**: Methane accumulation in underground mines is a major explosion hazard (the infamous Jharia mine disasters). Capturing CBM improves mine safety significantly.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Describe in detail the manufacturing process, composition, and industrial applications of **producer gas**. Add a neat block flow diagram of a producer gas plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A producer gas has the following composition by volume: CO = 24%, H₂ = 10%, CH₄ = 3%, CO₂ = 4%, N₂ = 59%. Calculate **(i)** the theoretical air required for complete combustion per m³ of gas, and **(ii)** the gross calorific value of the gas in kJ/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Manufacturing Process of Producer Gas**:
Producer gas is manufactured in a vertical cylindrical reactor called a **gas producer**. The reactor has a refractory-lined steel shell, a coal/coke feed hopper at the top, a water-cooled tuyère at the bottom, and an ash-discharging grate.

**Step-by-step operation**:
1. **Coke charging**: Sized coke (or coal) is fed from the top hopper.
2. **Air injection**: A controlled, limited quantity of air is blown through the tuyère at the bottom.
3. **Combustion zone (top)**: Near the tuyère, where oxygen is most abundant, coke burns exothermically:
   C + O₂ → CO₂ + heat
4. **Reduction zone (middle)**: As the CO₂ rises through the hot coke bed, it gets reduced:
   CO₂ + C → 2CO (endothermic)
5. **Ash zone (bottom)**: Ash falls onto the rotating grate and is discharged.
6. **Hot gas exit**: Producer gas exits from the top, carrying sensible heat.

**Composition of Producer Gas**:
- CO: 22 – 30%
- H₂: 8 – 12%
- CH₄: 1 – 3%
- CO₂: 3 – 6%
- N₂: 52 – 55%

**Industrial Applications**:
1. Open-hearth steel furnaces and re-rolling mills (for cheap, controllable heat)
2. Glass and ceramic kilns
3. Power generation in gas engines
4. Feedstock for Fischer-Tropsch and methanol synthesis
5. Cement industry rotary kilns

**Block Flow Diagram** (as per SECTION 4.1 of these notes).

**Mark Distribution**:
- Process explanation (3 marks)
- Composition (1 mark)
- Applications (2 marks)
- Diagram (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given**: CO = 24%, H₂ = 10%, CH₄ = 3%, CO₂ = 4%, N₂ = 59%

**Part (i): Theoretical Air Required**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$O_2 \text{ required} = 0.5a + 0.5b + 2c + 3.5d - e$$

where a = CO%, b = H₂%, c = CH₄%, d = C₂H₆%, e = O₂%.

Substituting (note: there is no O₂ in fuel, no C₂H₆):

$$O_2 = 0.5(24) + 0.5(10) + 2(3) + 0 - 0$$

Computing each term:

$$= 12 + 5 + 6 + 0 = 23 \text{ m}^3 \text{ O}_2 \text{ per 100 m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

So per m³ of fuel:

$$O_2 = 0.23 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

Theoretical air required:

$$Air = \frac{O_2}{0.21} = \frac{0.23}{0.21} = 1.095 \text{ m}^3 \text{ air/m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

**Part (ii): Gross Calorific Value**

Using standard calorific values:
- CO: 12,620 kJ/m³
- H₂: 12,620 kJ/m³
- CH₄: 39,750 kJ/m³

$$GCV = \sum v_i \cdot CV_i$$

$$GCV = 0.24(12620) + 0.10(12620) + 0.03(39750)$$

Computing term by term:

$$= 3028.8 + 1262 + 1192.5$$

$$= 5483.3 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$

**Answer**: Air required = **1.095 m³/m³**, GCV = **5483.3 kJ/m³**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the manufacture of **water gas** with a labeled flow diagram and discuss the necessity of the **blow-run cycle**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the production of **biogas** from agricultural waste. Explain the stages of anaerobic digestion and the factors affecting biogas yield. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Water Gas Manufacturing**:
Water gas is a mixture of CO (~40%) and H₂ (~50%) produced by passing steam over a bed of incandescent coke (or coal) at 1000–1100 °C in a **water gas generator**.

**The Primary Reaction (endothermic)**:
$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**The Blow-Run Cycle**:
Since the steam-carbon reaction is endothermic, it absorbs heat from the coke bed. If steam is blown continuously, the bed temperature drops rapidly below the reaction threshold (~1000 °C) and reaction stops. To maintain continuous operation, the process alternates between two phases:

**Blow Phase (Air Blast — ~1 minute)**:
- Air is forced through the bed: C + O₂ → CO₂ (highly exothermic)
- This heats the coke to ~1100–1300 °C
- The flue gases (mostly CO₂ + N₂) exit through a stack
- No useful gas is collected during this phase

**Run Phase (Steam Blast — ~4–5 minutes)**:
- Steam is now blown through the hot coke bed
- The endothermic reaction C + H₂O → CO + H₂ proceeds vigorously
- Water gas (CO + H₂) is collected in a gas holder
- Bed temperature drops to ~800 °C, ending the run

**Cycle Repeats**: The valves automatically switch between blow and run, controlled by a clock mechanism and temperature sensors.

**Block Flow Diagram** (as per SECTION 4.2 of these notes).

**Necessity of the Blow-Run Cycle**:
1. The reaction is endothermic → continuous steam blow cools the bed
2. Air blow supplies heat to maintain reaction temperature
3. Without cycling, water gas production would stop within minutes
4. The cycle ensures continuous, steady supply of water gas

**Mark Distribution**:
- Process (2 marks)
- Blow-run explanation (2 marks)
- Diagram (1 mark)
- Necessity (2 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Biogas from Agricultural Waste — Anaerobic Digestion**:

**Feedstocks**: Cattle dung, poultry litter, rice straw, sugarcane bagasse, kitchen waste, slaughterhouse waste, municipal sewage.

**Reactor**: A sealed, air-tight digester (KVIC fixed-dome, floating-drum, or modern UASB/CSTR).

**Four Stages of Anaerobic Digestion**:

**Stage 1 — Hydrolysis**:
Complex organic polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, fats) are broken down by hydrolytic bacteria into simpler monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids).

$$(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + nH_2O \rightarrow nC_6H_{12}O_6$$

**Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation)**:
Acidogenic bacteria convert monomers into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂.

Example: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$
Example: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_3COOH$

**Stage 3 — Acetogenesis**:
Acetogenic bacteria convert VFAs and alcohols into acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂.

$$CH_3CH_2OH + H_2O \rightarrow CH_3COOH + 2H_2$$

**Stage 4 — Methanogenesis**:
Two parallel pathways by methanogenic archaea:
- Acetoclastic: $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$ (≈ 70% of CH₄)
- Hydrogenotrophic: $CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$ (≈ 30% of CH₄)

**Factors Affecting Biogas Yield**:

| Factor | Optimal Range | Effect of Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 30 – 40 °C (mesophilic) or 50 – 60 °C (thermophilic) | Below 10 °C → digestion stops; above 60 °C → kills bacteria |
| pH | 6.8 – 7.5 | Below 6.0 → methanogens die (souring) |
| C/N Ratio | 20:1 to 30:1 | Too high N → ammonia toxicity; too high C → slow reaction |
| Retention Time | 20 – 40 days | Less → incomplete digestion; more → wastage of reactor volume |
| Moisture Content | 85 – 95% water | Dry solids → hydrolysis limited |
| Mixing | Gentle, intermittent | Excessive → disrupts bacterial colonies |

**Byproducts**: Spent slurry (high-N organic fertilizer), recovered water.

**Mark Distribution**:
- Process overview (2 marks)
- 4 stages (3 marks)
- Factors table (2 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE MARKS in this module**:
> 
> 1. **Confusing blow phase vs. run phase**: Many students mix up which phase is exothermic and which is endothermic. Remember: **Air blow = exothermic (heats bed), Steam run = endothermic (produces gas)**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract O₂ already present in the fuel**: When calculating theoretical air, students often forget the "$-e$" term for oxygen in the fuel. This causes a small but consistent error in the air requirement.
> 
> 3. **Wrong stoichiometric factor**: For water gas production, students sometimes write C + 2H₂O → CO₂ + 2H₂, which is INCORRECT. The correct reaction is C + H₂O → CO + H₂ (Boudouard-type steam reforming).
> 
> 4. **Producer gas vs. Water gas calorific values**: Students sometimes claim producer gas has higher calorific value than water gas. WRONG — producer gas is heavily N₂-diluted and has lower calorific value.
> 
> 5. **Confusing GCV and NCV**: For hydrogen-rich fuels like water gas, NCV is significantly lower than GCV because water leaves as vapor (latent heat lost). Examiners may ask both.
> 
> 6. **Not labeling Mermaid diagrams**: Always label axes, units, and conditions on diagrams. A diagram without proper labels loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 7. **Biogas stages**: Missing the "hydrolysis" stage in the 4-step sequence is a common error. Always list **all four**: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 — Gaseous Fuels**

**Top 25 Things You MUST Know Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

1. Gaseous fuels are classified into **Naturally Occurring, Biochemical, and Manufactured**.

2. **Natural Gas**: Primarily CH₄ (85–95%); calorific value **38,000 – 42,000 kJ/m³**; main use is CNG and power.

3. **CBM (Coal Bed Methane)**: Methane adsorbed in coal seams; CH₄ ~90%; reduces mine explosions and GHG emissions.

4. **Producer Gas**: Made by partial combustion of coke with air; key reactions C + O₂ → CO₂ followed by CO₂ + C → 2CO; composition dominated by **N₂ (~55%)**, hence low CV (~5,500 kJ/m³).

5. **Water Gas**: Made by passing steam over hot coke; reaction **C + H₂O → CO + H₂**; CV ≈ **11,000 kJ/m³**; requires cyclic blow-run operation due to endothermicity.

6. **Blow-Run Cycle**: Air blow (exothermic, ~1 min) heats bed → Steam run (endothermic, ~4–5 min) produces gas → repeats.

7. **Carburetted Water Gas**: Water gas enriched with higher hydrocarbons (oils sprayed during run phase) to increase calorific value.

8. **Biogas**: Anaerobic digestion of organic matter; CH₄ (50–70%) + CO₂ (25–45%); CV ~ **22,000 kJ/m³**.

9. **Four Stages of Biogas Production**: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.

10. **Methanogenesis Reactions**: Acetoclastic (CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂) and Hydrogenotrophic (CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O).

11. **Biogas Reactor Conditions**: pH 6.8–7.5, Temperature 30–40 °C (mesophilic), C/N ratio 20:1 to 30:1.

12. **Refinery Gas**: C₁ to C₄ hydrocarbons from petroleum refining; main use is **petrochemical feedstock** (ethylene, propylene).

13. **LPG**: Mixture of propane (C₃H₈) and butane (C₄H₁₀); stored as **liquid at 7–10 bar**; highest CV among gaseous fuels (**46,000 – 50,000 kJ/m³**).

14. **LPG Critical Properties**: Propane T_c = 96.7 °C, Butane T_c = 152 °C — both above ambient → easy liquefaction at moderate pressure.

15. **LPG Combustion**: C₃H₈ + 5O₂ → 3CO₂ + 4H₂O; stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass) ≈ **15.67:1** for propane.

16. **Formula for Theoretical Air**: 
    $Air = \frac{100}{21}\left[0.5 CO + 0.5 H_2 + 2 CH_4 + 3.5 C_2H_6 - O_2\right]$ m³/m³

17. **Formula for GCV of Gas Mixture**: 
    $GCV = \sum v_i \cdot CV_i$ where $v_i$ is volume fraction.

18. **Standard CV values (kJ/m³)**: CO = 12,620; H₂ = 12,620; CH₄ = 39,750; C₂H₆ = 63,750; C₃H₈ = 92,000; C₄H₁₀ = 121,000.

19. **High-N₂ fuels have low CV**: Producer gas (~5,500) < Water gas (~11,000) < Biogas (~22,000) < Natural gas (~40,000) < LPG (~48,000).

20. **CNG vs LNG**: CNG = compressed natural gas at **~250 bar** (room temp); LNG = liquefied natural gas at **-162 °C**.

21. **Safety note**: LPG is **heavier than air** → leaks settle at floor level. Natural gas is **lighter than air** → leaks rise to ceiling.

22. **Environmental advantage of biogas**: Mitigates methane emissions and produces **organic fertilizer as a byproduct**.

23. **Producer Gas Plant Components**: Hopper, gas producer, tuyère, ash grate, gas cooler, scrubber, gas holder.

24. **Water Gas Generator Components**: Same as producer gas, but with **4-way valve** to switch between air and steam blasts.

25. **Comparison Master Formula**: When comparing gaseous fuels, always state **(a) composition, (b) calorific value, (c) manufacturing process, (d) applications** — this 4-point structure fetches full marks in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cleaning and Purification of Gaseous Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cleaning and Purification of Gaseous Fuels ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cleaning of Gaseous Fuels** refers to the removal of **particulate matter** (dust, soot, tar fog, entrained solids) from the crude gas stream, whereas **Purification** refers to the removal of **gaseous chemical impurities** such as H₂S, CO₂, COS, NH₃, HCN, naphthalene, and water vapour to meet pipeline, environmental, and downstream process specifications. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the treatment train is conventionally classified into four functional stages: | Stage | Operation | Typical Target Contaminant | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Stage I | Dust & Particulate Removal | Fly ash, char, soot, tar mist | | Stage II | Cooling & Condensation | Water vapour, light oils | | Stage III | Chemical Absorption | H₂S, CO₂, NH₃, HCN | | Stage IV | Polishing / Drying | Traces of H₂O, residual H₂S | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the gaseous fuel leaving a gasifier or refinery as the *air in a crowded, dusty kitchen* — it contains visible particles (dust), invisible chemical irritants (smell of H₂S — rotten eggs), and moisture (steam). Cleaning is like **vacuuming the floor** (removing solids), while purification is like **opening the exhaust fan and using an air purifier** (removing chemicals and moisture). Just as you cannot cook safely in a dusty kitchen, a gas turbine or catalytic reformer cannot run efficiently on untreated gas — it would corrode, foul, or fail emissions norms. ### 1.3 Why Purification is Mandatory — KTU Standpoint > [!IMPORTANT] > - **H₂S** in natural gas is restricted to **≤ 4 ppmv** for pipeline spec (PNG) and **≤ 1 grain/100 scf** for LNG feed. > - **Total Sulphur** (H₂S + COS + mercaptans) in automotive CNG: **≤ 50 mg/Nm³**. > - **Particulate load** in syngas feeding gas turbines: **≤ 1 mg/Nm³** to protect turbine blades from erosion. ### 1.4 Classification of Impurities in Crude Gaseous Fuels 1. **Particulate Impurities** — dust, fly ash, char, soot, tar fog. 2. **Sulphur Compounds** — H₂S, COS, CS₂, mercaptans (RSH), thiophenes. 3. **Carbon Dioxide** — acid gas, reduces calorific value. 4. **Nitrogen Compounds** — NH₃, HCN, nitrogen oxides. 5. **Oxygenated Hydrocarbons** — phenols, light oils, naphthalene. 6. **Water Vapour** — causes hydrate formation below **0 °C at 10 bar**. The standard metric used throughout this module for reporting impurity concentration in dry gas basis is **mg/Nm³** (at STP: **0 °C, 1.01325 bar**). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Cleaning Methods for Particulate Removal #### 2.1.1 Cyclone Separator The cyclone exploits **centrifugal force** on a swirling gas stream. The terminal velocity of a particle in the cyclone field is given by: $$ v_t \;=\; \frac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_g)\,v_i^{\,2}}{18\,\mu_g\,r} $$ where $d_p$ is particle diameter, $\rho_p$ is particle density, $\rho_g$ is gas density, $v_i$ is inlet tangential velocity, $\mu_g$ is gas dynamic viscosity, and $r$ is cyclone radius. > [!NOTE] > - **Cut-size (d₅₀)** — the particle diameter collected at **50 % efficiency** — is the design benchmark. > - Typical cyclone efficiency for **dₚ > 10 µm** is **90–99 %**; below 5 µm it drops sharply. > - **Pressure drop**: 50–150 mm WC (≈ 500–1500 Pa). #### 2.1.2 Bag Filter (Fabric Filter) A porous fabric (Nomex, PTFE, PPS) retains dust on its surface, forming a **filter cake** that acts as the actual filtering medium. Collection efficiency for sub-micron particles can exceed **99.9 %**. $$ \eta_{overall} \;=\; 1 \;-\; (1-\eta_{single})^{n} $$ where $\eta_{single}$ is single-fibre efficiency and $n$ is the number of fibre layers traversed. #### 2.1.3 Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) Particles are charged by a **corona discharge** (negative ions attach to dust) and collected on positively charged plates. Governing migration velocity (Deutsch–Anderson equation): $$ \eta \;=\; 1 \;-\; \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{w\,A}{Q}\right) $$ where $w$ is particle drift velocity (m/s), $A$ is collection plate area (m²), $Q$ is gas volumetric flow (m³/s). #### 2.1.4 Wet Scrubbing (Venturi Scrubber) The gas is forced through a converging–diverging throat where it contacts liquid droplets. Collection efficiency (Calvert correlation): $$ \eta \;=\; 1 \;-\; \exp\!\left(-\,k\,\sqrt{\Psi}\right) $$ where $\Psi$ is the inertial parameter $\left( \dfrac{\rho_p\, d_p^{\,2} v}{18\,\mu_g\, d_d} \right)$ and $k$ is a geometry constant. ### 2.2 Purification Methods for Gaseous Impurities #### 2.2.1 Chemical Absorption — Amine Treating The most widely used industrial process for **H₂S and CO₂** removal. Aqueous alkanolamines react reversibly with acid gases: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MEA:} \quad & 2\,\text{RNH}_2 \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{S} \;\rightleftharpoons\; (\text{RNH}_3)_2\text{S} \\[2pt] \text{DEA/MDEA:} \quad & \text{R}_2\text{NH} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{S} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{R}_2\text{NH}_2^+ \cdot \text{HS}^- \\[2pt] \text{CO}_2\text{ (MEA):} \quad & 2\,\text{RNH}_2 \;+\; \text{CO}_2 \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; (\text{RNH}_3)_2\text{CO}_3 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > - **MEA (Monoethanolamine)**: cheap, high capacity, but high corrosion and heat of reaction. > - **DEA (Diethanolamine)**: lower regeneration energy. > - **MDEA (Methyldiethanolamine)**: **selective for H₂S over CO₂** — preferred in **Claus plant tail-gas treating** (energy saving). #### 2.2.2 Physical Absorption — Selexol, Rectisol, Sulfinol Uses organic solvents that absorb acid gases **without chemical reaction** (or partially). Suitable for **high-pressure, high-acid-gas** natural gas. | Process | Solvent | Operating T (°C) | Pressure (bar) | Best For | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Selexol | Dimethyl ether of PEG | 0–50 | 20–80 | CO₂ / H₂S, dehydration | | Rectisol | Cold methanol | −30 to −60 | 20–80 | Syngas, IGCC | | Sulfinol | Sulfolane + DIPA + H₂O | 40–80 | 20–60 | Refinery gases | #### 2.2.3 Solid Adsorption — Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) Adsorbents: **Zeolite 5A / 13X, Activated Carbon, Silica Gel, Activated Alumina**. Operates on cyclic pressurisation–depressurisation. Equilibrium loading given by **Langmuir isotherm**: $$ q \;=\; \dfrac{q_m\,b\,P}{1 \,+\, b\,P} $$ where $q$ is loading (mol/kg), $q_m$ is monolayer capacity, $b$ is affinity constant, $P$ is partial pressure. #### 2.2.4 Membrane Separation Asymmetric polymeric (cellulose acetate, polysulfone, polyimide) or inorganic (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, Pd-alloy) membranes. Driving force = **partial pressure gradient**. Flux equation (solution–diffusion model): $$ J_i \;=\; \dfrac{P_i}{l}\,\bigl(P_{feed,i} \,-\, P_{perm,i}\bigr) $$ where $P_i$ is permeability, $l$ is membrane thickness. #### 2.2.5 Cryogenic Separation Cooling the gas stream below the condensation temperature of contaminants (**CO₂ sublimes at −78.5 °C**, **H₂S boils at −60 °C**). Used in **LNG liquefaction trains** for bulk CO₂/H₂S removal. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorise before exam.** | # | Formula / Concept | Variables | Units / Boundary | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $d_p^{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\,\mu_g\, w}{g(\rho_p-\rho_g)}}$ | Stokes settling | m; valid $Re < 0.1$ | | 2 | $\eta_{ESP} = 1 - \exp(-wA/Q)$ | Deutsch–Anderson | dimensionless | | 3 | $\eta_{bag} = 1 - (1-\eta_{single})^{n}$ | Bag filter | dimensionless | | 4 | $J_i = (P_i/l)\,(P_f - P_p)$ | Membrane flux | mol/(m²·s) | | 5 | $q = q_m b P/(1+bP)$ | Langmuir | mol/kg | | 6 | $Q_{amine} = (G_{acid}/\alpha)$ | Solvent circulation | kg/h | | 7 | $\Delta P_{cyclone} = \xi\,\rho_g v_i^{2}/2$ | Cyclone loss | Pa | | 8 | $\text{Acid gas loading} = n_{acid}/n_{amine}$ | mol/mol | typical 0.3–0.5 MEA | Where $\alpha$ is amine solution acid-gas loading capacity (mol acid gas per kg solution), $G_{acid}$ is acid gas molar flow (kmol/h). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Oil & Gas Sector**: Amine units + SCOT (Shell Claus Offgas Treating) at every refinery to meet **BS-VI / Euro-VI** sulphur norms. - **IGCC Power Plants**: Hot-gas cleanup using **ceramic candle filters (SiC)** operating at **800–900 °C** before the gas turbine. - **Coal-to-Liquid (CTL)**: Rectisol wash removes **99.9 % of total sulphur** before Fischer–Tropsch synthesis to protect Co/Ru catalyst. - **Hydrogen Production**: PSA produces **99.99 % purity H₂** from SMR off-gas. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cyclone Cut-Size Calculation (7 marks standard) > **Problem:** A cyclone separator treats **10 000 Nm³/h** of producer gas (ρ_g = 1.15 kg/m³, μ_g = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s) at **200 °C**. Inlet velocity v_i = 18 m/s, cyclone radius r = 0.40 m, particle density ρ_p = 2200 kg/m³. Calculate the **cut-size (d₅₀)** assuming Stokes regime. **Step 1 — Identify the controlling equilibrium.** At cut-size, centrifugal force on the particle equals the drag force (radially inward). $$ \frac{m_p\,v_t^{\,2}}{r} \;=\; 3\,\pi\,\mu_g\,d_p\,v_t $$ **Step 2 — Express v_t in terms of inlet tangential velocity (Rankine vortex assumption).** For a free-vortex, $v_t \cdot r \approx v_i \cdot r_i \approx v_i \cdot (D/2)$ for inlet at radius $D/2$. $$ v_t \;\approx\; \frac{v_i\,r_i}{r} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the geometric relation into the force balance.** $$ \frac{\pi d_p^{\,3} \rho_p}{6}\,\frac{v_i^{\,2} r_i^{\,2}}{r^{\,3}} \;=\; 3\,\pi\,\mu_g\,d_p\,\frac{v_i\,r_i}{r} $$ **Step 4 — Cancel π and rearrange for d_p.** $$ d_p^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu_g\,r^{\,2}}{\rho_p\,v_i\,r_i} $$ **Step 5 — Numerical substitution.** Take inlet radius r_i = D/2 = 0.40 m (assumed equal for simplicity at the wall of the cyclone). $$ d_p^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{18 \times 1.8\times 10^{-5} \times (0.40)^{2}}{2200 \times 18 \times 0.40} $$ $$ d_p^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{18 \times 1.8\times 10^{-5} \times 0.16}{15\,840} \;=\; \frac{5.184\times 10^{-5}}{15\,840} $$ $$ d_p^{\,2} \;=\; 3.272\times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^2 $$ $$ d_p \;=\; \sqrt{3.272\times 10^{-9}} \;=\; 5.72\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} \;=\; 57.2\,\mu\text{m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The cyclone will collect **50 % of particles of ~57 µm diameter**. For sub-micron soot from a partial oxidation reactor, a downstream **bag filter or ESP** is mandatory. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — ESP Surface Area Requirement (7 marks standard) > **Problem:** A 250 MW IGCC power plant produces syngas at **Q = 600 000 Nm³/h**. Target efficiency ≥ 99.5 %. Assume effective drift velocity w = 0.10 m/s for the fly-ash. Determine the required **collection plate area A** (m²). **Step 1 — Write Deutsch–Anderson equation.** $$ \eta \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{w A}{Q}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Insert η = 0.995 and solve for the exponential.** $$ 0.995 \;=\; 1 - e^{-wA/Q} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad e^{-wA/Q} = 0.005 $$ **Step 3 — Take natural log.** $$ -\frac{wA}{Q} \;=\; \ln(0.005) \;=\; -5.298 $$ **Step 4 — Solve for A.** $$ A \;=\; \frac{5.298 \times Q}{w} \;=\; \frac{5.298 \times 600\,000/3600}{0.10} $$ Convert Q to m³/s: 600 000 / 3600 = **166.67 m³/s**. $$ A \;=\; \frac{5.298 \times 166.67}{0.10} \;=\; \frac{883.0}{0.10} \;=\; 8\,830\,\text{m}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Practical sizing.** With plate spacing 0.30 m and effective height 10 m, plates per section = 0.30/2 (one plate per side) × 10 = 33 m² per section. $$ \text{Number of sections} = 8830 / 33 \approx 268 \text{ ducts} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Industrial practice:** ESPs are built as parallel lanes; real installations use **3–6 lanes × 4–6 fields** with intermittent rapper systems. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Fuel-Gas Cleaning | Impurity | Preferred Method | Alternative | Operating T (°C) | Removal Efficiency | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Particulates (coarse >10 µm) | Cyclone | Gravity settler | 25–900 | 70–90 % | | Particulates (fine <10 µm) | Bag filter / ESP | Venturi scrubber | 25–200 | 99–99.9 % | | Hot dust (syngas) | Ceramic candle | Cyclone | 800–900 | 99.9 % | | H₂S (high pressure) | Amine (MDEA) | Lo-Cat, Sulferox | 40–60 | > 99.9 % | | H₂S (trace) | ZnO / CuO bed | Iron sponge | 25–400 | < 1 ppm | | CO₂ bulk removal | MEA / Rectisol | PSA / Membrane | 25–120 | 95–99 % | | NH₃ | Water wash | Acid wash | 25–80 | > 95 % | | Tar / heavy HC | Oil scrubber | Electrostatic | 25–150 | 90 % | | Naphthalene | Oil wash | Refrigeration | 5–25 | ~ 99 % | | Water vapour | Glycol (TEG) | Molecular sieve | 25–200 | < 1 ppm | ### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Membrane Area for CO₂ Removal > **Problem:** A biogas stream (60 % CH₄, 40 % CO₂) at **20 bar** is treated with a polyimide membrane (permeance P_CO₂/l = 0.50 m³(STP)/(m²·h·bar), selectivity α = 50 for CO₂/CH₄). Permeate pressure = 1 bar. Compute the **membrane area** for 1000 Nm³/h feed to obtain 95 % CO₂ removal. **Step 1 — Average driving force for CO₂.** For a well-mixed model, partial pressure drop arithmetic mean: $$ \overline{\Delta P_{CO_2}} \;=\; \frac{(\Delta P_f + \Delta P_p)}{2} $$ With 95 % removal, CO₂ in retentate ≈ 0.05 × 0.40 = 0.02 (mol frac). CO₂ in permeate (mass balance) is concentrated. For an order-of-estimate use $\overline{\Delta P} \approx 6$ bar. **Step 2 — Flux.** $$ J \;=\; 0.50 \times 6 \;=\; 3.0 \;\text{m}^3\text{(STP)}/(\text{m}^2\cdot\text{h}) $$ **Step 3 — CO₂ to be removed volumetrically.** 40 % of 1000 Nm³/h = **400 Nm³/h**. **Step 4 — Membrane area.** $$ A \;=\; \frac{400}{3.0} \;\approx\; 133.3\;\text{m}^2 $$ > [!NOTE] > Multi-stage cascades (two-stage with recompression) reduce this area by **30–40 %**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Process Flow — Syngas Cleaning & Purification Train ```mermaid flowchart LR A[rawSyngas
fromGasifier] --> B[cyclone1
coarseDust] B --> C[cooler1
quenchCooling] C --> D[venturiScrub
fineTarAndDust] D --> E[ceramicCandle
hotGasFilter] E --> F[amineUnit
h2sAndCo2Removal] F --> G[cosHydrolyser
organicSulphur] G --> H[znoGuardBed
polishH2s] H --> I[cleanSyngas
toGTorFT] F --> J[richAmine
stripper] J --> K[h2sClausUnit
elementalSulphur] K --> L[sulphurRecovery
99.9Percent] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture of an Amine Treating Unit ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph AbsorberColumn A1[feedGas
bottom] --> A2[trayPacking1] A2 --> A3[trayPacking2] A3 --> A4[trayPacking3] A4 --> A5[sweetGas
topExit] end subgraph RegeneratorColumn B1[richAmine
topFeed] --> B2[reboilerSection] B2 --> B3[refluxDrum] B3 --> B4[acidGas
toClaus] B4 --> B5[leanAmine
bottomReturn] end A1 -->|richAmine| B1 B5 -->|reboiledLean| A1 A5 -->|product| Output((SweetFuelGas)) ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree — Method Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What is the
target contaminant} Q1 -->|Solids| S1[Select Mechanical
or ESP] Q1 -->|AcidGas| S2[Select Absorption
or Adsorption] Q1 -->|Water| S3[Select TEG or
MolecularSieve] S1 --> S1a{Size} S1a -->|Coarse| S1aa[Cyclone] S1a -->|Fine| S1ab[BagOrESP] S2 --> S2a{Loading} S2a -->|HighPressure| S2aa[PhysicalSelexol] S2a -->|LowPressure| S2ab[ChemicalAmine] S3 --> S3a{Temp} S3a -->|Ambient| S3aa[TEGContactor] S3a -->|Cryogenic| S3ab[MolSievePSA] ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Coal Gas Cleaning Train (Lurgi / BGL) ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[RawProducerGas
DustH2STarNH3] --> R2[WasteHeatBoiler] R2 --> R3[VenturiScrubber] R3 --> R4[TarSeparator] R4 --> R5[NH3Scrubber
WaterWash] R5 --> R6[AcidGasRemoval
Rectisol] R6 --> R7[CosShiftConverter] R7 --> R8[FinalZnOBed] R8 --> P1((PureFuelGas
PipelineSpec)) R6 --> S1[h2sClausUnit] S1 --> S2[SulphurPellets] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **cleaning** and **purification** of gaseous fuels with one industrial example of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - *Cleaning* = removal of **particulate impurities** (dust, soot, tar fog, ash) using mechanical/electrical methods. Example: **Cyclone separator** removing fly-ash from the flue gas of a pulverised-coal combustor (3 marks partial — definition 1, example 1, mechanism 1). - *Purification* = removal of **gaseous chemical impurities** (H₂S, CO₂, NH₃, COS) using absorption/adsorption. Example: **Amine absorber** removing H₂S from natural gas before LNG liquefaction (1 mark). - **Key distinction**: Cleaning targets solids → physical separation; Purification targets gases → chemical/physical absorption or adsorption (1 mark). **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Why is **H₂S** considered the most critical impurity in natural gas? List two methods of its removal. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - H₂S is **toxic** (TLV = 10 ppm), **corrosive** (forms FeS in pipelines), **catalyst poison** for downstream reformers, and produces **SO₂** on combustion causing acid rain (2 marks). - Removal methods: (i) **Amine absorption** (MDEA selective process), (ii) **Solid-bed adsorption** (ZnO fixed bed, Iron sponge, or activated carbon) (1 mark). --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Choice Pattern) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **Amine Treating Process** for removal of H₂S and CO₂ from natural gas. Discuss the role of **MDEA** and how it differs from **MEA**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A natural gas stream of **50 000 Nm³/h** contains **2.5 % H₂S** and **3.0 % CO₂** by volume. It is to be treated in a **20 wt % MEA solution** to reduce H₂S to **≤ 5 ppm** and CO₂ to **≤ 100 ppm**. The acid-gas loading of MEA in the lean solution is **0.12 mol acid gas / mol MEA**, and the rich loading is **0.50 mol/mol**. Calculate the **circulation rate of MEA solution** required (kg/h). Molecular weight of MEA = 61.08; density of 20 % solution ≈ 1020 kg/m³. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution to (a): > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flow steps (write in this order — 5 marks for flow, 2 marks for MEA/MDEA comparison):** 1. **Feed** (sour gas) enters the **absorber** at the bottom and rises through trays/packing at 30–60 °C and 5–70 bar. 2. **Lean amine** enters at the top (~40 °C) and counter-currently absorbs H₂S and CO₂, yielding **sweet gas** at the top. 3. **Rich amine** leaves the bottom and passes through a **flash drum** (releases dissolved hydrocarbons) → **lean/rich heat exchanger** → **regenerator (stripper)** at 110–130 °C. 4. **Stripper overhead** contains concentrated H₂S + CO₂ → routed to **Claus plant** for sulphur recovery. 5. **Reboiled lean amine** is cooled, filtered, and recycled to the absorber. > **MEA vs MDEA comparison:** > - **MEA**: Primary amine; high capacity, fast kinetics, **non-selective** (absorbs both H₂S and CO₂); high heat of regeneration (~1920 kJ/kg CO₂); corrosive. > - **MDEA**: Tertiary amine; **kinetically selective for H₂S over CO₂**; lower regeneration energy; preferred when **partial CO₂ slip** is acceptable (e.g., CO₂-EOR, claus tail-gas). ##### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Compute molar flow of acid gases.** At STP, **22.414 Nm³ = 1 kmol**. Total molar flow: $$ F_{gas} \;=\; \frac{50\,000}{22.414} \;=\; 2230.7\;\text{kmol/h} $$ **Step 2 — H₂S to be removed (assume inlet 2.5 %, outlet 5 ppm ≈ 0.0005 %).** $$ n_{H_2S,\,removed} \;=\; 2230.7 \times (0.025 - 0.000005) \;=\; 2230.7 \times 0.024995 $$ $$ n_{H_2S} \;=\; 55.76\;\text{kmol/h} $$ **Step 3 — CO₂ to be removed (assume inlet 3.0 %, outlet 100 ppm ≈ 0.01 %).** $$ n_{CO_2} \;=\; 2230.7 \times (0.030 - 0.0001) \;=\; 2230.7 \times 0.0299 $$ $$ n_{CO_2} \;=\; 66.70\;\text{kmol/h} $$ **Step 4 — Total acid gas to be absorbed.** $$ n_{acid} \;=\; 55.76 + 66.70 \;=\; 122.46\;\text{kmol/h} $$ **Step 5 — Δ loading (mol acid picked up per mol MEA).** $$ \Delta\alpha \;=\; 0.50 - 0.12 \;=\; 0.38\;\text{mol/mol} $$ **Step 6 — Moles of MEA required.** $$ n_{MEA} \;=\; \frac{n_{acid}}{\Delta\alpha} \;=\; \frac{122.46}{0.38} \;=\; 322.26\;\text{kmol/h} $$ **Step 7 — Mass flow of pure MEA.** $$ \dot{m}_{MEA} \;=\; 322.26 \times 61.08 \;=\; 19\,683\;\text{kg/h} $$ **Step 8 — Mass flow of 20 wt % solution.** $$ \dot{m}_{soln} \;=\; \frac{19\,683}{0.20} \;=\; 98\,416\;\text{kg/h} \;\approx\; 98.4\;\text{tonnes/h} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Cross-check**: Density of solution ≈ 1020 kg/m³ → volumetric flow ≈ 96.5 m³/h. Typical industrial amine circulation for this duty is 80–120 m³/h ✔ **reasonable**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly **forget the Δloading step** and divide total acid gas by 0.50 directly, halving the answer. **You lose 2 marks** for this. Also, **do not use 22.4 L** if the gas is reported at Nm³ (it is 22.414 m³/kmol at STP). Marks deducted for STP confusion: 1 mark. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice for KTU ESE]** **(a)** Discuss the construction and working of an **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** for cleaning flue gases from a coal-based power plant. State the **Deutsch–Anderson equation** and indicate the effect of increasing gas flow rate on collection efficiency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A coal-fired boiler produces **250 000 Nm³/h** of flue gas at **150 °C** loaded with **15 g/Nm³ of fly-ash**. Two parallel ESP lanes are to be installed with drift velocity **w = 0.12 m/s** and target efficiency **99.8 %**. Calculate the **required collection plate area per lane** and the **number of plates** if each plate is **0.30 m wide × 12 m tall**, with spacing of **0.30 m**. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution to (a): > **Construction (3 marks):** ESP consists of a **housing (shell)** containing alternately spaced **discharge electrodes** (thin wires) and **collecting plates**. A high-voltage **DC source (30–100 kV)** establishes a **corona discharge** near the wires, generating negative ions. The flue gas flows horizontally between the electrodes; particles become negatively charged and migrate to the positively charged plates where they are deposited. **Rappers** periodically strike the plates to dislodge the dust into hoppers. > **Working Principle (2 marks):** Negative gas ions **attach to dust particles** by **field charging** and **diffusion charging**. Charged particles migrate to the collecting electrode under the electrostatic force $F = qE$. Collected dust layer is removed by rapping; the dislodged dust falls into hoppers and is conveyed to a storage silo. > **Deutsch–Anderson equation (1 mark):** $$ \eta \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{w A}{Q}\right) $$ > **Effect of increasing Q (1 mark):** From the equation, $\eta$ decreases as $Q$ increases (negative exponent → larger negative number → smaller $\eta$). Doubling $Q$ reduces efficiency exponentially; the ESP must be oversized or bypassed at higher loads. ##### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Convert gas flow to m³/s (at operating conditions).** $$ Q_{op} \;=\; Q_{STP} \times \frac{T_{op}}{T_{STP}} \times \frac{P_{STP}}{P_{op}} $$ Assuming atmospheric pressure on both sides: $$ Q_{op} \;=\; \frac{250\,000}{3600} \times \frac{423}{273} \;=\; 69.44 \times 1.549 \;=\; 107.6\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Deutsch–Anderson.** $$ 0.998 \;=\; 1 - e^{-wA/Q_{op}} $$ $$ e^{-wA/Q} = 0.002 $$ $$ -\frac{wA}{Q} = \ln(0.002) = -6.215 $$ **Step 3 — Solve for total area.** $$ A_{total} \;=\; \frac{6.215 \times 107.6}{0.12} \;=\; \frac{668.7}{0.12} \;=\; 5573\;\text{m}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Per-lane area (2 lanes).** $$ A_{lane} \;=\; \frac{5573}{2} \;=\; 2786\;\text{m}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Number of plates per lane.** Single-plate area = 0.30 × 12 = 3.60 m². $$ N_{plates} \;=\; \frac{2786}{3.60} \;\approx\; 774\;\text{plates/lane} $$ For a **two-sided plate** (both faces collect), the plate is shared between two gas passages → use **per-lane face count** = 774 plates (each plate contributes 2 collection surfaces). > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Do not double-count the plate area. Each plate has **2 collecting faces**, but in area-counting you already used plate face-area. If your plate area is "per face", halve it; if it's "per plate", keep as is. **State your assumption** to gain the mark. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Cleaning & Purification of Gaseous Fuels** - **Cleaning** ↔ solids; **Purification** ↔ gases. Never interchange the two in viva. - **Cyclone** for coarse dust (>10 µm); **ESP / Bag filter** for fine dust; **Venturi scrubber** if wet operation is acceptable. - **Deutsch–Anderson**: $\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$ — universal ESP sizing formula. - **Amine selection mnemonic**: **M**DEA = **M**ore **D**eeply **A**ware (selective for H₂S); MEA = non-selective, high capacity. - **Rectisol** uses **cold methanol (−60 °C)** — preferred for **syngas** because it also captures HCN, NH₃, and COS simultaneously. - **PSA** uses **pressure cycling**, not temperature — different from **TSA (Temperature Swing Adsorption)** used in molecular-sieve dehydration. - **Membranes**: Permeation flux $J_i = P_i \Delta P_i / l$; selectivity $\alpha_{ij} = P_i / P_j$. - **Acid-gas loading** = mol acid gas / mol amine; Δ loading × MEA moles = moles of acid gas absorbed. - **H₂S pipeline spec**: **≤ 4 ppmv (India PNGRB)** and **≤ 0.25 grain/100 scf** (USA 49 CFR). - **Lurgi / BGL gas** cleaning sequence: Quench → Venturi → Tar sep → NH₃ wash → Rectisol → COS shift → ZnO polish. - **Hot-gas cleanup** uses **ceramic (SiC / Al₂O₃) candle filters** at 800–900 °C — critical for **IGCC** efficiency. - **Cut-size (d₅₀)** is the particle diameter at which the cyclone collects **50 %** of the feed; never confuse with **d₁₀₀** (absolute cutoff). - **Stokes regime** holds for $Re_p < 0.1$; for higher Re, use the **Stokes–Cunningham correction factor**. - Common mistake: writing $\eta_{ESP} = e^{-wA/Q}$ (without the leading 1). It is $1 - e^{-wA/Q}$ — **the formula begins with 1 minus**. - **Cryogenic separation** is used only when **CO₂ concentration is high (>50 %)** and the product is LNG; otherwise too energy-intensive. - **Membrane modules**: hollow-fibre (high area) vs spiral-wound (easier cleaning); inorganic membranes withstand >200 °C, polymeric <100 °C. > [!WARNING] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Valuation Tips:** > - Always draw a **labelled block diagram** in long answers — 2 marks free. > - When numerical answers ask for "circulation rate", give answer in **kg/h** OR **m³/h**, **not in moles**. > - Always include **units** and round to **3 significant figures**. > - Mention **operating temperature and pressure** whenever you name a process. > - In Part A, a single sentence with the **exact definition** fetches full 3 marks — do not over-elaborate.

topics.title: Manufactured Fuels: Agro Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manufactured Fuels: Agro Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Agro Fuels (Biofuels)** are solid, liquid, or gaseous fuels derived from **biomass of recent biological origin** — primarily agricultural crops, residues, animal waste, and forestry by-products — through biological, thermal, or chemical conversion processes. They are considered **carbon-neutral renewable energy carriers** because the CO₂ released during combustion is reabsorbed by the next crop cycle.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme context (OECHT832 – Fuel Engineering, Module 3), agro fuels are classified as **manufactured fuels** because they require deliberate conversion processes (fermentation, transesterification, gasification, anaerobic digestion, pyrolysis) to transform raw biomass into usable combustion-grade fuels.

**Standard Classification of Agro Fuels (KTU Syllabus Taxonomy):**

| Generation | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | Edible crop-based | Ethanol from sugarcane, biodiesel from soybean |
| **2nd Gen** | Non-edible / waste | Biodiesel from Jatropha, ethanol from lignocellulose |
| **3rd Gen** | Algal-based | Biodiesel from microalgae |
| **4th Gen** | Engineered | Synthetic biofuels via gasification + Fischer-Tropsch |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen Garden Engine"

Imagine your **kitchen garden** produces sugarcane stalks, used cooking oil, and vegetable peels. Instead of throwing them away, imagine a small refinery in your backyard:
- The **sugarcane juice** is fermented into **alcohol (bioethanol)** → mixed with petrol to run your two-wheeler.
- The **used cooking oil** is chemically reacted with methanol to become **biodiesel** → blended with diesel for your car.
- The **vegetable peels and cow dung** are sealed in an underground tank → microbes digest them anaerobically → produce **biogas (methane)** that cooks your food.
- The **dry stalks and sawdust** are burned in a limited-oxygen chamber → produce **producer gas** to drive a pump-set.

**All four are agro fuels — fuels grown, not mined.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Insight:** The global agro-fuel market reached **USD 130+ billion (2023)**. India targets **20% ethanol blending (E20)** in petrol by **2025–26** under the Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme. This makes agro fuels one of the most examinable topics in KTU's Energy/Fuel Engineering papers.

## 1.3 Primary Categories of Agro Fuels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Agro Fuels (most critical for KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> 1. **Bioethanol** — Liquid fuel from sugar/starch fermentation.
> 2. **Biodiesel** — Liquid fuel via transesterification of oils/fats.
> 3. **Biogas** — Gaseous fuel (mainly CH₄) via anaerobic digestion.
> 4. **Producer Gas** — Gaseous fuel (CO + H₂ + N₂) via gasification.

Additional categories covered in advanced scope:
- **Solid Agro Fuels:** Briquettes, pellets (from agricultural residues like bagasse, rice husk, groundnut shell).
- **Bio-methanol & Bio-DME:** Upgraded gaseous agro fuels.
- **Synthetic Biofuels:** Produced via gasification + catalytic synthesis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bioethanol — Production, Chemistry & Properties

### 2.1.1 Production Pathway (Block Logic)

Bioethanol is produced by **fermenting sugars** using yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*).

**Step 1 — Feedstock Selection**
- **Sugar-rich:** Sugarcane juice, molasses, sugar beet, sweet sorghum.
- **Starch-rich:** Corn, wheat, cassava, potato (require hydrolysis first).
- **Lignocellulosic** (2nd Gen): Rice straw, bagasse, wood chips (require enzymatic hydrolysis).

**Step 2 — Hydrolysis (only for starch/lignocellulose)**
$$\text{(C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5\text{)}_n + n\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Acid/Enzyme}} n\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$$

**Step 3 — Fermentation (core reaction)**
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (Yeast, 25–35°C)}} 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical yield:** 0.511 g ethanol / g glucose (i.e., **51.1% by mass**). Industrial yield is lower (~46–48%) due to yeast metabolism for cell growth.

**Step 4 — Distillation & Dehydration**
- Fermented broth contains ~10% ethanol → distillation concentrates to **95.6% (azeotropic mixture)**.
- Further dehydration via **molecular sieves** gives anhydrous ethanol (**99.5%+**).

### 2.1.2 Properties & Engine Use

- **Octane Rating (RON):** 108–110 (much higher than petrol's 84–98) → excellent anti-knock.
- **Calorific Value:** ~**29.7 MJ/kg** (vs petrol's 44 MJ/kg) → ~33% lower energy density.
- **Blending Ratios in India:** E5 (5%), E10 (10%), E20 (20%) — under **EBP Programme**.
- **Disadvantage:** Hygroscopic (absorbs water) → causes corrosion; lower vapor pressure → cold-start issues.

## 2.2 Biodiesel — Production, Chemistry & Properties

### 2.2.1 Transesterification (Core Reaction)

Biodiesel is produced by reacting **triglycerides** (vegetable oils / animal fats) with **methanol (or ethanol)** in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH / KOH) to yield **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)**.

$$\underbrace{\text{Triglyceride}}_{\text{Oil/Fat}} + 3\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{NaOH/KOH (1%)}]{\text{60–65°C, 1 hr}} \underbrace{3\text{R-COOCH}_3}_{\text{Biodiesel (FAME)}} + \underbrace{\text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{(OH)}_3}_{\text{Glycerol (by-product)}}$$

**Example (using oleic acid triglyceride):**
$$\text{C}_{57}\text{H}_{104}\text{O}_6 + 3\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3\text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Molar Ratio Required:** **1 : 3** (oil : methanol) theoretically; in practice **1 : 6** is used to drive equilibrium forward.

### 2.2.2 Properties

- **Cetane Number:** 45–65 (vs diesel's 40–55) → better cold-start, smoother combustion.
- **Calorific Value:** ~**37–40 MJ/kg** (only ~8–10% lower than diesel).
- **Viscosity:** Higher than diesel → reduces engine wear; blends like **B20 (20% biodiesel)** balance performance.
- **Oxygen Content:** ~10–11% (vs 0% in diesel) → cleaner combustion, lower PM emissions.
- **Drawback:** Higher cloud point → gelling in cold climates; solvent action on rubber seals.

### 2.2.3 Feedstock Comparison

| Feedstock | Oil Yield (L/ha) | Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| **Palm** | 5,000–6,000 | Highest yield; SE Asia |
| **Jatropha** | 1,500–2,000 | Marginal land; **India's preferred** |
| **Soybean** | 400–600 | USA / Brazil |
| **Rapeseed** | 1,000–1,200 | Europe |
| **Used Cooking Oil (UCO)** | Variable | Waste-based; most sustainable |

## 2.3 Biogas — Anaerobic Digestion

### 2.3.1 Process & Chemistry

Biogas is produced by **anaerobic (oxygen-free) digestion** of organic matter by **methanogenic bacteria** in four stages:
1. **Hydrolysis** → Proteins/Carbs/Fats → Amino acids/Sugars/Fatty acids.
2. **Acidogenesis** → VFAs (acetic, propionic, butyric) + H₂ + CO₂.
3. **Acetogenesis** → Acetic acid + H₂ + CO₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** → **CH₄ + CO₂** (biogas).

**Overall Reaction (Buswell Equation):**
$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2$$

For carbohydrate (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ — typical of cellulose:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3\text{CH}_4 + 3\text{CO}_2$$

### 2.3.2 Composition & Calorific Value

- **Composition:** CH₄ (~50–70%), CO₂ (~30–40%), H₂S (~0.1–0.5%), N₂, traces of H₂O.
- **Calorific Value:** **18–26 MJ/m³** (depending on CH₄ content).
- **India's flagship:** **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** — 5,000+ biogas/CBG plants targeted by 2025.

## 2.4 Producer Gas — Gasification

### 2.4.1 Process & Chemistry

Producer gas is obtained by **partial combustion (gasification)** of solid biomass (wood, charcoal, bagasse, coconut shell) in a **limited supply of air/oxygen**.

**The Four Zones in a Gasifier:**
1. **Drying Zone** (100–150°C) — moisture removal.
2. **Pyrolysis Zone** (150–600°C) — volatile release.
3. **Reduction Zone** (800–1,100°C) — char + CO₂/H₂O → CO + H₂.
4. **Combustion Zone** (1,100–1,500°C) — C + O₂ → CO₂.

**Key Reduction Reactions (the heart of producer gas):**
$$\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \text{(Boudouard — endothermic, +172 kJ/mol)}$$
$$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Water-gas — endothermic, +131 kJ/mol)}$$
$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \text{(Combustion — exothermic, -394 kJ/mol)}$$
$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Water-gas shift — exothermic, -41 kJ/mol)}$$

### 2.4.2 Composition

- **Composition:** CO (~20–30%), H₂ (~10–20%), N₂ (~45–55%), CH₄ (~2–5%), CO₂ (~5%).
- **Calorific Value:** **4.5–6.5 MJ/m³** (low due to N₂ dilution).
- **Use:** Rural pump-sets, gas engines, small-scale power generation.
