# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates the **exhaustive step-by-step working** of three signature KTU numerical problems. Every algebraic step, every stoichiometric conversion, and every numerical substitution is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Solved Problem A — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (3-mark derivation type)

**Problem:** Calculate the **theoretical mass of ethanol** obtainable from **1,000 kg of pure glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** via fermentation. Comment on the industrial yield limitation.

### Step 1 — Write the balanced fermentation reaction

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$$

### Step 2 — Identify molecular masses

- Molar mass of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) = $6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 72 + 12 + 96 = \mathbf{180 \text{ g/mol}}$
- Molar mass of ethanol (C₂H₅OH) = $2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 24 + 6 + 16 = \mathbf{46 \text{ g/mol}}$

### Step 3 — Apply stoichiometric ratio

From the balanced equation: **1 mole glucose → 2 moles ethanol**

$$\text{Moles of glucose} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{180 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 5.556 \text{ kmol}$$

$$\text{Moles of ethanol} = 2 \times 5.556 = 11.111 \text{ kmol}$$

### Step 4 — Convert moles to mass

$$\text{Mass of ethanol} = 11.111 \text{ kmol} \times 46 \text{ kg/kmol} = \mathbf{511.1 \text{ kg}}$$

### Step 5 — Express as percentage yield

$$\text{Theoretical Yield (\%)} = \frac{511.1}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{51.1\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial reality:** Actual yield is **46–48%** because ~5–6% of glucose is consumed by yeast for cell growth and respiration. This ~5% loss is a **favourite KTU question**.

---

## 3.2 Solved Problem B — Stoichiometric Oxygen for Biogas Combustion (7-mark typical)

**Problem:** A biogas sample has the following composition by volume: CH₄ = 65%, CO₂ = 32%, N₂ = 3%. Calculate:
**(a)** the **stoichiometric oxygen required** for complete combustion per m³ of biogas.
**(b)** the **air required** (assuming 21% O₂ by volume in air).
**(c)** the **percentage composition of dry flue gas** (assuming complete combustion).

### Step 1 — Combustion Reactions of Individual Components

Only **CH₄** is combustible; CO₂ and N₂ are inert in this problem.

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ for CH₄ Fraction

Take basis: **1 m³ of biogas → 0.65 m³ CH₄**

From the equation: **1 vol CH₄ requires 2 vol O₂**

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ required (for CH}_4) = 0.65 \times 2 = \mathbf{1.30 \text{ m}^3}$$

CO₂ does not react with O₂ → contributes **0 m³ O₂**.
N₂ is inert → contributes **0 m³ O₂**.

**Total stoichiometric O₂ = 1.30 m³**

### Step 3 — Air Required

$$\text{Air Volume} = \frac{\text{O}_2}{0.21} = \frac{1.30}{0.21} = \mathbf{6.19 \text{ m}^3 \text{ air / m}^3 \text{ biogas}}$$

### Step 4 — Combustion Products (Dry Flue Gas Basis)

| Component | Source | Volume (m³) |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ (from CH₄ combustion) | $0.65 \times 1 = 0.65$ | 0.65 |
| CO₂ (original in biogas) | inert | 0.32 |
| N₂ (from air) | $6.19 \times 0.79 = 4.89$ | 4.89 |
| N₂ (original in biogas) | inert | 0.03 |
| H₂O (from CH₄) | $0.65 \times 2 = 1.30$ | (excluded — wet) |

**Dry flue gas total volume** = $0.65 + 0.32 + 4.89 + 0.03 = \mathbf{5.89 \text{ m}^3}$

### Step 5 — Percentage Composition of Dry Flue Gas

$$\%\text{CO}_2 = \frac{0.97}{5.89} \times 100 = \mathbf{16.47\%}$$

$$\%\text{N}_2 = \frac{4.92}{5.89} \times 100 = \mathbf{83.53\%}$$

**Verification:** $16.47 + 83.53 = 100.00\%$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Solved Problem C — Gasification Stoichiometry (14-mark / 7+7 type)

**Problem:** **5 kg of carbon** is gasified with **steam only** in a downdraft gasifier at 1,000°C. The producer gas obtained has the composition: CO = 40%, H₂ = 30%, CO₂ = 5%, N₂ = 25% (by volume). Calculate:
**(a)** Mass of steam consumed.
**(b)** Volume of producer gas at NTP.
**(c)** Calorific value of producer gas.

**Calorific values (lower heating values):** CO = 12.6 MJ/m³, H₂ = 10.8 MJ/m³, CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³.

### Step 1 — Apply Key Reduction Reactions

$$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$
$$\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$
$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

### Step 2 — Mass Balance on Carbon

Total carbon fed = 5 kg = $\frac{5000}{12} = 416.67$ mol C.

In producer gas, carbon is distributed in CO and CO₂ (N₂ and H₂ carry no C):

$$\text{Total carbon atoms in gas} = (\text{CO} + \text{CO}_2) \text{ mol}$$

Let total producer gas moles = $n$ mol.

$$\text{Moles of CO} = 0.40n, \quad \text{Moles of CO}_2 = 0.05n$$

$$\text{Carbon balance: } 0.40n + 0.05n = 416.67$$

$$0.45n = 416.67$$

$$n = \mathbf{925.93 \text{ mol of producer gas}}$$

### Step 3 — Hydrogen / Oxygen Balance to Find Steam Consumed

Moles of H₂ produced = $0.30 \times 925.93 = 277.78$ mol.

From reaction (i): **1 mol H₂O → 1 mol H₂**. So minimum steam required = 277.78 mol.

But reaction (iii) shows that CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ also consumes steam. Let extra steam consumed = $x$ mol. Then:

$$x = \text{CO}_2 \text{ produced} = 0.05 \times 925.93 = 46.30 \text{ mol}$$

**Total steam consumed** = $277.78 + 46.30 = 324.07$ mol

$$\text{Mass of steam} = 324.07 \times 18 \text{ g} = 5,833 \text{ g} = \mathbf{5.83 \text{ kg}}$$

### Step 4 — Volume of Producer Gas at NTP

At NTP, 1 mol of ideal gas = **22.4 L = 0.0224 m³**.

$$V_{\text{producer gas}} = 925.93 \times 0.0224 = \mathbf{20.74 \text{ m}^3}$$

### Step 5 — Calorific Value of Producer Gas

Using volumetric LHV for combustible components (CO and H₂ only):

$$\text{CV} = (0.40 \times 12.6) + (0.30 \times 10.8) = 5.04 + 3.24 = \mathbf{8.28 \text{ MJ/m}^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Typical producer gas CV ranges **4.5–6.5 MJ/m³** under industrial conditions (with N₂ dilution from air). Our higher value (8.28) reflects **pure steam gasification** (no N₂ dilution) — which is why **air-blown gasifiers give lower CV** than oxygen/steam-blown ones.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are patterned strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme OECHT832 – Fuel Engineering End Semester Examination** style. Two-part structure with internal choice is maintained per the latest KTU regulation. Each question carries the simulated KTU year tag for reference.

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual / Definition)

### Question 1 (Part A – 3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q:** Define **agro fuels**. List any **four categories** of agro fuels with **one example** for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Agro fuels** are solid, liquid, or gaseous fuels derived from biomass of recent biological origin (agricultural crops, residues, animal waste) through biological, thermal, or chemical conversion processes.

**Four Categories:**

| # | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Liquid — Bioethanol** | Ethanol from sugarcane molasses (fermentation) |
| 2 | **Liquid — Biodiesel** | Methyl ester from Jatropha oil (transesterification) |
| 3 | **Gaseous — Biogas** | Methane-rich gas from cow dung (anaerobic digestion) |
| 4 | **Gaseous — Producer Gas** | CO + H₂ from wood gasification |

---

### Question 2 (Part A – 3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** With a balanced chemical equation, explain the **production of biogas** from cattle dung.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Biogas is produced by **anaerobic digestion** (absence of O₂) of cattle dung by methanogenic bacteria in four sequential stages: hydrolysis → acidogenesis → acetogenesis → methanogenesis.

**Overall Reaction (Buswell Equation for cellulose-type substrate):**
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Methanogenic Bacteria}} 3\text{CH}_4 + 3\text{CO}_2$$

**Composition:** CH₄ (50–70%) + CO₂ (30–40%) + traces of H₂S, N₂, H₂O.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Set A]**

**(a)** With a neat **block diagram**, describe the **production of bioethanol** from sugarcane molasses. Discuss the chemistry of fermentation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **mass of ethanol** that can be theoretically obtained from **2,500 kg of glucose**. If the **actual yield is 47%**, find the **actual mass of ethanol produced** and the **mass of CO₂ released** during fermentation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The production chain consists of six stages:
1. **Feedstock Preparation:** Sugarcane molasses (a viscous by-product of sugar refining) is diluted with water to ~15–20% sugar concentration.
2. **Hydrolysis (if required):** Starch-based feedstocks are hydrolyzed with dilute H₂SO₄ or enzymes to yield glucose.
3. **Fermentation:** The sugar solution is charged into fermentation tanks along with yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) at **25–35°C and pH 4–5** for 48–72 hours.
4. **Distillation:** The fermented "beer" (~10% ethanol) is distilled in a multi-column distillation unit to concentrate to **95.6% (azeotropic)**.
5. **Dehydration:** Azeotropic ethanol is passed through **molecular sieves (3Å zeolite)** to remove the residual 4.4% water → produces anhydrous ethanol (>99.5%).
6. **Storage & Blending:** Anhydrous ethanol is denatured and blended with petrol in ratios E5, E10, or E20.

**2. Fermentation Chemistry (4 marks):**

The balanced stoichiometric reaction:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (Yeast)}} 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

**Molecular Masses:**
- Glucose: $6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180 \text{ g/mol}$
- Ethanol: $2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46 \text{ g/mol}$
- CO₂: $12 + 2(16) = 44 \text{ g/mol}$

**Yield:** 180 g glucose → 92 g ethanol + 88 g CO₂ → **Theoretical yield = 51.1%**

**Industrial Reality:** Actual yield is **~46–48%** due to yeast metabolism (5–6% sugar consumed for cell growth), product inhibition above ~12% ethanol, and contamination.

**Key Properties of Bioethanol:**
- Calorific value: **29.7 MJ/kg** (vs petrol 44 MJ/kg → 33% lower).
- Octane rating: **RON 108–110** (excellent anti-knock).
- Hygroscopic → corrosion risk; lower vapor pressure → cold-start difficulty.
- Blends: E5, E10, E20 under India's EBP Programme.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Mass of glucose = 2,500 kg; Actual yield = 47%.

**Step 1 — Moles of Glucose**
$$\text{Moles} = \frac{2500}{180} = 13.889 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Mass of Ethanol**

From stoichiometry: **1 kmol glucose → 2 kmol ethanol**.

$$\text{Moles of ethanol (theory)} = 2 \times 13.889 = 27.778 \text{ kmol}$$

$$\text{Theoretical mass} = 27.778 \times 46 = \mathbf{1277.8 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Mass of Ethanol**
$$\text{Actual mass} = 1277.8 \times 0.47 = \mathbf{600.6 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of CO₂ Released (Stoichiometric basis on actual ethanol)**

For every 2 mol ethanol, 2 mol CO₂ are produced → **molar ratio C₂H₅OH : CO₂ = 1 : 1**.

$$\text{Moles of CO}_2 = \text{Moles of ethanol (actual)} = \frac{600.6}{46} = 13.057 \text{ kmol}$$

$$\text{Mass of CO}_2 = 13.057 \times 44 = \mathbf{574.5 \text{ kg}}$$

**Final Answer Block:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Theoretical ethanol mass | **1277.8 kg** |
| Actual ethanol mass | **600.6 kg** |
| CO₂ released | **574.5 kg** |

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Set B]**

**(a)** Describe the **transesterification process** for biodiesel production with a balanced chemical equation. Compare the properties of **biodiesel with conventional diesel**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biogas plant produces **100 m³/day** of biogas with composition **CH₄ = 60%, CO₂ = 38%, N₂ = 2%** (by volume). Calculate:
  - **(i)** The **volume of air required** for complete combustion per day.
  - **(ii)** The **calorific value of the biogas** in MJ/m³.  
  *(Given LHV: CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³, H₂ = 10.8 MJ/m³, CO = 12.6 MJ/m³)*. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Transesterification Process Description (4 marks):**

Biodiesel is produced by reacting **triglycerides** (vegetable oils / animal fats) with **short-chain alcohols** (methanol preferred — cheaper and easier to separate) in the presence of an **alkaline catalyst** (NaOH or KOH at 1% w/w) at **60–65°C for ~1 hour** with continuous stirring.

**The Reaction:**
$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{NaOH/KOH}]{\text{60–65°C, 1 hr}} 3\text{R-COOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{(OH)}_3$$

**Example (using oleic acid triglyceride):**
$$\text{C}_{57}\text{H}_{104}\text{O}_6 + 3\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3\text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3$$

**Process Steps:**
1. Pre-treatment of oil (filtering, degumming, acid wash to remove free fatty acids).
2. Catalyst dissolution in methanol → sodium methoxide.
3. Reaction in stirred reactor for 1 hour.
4. Gravity separation in decanter — biodiesel (top) and glycerol (bottom).
5. Water washing of biodiesel to remove soap/catalyst residues.
6. Drying under vacuum.
7. Quality testing (viscosity, cetane, acid value).

**2. Comparison of Biodiesel vs Diesel (3 marks):**

| Property | Biodiesel (B100) | Diesel |
|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | **37–40** | 42–45 |
| Cetane Number | **45–65** | 40–55 |
| Density (kg/m³) | 880 | 830 |
| Viscosity (mm²/s @ 40°C) | 4–6 | 2–4 |
| Flash Point (°C) | **>130 (safer)** | 60–80 |
| Oxygen Content (%) | ~10–11 | 0 |
| Sulfur Content | **<10 ppm** | <50 ppm (BS-VI: 10 ppm) |
| Engine Modifications | None for blends | None |
| Cold-flow properties | Poor (cloud point high) | Better |
| Biodegradability | 95% in 28 days | Slow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Biodiesel's **higher cetane** → smoother combustion; its **oxygen content** → cleaner burning (~50% less PM, ~70% less CO). Its drawbacks are **NOx increase (~10%)** due to higher flame temperatures and **gelling at low temperatures**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** 100 m³/day biogas; CH₄ = 60%, CO₂ = 38%, N₂ = 2%.

**Step 1 — Component Volumes in 100 m³ Biogas**

- Volume of CH₄ = 100 × 0.60 = **60 m³**
- Volume of CO₂ = 100 × 0.38 = 38 m³ (inert)
- Volume of N₂ = 100 × 0.02 = 2 m³ (inert)

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ for Combustion**

Only CH₄ is combustible:
$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

1 m³ CH₄ requires 2 m³ O₂.

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 60 \times 2 = 120 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per day}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of Air Required**

$$\text{Air volume} = \frac{120}{0.21} = \mathbf{571.43 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per day}}$$

**Step 4 — Calorific Value of Biogas (per m³)**

Only CH₄ contributes to calorific value:

$$\text{CV} = 0.60 \times 35.8 + 0.38 \times 0 + 0.02 \times 0 = \mathbf{21.48 \text{ MJ/m}^3}$$

**Final Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Air required per day | **571.43 m³** |
| Calorific value of biogas | **21.48 MJ/m³** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top 3 Reasons Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Azeotrope:** Many students incorrectly state that distillation gives 100% ethanol. The correct ceiling is **95.6% v/v** — beyond this, an azeotrope forms. Anhydrous ethanol requires **molecular sieve dehydration**.
> 2. **Mixing Up Stoichiometric Ratios:** Transesterification requires **1 : 3 molar** (oil : methanol) **theoretically**, but in practice a **1 : 6 ratio** is used to push equilibrium forward. Writing 1 : 6 as theoretical is a common mistake.
> 3. **Confusing Calorific Value Bases:** Bioethanol CV = **29.7 MJ/kg** (lower heating value, mass basis), while Biogas CV = **~21 MJ/m³** (volume basis, depends on CH₄ %). Mixing kg and m³ units in numerical answers is the #1 cause of full-mark loss.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Mile Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3 (Agro Fuels)**

### ✅ Core Definitions (must memorize verbatim)
- **Agro fuels** = biomass-derived solid, liquid, or gaseous fuels via biological / thermal / chemical conversion.
- **Bioethanol** = ethanol produced by fermentation of sugars/starches (theoretical yield **51.1%** from glucose).
- **Biodiesel** = mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids (FAME) from transesterification of oils/fats.
- **Biogas** = CH₄ + CO₂ mixture produced by anaerobic digestion (typically **60:40**).
- **Producer gas** = CO + H₂ + N₂ mixture produced by partial oxidation of biomass (CV **4.5–6.5 MJ/m³**).

### ✅ Must-Know Balanced Equations (5 equations)
- Fermentation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$
- Ethanol combustion: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Transesterification: Triglyceride + 3 CH₃OH → 3 FAME + Glycerol
- Methane combustion: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Gasification (water-gas): $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$

### ✅ Numerical Constants to Memorize
- Molar masses: Glucose = **180**, Ethanol = **46**, CO₂ = **44**, Methanol = **32**, Water = **18**.
- Calorific values: Ethanol = **29.7 MJ/kg**, Biodiesel = **37–40 MJ/kg**, Biogas = **18–26 MJ/m³**, Producer Gas = **4.5–6.5 MJ/m³**.
- Stoichiometric air for CH₄ = **9.52 m³ air / m³ CH₄**.

### ✅ Common KTU "Compare" Pairs
- Bioethanol vs Petrol (octane, CV, hygroscopicity).
- Biodiesel vs Diesel (cetane, CV, NOx).
- Biogas vs Natural Gas (CV, composition).
- Producer gas vs Water gas (N₂ content, CV).

### ✅ Industrial / Government Schemes (favourite 2-mark KTU questions)
- **EBP Programme** (Ethanol Blended Petrol) — E20 target by 2025–26.
- **SATAT** (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) — 5,000 CBG plants by 2025.
- **National Biodiesel Mission** — Jatropha plantation on marginal lands.
- **GOBAR-DHAN** — Galvanizing Organic Bio-Agro Resources — converting cattle dung to biogas + compost.

### ✅ Process-Specific Engineering Details (high-yield)
- **Fermentation conditions:** 25–35°C, pH 4–5, yeast *S. cerevisiae*, 48–72 hrs.
- **Transesterification conditions:** 60–65°C, 1 hr, NaOH catalyst 1%, 1:6 molar ratio (practical).
- **Anaerobic digestion stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- **Gasifier zones:** Drying → Pyrolysis → Reduction → Combustion (top → bottom in downdraft).

### ✅ Environmental Advantages (commonly asked 3-mark question)
- **Carbon neutrality:** CO₂ released = CO₂ absorbed by next crop cycle.
- **Lower emissions:** SO₂, CO, PM, unburnt HC are all reduced (especially biodiesel).
- **Rural employment:** Decentralized production boosts rural economy.
- **Energy security:** Reduces fossil fuel import dependency.

---

**END OF NOTES — OECHT832 FUEL ENGINEERING, MODULE 3, AGRO FUELS**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio-Fuels: Types, Production Processes and Technologies, Applications
Combustion Stoichiometry and Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Combustion Stoichiometry and Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Combustion Stoichiometry** is the branch of combustion engineering that deals with the exact *quantitative mass and mole relationships* between the fuel, oxidizer (typically air or pure oxygen), and the products of combustion. The term is derived from the Greek word *stoicheion* (element) and *metrein* (to measure), literally meaning the *measurement of elemental proportions*. It addresses two fundamental questions central to fuel engineering:

1. **How much air (or oxygen) is chemically required to burn a unit mass or mole of fuel completely?** → The **Stoichiometric Air Requirement** (also called *theoretical air* or *chemically correct air*).
2. **What is the composition and quantity of combustion products formed under that condition?** → The **Stoichiometric Flue Gas Analysis**.

**Combustion Thermodynamics**, on the other hand, applies the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (energy conservation) and the **Second Law** (entropy / availability analysis) to combustion systems. It quantifies the *energy released* (calorific value), the *maximum attainable temperature* (adiabatic flame temperature), the *equilibrium composition* of products, and the *work potential* (exergy) of a reacting fuel-oxidizer mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 4 expects students to master *both* the mole-balance approach (used in gas-fuel problems) and the *mass-balance / ultimate-analysis* approach (used in solid and liquid fuel problems). Marks are split roughly **40 % Stoichiometry + 60 % Thermodynamics**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of combustion as **baking a cake**:

- The **fuel** is the *flour, sugar, and butter* (the energy-bearing ingredients).
- The **oxygen (from air)** is the *egg* that binds and reacts with everything.
- **Stoichiometric air** is the **exact, perfect amount of egg** — not too little (cake stays dry and raw), not too much (cake tastes eggy and wastes energy heating up excess water).
- **Excess air** is the *extra egg* you add to be safe in a real kitchen.
- The **calorific value** is the *sweetness and richness* of the cake — the chemical energy stored in the bonds.
- The **adiabatic flame temperature** is the *peak oven temperature* you would theoretically measure if the oven walls were perfectly insulated (no heat loss).

If you under-burn the fuel (insufficient air) → **unburnt hydrocarbons, CO, soot, smoke** → pollution + lost energy. If you over-burn (excess air) → the *extra nitrogen and unused oxygen* absorb heat, *quench* the flame temperature, and *reduce furnace efficiency*. **Stoichiometry finds the perfect recipe.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants You Must Memorise (will appear in numericals):**
> - Molecular weight of air **M_air = 28.97 ≈ 29 kg/kmol**
> - O₂ in air by volume = **21 %**, by mass = **23.2 %**
> - N₂/O₂ mole ratio in air = **79/21 = 3.76**
> - Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 25 °C = **2442 kJ/kg** (or 585 kcal/kg)
> - Standard enthalpy of formation of H₂O (l) = **-285.8 MJ/kmol**, of CO₂ = **-393.5 MJ/kmol**
> - Universal gas constant **R = 8.314 kJ/kmol·K**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Stoichiometric Quantities

A KTU numerical on stoichiometry will *always* reduce to one of these three calculations:

**A. Stoichiometric Oxygen per kmol of fuel (C_xH_yO_z):**

$$O_2 \text{ required (kmol)} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}$$

**B. Stoichiometric Air per kmol of fuel:**

$$\text{Air}_{stoich} = \left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right) \times \frac{100}{21} \text{ kmol of air}$$

Equivalently, in terms of O₂ and N₂ separately:

$$n_{O_2} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}, \qquad n_{N_2} = 3.76 \times n_{O_2}$$

**C. Air–Fuel Ratio (A/F) on a mass basis:**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{mass} = \frac{n_{air} \times 29}{\text{Molecular weight of fuel}}$$

### 2.2 The Two Universal Combustion Reactions You MUST Know

**Hydrocarbon (gas fuel) — stoichiometric, dry products:**

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O$$

**Carbon monoxide (incomplete combustion / flue gas):**

$$CO + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

**Why these two matter in KTU papers:** Every flue-gas Orsat analysis (CO₂ %, O₂ %, CO %, N₂ %) ultimately depends on the stoichiometric oxygen equation above. If you remember just these two reactions, you can solve 80 % of the Part B numericals.

### 2.3 Stoichiometry on a Mass Basis (Solid / Liquid Fuels)

For a solid fuel with ultimate analysis (C %, H %, O %, S %, N %, Ash %), the air required **per kg of fuel** is:

$$\text{stoichiometric air (kg/kg fuel)} = \frac{8}{3}\,C + 8\,H + S - O$$

where the constants come from:

- For C + O₂ → CO₂: 1 kg C needs **8/3 kg O₂** (since 12 kg C reacts with 32 kg O₂)
- For 4H + O₂ → 2H₂O: 1 kg H needs **8 kg O₂** (since 4 kg H reacts with 32 kg O₂)
- For S + O₂ → SO₂: 1 kg S needs **1 kg O₂** (since 32 kg S reacts with 32 kg O₂)
- Oxygen already present in fuel *subtracts* from the requirement.

Converting to *air* (since air = O₂ / 0.232 by mass):

$$\boxed{\text{stoichiometric air (kg/kg fuel)} = \frac{11.6\,C + 34.8\,(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S}{100}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The factor 11.6** = 8/3 ÷ 0.232 ; **34.8** = 8 ÷ 0.23 (using 23 % by mass rounded); **4.35** = 1 ÷ 0.23. KTU examiners *love* this formula — practice deriving it from first principles in the exam, do not just memorise!

### 2.4 Excess Air Theory

Real furnaces always supply **more** than the stoichiometric air. Define:

$$\%\,\text{Excess Air} = \frac{\text{Actual air} - \text{Stoichiometric air}}{\text{Stoichiometric air}} \times 100$$

$$\%\,\text{Theoretical Air} = \frac{\text{Actual air}}{\text{Stoichiometric air}} \times 100$$

Effects of excess air (must be mentioned in any 7-mark theory question):

- ↓ adiabatic flame temperature (quenching effect)
- ↑ flue gas volume (more heat carried out as sensible heat in stack gases)
- ↑ furnace heat loss → **lower thermal efficiency**
- BUT: ensures *complete* combustion, no CO, no soot → *lower* pollution

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | O₂ required per kmol of C_xH_yO_z | $x + y/4 - z/2$ | kmol O₂/kmol fuel | General gaseous fuel |
| 2 | Stoich. air per kmol fuel | $(x+y/4-z/2) \times (100/21)$ | kmol air | 100/21 = 4.76 |
| 3 | Stoich. air per kg of solid fuel | $\frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ (kg O₂/kg fuel) | kg O₂/kg fuel | Ultimate analysis basis |
| 4 | Stoich. air (mass) per kg fuel | $\frac{11.6C + 34.8(H-O/8) + 4.35S}{100}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Use 23 % O₂ in air |
| 5 | Air–Fuel ratio (mass) | $A/F = n_{air}\times 29 / M_{fuel}$ | kg air/kg fuel | For gases |
| 6 | % Excess air | $\frac{A_{actual}-A_{stoich}}{A_{stoich}}\times 100$ | % | Standard definition |
| 7 | Higher Calorific Value (HCV) | $-\sum \nu_i \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) + \sum \nu_i \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$ | kJ/kmol | From formation enthalpies |
| 8 | HCV – LCV relation | $HCV - LCV = 9 \times \dfrac{H}{100} \times 2442$ | kJ/kg fuel | H is % H in fuel |
| 9 | Dulong's formula (HCV) | $\dfrac{8080\,C + 34500\,(H-O/8) + 2240\,S}{100}$ | kJ/kg | C, H, O, S in % |
| 10 | Air supplied / O₂ supplied (vol) | $\dfrac{100}{21} = 4.76$ | — | For stoichiometric O₂ |
| 11 | Stoich. flue gas / air ratio | $1 + \frac{79}{21}\times$ stoich. O₂ | mol/mol | For complete combustion |
| 12 | Adiabatic flame temp. (AFT) | $\sum n_i \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,i}\,dT = -\,HCV$ | K | Energy balance, no heat loss |
| 13 | Air-fuel ratio by mass (general) | $A/F = (C/0.232)(8/3)$ for C-only fuel | kg/kg | Useful shortcut |
| 14 | Orsat CO₂ (max, stoich) | $\dfrac{C/12}{x+y/4-z/2 + (79/21)(x+y/4-z/2)} \times 100$ | % | Dry basis |
| 15 | Flue gas N₂ / O₂ (Orsat) | $N_2/O_2 = 79/21$ | — | When only excess air present |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise rows 3, 4, 8, 9, 12** — these five alone fetch ~10 of the 14 marks in a typical Part B numerical.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler design (thermal power plants, Kerala NTPC Kayamkulam):** Stoichiometry fixes the *furnace air register size*; excess air is set to **15–20 %** for oil and **20–30 %** for coal to balance efficiency vs. unburnt loss.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Petrol engines operate near stoichiometric A/F = **14.7** to enable three-way catalytic converter operation; diesel engines run *lean* (A/F ≈ 18–25).
- **Gas Turbine Combustors:** Must achieve *complete combustion in < 5 ms residence time* — requires very precise air staging based on stoichiometry.
- **Flare design (refineries, BPCL Kochi):** Stoichiometric calculations determine the *minimum pilot gas flow* for safe smokeless flaring.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for a Hydrocarbon C_xH_y (General Gas Fuel)

**Start with the balanced reaction:**

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O$$

**Step 1 — Count oxygen atoms on the left:**
1 molecule of C_xH_y contains *no* oxygen. So all O atoms must come from O₂.

O atoms needed on right = $2x$ (in CO₂) $+ \frac{y}{2}$ (in H₂O).

**Step 2 — Equate oxygen atoms:**
$$2\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) = 2x + \frac{y}{2}$$

Both sides equal $2x + y/2$ — confirms the coefficient is self-consistent.

**Step 3 — Convert from pure O₂ to air:**
Since air is only **21 % O₂ by volume**, the moles of air required = O₂ moles ÷ 0.21.

$$n_{air} = \frac{x + y/4}{0.21} = \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \times \frac{100}{21} \text{ kmol air/kmol fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Express nitrogen separately (since dry flue-gas analysis is on N₂ + O₂ + CO₂ basis):**

$$n_{N_2} = 0.79 \times n_{air} = 0.79 \times \frac{x + y/4}{0.21} = \frac{79}{21}\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) = 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Air–Fuel ratio by mass:**

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{n_{air} \times M_{air}}{1 \times M_{fuel}} = \frac{(x+y/4)(100/21)\times 29}{12x + y}$$

where $M_{fuel} = 12x + y$ (kg/kmol for a pure hydrocarbon).

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for Coal (Mass Basis)

Consider 1 kg of coal with ultimate analysis: C = 70 %, H = 5 %, O = 8 %, S = 3 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 12.5 %.

**Step 1 — Compute masses of combustible elements per kg of coal:**

$$m_C = 0.70 \text{ kg}, \quad m_H = 0.05 \text{ kg}, \quad m_O = 0.08 \text{ kg}, \quad m_S = 0.03 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Oxygen required by carbon (C + O₂ → CO₂):**
The molar mass ratio gives:

$$\frac{32 \text{ kg O}_2}{12 \text{ kg C}} = \frac{8}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad O_2 \text{ for C} = \frac{8}{3}\times 0.70 = 1.8667 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Oxygen required by hydrogen (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O):**
Molar ratio: 32 kg O₂ per 4 kg H₂ (i.e., per 4 kg H atoms) →

$$O_2 \text{ for H} = 8 \times 0.05 = 0.4000 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Oxygen required by sulphur (S + O₂ → SO₂):**
Molar ratio: 32 kg O₂ per 32 kg S → 1 kg O₂ per kg S →

$$O_2 \text{ for S} = 1 \times 0.03 = 0.0300 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Total oxygen required:**

$$O_2^{gross} = 1.8667 + 0.4000 + 0.0300 = 2.2967 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{/kg coal}$$

**Step 6 — Credit the oxygen already present in the fuel:**

$$O_2^{net} = 2.2967 - 0.08 = 2.2167 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{/kg coal}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to air using 23.2 % O₂ by mass in air:**

$$\text{Stoich. air} = \frac{2.2167}{0.232} = 9.555 \text{ kg air/kg coal}$$

**Verification using row-4 formula:**

$$\frac{11.6(70) + 34.8(5 - 8/8) + 4.35(3)}{100} = \frac{812 + 34.8(4) + 13.05}{100} = \frac{812 + 139.2 + 13.05}{100} = \frac{964.25}{100}$$

$$= 9.6425 \text{ kg air/kg coal}$$

The ~1 % discrepancy between 9.555 and 9.6425 is due to using 23 % vs. 23.2 % for the O₂ mass fraction. **Always state the assumed value in your answer.**

### 3.3 Complete Numerical: Combustion of Methane with 20 % Excess Air

**Problem (KTU-style):** Methane (CH₄) is burned with **20 % excess air**. Calculate:
(a) Air supplied per kmol of CH₄
(b) Air–Fuel ratio (mass basis)
(c) Orsat analysis of dry flue gas
(d) Mass of wet flue gas per kg of fuel

**Given:** CH₄, M = 16 kg/kmol, Air M = 29 kg/kmol, Excess air = 20 %

**Reaction:**

$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 + 2(3.76)\,N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + (\text{excess O}_2) + N_2$$

**Step (a) — Stoichiometric O₂ = 2 kmol, stoichiometric air = 2 × (100/21) = 9.524 kmol.**
With 20 % excess: $A_{actual} = 1.20 \times 9.524 = 11.4286$ kmol air.
Excess O₂ supplied = 0.20 × 2 = 0.4 kmol.

**Step (b) — Air-Fuel ratio (mass):**

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{11.4286 \times 29}{1 \times 16} = \frac{331.43}{16} = 20.71 \text{ kg air/kg CH}_4$$

**Step (c) — Flue gas composition (dry basis, in kmol):**

| Component | kmol |
|-----------|------|
| CO₂ | 1 |
| H₂O | 2 (dropped from dry basis) |
| O₂ | 0.4 |
| N₂ | 2 × 3.76 = 7.52 |
| **Dry total** | **8.92** |

Orsat %:

$$CO_2 = \frac{1}{8.92}\times 100 = 11.21\%$$
$$O_2 = \frac{0.4}{8.92}\times 100 = 4.48\%$$
$$N_2 = \frac{7.52}{8.92}\times 100 = 84.30\%$$

Sum = 100 % ✓

**Step (d) — Wet flue gas per kg of CH₄:**
Total flue gas (wet) = 1 + 2 + 0.4 + 7.52 = 10.92 kmol per kmol CH₄
Per kg of CH₄: $\dfrac{10.92 \times 29}{16} = 19.79$ kg wet flue gas/kg CH₄

### 3.4 Derivation: Dulong's Formula for HCV

Start with the Hess-cycle. For 1 kg of fuel containing C, H, O, S (kg), the heat released at constant pressure is:

$$HCV = \left(\frac{8}{3}\right) \cdot C \cdot \Delta H_{C \to CO_2} + \left(\frac{8}{1}\right) \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) \cdot \Delta H_{H_2 \to H_2O(l)} + 1 \cdot S \cdot \Delta H_{S \to SO_2}$$

where the subtracted O/8 accounts for oxygen already bound to H in the fuel as moisture or hydroxyl groups. Substituting the standard specific enthalpies of combustion (in kJ/kg of element):

- C → CO₂: 32 800 kJ/kg C → for 8/3 kg O₂ you get 8080 kcal ≈ 33 800 kJ per unit carbon (using 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ)
- H → H₂O(l): 142 000 kJ/kg H → for 8 kg O₂ you get 34 500 kcal
- S → SO₂: 9 250 kJ/kg S

This gives Dulong's final form (in kJ/kg of fuel):

$$\boxed{HCV = \frac{8080\,C + 34500\,(H - O/8) + 2240\,S}{100}}$$

where C, H, O, S are in percentage by mass.

> [!NOTE]
> The conversion between HCV and LCV:
> $LCV = HCV - 9 \times \dfrac{H}{100} \times 2442$ kJ/kg, where 2442 kJ/kg is the latent heat of vaporisation of water at 25 °C, and the factor 9 comes from 18/2 = 9 kg of water formed per kg of hydrogen burned.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Combustion Process Block Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Fuel C H O S N Ash] --> MIX[Mixing Zone with Oxidant]
    A1[Air 21 percent O2 and 79 percent N2] --> MIX
    MIX --> IGN[Ignition Source]
    IGN --> RXN[Combustion Reaction Zone]
    RXN --> P1[Products CO2 H2O SO2]
    RXN --> P2[Excess O2 if any]
    RXN --> P3[N2 from Air]
    RXN --> P4[Unburnt CO Soot if incomplete]
    P1 --> STK[Stack to Atmosphere]
    P2 --> STK
    P3 --> STK
    P4 --> LOSS[Combustion Inefficiency]
    RXN --> HEAT[Heat Release Q equals minus Delta H comb]
    HEAT --> USES[Boiler Turbine Furnace Kiln]
```

### 4.2 Combustion Thermodynamics Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Reactants Inlet]
        F[Fuel at T1]
        A[Air at T1]
    end
    INPUT --> CTRL[Combustion Chamber Control]
    CTRL --> HX[Energy Balance First Law Q equals Delta H]
    HX --> OUT1[Flue Gas at T2 higher]
    HX --> OUT2[Work Output in Engine Turbine]
    HX --> OUT3[Heat to Process in Boiler]
    HX --> METRICS[Performance Metrics]
    METRICS --> M1[Adiabatic Flame Temperature]
    METRICS --> M2[Combustion Efficiency]
    METRICS --> M3[Exergy Destruction]
    METRICS --> M4[Equilibrium Composition]
    OUT1 --> EXH[Exhaust to Stack]
    OUT2 --> SHAFT[Shaft Work to Generator]
    OUT3 --> PROC[Industrial Process Steam]
```

### 4.3 Stoichiometry Decision Flowchart (for KTU numericals)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read the Problem] --> B{Fuel Given as Gas or Solid Liquid}
    B -- Gas --> C1[Use kmol approach with C x H y O z]
    B -- Solid or Liquid --> C2[Use kg approach with Ultimate Analysis]
    C1 --> D1[Find x plus y over 4 minus z over 2 kmol O2]
    C2 --> D2[Find 8 over 3 C plus 8 H plus S minus O kg O2]
    D1 --> E1[Multiply by 100 over 21 for air]
    D2 --> E2[Divide by 0.232 for air]
    E1 --> F1[Compute Air Fuel ratio]
    E2 --> F2[Compute Air Fuel ratio]
    F1 --> G1[Add excess air if stated then recompute]
    F2 --> G2[Add excess air if stated then recompute]
    G1 --> H1[Write Flue Gas Composition and Orsat Analysis]
    G2 --> H2[Write Flue Gas Composition and Orsat Analysis]
    H1 --> I[Cross check Sum of Orsat equals 100 percent]
    H2 --> I
```

### 4.4 Energy Distribution Sankey-Style Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HC[Higher Calorific Value Input] --> B1[Latent Heat of Water Vapour 9 times H over 100 times 2442]
    HC --> B2[Lower Calorific Value Sensible Heat Available]
    B2 --> C1[Useful Heat in Process]
    B2 --> C2[Stack Loss due to Hot Flue Gas]
    B2 --> C3[Radiation and Convection Loss]
    B2 --> C4[Unburnt Fuel Loss]
    B2 --> C5[Moisture in Fuel Loss]
    C1 --> D[Thermal Efficiency Output]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the following terms with one example each:
(a) Stoichiometric air
(b) Excess air
(c) Orsat analysis

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Stoichiometric air** is the *minimum quantity of air* required to supply just enough oxygen for the *complete combustion* of a unit mass or mole of fuel. For example, methane (CH₄) requires 2 kmol of O₂ = 2 × (100/21) = **9.524 kmol of air** per kmol of CH₄. (2 marks)

(b) **Excess air** is the quantity of air supplied *over and above* the stoichiometric requirement, expressed as a percentage of stoichiometric air. It is deliberately supplied in real furnaces to ensure *complete* combustion and avoid CO formation. (½ mark)

(c) **Orsat analysis** is the *volumetric (mole) percentage analysis of dry flue gas* — typically reporting % CO₂, % O₂, % CO, and % N₂ — obtained by absorbing the gas in selective chemical absorbents in an Orsat apparatus. (½ mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State **Dulong's formula** for the Higher Calorific Value of a solid fuel and explain the significance of the term (H – O/8).

**Model Answer:**

$$\boxed{HCV \text{ (kJ/kg)} = \frac{8080\,C + 34500\,(H - O/8) + 2240\,S}{100}}$$

where C, H, O, S are mass percentages of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and sulphur in the fuel on an *ultimate-analysis* basis. (2 marks)

**Significance of (H – O/8):** The term O/8 represents the mass of hydrogen that is *already pre-combusted* with the inherent oxygen in the fuel (present as moisture, –OH groups, or chemically bound O). Such hydrogen *does not* release heat upon combustion because it is already oxidised. Hence, the *net combustible hydrogen* available to release heat is (H – O/8), and only this quantity should be counted in the calorific value. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 72 %, H = 5 %, O = 8 %, S = 3 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 10.5 %. It is burnt with **30 % excess air**.

**(a)** Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of coal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the Orsat analysis of the dry flue gas and the air–fuel ratio by mass. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the per-kg masses:**

$$m_C = 0.72, \;\; m_H = 0.05, \;\; m_O = 0.08, \;\; m_S = 0.03 \text{ kg (per kg coal)}$$

**Step 2 — Oxygen required for each combustible element:**

- For C: $\frac{8}{3}\times 0.72 = 1.920$ kg O₂
- For H: $8 \times 0.05 = 0.400$ kg O₂
- For S: $1 \times 0.03 = 0.030$ kg O₂

**Step 3 — Total O₂ required (gross):**

$$O_2^{gross} = 1.920 + 0.400 + 0.030 = 2.350 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

**Step 4 — Net O₂ (subtract oxygen already in fuel):**

$$O_2^{net} = 2.350 - 0.08 = 2.270 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to air using 23.2 % O₂ by mass:**

$$\text{Stoich. air} = \frac{2.270}{0.232} = 9.784 \text{ kg air/kg coal}$$

**Answer (a):** Stoichiometric air = **9.78 kg air/kg coal**.

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Actual air supplied with 30 % excess:**

$$A_{actual} = 1.30 \times 9.784 = 12.72 \text{ kg air/kg coal}$$

**Step 2 — Actual O₂ supplied:**

$$O_2^{actual} = 0.232 \times 12.72 = 2.951 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

**Step 3 — Excess O₂ in flue gas:**

$$O_2^{excess} = 2.951 - 2.270 = 0.681 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate moles of each product per kg of coal:**

| Species | Mass (kg/kg coal) | M (kg/kmol) | kmol/kg coal |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|--------------|
| CO₂ | $0.72 \times (44/12) = 2.640$ | 44 | 0.0600 |
| SO₂ | $0.03 \times (64/32) = 0.060$ | 64 | 0.000938 |
| H₂O | $0.05 \times (18/2) = 0.450$ | 18 | 0.0250 |
| O₂ (excess) | 0.681 | 32 | 0.02128 |
| N₂ | $0.768 \times 12.72 = 9.769$ | 28 | 0.3489 |

**Step 5 — Dry flue gas kmol total (excluding H₂O):**

$$n_{dry} = 0.0600 + 0.000938 + 0.02128 + 0.3489 = 0.4311 \text{ kmol/kg coal}$$

**Step 6 — Orsat analysis (% by volume, dry basis):**

$$CO_2 = \frac{0.0600}{0.4311}\times 100 = 13.92\%$$
$$SO_2 = \frac{0.000938}{0.4311}\times 100 = 0.22\%$$
$$O_2 = \frac{0.02128}{0.4311}\times 100 = 4.94\%$$
$$N_2 = \frac{0.3489}{0.4311}\times 100 = 80.93\%$$

**Check:** 13.92 + 0.22 + 4.94 + 80.93 = 100.01 % ≈ 100 % ✓

**Step 7 — Air–Fuel ratio (mass):**

$$\frac{A}{F} = 12.72 \text{ kg air/kg coal}$$

**Answer (b):** Orsat = CO₂ 13.92 %, SO₂ 0.22 %, O₂ 4.94 %, N₂ 80.93 %; A/F = 12.72 kg/kg.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

A gaseous fuel having the composition **CH₄ = 60 %, C₂H₆ = 25 %, CO = 10 %, N₂ = 5 %** (by volume) is burnt with **20 % excess air**.

**(a)** Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kmol of the fuel mixture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the wet and dry flue-gas compositions, and the **higher calorific value (HCV)** of the fuel mixture in kJ/kmol of fuel. Given standard enthalpies of combustion: CH₄ = 802.3 MJ/kmol, C₂H₆ = 1428 MJ/kmol, CO = 283 MJ/kmol. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂ for each component per kmol of component:**

- CH₄ + 2 O₂ → CO₂ + 2 H₂O: requires **2 kmol O₂**
- C₂H₆ + 3.5 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 3 H₂O: requires **3.5 kmol O₂**
- CO + 0.5 O₂ → CO₂: requires **0.5 kmol O₂**
- N₂: inert, requires **0 kmol O₂**

**Step 2 — O₂ required per kmol of fuel mixture (multiply by mole fractions):**

$$O_2^{stoich} = 0.60(2) + 0.25(3.5) + 0.10(0.5) + 0.05(0)$$

$$= 1.200 + 0.875 + 0.050 + 0 = 2.125 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kmol fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to air:**

$$\text{Stoich. air} = 2.125 \times \frac{100}{21} = 10.119 \text{ kmol air/kmol fuel}$$

**Answer (a):** **10.12 kmol of air per kmol of fuel.**

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Actual O₂ supplied (with 20 % excess):**

$$O_2^{actual} = 1.20 \times 2.125 = 2.550 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kmol fuel}$$

Excess O₂ in flue gas = 2.550 − 2.125 = 0.425 kmol.

**Step 2 — N₂ from air:**

$$N_2 = \frac{79}{21}\times 2.550 = 9.593 \text{ kmol N}_2/\text{kmol fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Products of combustion (kmol per kmol fuel):**

| Product | From CH₄ | From C₂H₆ | From CO | Total |
|---------|----------|-----------|---------|-------|
| CO₂ | 0.60 × 1 = 0.60 | 0.25 × 2 = 0.50 | 0.10 × 1 = 0.10 | **1.20** |
| H₂O | 0.60 × 2 = 1.20 | 0.25 × 3 = 0.75 | — | **1.95** |
| O₂ (excess) | — | — | — | **0.425** |
| N₂ | — | — | — | **9.593** |

**Step 4 — Wet flue gas total kmol:**

$$n_{wet} = 1.20 + 1.95 + 0.425 + 9.593 = 13.168 \text{ kmol/kmol fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Dry flue gas total kmol:**

$$n_{dry} = 1.20 + 0.425 + 9.593 = 11.218 \text{ kmol/kmol fuel}$$

**Step 6 — Wet flue gas composition (%):**

$$CO_2 = \frac{1.20}{13.168}\times 100 = 9.11\%$$
$$H_2O = \frac{1.95}{13.168}\times 100 = 14.81\%$$
$$O_2 = \frac{0.425}{13.168}\times 100 = 3.23\%$$
$$N_2 = \frac{9.593}{13.168}\times 100 = 72.85\%$$

Sum = 100.00 % ✓

**Step 7 — Dry flue gas composition (%):**

$$CO_2 = \frac{1.20}{11.218}\times 100 = 10.70\%$$
$$O_2 = \frac{0.425}{11.218}\times 100 = 3.79\%$$
$$N_2 = \frac{9.593}{11.218}\times 100 = 85.51\%$$

Sum = 100.00 % ✓

**Step 8 — HCV of the fuel mixture (per kmol):**

$$HCV = 0.60(802.3) + 0.25(1428) + 0.10(283) + 0.05(0)$$

$$= 481.38 + 357.00 + 28.30 + 0 = 866.68 \text{ MJ/kmol fuel}$$

**Answer (b):**
- Wet flue gas: CO₂ 9.11 %, H₂O 14.81 %, O₂ 3.23 %, N₂ 72.85 %
- Dry flue gas: CO₂ 10.70 %, O₂ 3.79 %, N₂ 85.51 %
- **HCV = 866.68 MJ/kmol of fuel**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2-3 Marks in Every Numerical:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract inherent oxygen** (O/8 or full O depending on formula) when computing stoichiometric air for solid/liquid fuels. **Marks lost: 1-2 per question.**
> 2. **Reporting wet flue gas as "Orsat analysis"** — Orsat is *always* on a **dry basis**. Always drop H₂O from the total before computing %.
> 3. **Using 21 % (volume) to convert kg-O₂ to kg-air** — that gives *moles*. For *mass* conversion, use **23.2 % O₂ in air by mass**.
> 4. **Not stating molecular weights explicitly** in the solution — KTU examiners award step marks for showing $M_{CO_2} = 44$, $M_{H_2O} = 18$, $M_{N_2} = 28$ in the table.
> 5. **Confusing A/F ratio definitions** — A/F = (mass of air)/(mass of fuel), F/A = reciprocal. Stating F/A when A/F is asked loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Rounding too early** — keep at least 4 significant figures till the last step. Many students report 9.5 instead of 9.78, losing precision marks.
> 7. **Skipping the cross-check** — always verify $\sum$ Orsat % = 100 % (within rounding). Examiners love to see this self-verification.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stoichiometric air** = minimum air for complete combustion (no free O₂, no CO, no soot in products).
- **Theoretical air** = air actually supplied (≥ stoich. air). Difference = excess air.
- **Orsat analysis** is the *dry* volumetric flue-gas composition.
- For a hydrocarbon C_xH_yO_z: $O_2 = x + y/4 - z/2$ kmol/kmol fuel.
- For solid fuel with ultimate analysis: $O_2 \text{ (kg/kg fuel)} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ ; then divide by 0.232 for kg air/kg fuel.
- **Dulong's formula**: $HCV = \frac{8080\,C + 34500\,(H - O/8) + 2240\,S}{100}$ kJ/kg of fuel.
- **HCV – LCV** = $9 \times H/100 \times 2442$ kJ/kg, where 2442 kJ/kg is the latent heat of water at 25 °C.
- **Adiabatic flame temperature** is found by equating the heat released (HCV) to the sensible enthalpy rise of products: $\sum n_i \int C_{p,i}\,dT = HCV$.
- **Excess air** *decreases* adiabatic flame temperature and furnace efficiency but ensures complete combustion.
- **N₂/O₂ mole ratio in air** = 3.76 (use this everywhere unless specified otherwise).
- **For numericals**: always tabulate products in kmol, show molecular weights, then convert to %.
- **Standard heats of formation** you must know: $\Delta H_f^\circ(CO_2) = -393.5$ MJ/kmol, $\Delta H_f^\circ(H_2O, l) = -285.8$ MJ/kmol, $\Delta H_f^\circ(H_2O, g) = -241.8$ MJ/kmol.
- **Mole fraction = Volume fraction = Partial pressure fraction** for ideal gases — use this in flue-gas equilibrium problems.
- A **complete stoichiometry problem** must give: (i) Stoich. air, (ii) Actual air, (iii) A/F ratio, (iv) Flue gas composition (dry and wet), (v) m³ of flue gas per m³ of fuel (at NTP).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Heat of Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Heat of Combustion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Heat of Combustion (Calorific Value)** is defined as the amount of heat liberated when a unit mass (1 g or 1 kg) of a fuel is completely burnt (oxidized) in oxygen (or air), and the products of combustion are cooled back to the original temperature of the fuel, typically **25 °C (298 K)** under standard atmospheric pressure of **101.325 kPa**.

It is expressed in:
- **cal/g** (calories per gram) — CGS system
- **kcal/kg** (kilocalories per kilogram) — numerically identical to cal/g
- **kJ/kg** or **MJ/kg** — SI system (1 kcal = 4.186 kJ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Distinct Variants of Heat of Combustion:**
>
> 1. **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** / **Higher Heating Value (HHV)**: Heat released assuming all water vapor produced during combustion is condensed back to liquid, releasing its latent heat of vaporization. This is what a **Bomb Calorimeter** measures directly.
>
> 2. **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** / **Lower Heating Value (LHV)**: Heat released when the water formed during combustion is assumed to leave as vapor (i.e., the latent heat of vaporization is **NOT** recovered). This is the value used in **boiler and engine** design because flue gases leave the system as steam.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Wet Towel" of Combustion:**
> 
> Imagine you are cooking a meal on a stove. The flame releases heat. But notice the **water droplets** on the lid of your pan — those are the water vapor produced from burning the fuel (hydrogen in coal, oil, or gas) condensing back to liquid form. That condensation itself releases extra heat.
> 
> - **GCV** is the heat released if you could "catch" that condensation heat (like a closed-pressure bomb calorimeter that condenses the water).
> - **NCV** is the heat released if the steam simply escapes to the atmosphere — the condensation heat is "lost" up the chimney.
> 
> So: **GCV = NCV + (Heat carried away by the latent heat of the steam)**. The difference is purely the latent heat of water vapor.

### 1.3 Why Hydrogen Matters So Much

A small percentage of hydrogen in fuel (typically **2–6%** in coal) creates a disproportionately large correction because:
- The mass of water produced = **9 times** the mass of hydrogen (since H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O, i.e., 2 g H gives 18 g H₂O).
- The latent heat of vaporization of water is very high: **~587 cal/g (2454 kJ/kg)** at 25 °C.
- Therefore, even 5% hydrogen results in a 45% mass as water → significant latent heat loss.

| Constant | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C | **587** | cal/g |
| Latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C | **2454** | kJ/kg |
| Conversion factor | 1 kcal | **4.1868 kJ** |
| Standard reference temperature | **25 °C (298 K)** | K |
| Standard pressure | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** | kPa |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why a Formula Exists

Direct experimental determination of GCV requires a **Bomb Calorimeter**, which is a time-consuming procedure (~30 minutes per test) and needs skilled operators and expensive equipment. For routine industrial analysis of large fuel consignments, engineers developed **empirical/theoretical formulas** that predict GCV from the **ultimate analysis** (C, H, O, N, S percentages).

The pioneer formula is **Dulong's Formula** (1880), still used in industry as a quick first estimate.

### 2.2 Theoretical Basis of Dulong's Formula

Consider 1 kg of fuel containing mass fractions:
- C (carbon) → burns to CO₂, releasing **8080 cal/g** (or **33,810 kJ/kg**)
- H (hydrogen) → burns to H₂O, releasing **34,500 cal/g per g of H** (or **143,000 kJ/kg**)
- S (sulfur) → burns to SO₂, releasing **2240 cal/g** (or **9,400 kJ/kg**)
- O (oxygen) → already combined with some hydrogen, so it **reduces** the available H

The oxygen correction: 1 g of O combines with (1/8) g of H (because 8 g O = 1 g H stoichiometrically in H₂O). Therefore, the **net effective hydrogen** is (H − O/8).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA SHEET — Calculations of Heat of Combustion (KTU Module 4)**

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dulong's Formula** $GCV = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]$ | C, H, O, S = mass % of each element in fuel | cal/g (or kcal/kg) | Predicts GCV from ultimate analysis |
| 2 | Dulong's Formula (SI form) $GCV = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[33800\,C + 143000\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9400\,S\right]$ | Same as above | kJ/kg | SI version |
| 3 | Hydrogen-to-Water Mass Relation $m_{H_2O} = 9 \times H\% \text{ (per 100 g fuel)}$ | H = mass % of H | g per 100 g | Water generated from H combustion |
| 4 | Net Calorific Value $NCV = GCV - \dfrac{9\,H}{100} \times 587$ | H in %, 587 = latent heat cal/g | cal/g (kcal/kg) | Subtracts latent heat lost in steam |
| 5 | Simplified NCV (latent heat = 6) $NCV = GCV - 9H \times 6$ (using 6 cal/g in CGS, where 9H = grams of H per 100g, total water 9H per 100g, so loss = 9H × 6/100; **NOT standard form** — use Form 4) | Use Formula 4 instead | — | — |
| 6 | GCV → NCV (SI units) $NCV = GCV - 9 \times H\% \times 2454 / 100$ | H in %, 2454 = latent heat kJ/kg | kJ/kg | SI version |
| 7 | Calorific Value by Bomb Calorimeter (corrected) $GCV = \dfrac{(W \cdot \Delta T)}{m} - (e_1 + e_2)$ | W = water equivalent cal/°C, ΔT = temp rise, m = fuel mass, e₁ = fuse correction, e₂ = acid correction | cal/g | Experimental GCV |
| 8 | Acid correction $e_2 = \dfrac{m_{H_2SO_4} \times 58700}{m_{fuel}} + \dfrac{m_{HNO_3} \times 56700}{m_{fuel}}$ | 58700, 56700 = heat of formation cal/mol divided by molar mass; often tabular constants | cal/g | Accounts for H₂SO₄ and HNO₃ formed from S and N |
| 9 | Fuse/Ignition correction $e_1 = \dfrac{\text{Heat released by fuse wire}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Calorimeter-specific | cal/g | Small heat contribution from ignition wire |
| 10 | Goutal's Formula (for coal) $GCV = 82C + aV$ where 'a' depends on V/C ratio | a = 75 (V/C ≤ 0.15); 100 (0.15 < V/C < 0.40); 130 (V/C > 0.40) | cal/g (with C%, V = volatile matter %) | Alternative for coal |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in real KTU-relevant engineering practice:**
> - **Boiler engineers** use NCV for efficiency calculations because flue gases escape as steam.
> - **Internal combustion engine designers** use NCV for fuel-air mixture energy content.
> - **Coal traders** quote prices on GCV basis (kcal/kg or BTU/lb) since it is the universally accepted commercial metric.
> - **Power plant thermal audits** require both GCV and NCV to compute actual heat balance.
> - **Emission calculations** for CO₂ use the carbon content and GCV together (e.g., emission factor = 3.67 × C% × 1000 / GCV in tCO₂/TJ).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Rigorous Derivation of Dulong's Formula

**Starting Premise:** Consider 1 kg (or 100 g) of fuel with ultimate analysis: C% carbon, H% hydrogen, O% oxygen, S% sulfur, N% nitrogen, A% ash. Assume that N, A and moisture do **not** contribute to the heat of combustion.

**Step 1: Mass of each combustible element per 100 g of fuel**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of C} &= C \text{ g} \\
\text{Mass of H} &= H \text{ g} \\
\text{Mass of S} &= S \text{ g} \\
\text{Mass of O (already combined)} &= O \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Stoichiometry of oxygen consumed**

For combustion of:
- 12 g C requires 32 g O₂ → forms 44 g CO₂, releases **94.4 kcal** (heat of combustion of C per gram = 8080 cal)
- 2 g H₂ requires 16 g O₂ → forms 18 g H₂O, releases **68.4 kcal** (per gram of H = 34,500 cal)
- 32 g S requires 32 g O₂ → forms 64 g SO₂, releases **71.0 kcal** (per gram of S = 2240 cal)

> This gives the per-gram heat release constants used in Dulong's formula.

**Step 3: Oxygen correction for hydrogen**

The O present in fuel is assumed to be already combined with some hydrogen as water (bound water). The stoichiometry of H₂O formation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From } H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 &\to H_2O \\
\text{2 g H requires 16 g O, so 1 g H requires 8 g O} \\
\therefore \text{Hydrogen bound by O} &= \dfrac{O}{8} \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Net free (combustible) hydrogen**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{net}} = H - \dfrac{O}{8} \quad \text{(g of free hydrogen per 100 g of fuel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total heat released per 100 g of fuel**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} = 8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S \quad \text{(cal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Heat per gram of fuel (dividing by 100)**

$$
\boxed{GCV = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right] \;\; \text{cal/g (or kcal/kg)}}
$$

> This is **Dulong's Formula** for GCV. The `(1/100)` factor appears because the formula's constants are per gram of element, but C, H, O, S in ultimate analysis are expressed as percentages (per 100 g of fuel).

**Step 7: Derivation of NCV from GCV**

Water produced from net hydrogen:
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2O} &= 9 \cdot H_{\text{net}} = 9 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) \text{ g per 100 g fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

When this water leaves as steam (not condensed), latent heat lost = $9(H - O/8) \times 587$ cal per 100 g fuel = $0.09 \cdot (H - O/8) \times 587$ cal/g fuel.

$$
\boxed{NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times 587 \times \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) \;\; \text{kcal/kg}}
$$

In standard form (using H rather than net H, since O is usually small):

$$
NCV = GCV - 9 \cdot H \times 587 / 100 = GCV - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot 587
$$

> Many textbooks write this as $NCV = GCV - 9H \times 6$ where the constants are absorbed into a combined value. Always use **587 cal/g** (latent heat at 25 °C) for the most accurate result.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Example Problems (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU-style problem format:** The question gives ultimate analysis and asks to calculate GCV and NCV. Memorize the formula and apply mechanically.

---

**PROBLEM 1 (Standard KTU type):**

A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis: C = 80%, H = 5%, O = 10%, S = 2%, N = 3% (by mass). Calculate (a) GCV by Dulong's formula, (b) NCV.

**Solution:**

**(a) GCV by Dulong's Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080 \times 80 + 34500 \times \left(5 - \dfrac{10}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 2\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[646400 + 34500 \times (5 - 1.25) + 4480\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[646400 + 34500 \times 3.75 + 4480\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[646400 + 129375 + 4480\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[780255\right] \\
GCV &= 7802.55 \;\; \text{cal/g} = 7802.55 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) NCV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV &= GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587 \\
&= 7802.55 - 0.09 \times 5 \times 587 \\
&= 7802.55 - 264.15 \\
NCV &= 7538.40 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** GCV = **7802.55 kcal/kg**; NCV = **7538.40 kcal/kg**

---

**PROBLEM 2 (With moisture and ash adjustment):**

A fuel oil has the following composition: C = 85%, H = 12%, S = 3% (no O₂). Calculate GCV and NCV.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080 \times 85 + 34500 \times (12 - 0) + 2240 \times 3\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[686800 + 414000 + 6720\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[1107520\right] \\
GCV &= 11075.20 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV &= 11075.20 - 0.09 \times 12 \times 587 \\
&= 11075.20 - 633.96 \\
NCV &= 10441.24 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** GCV = **11075.20 kcal/kg**; NCV = **10441.24 kcal/kg**

---

**PROBLEM 3 (Bomb Calorimeter — Experimental GCV):**

In a bomb calorimeter experiment:
- Mass of fuel burnt, m = 0.85 g
- Water equivalent of bomb + water, W = 2400 cal/°C
- Rise in temperature, ΔT = 2.05 °C
- Fuse wire correction, e₁ = 10 cal
- Acid correction (combined for H₂SO₄ + HNO₃), e₂ = 50 cal

Calculate the GCV of the fuel.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= \dfrac{W \cdot \Delta T - e_1 - e_2}{m} \\
&= \dfrac{2400 \times 2.05 - 10 - 50}{0.85} \\
&= \dfrac{4920 - 60}{0.85} \\
&= \dfrac{4860}{0.85} \\
GCV &= 5717.65 \;\; \text{cal/g} = 5717.65 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Bomb Calorimeter measures **GCV directly** because the closed vessel condenses all water vapor back to liquid. The corrections e₁ and e₂ account for side reactions (sulfur → H₂SO₄, nitrogen → HNO₃, fuse wire combustion).

---

**PROBLEM 4 (Comparison of Experimental vs Theoretical):**

The experimental GCV of a coal (from Bomb Calorimeter) is **6800 kcal/kg**. The fuel has 4% hydrogen. Calculate (a) NCV from this GCV, (b) the expected GCV by Dulong's formula if ultimate analysis is C = 70%, H = 4%, O = 8%, S = 2%, N = 5%, and (c) the percentage deviation.

**Solution:**

**(a) NCV from experimental GCV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV &= 6800 - 0.09 \times 4 \times 587 \\
&= 6800 - 211.32 \\
&= 6588.68 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Dulong's GCV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV_{th} &= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080 \times 70 + 34500 \times \left(4 - \dfrac{8}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 2\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[565600 + 34500 \times 3 + 4480\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[565600 + 103500 + 4480\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[673580\right] \\
GCV_{th} &= 6735.80 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Percentage deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ deviation} &= \dfrac{|6800 - 6735.80|}{6800} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{64.20}{6800} \times 100 \\
&= 0.944 \;\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> This small deviation (<1%) confirms that Dulong's formula is a reliable **engineering estimate**.

---

**PROBLEM 5 (Goutal's Formula — for coal with V/C ratio):**

A coal contains: C = 78%, Volatile Matter (V) = 22%, fixed carbon (FC) by difference. Using Goutal's formula, find GCV.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V/C &= 22/78 = 0.282 \\
\text{Since } 0.15 < V/C < 0.40, \text{ use } a &= 100 \\
GCV &= 82C + aV = 82 \times 78 + 100 \times 22 \\
&= 6396 + 2200 \\
GCV &= 8596 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Dulong's Formula Computational Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["START: Fuel Ultimate Analysis Given"] --> node2["Extract Mass Percentages: C%, H%, O%, S%, N%"]
    node2 --> node3["Compute Net Hydrogen Term H - O divided by 8"]
    node3 --> node4["Apply Dulong's Formula 8080C + 34500 times H minus O by 8 + 2240S"]
    node4 --> node5["Divide by 100 to obtain GCV in kcal per kg"]
    node5 --> node6{"Need Net Calorific Value?"}
    node6 -- YES --> node7["Compute Water Mass 9 times H"]
    node7 --> node8["Subtract 0.09 times H times 587 from GCV"]
    node8 --> node9["Final Output: NCV in kcal per kg"]
    node6 -- NO --> node10["Final Output: GCV in kcal per kg"]
    node9 --> node11["END"]
    node10 --> node11["END"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pathways to Calculate Heat of Combustion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ExperimentalMethod["EXPERIMENTAL PATHWAY"]
        stepA1["Bomb Calorimeter Setup"] --> stepA2["Weigh Fuel Mass m grams"]
        stepA2 --> stepA3["Ignite Fuel in Oxygen Atmosphere"]
        stepA3 --> stepA4["Measure Temperature Rise Delta T"]
        stepA4 --> stepA5["Apply Corrections e1 Fuse and e2 Acid"]
        stepA5 --> stepA6["Compute GCV equals W times Delta T minus e1 minus e2 over m"]
    end
    subgraph TheoreticalMethod["THEORETICAL PATHWAY"]
        stepB1["Ultimate Analysis Performed"] --> stepB2["Determine C H O S N Percentages"]
        stepB2 --> stepB3["Compute Net Hydrogen H minus O by 8"]
        stepB3 --> stepB4["Apply Dulong Formula for GCV"]
        stepB4 --> stepB5["Subtract Latent Heat Loss for NCV"]
    end
    ExperimentalMethod --> compare{"Compare Both Results"}
    TheoreticalMethod --> compare
    compare --> final["Report GCV and NCV with Units kcal per kg"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Effect of Hydrogen on NCV Correction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Hydrogen in Fuel Burns to Water"] --> node2["Mass of Water equals 9 times H"]
    node2 --> node3{"Water Condenses or Escapes as Steam?"}
    node3 -- CONDENSES (Bomb / GCV condition) --> node4["Latent Heat 587 cal per g Recovered"]
    node3 -- ESCAPES (Boiler / NCV condition) --> node5["Latent Heat 587 cal per g LOST to Stack"]
    node4 --> node6["GCV = Higher Value"]
    node5 --> node7["NCV = Lower Value"]
    node6 --> node8["Difference equals 0.09 times H times 587 kcal per kg"]
    node7 --> node8
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — Energy Distribution during Combustion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FuelEnergy["TOTAL HEAT FROM FUEL GCV"]
        blockA["Useful Heat Recovered equals NCV"]
        blockB["Latent Heat Lost in Flue Water Vapor equals 0.09 H times 587"]
        blockC["Stack Gas Sensible Heat Losses"]
        blockD["Other Losses Radiation Convection"]
    end
    FuelEnergy --> blockA
    FuelEnergy --> blockB
    FuelEnergy --> blockC
    FuelEnergy --> blockD
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
>
> **Q1. Define (a) Gross Calorific Value (GCV) and (b) Net Calorific Value (NCV). State the relationship between them.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> **(a) GCV** is the total heat released when 1 unit mass of fuel is completely burnt and the products of combustion are cooled to **25 °C**, with the water vapor produced being condensed back to liquid. It is determined experimentally using a **Bomb Calorimeter**.
>
> **(b) NCV** is the net heat released when 1 unit mass of fuel is completely burnt and the products of combustion leave the system at a temperature above the dew point, with water remaining as vapor. This is the value relevant to **boiler and engine** applications.
>
> **Relationship:**
> $$
> NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times L
> $$
> where **H** is the mass percent of hydrogen in the fuel, and **L = 587 cal/g (2454 kJ/kg)** is the latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C. The term `0.09 × H` represents the grams of water produced per gram of fuel.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
>
> **Q2. Why is the latent heat of vaporization of water subtracted in the calculation of NCV?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The latent heat is subtracted because in practical systems like boilers, internal combustion engines, and gas turbines, the water formed from hydrogen combustion leaves the system as **superheated steam** (along with the flue gas), and this latent heat is **physically lost** to the atmosphere/stack — it cannot be recovered for useful work.
>
> The Bomb Calorimeter, being a **closed vessel**, allows the water to condense back to liquid, releasing the latent heat, so it gives GCV. In open industrial systems, the water never condenses, so the latent heat is lost → hence the subtraction gives NCV.
>
> Mathematically, the latent heat correction is:
> $$
> \Delta Q = 0.09 \times H \times 587 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}
> $$
> (where H is % hydrogen by mass).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4]`**
>
> **PART B — Question A (14 Marks):**
>
> A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass:
> C = 72%, H = 5%, O = 8%, S = 3%, N = 1.5%, Moisture = 1%, Ash = 9.5%.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** using **Dulong's Formula**. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Calculate the **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** and the **percentage reduction** from GCV. (7 marks)

### **Detailed Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Calculation of GCV using Dulong's Formula:**

**Given:** C = 72%, H = 5%, O = 8%, S = 3%

**Step 1:** Identify the relevant elements for Dulong's formula. Nitrogen, moisture, and ash do not contribute to combustion heat.

**Step 2:** Compute the net hydrogen term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{net} &= H - \dfrac{O}{8} = 5 - \dfrac{8}{8} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ (in %)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into Dulong's formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080 \times 72 + 34500 \times 4 + 2240 \times 3\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[581760 + 138000 + 6720\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[726480\right] \\
\boxed{GCV = 7264.80 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Calculation of NCV and percentage reduction:**

**Step 1:** Use the relationship between NCV and GCV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV &= GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587 \\
&= 7264.80 - 0.09 \times 5 \times 587 \\
&= 7264.80 - 264.15 \\
\boxed{NCV = 7000.65 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute percentage reduction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ reduction} &= \dfrac{GCV - NCV}{GCV} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{264.15}{7264.80} \times 100 \\
&= 3.636 \;\;\% \\
\boxed{\% \text{ reduction} \approx 3.64 \text{ \%}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The percentage reduction represents the fraction of total heat carried away by water vapor in the flue gas — useful in assessing **stack heat loss** for boiler design.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4]`**
>
> **PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
>
> A fuel is tested in a **Bomb Calorimeter** with the following observations:
> - Mass of fuel burnt, m = 0.75 g
> - Water equivalent of calorimeter, W = 2200 cal/°C
> - Initial temperature = 26.4 °C
> - Final temperature = 28.7 °C
> - Fuse wire (iron) correction, e₁ = 12 cal
> - Acid (H₂SO₄) formed = 0.015 g; acid correction, e₂ = 36 cal
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **GCV** of the fuel. (7 marks)
> **(b)** If the fuel contains **4.5% hydrogen**, calculate the **NCV** in kcal/kg and convert to **kJ/kg**. (7 marks)

### **Detailed Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Calculation of GCV from Bomb Calorimeter:**

**Given:**
- m = 0.75 g
- W = 2200 cal/°C
- ΔT = 28.7 − 26.4 = 2.3 °C
- e₁ = 12 cal, e₂ = 36 cal

**Step 1:** Apply the Bomb Calorimeter formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= \dfrac{W \cdot \Delta T - e_1 - e_2}{m} \\
&= \dfrac{2200 \times 2.3 - 12 - 36}{0.75} \\
&= \dfrac{5060 - 48}{0.75} \\
&= \dfrac{5012}{0.75} \\
\boxed{GCV = 6682.67 \;\; \text{cal/g} = 6682.67 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Calculation of NCV and conversion to kJ/kg:**

**Step 1:** Calculate NCV using latent heat correction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV &= GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587 \\
&= 6682.67 - 0.09 \times 4.5 \times 587 \\
&= 6682.67 - 237.615 \\
\boxed{NCV = 6445.06 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to kJ/kg using 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV_{kJ} &= 6445.06 \times 4.186 \\
&= 26,979.1 \;\; \text{kJ/kg} \\
\boxed{NCV = 26,979.1 \;\; \text{kJ/kg} \approx 27.0 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This value is typical of **medium-quality bituminous coal** (NCV ~ 25–30 MJ/kg). High-rank anthracite reaches ~ 32–35 MJ/kg; lignite only 10–17 MJ/kg.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MARK-LOSING PITFALLS — KTU Valuation Pattern Analysis (Based on Dec 2023 & May 2024 papers):**
>
> **Pitfall 1 — Forgetting to divide by 100 in Dulong's formula.**
> Students often write $GCV = 8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S$ without the factor $1/100$. This gives a value **100 times too large**, losing **3 marks** for the wrong magnitude.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing the latent heat constant.**
> Use **587 cal/g** (at 25 °C) consistently. Some textbooks use 600, 580, or 540 cal/g. In KTU answer keys, **587 cal/g** is the canonical value. Adopting a wrong constant loses **1 mark** even with correct procedure.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Using H or (H − O/8) inconsistently.**
> The GCV formula **uses (H − O/8)**, but the standard NCV formula **uses H alone** (since the O is assumed to have already taken its hydrogen as "bound water"). Mixing them up gives an answer off by ~ 50–100 kcal/kg. **Mark loss: 2 marks.**
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Ignoring the units declaration.**
> KTU board examiners want explicit unit mention: "kcal/kg", "cal/g", or "kJ/kg". Writing just the number loses **0.5 mark** for "no unit".
>
> **Pitfall 5 — In Bomb Calorimeter problems, students forget the acid correction (e₂) for sulfur and nitrogen.**
> Always check whether the problem mentions acid formation or gives sulfur/nitrogen content. If yes, **include e₂**; if not, omit it. Missing e₂ loses **1.5 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Heat of Combustion Calculations**

- ✅ **GCV** = total heat released with water condensed. Measured by **Bomb Calorimeter**.
- ✅ **NCV** = heat released with water as vapor. Used for **boiler/engine** design.
- ✅ **Dulong's Formula (most important):**
  $GCV = \frac{1}{100}[8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S]$ kcal/kg
- ✅ **NCV Formula:**
  $NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587$ kcal/kg
  (where H = % hydrogen by mass; 587 = latent heat of vaporization cal/g)
- ✅ Water produced per kg of fuel = **9 × H** grams (where H is in %).
- ✅ **Nitrogen, ash, and moisture** do **NOT** contribute to heat of combustion.
- ✅ **Carbon's contribution** is dominant: 8080 cal/g — almost 80% of GCV in high-rank coal.
- ✅ **Oxygen correction** term `(H − O/8)` is critical — never omit when oxygen is present.
- ✅ **Bomb Calorimeter formula:**
  $GCV = \frac{W \cdot \Delta T - e_1 - e_2}{m}$ cal/g
- ✅ **Corrections** to remember:
  - **e₁** (fuse wire correction) — energy of ignition wire
  - **e₂** (acid correction) — H₂SO₄ (from S) + HNO₃ (from N)
- ✅ **Conversion:** 1 kcal = **4.186 kJ** = **3.968 BTU**
- ✅ **Goutal's formula** (for coal only): GCV = 82C + aV; constant 'a' depends on V/C ratio.
- ✅ **Why NCV < GCV:** Latent heat of water vapor (587 cal/g per gram of water) is lost up the chimney in industrial systems.
- ✅ **NCV difference is small for low-hydrogen fuels** (coke, anthracite) but large for high-hydrogen fuels (oil, natural gas).
- ✅ **Always declare units** explicitly: cal/g, kcal/kg, kJ/kg, or MJ/kg.
- ✅ **Always divide by 100** in Dulong's formula — this is the #1 KTU mark-loser.
- ✅ **Practical fuel ranges to remember:**
  - Anthracite: 28–35 MJ/kg
  - Bituminous coal: 24–35 MJ/kg
  - Lignite: 10–17 MJ/kg
  - Fuel oil: 40–45 MJ/kg
  - Natural gas: 35–55 MJ/Nm³
- ✅ **KTU Module 4 focus:** Dulong's formula + Bomb Calorimeter interpretation + Goutal's formula + numerical problems on coal and oil.

---

topics.title: Theoretical and Actual Combustion Processes - Air-Fuel Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Theoretical and Actual Combustion Processes - Air-Fuel Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Theoretical Combustion (Stoichiometric Combustion):** It is the idealized combustion process in which a fuel is burned with *exactly* the chemically required quantity of oxygen (or air) to convert all combustible elements completely into their highest stable oxidation products — Carbon → **CO₂**, Hydrogen → **H₂O**, and Sulphur → **SO₂** — with no oxygen left over.

**Actual Combustion:** The real-world combustion process in which a fuel is burned with a quantity of air either greater than (most common), equal to, or less than the stoichiometric requirement, in order to achieve practical flame stability and complete burnout under furnace/boiler conditions.

**Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F):** The mass ratio of the air supplied per unit mass of fuel fed into the combustion zone.

$$A/F \;=\; \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme expects you to clearly distinguish between **Theoretical Air**, **Actual Air**, **Excess Air (%)**, **Air-Fuel Ratio**, and **Equivalence Ratio (φ)** — and to compute each numerically given a fuel's ultimate analysis (CHNO + Ash).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of lighting a campfire:
- **Theoretical air** is like the *exact* number of matchsticks theoretically needed to light a pile of wood completely. No match wasted, no match missing.
- **Actual air** is what you *really* use — you always blow in **more** air (oxygen) than the minimum, because if you supply *less* than theoretical, you get **smoke, soot, and unburnt carbon** (incomplete combustion).
- The **extra** air you supply beyond the theoretical minimum is the **excess air** — like the "buffer" you keep to make sure the fire doesn't suffocate.

In industry, a controlled amount of **10–25 % excess air** is normally maintained for solid, liquid, and gaseous fuels to ensure complete combustion economically.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Constants

| Constant | Value | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Mass % of O₂ in air | **23.2 %** | By mass (also written as 0.232) |
| Volume % of O₂ in air | **21.0 %** | By volume (also 0.21) |
| Molecular weight of O₂ | **32 kg/kmol** | |
| Molecular weight of air | **28.97 kg/kmol** | |
| Gravimetric ratio air:O₂ | **1 : 0.232** | 1 kg air contains 0.232 kg O₂ |

> [!NOTE]
> Students frequently confuse 21 % (volume) with 23.2 % (mass). For A/F ratio *by mass* — which is what KTU questions demand — always use **0.232**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Combustion Reactions (Per kg-mol)

The three elementary combustion reactions that form the backbone of every stoichiometric problem are:

$$C + O_{2} \;\longrightarrow\; CO_{2} \qquad (1\; kmol\; C \equiv 1\; kmol\; O_{2})$$

$$H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \;\longrightarrow\; H_{2}O \qquad (1\; kmol\; H_{2} \equiv 0.5\; kmol\; O_{2})$$

$$S + O_{2} \;\longrightarrow\; SO_{2} \qquad (1\; kmol\; S \equiv 1\; kmol\; O_{2})$$

### 2.2 Mass of O₂ Required per kg of Fuel

Let C, H, S, O represent the **mass fractions** of carbon, hydrogen, sulphur, and oxygen in 1 kg of fuel (dry ash-free or as-fired basis as specified).

- 1 kg of C needs 32/12 = **2.667 kg O₂**
- 1 kg of H needs 32/4 = **8.00 kg O₂** (since 1 kg H ≡ 0.5 kmol H₂, needs 0.25 kmol O₂ = 8 kg)
- 1 kg of S needs 32/32 = **1.00 kg O₂**
- The oxygen *already present* in the fuel is available to its own combustible elements; hence it is **subtracted**.

$$m_{O_{2}}^{th} \;=\; \frac{32}{12}C + \frac{32}{4}H + \frac{32}{32}S - O \;=\; 2.667\,C + 8\,H + 1.0\,S - O$$

### 2.3 Theoretical Air Required

Since air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass, the theoretical air requirement per kg of fuel is:

$$A_{0} \;=\; \frac{m_{O_{2}}^{th}}{0.232} \;=\; 11.5\,C + 34.5\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 4.3\,S \;\; \left[\tfrac{kg\;air}{kg\;fuel}\right]$$

### 2.4 Excess Air and Actual Air

$$A_{actual} \;=\; A_{0}\!\left(1 + \tfrac{e}{100}\right)$$

$$\%\,Excess\;Air \;=\; e \;=\; \frac{A_{actual} - A_{0}}{A_{0}} \times 100$$

### 2.5 Air-Fuel Ratio Variants

| Term | Symbol | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Theoretical (stoichiometric) A/F | $(A/F)_{th}$ | $A_{0}$ |
| Actual A/F | $(A/F)_{act}$ | $A_{0}\!\left(1 + \tfrac{e}{100}\right)$ |
| Equivalence ratio | $\phi$ | $(A/F)_{th} \big/ (A/F)_{act}$ |
| Stoichiometric air requirement (volume) | $V_{0}$ | $\dfrac{m_{O_{2}}^{th}/32}{0.21} \times 22.4 \;\; m^{3}/kg$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - $\phi < 1$ → **lean mixture** (excess air).
> - $\phi = 1$ → **stoichiometric** (theoretical).
> - $\phi > 1$ → **rich mixture** (deficient air — possible CO, soot).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $O_{2}$ required (mass) | $2.667\,C + 8\,H + 1.0\,S - O$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel |
| 2 | Theoretical air (mass) | $\dfrac{2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O}{0.232}$ | kg air / kg fuel |
