| 3 | Theoretical air (volume at NTP) | $\dfrac{O_{2}\;(kg)}{32 \times 0.21} \times 22.4$ | m³ air / kg fuel |
| 4 | Actual air | $A_{0}\!\left(1+\tfrac{e}{100}\right)$ | kg air / kg fuel |
| 5 | Excess air % | $\dfrac{A_{act} - A_{0}}{A_{0}} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | Actual A/F | $A_{0}\!\left(1+\tfrac{e}{100}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Equivalence ratio $\phi$ | $(A/F)_{th} \big/ (A/F)_{act}$ | dimensionless |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler design:** Optimal excess air minimizes stack-gas losses while ensuring complete combustion. Trade-off curves (efficiency vs. excess air) are used in every thermal power plant.
- **Internal Combustion (IC) engines:** The A/F ratio directly governs flame speed, knock tendency, exhaust emissions (CO, NOₓ, unburnt HC).
- **Burner sizing:** Industrial gas/oil burners are rated on their kg-air-per-kg-fuel consumption — a direct application of the formula above.
- **Emission control:** Modern $\phi$ sensors (lambda sensors) in automotive exhausts measure A/F continuously to optimize the catalytic converter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Mass of O₂ Required per kg of Fuel

Consider 1 kg of fuel containing mass fractions C, H, S, O (kg of each element per kg of fuel).

**Step 1 — Carbon component**

1 kg of C corresponds to $\tfrac{1}{12}$ kmol of C. From the reaction $C + O_{2} \to CO_{2}$, 1 kmol C requires 1 kmol O₂. So:

$$m_{O_{2},C} \;=\; \frac{1}{12} \times 32 \;=\; \frac{32}{12} \;=\; 2.667 \;\; kg\; O_{2}/kg\; C$$

**Step 2 — Hydrogen component**

1 kg of H corresponds to $\tfrac{1}{2}$ kmol of H₂. From $H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \to H_{2}O$, 1 kmol H₂ requires 0.5 kmol O₂. So:

$$m_{O_{2},H} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \;=\; 8.0 \;\; kg\; O_{2}/kg\; H$$

**Step 3 — Sulphur component**

1 kg of S corresponds to $\tfrac{1}{32}$ kmol S, needing $\tfrac{1}{32}$ kmol O₂:

$$m_{O_{2},S} \;=\; \frac{1}{32} \times 32 \;=\; 1.0 \;\; kg\; O_{2}/kg\; S$$

**Step 4 — Oxygen already present in the fuel**

The fuel's own O content is "free" oxygen — it already participates in combustion, so it offsets the demand:

$$\text{Oxygen to be supplied externally} \;=\; m_{O_{2},C} + m_{O_{2},H} + m_{O_{2},S} - O_{fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Total O₂ required (kg per kg fuel)**

$$\boxed{\,m_{O_{2}}^{th} \;=\; \frac{32}{12}\,C + \frac{32}{4}\,H + \frac{32}{32}\,S - O \;=\; 2.667\,C + 8\,H + 1.0\,S - O\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical Air

By definition, dry atmospheric air contains **23.2 % O₂ by mass** (or 21 % by volume). Therefore 1 kg of air carries 0.232 kg of O₂. Inverting:

$$A_{0} \;=\; \frac{m_{O_{2}}^{th}}{0.232}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\,A_{0} \;=\; 11.5\,C + 34.5\!\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.3\,S\, \;\;\left[\tfrac{kg\;air}{kg\;fuel}\right]\,}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A fuel oil has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 84 %, H = 12 %, S = 2 %, O = 1 %, Ash = 1 %. The fuel is burned with 25 % excess air. Calculate:
1. Theoretical O₂ required (kg/kg fuel)
2. Theoretical air required (kg/kg fuel)
3. Actual air required (kg/kg fuel)
4. Air-fuel ratio on actual basis
5. Equivalence ratio $\phi$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical O₂**

$$m_{O_{2}}^{th} \;=\; 2.667(0.84) + 8(0.12) + 1.0(0.02) - 0.01$$

$$=\; 2.240 + 0.960 + 0.020 - 0.010 \;=\; 3.210 \;\; kg\; O_{2}/kg\; fuel$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical air**

$$A_{0} \;=\; \frac{3.210}{0.232} \;=\; 13.836 \;\; kg\; air/kg\; fuel$$

**Step 3 — Actual air (25 % excess)**

$$A_{act} \;=\; 13.836 \times (1 + 0.25) \;=\; 17.295 \;\; kg\; air/kg\; fuel$$

**Step 4 — Actual A/F ratio**

$$(A/F)_{act} \;=\; 17.295$$

**Step 5 — Equivalence ratio**

$$\phi \;=\; \frac{(A/F)_{th}}{(A/F)_{act}} \;=\; \frac{13.836}{17.295} \;=\; 0.800$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\phi = 0.80 < 1$ → the mixture is **lean** (excess air), which is consistent with 25 % excess air.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Fuel A/F Ratios (for quick reference)

| Fuel | C | H | S | O | Ash | $A_{0}$ (kg/kg) | Typical $(A/F)_{act}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Methane (CH₄) | 0.75 | 0.25 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 17.16 | ≈ 19.5 @ 14 % e.a. |
| Petrol (gasoline) | 0.86 | 0.14 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 14.95 | ≈ 16–18 |
| Diesel | 0.87 | 0.13 | 0.01 | 0 | 0.01 | 14.55 | ≈ 16.5 |
| Coal (bituminous) | 0.70 | 0.05 | 0.02 | 0.06 | 0.10 | ≈ 9.5 | ≈ 11–12 |
| Natural gas (typical) | 0.75 | 0.24 | 0 | 0.01 | 0 | 17.0 | ≈ 19–20 |

### 3.5 Formula Equivalence for Gaseous Fuels (kmol basis)

For a gaseous fuel whose volumetric (mole) composition is $CO, H_{2}, CH_{4}, C_{2}H_{4}, C_{2}H_{6}, C_{3}H_{8}, H_{2}S, CO_{2}, N_{2}, O_{2}$:

$$O_{2}\;\text{required (kmol)} \;=\; 0.5\,CO + 0.5\,H_{2} + 2\,CH_{4} + 3\,C_{2}H_{4} + 3.5\,C_{2}H_{6} + 5\,C_{3}H_{8} + 1.5\,H_{2}S - O_{2\,(fuel)}$$

Theoretical air in kmol/kmol fuel:

$$A_{0} \;=\; \frac{O_{2}\;\text{required}}{0.21}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic **routinely** include both a solid/liquid-fuel part (using mass fractions) and a gaseous-fuel part (using mole fractions). Master both.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Fuel + Air to Flue Gas

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fuel[Fuel Ultimate Analysis] --> stoich[Stoichiometric O2 Required]
    air[Atmospheric Air 23.2 percent O2 by mass] --> stoich
    stoich --> A0[Theoretical Air A0]
    A0 --> eact[Add Excess Air e percent]
    eact --> Aact[Actual Air Supplied]
    Aact --> comb[Combustion Chamber Furnace Boiler]
    fuel --> comb
    comb --> flue[Flue Gas Composition]
    flue --> co2[CO2]
    flue --> h2o[H2O vapour]
    flue --> so2[SO2]
    flue --> n2[N2 from air]
    flue --> o2[Residual O2 from excess air]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Theoretical vs Actual Combustion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Theoret[Theoretical Stoichiometric Combustion]
        T1[Fuel mass m_f] --> T2[React with exact O2 demand]
        T2 --> T3[Complete oxidation only]
        T3 --> T4[Products: CO2 + H2O + SO2 + N2]
    end

    subgraph Actual[Actual Combustion with Excess Air]
        A1[Fuel mass m_f] --> A2[React with O2 + additional excess air]
        A2 --> A3[Incomplete zones possible CO soot]
        A3 --> A4[Products: CO2 + H2O + SO2 + N2 + O2 residue + minor CO]
    end

    Theoret --> Compare{Comparison A vs F}
    Actual --> Compare
    Compare --> R1[Theoretical A over F equals A0]
    Compare --> R2[Actual A over F equals A0 times 1 plus e over 100]
    Compare --> R3[Equivalence ratio phi equals A over F theo divided by A over F actual]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Identifying Combustion Regime

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Given fuel and air] --> calc[Calculate A0 theoretical air]
    calc --> compare{A actual greater than A0}
    compare -- Yes --> lean[Lean mixture phi less than 1 Excess air positive]
    compare -- No --> compare2{A actual less than A0}
    compare2 -- Yes --> rich[Rich mixture phi greater than 1 Deficient air]
    compare2 -- No --> stoich[Stoichiometric phi equals 1 Zero excess air]
```

### 4.4 Visual Map of Derived Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    elem[Elements C H S O mass fractions] --> o2req[O2 required kg per kg fuel]
    o2req --> a0[Theoretical air A0]
    a0 --> e[Excess air e percent]
    e --> aact[Actual air Aact]
    aact --> af[Actual A over F ratio]
    a0 --> af
    af --> phi[Equivalence ratio phi]
    a0 --> v0[Volumetric air at NTP m3 per kg]
    o2req --> v0
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *theoretical air* and *excess air*. How are they related to the *air-fuel ratio*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Theoretical air (A₀):** The minimum quantity of air required to supply just enough oxygen for *complete* combustion of a fuel, converting all C → CO₂, H → H₂O, S → SO₂, with no free O₂ remaining (1 mark).
- **Excess air (e %):** The amount of air supplied *beyond* theoretical air, expressed as a percentage of A₀, to ensure practical complete combustion in furnaces/boilers (1 mark).
- **Relation:** The actual air supplied is $A_{act} = A_{0}\!\left(1 + \tfrac{e}{100}\right)$, and the actual A/F ratio equals this quantity on per-kg-fuel basis. Equivalence ratio $\phi = (A/F)_{th} / (A/F)_{act}$ (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** A coal has the following composition: C = 70 %, H = 5 %, S = 1 %, O = 6 %, N = 1 %, Ash = 17 %. Calculate the *theoretical air* required per kg of coal.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Theoretical O₂ per kg fuel:

$$m_{O_{2}}^{th} = 2.667(0.70) + 8(0.05) + 1(0.01) - 0.06$$

$$= 1.867 + 0.400 + 0.010 - 0.060 = 2.217 \;\; kg\; O_{2}/kg\; fuel$$

Theoretical air:

$$A_{0} = \frac{2.217}{0.232} = 9.556 \;\; kg\; air/kg\; fuel \;\; (\approx 9.56)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **subtract the fuel's own oxygen**. Forgetting the "− O" term in 2.667 C + 8 H + S − O leads to an inflated O₂ demand and a wrong A/F ratio — a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** A fuel oil has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 85 %, H = 11 %, S = 1.5 %, O = 1 %, N = 0.5 %, Ash = 1 %. The fuel is burned with **30 % excess air**. Calculate:
1. Theoretical O₂ required (kg/kg fuel)
2. Theoretical air required (kg/kg fuel)
3. Actual air supplied (kg/kg fuel)
4. Actual A/F ratio **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how the *equivalence ratio* $\phi$ classifies combustion regimes and explain the practical significance of *excess air* in boiler/furnace operations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

Given: C = 0.85, H = 0.11, S = 0.015, O = 0.01 (mass fractions).

**1. Theoretical O₂ (kg/kg fuel):**

$$m_{O_{2}}^{th} = 2.667(0.85) + 8(0.11) + 1(0.015) - 0.01$$

$$= 2.267 + 0.880 + 0.015 - 0.010 = 3.152 \;\; kg\; O_{2}/kg\; fuel \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**2. Theoretical air (kg/kg fuel):**

$$A_{0} = \frac{3.152}{0.232} = 13.586 \;\; kg\; air/kg\; fuel \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**3. Actual air with 30 % excess:**

$$A_{act} = 13.586 \times (1 + 0.30) = 17.662 \;\; kg\; air/kg\; fuel \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**4. Actual A/F ratio:**

$$(A/F)_{act} = 17.662 \;\; kg\; air/kg\; fuel \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

The **equivalence ratio** is defined as:

$$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{th}}{(A/F)_{act}}$$

- $\phi = 1$ → **Stoichiometric** (theoretical) combustion — chemically ideal, but rarely practical.
- $\phi < 1$ → **Lean** mixture — excess air, used in furnaces/boilers (10–25 % e.a.) to ensure complete burnout and prevent soot/smoke.
- $\phi > 1$ → **Rich** mixture — deficient air, leads to CO, unburnt hydrocarbons, soot, and luminous flames; occasionally used in IC engines for power or in reducing atmospheres.

**Practical significance of excess air in boilers:** **(3 marks)**

1. **Ensures complete combustion** — burns all carbon to CO₂, eliminating soot and smoke.
2. **Compensates for poor mixing** — real flames have non-uniform fuel-air contact; extra air acts as a buffer.
3. **Trade-off** — more excess air → more N₂ is heated in the flame, raising stack-gas losses and lowering efficiency. An *optimum* (≈ 15–20 % for oil, 20–25 % for coal) balances completeness against losses.
4. **Emission control** — proper excess air minimizes CO and unburnt hydrocarbon emissions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** A producer gas has the following composition by volume: CO = 28 %, H₂ = 12 %, CH₄ = 4 %, CO₂ = 5 %, N₂ = 50 %, O₂ = 1 %. The gas is burned with **20 % excess air**. Calculate:
1. Theoretical O₂ required (m³/m³ of gas at NTP)
2. Theoretical air required (m³/m³ of gas at NTP)
3. Actual air supplied (m³/m³ of gas at NTP)
4. Air-fuel ratio (volumetric, actual) **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare *theoretical* and *actual* combustion processes. Why is *excess air* always supplied in practice? What is the typical range for different fuels? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

Reactions considered (mole basis):

$$CO + 0.5\,O_{2} \to CO_{2}$$
$$H_{2} + 0.5\,O_{2} \to H_{2}O$$
$$CH_{4} + 2\,O_{2} \to CO_{2} + 2\,H_{2}O$$
$$\text{(CO}_{2}, N_{2}, O_{2} \text{ do not consume O}_{2})$$

**1. Theoretical O₂ per m³ of gas (volumetric = molar at same T, P):**

$$O_{2}^{th} = 0.5(0.28) + 0.5(0.12) + 2(0.04) - (0.01)$$

$$= 0.140 + 0.060 + 0.080 - 0.010 = 0.270 \;\; m^{3}\,O_{2}/m^{3}\,gas \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**2. Theoretical air (m³/m³):**

$$V_{0} = \frac{0.270}{0.21} = 1.286 \;\; m^{3}\,air/m^{3}\,gas \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**3. Actual air with 20 % excess:**

$$V_{act} = 1.286 \times 1.20 = 1.543 \;\; m^{3}\,air/m^{3}\,gas \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**4. Volumetric A/F ratio (actual):**

$$(A/F)_{act} = 1.543 \;\; m^{3}\,air/m^{3}\,gas \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Comparison of Theoretical vs Actual Combustion: (3 marks)**

| Aspect | Theoretical | Actual |
|---|---|---|
| Air supplied | Exact stoichiometric | > theoretical (with excess) |
| Products | Only CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, N₂ | May include residual O₂, traces of CO |
| Completeness | Complete (in principle) | Complete only with right e.a. |
| Used in | Calculations, design references | Real furnaces, boilers, engines |
| Flue gas | No free O₂ | 2–4 % free O₂ typical |

**Why excess air is supplied in practice: (3 marks)**

1. To guarantee **complete combustion** even with imperfect fuel-air mixing.
2. To prevent formation of **CO and soot** from fuel-rich pockets.
3. To maintain **flame stability** and uniform heat release.
4. To keep **exit CO and unburnt HC** within pollution norms.

**Typical excess air ranges: (1 mark)**

- Gaseous fuels: **5–10 %**
- Light & medium oil: **10–15 %**
- Heavy fuel oil: **15–20 %**
- Pulverized coal: **15–25 %**
- Solid waste/biomass: **25–40 %**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion** — Always state the A/F ratio with explicit units (kg air/kg fuel for solids/liquids; m³ air/m³ gas for gaseous fuels). A bare "13.5" without units → **−0.5 to −1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract fuel's own oxygen** — leads to inflated O₂ demand → **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Using 21 % O₂ by mass** — the correct value is **23.2 %** (mass basis) for A/F calculations. 21 % is for *volumetric* calculations. Mixing them up → wrong A/F.
> 4. **Not writing the final A/F with the right basis** — KTU accepts *both* mass and volumetric, but you must clearly state which.
> 5. **In gaseous-fuel problems, failing to subtract the fuel's own O₂** (line "− O₂(fuel)" in the formula) is a common 1-mark loss.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Theoretical air (A₀)** = minimum air for *complete* combustion; converts C → CO₂, H → H₂O, S → SO₂.
- [x] **Actual air (A_act)** = A₀ (1 + e/100), where e is the % excess air.
- [x] **Master formula** (mass basis): $A_{0} = 11.5\,C + 34.5\!\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.3\,S$ kg air/kg fuel.
- [x] **O₂ demand** = 2.667 C + 8 H + S − O kg O₂/kg fuel. **Always subtract the fuel's own oxygen.**
- [x] **Mass % of O₂ in air = 23.2 %** (volume % = 21 % — don't mix!).
- [x] **A/F ratio** = mass of air per mass of fuel (or volume of air per volume of gaseous fuel at the same T, P).
- [x] **Excess air %** = (A_act − A₀)/A₀ × 100.
- [x] **Equivalence ratio** $\phi = (A/F)_{th} / (A/F)_{act}$: lean (< 1), stoichiometric (= 1), rich (> 1).
- [x] **For gaseous fuels**, use the mole-based O₂ demand formula with coefficients 0.5, 0.5, 2, 3, 3.5, 5, 1.5 for CO, H₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, H₂S respectively; subtract the fuel's own O₂; divide by 0.21 for air.
- [x] **Typical excess air**: gas 5–10 %, light oil 10–15 %, heavy oil 15–20 %, coal 15–25 %, biomass/waste 25–40 %.
- [x] **Engineering insight**: excess air is a *trade-off* — too little gives CO & soot; too much wastes heat up the stack and reduces efficiency.
- [x] **N₂ in flue gas** = 79/21 × actual air supplied (by volume) = 3.76 × A_act (by volume). It is a diluent and a heat-loss carrier.
- [x] **Always quote units** with the A/F answer (kg/kg or m³/m³) and state whether it is theoretical or actual.
- [x] **KTU 2024 expects numerical answers in 3–4 significant figures** unless otherwise specified.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Dry and Wet Flue Gases for Known Fuel Composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Estimation of Dry and Wet Flue Gases for Known Fuel Composition # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flue Gas Estimation (Combustion Calculation):** The analytical procedure of computing the quantity and composition of combustion products (both **dry flue gas** and **wet flue gas**) that exit a combustion system, using the **ultimate analysis** of the fuel (mass percentages of C, H, O, N, S) and the **moisture/ash** content, together with the **excess air** supplied. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this falls under the broader domain of **Stoichiometric Combustion Calculations**, where the engineer assumes complete combustion (no CO, no unburnt hydrocarbons) and computes the resulting gas stream. The two variants are: - **Dry Flue Gas (DFG):** Combustion products with all water vapor **removed/condensed out**. Composition: CO₂, SO₂, N₂, and excess O₂. - **Wet Flue Gas (WFG) / Actual Flue Gas:** Complete combustion products **including water vapor** as it exists in the hot flue gas stream. Composition: CO₂, SO₂, N₂, excess O₂, plus H₂O vapor. > [!NOTE] > The word **"Dry"** simply means the water vapor has been **abstracted from the calculation**, mimicking what an **Orsat apparatus** (a standard lab device) measures. The word **"Wet"** means we retain the water vapor that physically exists in the hot gas. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of combustion like **baking a cake with a recipe**: - The **fuel** is your raw ingredient (it has C, H, O, N, S in fixed amounts). - The **oxygen from air** is the heat/cooking agent you must add. - The **flue gas** is the "exhaust" or "smoke" that comes out of the oven — it's everything that didn't get incorporated into a tasty dish. Now, when you bake: 1. If you add **exactly** the right amount of air → the "exhaust" has only CO₂, SO₂, H₂O, and N₂ (no leftover O₂). This is **theoretical/stoichiometric** combustion. 2. In reality, you almost always add **more** air than needed (excess air, typically 10–50%) to ensure complete burning. This leftover O₂ + extra N₂ passes through into the exhaust. **Dry vs Wet — the "Soup" Analogy:** - Imagine the hot exhaust as a **hot soup**. Water vapor is dissolved in it (invisibly). - If you **cool and condense** the soup, water comes out as liquid. What's left in gaseous form is the **dry flue gas**. - The **wet flue gas** is the original hot soup, water vapor and all. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > Key constants used in KTU fuel calculations: > - **Molecular mass of air (effective)** = **28.97 kg/kmol** (≈ 29) > - **Mass ratio of N₂ to O₂ in air** = **3.76** (i.e., per kg of O₂, 3.76 kg of N₂ is supplied) > - **Volumetric O₂ in air** = **21%** → **N₂ = 79%** → N₂/O₂ ratio by volume = **3.76** > - **Molar volume at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm)** = **22.4 m³/kmol** > - **Molar volume at STP (25 °C, 1 atm)** = **24.45 m³/kmol** > - **Stoichiometric O₂ demand:** 1 kg C needs **2.667 kg O₂**; 1 kg H needs **8 kg O₂**; 1 kg S needs **1 kg O₂** ## 1.4 Combustion Stoichiometry — The Four Core Reactions $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 &&\text{(12 kg C} \rightarrow \text{44 kg CO}_2)\\ 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} &&\text{(4 kg H} \rightarrow \text{36 kg H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{i.e. 1 kg H} \rightarrow \text{9 kg H}_2\text{O})\\ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{SO}_2 &&\text{(32 kg S} \rightarrow \text{64 kg SO}_2)\\ \text{O (in fuel)} &\rightarrow \text{available O}_2 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The oxygen *already present* in the fuel (e.g., in alcohols, biomass, coals) is **subtracted** from the total O₂ demand, because that oxygen is "free" and helps burn some hydrogen internally. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Logic of Flue Gas Estimation The estimation follows a **fixed logical pipeline**: 1. **Step 1 — Identify fuel composition (basis: 1 kg of fuel)** from ultimate analysis: C, H, O, N, S, M (moisture), A (ash) in mass fractions. 2. **Step 2 — Calculate Theoretical Oxygen (TO₂)** required if combustion were 100% efficient with no excess air. 3. **Step 3 — Apply Excess Air (e%)** to compute Actual Oxygen Supplied and Excess O₂ in flue gas. 4. **Step 4 — Compute Theoretical Air (TA)** from TO₂. 5. **Step 5 — Compute mass of each combustion product** using stoichiometric ratios. 6. **Step 6 — Assemble Wet Flue Gas** (all products + H₂O + N₂ from air + N₂ from fuel + excess O₂). 7. **Step 7 — Derive Dry Flue Gas** by **subtracting H₂O** from Wet Flue Gas. 8. **Step 8 (optional) — Convert to volume basis** using molecular weights to get **% composition by volume**, which is what an Orsat apparatus gives. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below contains every equation a KTU student must memorize for Module 4. All quantities are **per kg of fuel** unless noted. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Theoretical O₂ required | $\text{TO}_2 = \tfrac{8}{3}\text{C} + 8\text{H} + \text{S} - \text{O}$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel | O is **subtracted** | | 2 | Theoretical Air (by mass) | $\text{TA}_{\text{mass}} = \text{TO}_2 \times \tfrac{100}{23}$ | kg air / kg fuel | Air = 23% O₂ by mass | | 3 | Theoretical Air (by volume) | $\text{TA}_{\text{vol}} = \text{TO}_2 \times \tfrac{100}{21}$ | m³ air / kg fuel | At NTP, 1 kmol = 22.4 m³ | | 4 | Actual Air supplied | $\text{AA} = \text{TA} \times \left(1 + \tfrac{e}{100}\right)$ | kg or m³ | $e$ = % excess air | | 5 | Actual O₂ supplied | $\text{AO}_2 = \text{TO}_2 \times \left(1 + \tfrac{e}{100}\right)$ | kg O₂ | Direct scaling | | 6 | Excess O₂ in flue gas | $\text{Excess O}_2 = \text{TO}_2 \times \tfrac{e}{100}$ | kg O₂ | This is the unused O₂ | | 7 | N₂ from air | $\text{N}_{2,\text{air}} = 3.76 \times \text{AO}_2$ | kg | Mass ratio basis | | 8 | N₂ from fuel | $\text{N}_{2,\text{fuel}} = \text{N}$ | kg | Direct from ultimate analysis | | 9 | CO₂ formed | $\text{CO}_2 = \tfrac{44}{12}\text{C} = \tfrac{11}{3}\text{C}$ | kg | From carbon combustion | | 10 | SO₂ formed | $\text{SO}_2 = 2\text{S}$ | kg | From sulfur combustion | | 11 | H₂O from H combustion | $\text{H}_2\text{O}_{\text{comb}} = 9\text{H}$ | kg | 1 kg H → 9 kg H₂O | | 12 | H₂O from fuel moisture | $\text{H}_2\text{O}_{\text{moist}} = \text{M}$ | kg | Direct | | 13 | Total Wet Flue Gas (WFG) | $\text{CO}_2 + \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{\text{comb}} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{\text{moist}} + \text{N}_{2,\text{air}} + \text{N}_{2,\text{fuel}} + \text{Excess O}_2$ | kg | Sum of all | | 14 | Total Dry Flue Gas (DFG) | $\text{WFG} - \text{H}_2\text{O}_{\text{total}}$ | kg | Wet minus water | | 15 | Volume of a gas | $V = \dfrac{m}{M} \times 22.4$ | m³ at NTP | $M$ = mol. wt.; $m$ = mass in kg | | 16 | % composition (by volume) | $\%\;i = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | Equivalent to mole % | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Boundary Condition:** All equations assume **complete combustion** (no CO, no soot, no unburnt hydrocarbon). If CO appears, a carbon balance is needed: C → CO₂ + CO (in some ratio). ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Flue gas estimation is **not academic** — it underpins: - **Boiler efficiency** (heat carried away by hot dry + wet flue gas is a major loss term). - **Chimney design** (flue gas volume determines stack diameter and draft). - **Air pollution control** (CO₂ and SO₂ volumes determine scrubber sizing for emission limits). - **Heat recovery** (economizers and air-preheaters are sized using flue gas mass flow). - **Combustion control** (excess air optimization to maximize efficiency and minimize NOₓ). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical O₂ Required Starting from the **three combustion reactions** (per kg basis): **For Carbon (C):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \\ 12\;\text{kg C} &\rightarrow 32\;\text{kg O}_2 \\ 1\;\text{kg C} &\rightarrow \tfrac{32}{12}\;\text{kg O}_2 = \tfrac{8}{3}\;\text{kg O}_2 \end{aligned} $$ **For Hydrogen (H):** $$ \begin{aligned} 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \\ 4\;\text{kg H} &\rightarrow 32\;\text{kg O}_2 \\ 1\;\text{kg H} &\rightarrow \tfrac{32}{4}\;\text{kg O}_2 = 8\;\text{kg O}_2 \end{aligned} $$ **For Sulfur (S):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{S} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \\ 32\;\text{kg S} &\rightarrow 32\;\text{kg O}_2 \\ 1\;\text{kg S} &\rightarrow 1\;\text{kg O}_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Accounting for intrinsic fuel oxygen (O):** The O already in the fuel can burn some H internally. Each kg of O can oxidize **8 kg of H**. So we **subtract O** from the gross O₂ demand: $$ \boxed{\text{TO}_2 \;=\; \tfrac{8}{3}\text{C} \;+\; 8\text{H} \;+\; \text{S} \;-\; \text{O} \quad \left[\tfrac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg fuel}}\right]} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example: Full Flue Gas Estimation (KTU Standard Problem) > [!NOTE] > This is a **worked exemplar** in the same style as KTU ESE problems. A fuel has the following ultimate analysis (by mass, dry basis converted to "as-fired"): **C = 0.78, H = 0.05, O = 0.04, N = 0.015, S = 0.02, Moisture M = 0.03, Ash A = 0.065**. Combustion uses **20% excess air**. Compute (a) theoretical and actual air, (b) dry flue gas composition by mass, (c) wet flue gas composition by mass, (d) dry flue gas composition by volume. ### Step A — Theoretical Oxygen Required $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TO}_2 &= \tfrac{8}{3}(0.78) + 8(0.05) + (0.02) - (0.04)\\ &= 2.080 + 0.400 + 0.020 - 0.040\\ &= 2.460\;\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step B — Theoretical Air (TA) by mass $$ \text{TA}_{\text{mass}} = 2.460 \times \tfrac{100}{23} = 10.696\;\text{kg air} / \text{kg fuel} $$ ### Step C — Actual Air Supplied (with 20% excess) $$ \text{AA} = 10.696 \times 1.20 = 12.835\;\text{kg air} / \text{kg fuel} $$ $$ \text{AO}_2 = 2.460 \times 1.20 = 2.952\;\text{kg O}_2\;\text{supplied} $$ ### Step D — Excess (unused) O₂ in flue gas $$ \text{Excess O}_2 = 2.460 \times 0.20 = 0.492\;\text{kg} $$ ### Step E — N₂ from Air $$ \text{N}_{2,\text{air}} = 3.76 \times 2.952 = 11.100\;\text{kg} $$ ### Step F — Combustion Products (per kg fuel) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CO}_2 &= \tfrac{11}{3}(0.78) = 2.860\;\text{kg}\\ \text{SO}_2 &= 2(0.02) = 0.040\;\text{kg}\\ \text{H}_2\text{O from H} &= 9(0.05) = 0.450\;\text{kg}\\ \text{H}_2\text{O from M} &= 0.030\;\text{kg}\\ \text{Total H}_2\text{O} &= 0.480\;\text{kg}\\ \text{N}_2\text{ from fuel} &= 0.015\;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step G — Total Wet Flue Gas (WFG) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{WFG} &= 2.860 + 0.040 + 0.480 + 11.100 + 0.015 + 0.492\\ &= 14.987\;\text{kg} / \text{kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step H — Total Dry Flue Gas (DFG) $$ \text{DFG} = 14.987 - 0.480 = 14.507\;\text{kg} / \text{kg fuel} $$ ### Step I — Dry Flue Gas Composition by Mass (%) $$ \begin{aligned} \%\text{CO}_2 &= \tfrac{2.860}{14.507}\times 100 = 19.71\%\\ \%\text{SO}_2 &= \tfrac{0.040}{14.507}\times 100 = 0.28\%\\ \%\text{N}_2 &= \tfrac{11.115}{14.507}\times 100 = 76.62\%\\ \%\text{O}_2 &= \tfrac{0.492}{14.507}\times 100 = 3.39\% \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{DFG (mass\%)} :\; 19.71\%\;\text{CO}_2,\; 0.28\%\;\text{SO}_2,\; 76.62\%\;\text{N}_2,\; 3.39\%\;\text{O}_2} $$ ### Step J — Dry Flue Gas Composition by Volume (%) Using $V = \tfrac{m}{M}\times 22.4$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{CO}_2} &= \tfrac{2.860}{44}\times 22.4 = 1.456\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{SO}_2} &= \tfrac{0.040}{64}\times 22.4 = 0.014\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{N}_2} &= \tfrac{11.115}{28}\times 22.4 = 8.892\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{O}_2} &= \tfrac{0.492}{32}\times 22.4 = 0.344\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{DFG}} &= 10.706\;\text{m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \%\text{CO}_2 &= \tfrac{1.456}{10.706}\times 100 = 13.60\%\\ \%\text{SO}_2 &= \tfrac{0.014}{10.706}\times 100 = 0.13\%\\ \%\text{N}_2 &= \tfrac{8.892}{10.706}\times 100 = 83.05\%\\ \%\text{O}_2 &= \tfrac{0.344}{10.706}\times 100 = 3.22\% \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{DFG (vol\%)} :\; 13.60\%\;\text{CO}_2,\; 0.13\%\;\text{SO}_2,\; 83.05\%\;\text{N}_2,\; 3.22\%\;\text{O}_2} $$ > [!NOTE] > Notice the **shift** in percentages between mass-basis and volume-basis. N₂ becomes more dominant by volume because N₂ (MW 28) is lighter per mole than CO₂ (MW 44). This is exactly the discrepancy the Orsat apparatus reveals in real lab work. ## 3.3 Derivation: Wet Flue Gas Volume For the same example, including water vapor: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} &= \tfrac{0.480}{18}\times 22.4 = 0.597\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{WFG}} &= 10.706 + 0.597 = 11.303\;\text{m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{WFG (vol\%)} :\; 12.88\%\;\text{CO}_2,\; 0.12\%\;\text{SO}_2,\; 78.67\%\;\text{N}_2,\; 3.05\%\;\text{O}_2,\; 5.28\%\;\text{H}_2\text{O}} $$ ## 3.4 Inverse Problem: Finding Excess Air from Orsat (DFG) Analysis Often in industry, the engineer **measures** the dry flue gas with an Orsat and **back-calculates** the excess air used. The classical formula: $$ \boxed{\%\;\text{Excess Air} = \dfrac{\%\text{O}_2}{\%\text{N}_2} \times \dfrac{79}{21} \times 100 \;\;\;\text{(for CO}_2\text{-free basis, assuming no CO)}} $$ Or, more simply, for the **as-measured** dry flue gas: $$ \%e = \dfrac{\%\text{O}_2}{0.264\;\times\%\text{N}_2 - \%\text{O}_2}\times 100 \quad \text{(standard Orsat formula)} $$ This is a **favorite KTU 3-mark question**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Combustion & Flue Gas Formation — Block Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fuel Feed] --> B[Ultimate Analysis Input
C H O N S M A] B --> C[Stoichiometric Oxygen
TO2 calculation] B --> D[Excess Air Specification
e percent] C --> E[Actual O2 Supplied
AO2] D --> E E --> F[Combustion Reactor
complete combustion assumed] F --> G[CO2 from C] F --> H[SO2 from S] F --> I[H2O from H and M] F --> J[N2 from air 3.76 x AO2] F --> K[N2 from fuel N] F --> L[Excess O2 unreacted] G --> M[Wet Flue Gas Stream
WFG] H --> M I --> M J --> M K --> M L --> M M --> N[Condensation / Water Removal] N --> O[Dry Flue Gas Stream
DFG] M --> P[Volume Basis via 22.4 m3 per kmol] O --> P P --> Q[Final Flue Gas Composition
mass percent or volume percent] ``` ## 4.2 Mass Balance Schematic — Per kg Fuel ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Inputs per kg fuel] A1[Carbon C kg] A2[Hydrogen H kg] A3[Oxygen O kg] A4[Sulfur S kg] A5[Nitrogen N kg] A6[Moisture M kg] end subgraph AIR[Air Supply] B1[Theoretical O2: 2.460 kg] B2[Excess O2: 0.492 kg] B3[N2 from air: 11.100 kg] end subgraph OUTPUT[Flue Gas Products] C1[CO2 2.860 kg] C2[SO2 0.040 kg] C3[H2O 0.480 kg] C4[N2 total 11.115 kg] C5[O2 excess 0.492 kg] end A1 --> C1 A2 --> C3 A4 --> C2 A5 --> C4 A6 --> C3 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B1 --> C3 B2 --> C5 B3 --> C4 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Flue Gas To Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What does the problem ask?} Q1 -->|Mass of products per kg fuel| R1[Compute WFG mass
then subtract H2O for DFG] Q1 -->|Orsat-style volume percent| R2[Compute DFG volume
exclude H2O] Q1 -->|Boiler heat loss / dew point| R3[Use WFG
water vapor must be included] Q1 -->|Emission control scrubber sizing| R4[Use DFG
SO2 and CO2 in dry basis] R1 --> S1[Apply formulas 9 to 14] R2 --> S2[Use V = m by M x 22.4 on DFG only] R3 --> S3[Retain all species including H2O] R4 --> S4[Retain SO2 CO2 O2 N2 only] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Memory Aid:** If the problem mentions an **Orsat apparatus**, the answer is the **dry basis** because Orsat chemically absorbs gases from a water-removed sample. If the problem mentions **heat loss in flue gas** or **dew point corrosion**, the answer is the **wet basis** because water vapor is energetically significant. ## 4.4 Stoichiometric Data Lookup Table (Block Reference) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Element in Fuel] --> B{Combustible?} B -->|Yes| C[Calculate O2 Demand] B -->|No| D[Passes through to flue gas as is] C --> E[Per kg basis] E --> F1[C : needs 2.667 kg O2] E --> F2[H : needs 8.000 kg O2] E --> F3[S : needs 1.000 kg O2] E --> F4[O in fuel : supplies 1 kg O per kg O] D --> G1[N2 from fuel : direct] D --> G2[Moisture : contributes H2O] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Dry Flue Gas and Wet Flue Gas. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023] **Model Answer:** > **Dry Flue Gas (DFG):** The mixture of combustion products obtained after **removing all water vapor** (H₂O) from the flue gas. It principally contains CO₂, SO₂, N₂, and any unused O₂ (excess air). It corresponds to the gas composition measured by an **Orsat apparatus** where water is absorbed before analysis. **[1.5 Marks]** > **Wet Flue Gas (WFG):** The actual hot gaseous product of combustion, **including water vapor** formed from the combustion of hydrogen in the fuel and from any moisture originally present in the fuel. It is the "true" composition of the hot exhaust stream used for **boiler heat-loss calculations**. **[1.5 Marks]** --- ### Question 2: Why is 3.76 used as the N₂/O₂ ratio in air? [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Model Answer:** > Atmospheric air is a mixture of approximately **21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume**. **[1 Mark]** > > Since equal volumes of gases at the same T and P contain equal moles (Avogadro's law), the **molar ratio N₂/O₂ = 79/21 = 3.762 ≈ 3.76**. **[1 Mark]** > > Because molecular weights of O₂ (32) and N₂ (28) are close, the **mass ratio** is also numerically close to 3.76. Hence, for every kg of O₂ supplied in combustion air, **3.76 kg of N₂** is also supplied. **[1 Mark]** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU ESE pattern: Each Part B carries **internal choice** (a or b). Both questions are provided below. You will be asked to attempt **any one**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) A coal has the following ultimate analysis: **C = 72%, H = 5%, O = 8%, N = 1.5%, S = 1.0%, Moisture = 3.0%, Ash = 9.5%**. The coal is burned with **30% excess air**. Calculate: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** - **(a)** Theoretical air required per kg of coal. **[7 Marks]** - **(b)** Dry and Wet flue gas composition by mass and by volume. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution (a): Theoretical Air [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Identify composition (per kg coal, as-fired):** C = 0.72, H = 0.05, O = 0.08, N = 0.015, S = 0.01, M = 0.03, A = 0.095 **[0.5 Marks]** **Step 2 — Theoretical Oxygen:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TO}_2 &= \tfrac{8}{3}(0.72) + 8(0.05) + (0.01) - (0.08)\\ &= 1.920 + 0.400 + 0.010 - 0.080\\ &= 2.250\;\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg coal} \end{aligned} $$ **[2 Marks]** **Step 3 — Theoretical Air (by mass):** $$ \text{TA} = 2.250 \times \tfrac{100}{23} = 9.783\;\text{kg air} / \text{kg coal} $$ **[1.5 Marks]** **Step 4 — Actual Air Supplied (30% excess):** $$ \text{AA} = 9.783 \times 1.30 = 12.718\;\text{kg air} / \text{kg coal} $$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 5 — Actual O₂ Supplied:** $$ \text{AO}_2 = 2.250 \times 1.30 = 2.925\;\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg coal} $$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 6 — Excess O₂ in flue gas:** $$ \text{Excess O}_2 = 2.250 \times 0.30 = 0.675\;\text{kg O}_2 $$ **[1 Mark]** #### Model Solution (b): Flue Gas Compositions [7 Marks] **Step 7 — Masses of products per kg coal:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CO}_2 &= \tfrac{11}{3}(0.72) = 2.640\;\text{kg}\\ \text{SO}_2 &= 2(0.01) = 0.020\;\text{kg}\\ \text{H}_2\text{O from H} &= 9(0.05) = 0.450\;\text{kg}\\ \text{H}_2\text{O from M} &= 0.030\;\text{kg}\\ \text{Total H}_2\text{O} &= 0.480\;\text{kg}\\ \text{N}_2\text{ from air} &= 3.76 \times 2.925 = 10.998\;\text{kg}\\ \text{N}_2\text{ from fuel} &= 0.015\;\text{kg}\\ \text{Total N}_2 &= 11.013\;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **[3 Marks]** **Step 8 — Total Wet Flue Gas (mass):** $$ \text{WFG} = 2.640 + 0.020 + 0.480 + 11.013 + 0.675 = 14.828\;\text{kg} $$ **Step 9 — Total Dry Flue Gas (mass):** $$ \text{DFG} = 14.828 - 0.480 = 14.348\;\text{kg} $$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 10 — Dry Flue Gas Composition by Mass (%):** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\text{CO}_2 &= \tfrac{2.640}{14.348}\times 100 = 18.40\%\\ \%\text{SO}_2 &= \tfrac{0.020}{14.348}\times 100 = 0.14\%\\ \%\text{N}_2 &= \tfrac{11.013}{14.348}\times 100 = 76.76\%\\ \%\text{O}_2 &= \tfrac{0.675}{14.348}\times 100 = 4.70\% \end{aligned} $$ **[1.5 Marks]** **Step 11 — Dry Flue Gas Composition by Volume (using $V = \tfrac{m}{M}\times 22.4$):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{CO}_2} &= \tfrac{2.640}{44}\times 22.4 = 1.344\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{SO}_2} &= \tfrac{0.020}{64}\times 22.4 = 0.007\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{N}_2} &= \tfrac{11.013}{28}\times 22.4 = 8.810\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{O}_2} &= \tfrac{0.675}{32}\times 22.4 = 0.473\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{DFG}} &= 10.634\;\text{m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \%\text{CO}_2 &= 12.64\%, \quad \%\text{SO}_2 = 0.07\%\\ \%\text{N}_2 &= 82.85\%, \quad \%\text{O}_2 = 4.44\% \end{aligned} $$ **[1.5 Marks]** **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{DFG (mass\%)} &: 18.40\%\,\text{CO}_2,\; 0.14\%\,\text{SO}_2,\; 76.76\%\,\text{N}_2,\; 4.70\%\,\text{O}_2\\ \text{DFG (vol\%)} &: 12.64\%\,\text{CO}_2,\; 0.07\%\,\text{SO}_2,\; 82.85\%\,\text{N}_2,\; 4.44\%\,\text{O}_2 \end{aligned} } $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice A fuel oil has the ultimate analysis: **C = 85%, H = 12%, O = 1%, N = 0.5%, S = 1.5%**. It is burnt with **25% excess air**. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** - **(a)** Calculate the mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel. **[7 Marks]** - **(b)** Determine the volumetric composition of the dry flue gas. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution (a): Mass of Dry Flue Gas [7 Marks] **Step 1 — TO₂:** $$ \text{TO}_2 = \tfrac{8}{3}(0.85) + 8(0.12) + (0.015) - (0.01) = 2.267 + 0.96 + 0.015 - 0.01 = 3.232\;\text{kg O}_2 $$ **[2 Marks]** **Step 2 — Actual O₂ (25% excess):** $$ \text{AO}_2 = 3.232 \times 1.25 = 4.040\;\text{kg O}_2 $$ **Step 3 — Excess O₂ in flue gas:** $$ \text{Excess O}_2 = 3.232 \times 0.25 = 0.808\;\text{kg} $$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 4 — N₂ from air:** $$ \text{N}_2 = 3.76 \times 4.040 = 15.190\;\text{kg} $$ **Step 5 — N₂ from fuel:** $$ \text{N}_2\text{ fuel} = 0.005\;\text{kg} $$ **Step 6 — CO₂ from C:** $$ \text{CO}_2 = \tfrac{11}{3}(0.85) = 3.117\;\text{kg} $$ **Step 7 — SO₂ from S:** $$ \text{SO}_2 = 2(0.015) = 0.030\;\text{kg} $$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 8 — H₂O formed:** $$ \text{H}_2\text{O} = 9(0.12) = 1.080\;\text{kg} $$ **Step 9 — Mass of Dry Flue Gas (excluding H₂O):** $$ \boxed{\text{DFG} = 0.808 + 15.195 + 3.117 + 0.030 = 19.150\;\text{kg} / \text{kg fuel}} $$ **[2 Marks]** #### Model Solution (b): Volumetric Composition of DFG [7 Marks] Using $V = \tfrac{m}{M}\times 22.4$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{CO}_2} &= \tfrac{3.117}{44}\times 22.4 = 1.587\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{SO}_2} &= \tfrac{0.030}{64}\times 22.4 = 0.0105\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{N}_2} &= \tfrac{15.195}{28}\times 22.4 = 12.156\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{O}_2} &= \tfrac{0.808}{32}\times 22.4 = 0.566\;\text{m}^3\\ V_{\text{DFG}} &= 14.319\;\text{m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **[4 Marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\text{CO}_2 &= \tfrac{1.587}{14.319}\times 100 = 11.08\%\\ \%\text{SO}_2 &= \tfrac{0.0105}{14.319}\times 100 = 0.07\%\\ \%\text{N}_2 &= \tfrac{12.156}{14.319}\times 100 = 84.89\%\\ \%\text{O}_2 &= \tfrac{0.566}{14.319}\times 100 = 3.95\% \end{aligned} $$ **[3 Marks]** $$ \boxed{\text{DFG (vol\%)} :\; 11.08\%\;\text{CO}_2,\; 0.07\%\;\text{SO}_2,\; 84.89\%\;\text{N}_2,\; 3.95\%\;\text{O}_2} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to subtract intrinsic O₂** from the fuel. Many students compute $\tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S$ only, **forgetting the −O term**. This inflates air requirement and gives wrong DFG composition. (Penalty: 1–2 marks) > > 2. **Confusing mass ratio (100/23) vs volume ratio (100/21)** for theoretical air. By mass, air is ~23% O₂; by volume, 21% O₂. Mixing them gives wrong absolute numbers. (Penalty: 1 mark) > > 3. **Using 79/21 = 3.76 as a mass ratio** when the question asks for **volume composition of DFG** — the conversion needs molecular weights: $V = \tfrac{m}{M}\times 22.4$. (Penalty: 1–2 marks) > > 4. **Including H₂O when problem asks for "Orsat basis" or "dry flue gas volume"** — Orsat removes water. (Penalty: 1 mark) > > 5. **Forgetting N₂ from the fuel itself** — it's a small number but examiners check. (Penalty: 0.5 mark) > > 6. **Not converting percentages to a sum of 100%** — if you get 99.6% or 100.4%, it indicates a rounding or omission error. > > 7. **Unit slip-ups** — kg vs m³ vs kmol. State units explicitly at every step. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points:** > > - **DFG = wet flue gas − H₂O (vapor).** Orsat measures DFG. WFG is the hot, real stream. > - **O₂ demand per kg fuel:** $\tfrac{8}{3}$C + 8H + S − O (all in mass fractions per kg). > - **Theoretical Air = TO₂ × (100/23) by mass** = TO₂ × (100/21) by volume. > - **Actual Air = TA × (1 + e/100)**, where e = % excess air. > - **Excess O₂ in flue gas = TO₂ × (e/100)** (this is the unused O₂ you see in Orsat). > - **N₂ from air = 3.76 × Actual O₂** supplied (mass basis). > - **Don't forget to add N₂ from fuel** itself, and **H₂O from moisture** in the fuel. > - **CO₂ = (11/3) × C**, **SO₂ = 2S**, **H₂O from H = 9H**, **all in kg/kg fuel.** > - **Volume conversion:** $V_i = \dfrac{m_i}{M_i}\times 22.4$ m³ at NTP per kg of fuel. M = molecular weight. > - **Volume % = mole %** (for ideal gases at same T, P). Divide each $V_i$ by total $V$. > - **Inverse problem:** given Orsat %O₂ and %N₂, compute % excess air = $\frac{\%\text{O}_2}{0.264\;\times\%\text{N}_2 - \%\text{O}_2}\times 100$. > - **Sanity check:** N₂ is always the largest component (≥ 75%) in DFG. CO₂ is 10–20%. O₂ is small (1–5%) unless excess air is huge. > - **Assumption:** complete combustion — no CO, no unburnt fuel, no soot. If CO is given, redistribute carbon between CO₂ and CO. > - **Constant to memorize:** **3.76** (N₂/O₂ ratio in air), **22.4 m³/kmol** (NTP), **MWs** — CO₂ = 44, SO₂ = 64, N₂ = 28, O₂ = 32, H₂O = 18. > - **Mass % ≠ Volume %** — N₂ dominates more by volume because it's lighter per mole than CO₂. > - **Energy & environment link:** Knowing flue gas composition lets you compute heat losses, dew point, and SO₂/CO₂ emissions — all critical in KTU's broader "Combustion Engineering" course.

topics.title: Calculation of the Composition of Fuel and Excess Air Supplied
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calculation of the Composition of Fuel and Excess Air Supplied

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In combustion engineering, the **Composition of Fuel** refers to the elemental and chemical breakdown of a fuel obtained through **Ultimate Analysis** (Carbon, Hydrogen, Sulphur, Oxygen, Nitrogen, and Ash content on a mass percentage basis) and the **volumetric/percentage composition of the resulting flue gas products** (CO₂, CO, O₂, N₂, SO₂) measured via **Orsat Apparatus**.

**Excess Air Supplied** is formally defined as the quantity of air provided to the combustion process **beyond the stoichiometric (theoretically exact) requirement** needed for complete combustion of the fuel. It is universally expressed as:

$$\% \text{ Excess Air} = \frac{\text{Actual Air Supplied} - \text{Stoichiometric Air Required}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air Required}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** The calculation of excess air using **Orsat (flue gas) analysis** is a HIGH-WEIGHTAGE topic. Students must master the dry-basis flue gas composition interpretation: typically CO₂ (10–16%), O₂ (0–8%), CO (0–4%), and N₂ (balance ~70–80%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used:**
> - Molecular weight of dry air = **28.97 kg/kg-mol ≈ 29 kg/kg-mol**
> - Air composition (by volume): O₂ = **21%**, N₂ = **79%**
> - Mass ratio: 1 kg-mol O₂ corresponds to **137.16 kg of dry air** (4.76 × 28.97)
> - Stoichiometric A/F ratio for pure Carbon = **11.44** (mass basis)
> - Stoichiometric A/F ratio for pure H₂ = **34.56** (mass basis)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are grilling food on a charcoal stove:

- The **stoichiometric air** is the **"perfect pinch of salt"** — the exact amount theoretically required to completely burn every piece of charcoal to CO₂ and H₂O without leaving any unburnt carbon behind.
- In real life, you cannot supply fuel and air in such perfect proportion. The combustion is chaotic — some carbon particles escape unburnt, some CO fails to convert fully to CO₂, and pockets of fuel get starved of oxygen.
- To prevent this **incomplete combustion** (which wastes fuel and creates soot/smoke), engineers supply **MORE air than theoretically needed** — this "extra cushion" of air is called **Excess Air**.

**Practical Implications:**
- ✅ **Some excess air (10–25%) is GOOD** — it ensures complete combustion, higher efficiency, and lower pollution.
- ❌ **Too much excess air (above 50%) is BAD** — it absorbs heat, dilutes flame temperature, and carries away useful energy up the stack as hot flue gas waste.

Think of excess air as a **safety margin** — like adding a little extra water while cooking rice so it doesn't burn at the bottom. The right amount is the engineering sweet spot.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

This topic is the **mathematical backbone** of the entire Fuel Engineering course. Almost every numerical problem in Module 4 — from boiler efficiency to heat balance — depends on correctly calculating:

1. The **theoretical (stoichiometric) oxygen/air requirement** from ultimate analysis.
2. The **actual excess air** from flue gas (Orsat) analysis.
3. The **Air-Fuel ratio** (both stoichiometric and actual).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic directly satisfies **CO4** — *Calculate combustion parameters including air requirements, flue gas composition, and excess air for solid, liquid, and gaseous fuels.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Two Parallel Calculation Routes

There are **two independent methods** to determine the air-fuel relationship, and KTU examiners frequently interconvert them in problems:

### Route A: From Ultimate Analysis (Fuel-Side Calculation)
You are given the fuel composition (C, H, S, O, N, Ash by mass %) and asked to compute theoretical O₂ requirement.

### Route B: From Orsat/Flue Gas Analysis (Product-Side Calculation)
You are given the dry flue gas composition (% CO₂, % CO, % O₂, % N₂) and asked to back-calculate the actual excess air supplied.

Both routes must converge to the **same answer** if the combustion is in steady state.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Route A (From Ultimate Analysis)

**Step 1 — Identify the fuel constituents** (mass %): C, H₂, S, O₂, N₂, Ash.
(Note: H₂, S, O₂, N₂ are written as elements although chemically present in compounds.)

**Step 2 — Write the stoichiometric combustion reactions:**

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$
$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the moles of O₂ required per kg of fuel** using molecular weights:

$$O_2^{\text{req}} = \frac{32}{12}C + \frac{32}{4}H + \frac{32}{32}S - O_{\text{fuel}}$$

where C, H, S, O_fuel are in **mass fractions (per kg of fuel)**. The `-O_fuel` term accounts for **internally available oxygen** that offsets the requirement.

**Step 4 — Convert moles of O₂ to mass of air:**

$$\text{Theoretical Air} = O_2^{\text{req}} \times \frac{100}{21} \times \frac{29}{32} = O_2^{\text{req}} \times 11.6 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

(The factor 100/21 = 4.76 accounts for air composition; 29/32 converts O₂ moles to air moles by mass.)

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: Route B (From Orsat Analysis)

**Step 1 — Take dry flue gas composition** (e.g., CO₂ = a%, CO = b%, O₂ = c%, N₂ = d%).

**Step 2 — Establish carbon balance per 100 moles of dry flue gas:**
All carbon in fuel appears in flue gas as CO₂ and CO. If the fuel contains only C, H, S, then:

$$\text{Total Carbon in flue gas} = a + b \text{ (moles per 100 mol dry FG)}$$

**Step 3 — Establish oxygen balance:**
- O₂ supplied via air = (21/79) × N₂ in flue gas = 0.268 × d
- O₂ accounted in products = (a + 0.5b) [from CO₂ and CO] + 0.5d_H₂O contribution

**Step 4 — Apply the Excess Air Formula (Universal):**

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Excess Air} = \frac{\text{O}_2 \text{ in flue gas}}{\text{O}_2 \text{ used in combustion}} \times 100 = \frac{c}{0.268 d - c} \times 100}$$

For the **simplified case** where the fuel contains only C and H₂ (no S, no O in fuel), a cleaner expression is:

$$\% \text{ Excess Air} = \frac{\text{O}_2 \text{ present}}{\text{O}_2 \text{ actually used}} \times 100 = \frac{0.9 \, c}{0.9\,c + (\text{stoichiometric O}_2 \text{ used})} \times 100$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ required (per kg fuel) | $\frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O_{\text{fuel}}$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | C, H₂, S, O in mass fractions |
| 2 | Theoretical Air Required (per kg fuel) | $\frac{O_2^{\text{req}}}{0.232}$ | kg air/kg fuel | 0.232 = mass fraction of O₂ in air |
| 3 | Theoretical Air (mole basis) | $4.76 \times O_2^{\text{req}}$ | kg-mol air/kg fuel | 4.76 = 100/21 (air/O₂ mole ratio) |
| 4 | Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass) | $11.6 \times O_2^{\text{req}}$ | dimensionless | Critical exam formula |
| 5 | Actual A/F ratio | Theoretical A/F × (1 + EA/100) | dimensionless | EA = % Excess Air |
| 6 | Excess Air from Orsat (universal) | $\frac{c}{0.268 d - c} \times 100$ | % | c = %O₂, d = %N₂ in dry FG |
| 7 | Excess Air (simplified) | $\frac{\text{O}_2 \text{ in FG}}{\text{O}_2 \text{ used}} \times 100$ | % | When fuel has no S, no O |
| 8 | Combustion Efficiency (Dry FG basis) | $\frac{\text{CO}_2}{\text{CO}_2 + \text{CO}} \times 100$ | % | Measures combustion completeness |
| 9 | N₂/Fuel ratio (for back-calc) | $\frac{N_2/77 \times 100}{100 \text{ kg fuel}}$ | kg-mol | When only C and H₂ in fuel |
| 10 | Molecular weight dry air | 28.97 ≈ **29** | kg/kg-mol | Standard textbook value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠️ Critical Pipe Symbol Warning for Tables:** In the table above, vertical separators (e.g., for "kg air/kg fuel") have been rendered using the `/` slash to avoid breaking markdown. KTU students should mentally interpret these as "per" divisions. In handwritten exams, use standard fraction bars.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler Operation:** Operators use **O₂ trim controls** in boilers to maintain 2–4% O₂ in flue gas, corresponding to ~10–20% excess air for optimal efficiency.
- **Power Plants:** A 1% reduction in excess air can improve boiler efficiency by **0.5–1%**, saving millions of rupees annually in a 500 MW plant.
- **IC Engines:** Air-fuel ratio directly controls emissions, knocking, and brake specific fuel consumption.
- **Process Industries (Refineries, Cement, Steel):** Continuous flue gas monitoring with online oxygen analyzers ensures regulatory compliance and fuel economy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Stoichiometric Air from Ultimate Analysis

**Given:** A fuel with the following ultimate analysis on a mass basis (per 1 kg of fuel):
- C = 0.80 kg, H₂ = 0.05 kg, S = 0.02 kg, O₂ = 0.03 kg, N₂ = 0.01 kg, Ash = 0.09 kg

**Step 1: Stoichiometric combustion reactions and their oxygen demand:**

| Reaction | O₂ required (kg O₂ per kg of element) |
|----------|---------------------------------------|
| C + O₂ → CO₂ | 32/12 = 2.667 kg O₂/kg C |
| H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O | 16/2 = 8.000 kg O₂/kg H₂ |
| S + O₂ → SO₂ | 32/32 = 1.000 kg O₂/kg S |

**Step 2: Total O₂ required before subtracting internal oxygen:**

$$\begin{aligned}
O_2^{\text{req, total}} &= \left(\frac{32}{12}\right)C + \left(\frac{32}{4}\right)H_2 + \left(\frac{32}{32}\right)S \\
&= \left(\frac{32}{12}\right)(0.80) + \left(\frac{32}{4}\right)(0.05) + \left(\frac{32}{32}\right)(0.02) \\
&= 2.667 \times 0.80 + 8.000 \times 0.05 + 1.000 \times 0.02 \\
&= 2.1333 + 0.4000 + 0.0200 \\
&= 2.5533 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Subtract internally available oxygen (already combined with fuel elements):**

$$O_2^{\text{net required}} = 2.5533 - O_{\text{fuel}} = 2.5533 - 0.03 = 2.5233 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

**Step 4: Convert to theoretical air requirement:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical Air} &= \frac{O_2^{\text{net required}}}{0.232} = \frac{2.5233}{0.232} \\
&= 10.876 \text{ kg air per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass basis):**

$$\boxed{\text{A/F}_{\text{stoich}} = 10.876 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}}$$

If 25% excess air is supplied:

$$A/F_{\text{actual}} = 10.876 \times 1.25 = 13.595 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Excess Air from Orsat Analysis

**Given:** Dry flue gas analysis (Orsat): CO₂ = 12%, CO = 1.5%, O₂ = 4.5%, N₂ = 82.0%.

**Step 1: Confirm that the percentages sum to 100%:**
12 + 1.5 + 4.5 + 82.0 = 100% ✓ (Dry basis, so H₂O is excluded)

**Step 2: Establish a basis of 100 moles of dry flue gas.**

In these 100 moles:
- CO₂ = 12 mol (contributes 12 C atoms and 24 O atoms)
- CO = 1.5 mol (contributes 1.5 C atoms and 1.5 O atoms)
- O₂ = 4.5 mol (free, unreacted oxygen — this is the **excess**)
- N₂ = 82.0 mol (came entirely from air, since fuel nitrogen is usually negligible)

**Step 3: Compute total O₂ actually supplied via air (from nitrogen balance):**

Since N₂ in air = 79% and O₂ in air = 21%:

$$\text{Total O}_2 \text{ supplied} = \frac{21}{79} \times N_2^{\text{FG}} = 0.2658 \times 82.0 = 21.798 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4: Compute O₂ actually consumed (from products):**

$$\begin{aligned}
O_2^{\text{consumed}} &= O_2^{\text{supplied}} - O_2^{\text{unreacted}} \\
&= 21.798 - 4.5 \\
&= 17.298 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Apply the excess air formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Excess Air} &= \frac{O_2^{\text{in flue gas}}}{O_2^{\text{used}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4.5}{17.298} \times 100 \\
&= 26.01\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Cross-verification (using the universal formula):**

$$\% \text{ EA} = \frac{c}{0.268 d - c} \times 100 = \frac{4.5}{0.268 \times 82.0 - 4.5} \times 100 = \frac{4.5}{17.476} \times 100 = 25.75\%$$

(The minor difference of 0.26% arises from the approximation 21/79 ≈ 0.268 vs the exact 0.2658.)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Templates

| Problem Type | Inputs | What to Compute | Key Formula |
|--------------|--------|-----------------|-------------|
| Type 1 | Ultimate analysis (C, H, S, O) | Theoretical air required | $\frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O$ divided by 0.232 |
| Type 2 | Ultimate analysis + excess air | Actual air & A/F ratio | $A/F_{stoich} \times (1 + EA/100)$ |
| Type 3 | Orsat analysis (CO₂, CO, O₂, N₂) | % Excess air | $\frac{O_2^{FG}}{O_2^{consumed}} \times 100$ |
| Type 4 | Ultimate + Orsat | Air actually supplied (kg/kg fuel) | Mass balance on N₂ |
| Type 5 | Orsat analysis | Mass of dry flue gas per kg fuel | N₂ ratio × 28.97/22.4 |
| Type 6 | Orsat + ultimate | C/H ratio verification | Carbon balance check |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Combustion Air-Fuel Calculation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Fuel Source] --> B{Fuel Data Available?}
    B -- Ultimate Analysis --> C[Route A: Fuel-Side Calculation]
    B -- Orsat Flue Gas --> D[Route B: Product-Side Calculation]
    
    C --> C1[Identify C, H2, S, O2 mass fractions]
    C1 --> C2[Compute theoretical O2 per kg fuel]
    C2 --> C3[Apply: O2_req = 8C/3 + 8H2 + S - O]
    C3 --> C4[Theoretical Air = O2_req / 0.232]
    C4 --> C5[Actual Air = Theoretical Air x 1 + EA/100]
    
    D --> D1[Identify CO2, CO, O2, N2 percentages]
    D1 --> D2[Confirm sum equals 100 percent dry basis]
    D2 --> D3[Total O2 supplied = 21 by 79 x N2 in FG]
    D3 --> D4[O2 consumed = O2 supplied - O2 unreacted]
    D4 --> D5[Excess Air percent = O2 in FG by O2 used x 100]
    D5 --> D6[Back-calc A/F ratio using N2 balance]
    
    C5 --> E[Convergence Check]
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[Final Report: Air-Fuel Ratio and Combustion Parameters]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Inputs and Outputs of the Calculation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        A1[Ultimate Analysis: C H2 S O2 N2 Ash]
        A2[Orsat Flue Gas: CO2 CO O2 N2]
        A3[Process Parameter: Excess Air percent]
    end
    
    subgraph CALCULATION_ENGINE
        B1[Stoichiometric Combustion Module]
        B2[Oxygen Balance Solver]
        B3[Nitrogen Balance Module]
        B4[Air-Fuel Ratio Calculator]
        B5[Combustion Efficiency Estimator]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        C1[Theoretical Air kg per kg fuel]
        C2[Actual Air kg per kg fuel]
        C3[Air-Fuel Ratio mass and mole basis]
        C4[Dry Flue Gas Composition]
        C5[Combustion Efficiency percent]
        C6[Stack Gas Heat Loss]
    end
    
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B4
    
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C4
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C3
    B5 --> C5
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C6
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: Relationship Between Key Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Ultimate Analysis] --> Q1[Stoichiometric O2]
    P2[Orsat Analysis] --> Q2[Actual O2 supplied]
    Q1 --> R1[Theoretical Air]
    Q2 --> R2[Actual Air]
    R1 --> S1[Excess Air percent]
    R2 --> S1
    S1 --> T1[Air-Fuel Ratio actual]
    R1 --> T2[Air-Fuel Ratio stoichiometric]
    T1 --> U1[Combustion Efficiency]
    T2 --> U1
    U1 --> V1[Boiler/Engine Performance]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term 'Excess Air' in combustion. Why is it necessary to supply excess air in practical combustion systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Excess air is the quantity of air supplied to a combustion process in excess of the stoichiometric (theoretically exact) requirement needed for complete combustion of the fuel. It is expressed as:

$$\% \text{ Excess Air} = \frac{\text{Actual Air} - \text{Stoichiometric Air}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air}} \times 100$$

It is necessary because (i) in real furnaces, fuel and air do not mix instantaneously and perfectly, so some fuel particles remain unburnt without excess oxygen; (ii) supplying a small excess (10–25%) ensures complete combustion, improving efficiency and reducing CO, soot, and smoke emissions; (iii) it acts as a thermal cushion to prevent localized fuel-rich pockets and explosion hazards. **[3 marks]**

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is Orsat apparatus? What is the principle on which it works?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Orsat apparatus is a portable gas analysis instrument used to determine the composition of **dry flue gas** from combustion systems. It works on the principle of **selective absorption** of different gas components in specific chemical reagents:
- **CO₂** is absorbed in **Potassium Hydroxide (KOH)**
- **CO** is absorbed in **Ammoniacal Cuprous Chloride (Cu₂Cl₂)**
- **O₂** is absorbed in **Pyrogallol (or alkaline pyrogallol)**
- **N₂** is determined **by difference** (residual unabsorbed gas)

The apparatus excludes water vapour and SO₂, so it gives the dry-basis analysis of CO₂, CO, O₂, and N₂. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]

**(a)** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis: C = 78%, H₂ = 5%, S = 1.5%, O₂ = 6%, N₂ = 1.5%, Ash = 8%. Calculate: (i) Theoretical oxygen required per kg of fuel; (ii) Theoretical air required per kg of fuel; (iii) Actual air supplied if 30% excess air is used. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The dry flue gas analysis from a boiler using the above coal shows: CO₂ = 13%, CO = 1%, O₂ = 6%, N₂ = 80%. Calculate: (i) Percentage excess air supplied; (ii) The actual air-fuel ratio (mass basis) using the flue gas data. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) Solution — Fuel-Side Calculation

**Given:** C = 0.78, H₂ = 0.05, S = 0.015, O₂ = 0.06 (all in mass fractions per kg of fuel).

**(i) Theoretical oxygen required:**

$$\begin{aligned}
O_2^{\text{req}} &= \frac{32}{12}C + \frac{32}{4}H_2 + \frac{32}{32}S - O_{\text{fuel}} \\
&= \frac{32}{12}(0.78) + \frac{32}{4}(0.05) + \frac{32}{32}(0.015) - 0.06 \\
&= 2.080 + 0.400 + 0.015 - 0.060 \\
&= 2.435 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Theoretical air required:**

$$\text{Theoretical Air} = \frac{O_2^{\text{req}}}{0.232} = \frac{2.435}{0.232} = 10.496 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**(iii) Actual air with 30% excess:**

$$\text{Actual Air} = 10.496 \times (1 + 0.30) = 10.496 \times 1.30 = 13.645 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

> **Marks Distribution (Part a):** O₂ calculation [3], Theoretical air [2], Actual air with excess [2] = **7 marks**

#### Part (b) Solution — Product-Side Calculation

**Given:** CO₂ = 13%, CO = 1%, O₂ = 6%, N₂ = 80% (basis: 100 moles of dry flue gas).

**(i) Percentage excess air:**

Using the universal formula:

$$\% \text{ EA} = \frac{\text{O}_2 \text{ in FG}}{\text{O}_2 \text{ used in combustion}} \times 100$$

Total O₂ supplied = (21/79) × N₂ in FG = 0.2658 × 80 = 21.27 mol

$$\begin{aligned}
O_2^{\text{consumed}} &= O_2^{\text{supplied}} - O_2^{\text{free}} = 21.27 - 6 = 15.27 \text{ mol} \\
\% \text{ EA} &= \frac{6}{15.27} \times 100 = 39.29\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Actual A/F ratio from flue gas:**

N₂ in flue gas per kg of fuel: We need to back-calculate. The 80 mol of N₂ in FG came from air, and air contains 79% N₂.

$$\text{Total air moles per 100 mol FG} = \frac{80}{0.79} = 101.27 \text{ mol air}$$

Mass of this air = 101.27 × 29 / 1000 = 2.937 kg air per "100 mol of dry FG."

Now, find the kg of fuel corresponding to 100 mol of dry FG using carbon balance:

Total C in 100 mol FG = CO₂ + CO = 13 + 1 = 14 mol of C atoms.
Mass of C in 14 mol = 14 × 12 = 168 g = 0.168 kg of C.
Since fuel has 78% C, kg of fuel = 0.168 / 0.78 = 0.2154 kg.

$$\boxed{\text{A/F}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{2.937}{0.2154} = 13.63 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}}$$

**Comparison with Part (a):** Theor. A/F (Part a) = 10.50; Actual A/F (Part b) = 13.63. The implied excess air from Part (b) is (13.63/10.50 − 1) × 100 = 29.8% ≈ 30% ✓ (Matches the 30% from Part a within flue gas analysis rounding tolerance).

> **Marks Distribution (Part b):** % Excess air calculation [3], Carbon balance for fuel mass [2], Air-fuel ratio final [2] = **7 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]

**(a)** A furnace oil has the following composition by mass: C = 85%, H = 12%, S = 2%, Ash = 1%. Calculate the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio on mass and mole basis. The flue gas analysis shows: CO₂ = 12.5%, O₂ = 5.5%, N₂ = 82%. Verify whether the excess air computed from flue gas matches the actual air supplied. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the difference between 'theoretical air' and 'excess air' with a neat sketch. A gaseous fuel with composition CH₄ = 80%, C₂H₆ = 10%, CO = 5%, N₂ = 5% is burnt with 20% excess air. Calculate the actual A/F ratio on volume basis. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) Solution

**Given:** C = 0.85, H = 0.12, S = 0.02, Ash = 0.01 (mass basis), O₂ in fuel = 0.

**Stoichiometric O₂:**

$$O_2^{\text{req}} = \frac{32}{12}(0.85) + \frac{32}{4}(0.12) + \frac{32}{32}(0.02) = 2.267 + 0.960 + 0.020 = 3.247 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Stoichiometric Air-Fuel ratio (mass):**

$$\text{A/F}_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{3.247}{0.232} = 13.99 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Stoichiometric Air-Fuel ratio (mole):**
- O₂ moles = 3.247 / 32 = 0.1015 kg-mol O₂
- Air moles = 0.1015 × (100/21) = 0.4833 kg-mol air
- Mole A/F = 0.4833 / (1/approx MW_fuel) → for 1 kg basis: **4.833 kg-mol air / kg-mol fuel basis approximation** = **14.00 kg air/kg fuel** (numerically same as mass for unit basis).

**Excess air from flue gas (CO₂ = 12.5%, O₂ = 5.5%, N₂ = 82%):**

$$\% \text{ EA} = \frac{5.5}{(21/79)(82) - 5.5} \times 100 = \frac{5.5}{21.785 - 5.5} \times 100 = \frac{5.5}{16.285} \times 100 = 33.77\%$$

**Verification:** Actual A/F = 13.99 × 1.3377 = 18.71 kg air/kg fuel.

> **Marks Distribution:** Stoich O₂ [2], Stoich A/F [2], Excess air from FG [2], Comparison [1] = **7 marks**

#### Part (b) Solution

**Theoretical air** is the minimum air required for complete combustion with no unburnt fuel. **Excess air** is the air supplied beyond this theoretical minimum.

**For the gaseous fuel (per 100 moles):** CH₄ = 80, C₂H₆ = 10, CO = 5, N₂ = 5.

Stoichiometric O₂ required:
- CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O → 80 × 2 = 160 mol O₂
- C₂H₆ + 3.5O₂ → 2CO₂ + 3H₂O → 10 × 3.5 = 35 mol O₂
- CO + 0.5O₂ → CO₂ → 5 × 0.5 = 2.5 mol O₂
- N₂: 0 mol O₂

$$\text{Total O}_2^{\text{req}} = 160 + 35 + 2.5 = 197.5 \text{ mol O}_2$$

$$\text{Theoretical air} = 197.5 \times \frac{100}{21} = 940.48 \text{ mol air}$$

**Actual air with 20% excess:**

$$\text{Actual air} = 940.48 \times 1.20 = 1128.57 \text{ mol air}$$

**A/F ratio (volume basis, since gases have same molar volume):**

$$\boxed{\text{A/F}_{\text{volume}} = \frac{1128.57}{100} = 11.29 \text{ m}^3 \text{ air/m}^3 \text{ fuel}}$$

> **Marks Distribution:** Definitions + sketch [2], Stoich O₂ for gas [2], Theoretical air [1], Actual A/F [2] = **7 marks**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract fuel oxygen:** Many students compute O₂ as 8C/3 + 8H₂ + S without subtracting O₂ present in the fuel. This inflates the air requirement by 5–15%. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Using 0.21 instead of 0.232:** Air contains 21% O₂ by **volume** (mole basis) but **23.2% by mass**. Using 0.21 in mass-basis A/F calculations is a **classic error**. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Confusing "excess air" with "stoichiometric air":** The question may ask for actual air; don't stop at theoretical air. Always add the excess air step. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Moisture in fuel (H₂ includes combined moisture):** Ultimate analysis H₂ value is total hydrogen, NOT free moisture. Don't try to subtract moisture twice. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Orsat basis (dry gas):** Water vapour in flue gas is condensed and excluded in Orsat. The N₂ value in Orsat represents only dry N₂. Don't include H₂O moles in the dry gas balance. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Unit inconsistency:** O₂ in kg-mol vs kg — always check the units in the denominator (0.232 = mass fraction, 21% = volume fraction). Mixing them = **wrong answer, 2 marks lost.**
>
> 7. **Carbon balance neglect:** When back-calculating A/F from Orsat, students often forget to find the kg of fuel per 100 mol of dry FG. The fuel mass comes from the carbon balance (C content × kg of fuel). **Loss: 2 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ **Stoichiometric Air Formula (master this!):** 
  $$\text{Air}_{\text{theo}} = \frac{8C/3 + 8H_2 + S - O}{0.232} \text{ kg/kg fuel}$$

- ✅ **Two key constants to memorize:** Air MW = **29 kg/kg-mol**; O₂ mass fraction in air = **0.232**; Mole ratio air:O₂ = **4.76**

- ✅ **Excess Air formula (Orsat basis):** 
  $$\% \text{EA} = \frac{O_2^{FG}}{O_2^{consumed}} \times 100$$

- ✅ **Universal N₂/O₂ air ratio:** (21/79) = **0.2658** ≈ 0.268

- ✅ **Orsat reagents:** CO₂ → **KOH**; CO → **Cu₂Cl₂**; O₂ → **Pyrogallol**; N₂ → **by difference**

- ✅ **Combustion efficiency shortcut:** $\eta_c = \frac{\text{CO}_2}{\text{CO}_2 + \text{CO}} \times 100\%$

- ✅ **A/F ratio general formula:** $A/F_{actual} = A/F_{stoich} \times (1 + EA/100)$

- ✅ **Typical KTU numerical pattern:** "Given ultimate analysis, find theoretical air → Given Orsat, find % excess air → Verify both routes match."

- ✅ **Common fuel A/F values (memorize for MCQs):** Pure C = 11.44; Pure H₂ = 34.56; Methane CH₄ ≈ 17.16; Coal ≈ 10–13; Furnace oil ≈ 13–15; Natural gas ≈ 14–18.

- ✅ **Dry vs Wet basis:** Orsat gives **dry** basis. H₂O in flue gas is **NOT** measured. To convert to wet basis, you must add moles of H₂O formed from H₂ in fuel.

- ✅ **Molecular weight shortcuts:** CO₂ = 44; CO = 28; O₂ = 32; N₂ = 28; SO₂ = 64; H₂O = 18; air = 29

- ✅ **Excess air too high (>50%):** Wasted heat in flue gas, lower flame temperature.
- ✅ **Excess air too low (<5%):** Incomplete combustion, CO, smoke, soot, fuel wastage.
- ✅ **Optimal excess air:** 10–25% for solid fuels; 5–15% for liquid fuels; 0–5% for gaseous fuels (since they mix better).

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Calculation of the Composition of Fuel and Excess Air Supplied**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — Outcome-Based Education Framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flue Gas Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flue Gas Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FLUE GAS ANALYSIS (DEFINITION)**
> Flue gas analysis is the systematic determination of the volumetric (or molar) composition of the gaseous products of combustion leaving a furnace, boiler, or internal combustion engine. The standard dry flue gas components analyzed are **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), Carbon Monoxide (CO), Oxygen (O₂), and Nitrogen (N₂)**, expressed as **percentage by volume (% v/v)** on a dry basis. The analysis is typically performed using an **Orsat Apparatus** or modern electronic gas analyzers (based on infrared, paramagnetic, or electrochemical sensors).

The procedure quantifies:
1. **The extent of completion of combustion** (presence of CO indicates incomplete combustion).
2. **The amount of excess air supplied** (excess O₂ indicates wasted heat carrying air).
3. **The Air–Fuel Ratio actually employed** at the burner.
4. **The combustion efficiency** of the furnace/boiler.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **INTUITION (THE "FOOD COOKING" ANALOGY):**
> Imagine you are roasting a chicken in an oven:
> - **Just enough air (oxygen)** → the chicken roasts perfectly, smoke is thin and the kitchen smells of cooked meat (≈ **complete combustion**, no CO, low excess air).
> - **Too little air** → the chicken is charred on the outside, raw inside, and the kitchen fills with thick black smoke (≈ **incomplete combustion**, lots of CO and soot — wasted fuel).
> - **Too much air (oven door open)** → the chicken is dry and overcooked because the excess hot air escaped without doing work; the oven also wastes energy heating that extra air (≈ **high excess air**, high O₂ in flue gas, lower efficiency).
> **Flue gas analysis** is like taking a sample of the kitchen "exhaust air" and chemically testing it to figure out exactly how well the oven was set. The test results (CO₂%, CO%, O₂%, N₂%) tell the chef whether to open or close the damper next time.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molar volume of ideal gas at STP | Vₘ | **22.414** | L/mol |
| Molar volume at NTP (0°C, 1 atm) | Vₘ | **22.711** | L/mol |
| Molecular weight of dry air | Mₐᵢᵣ | **28.97** | kg/kmol |
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Reference temperature (KTU standard) | T₀ | **0 °C = 273.15 K** | K |
| Reference pressure | P₀ | **101.325 kPa = 1 atm** | kPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU BOARD HIGHLIGHT — DRY vs WET BASIS:**
> Flue gas from a fuel burning with H₂ always contains water vapor. The Orsat apparatus absorbs water first (or it is condensed out), so the reported % composition is on a **dry basis**. All stoichiometric equations in flue gas analysis use the dry basis: %CO₂ + %CO + %O₂ + %N₂ = **100 %**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Combustion Reaction — Foundation

Consider combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel CₓHᵧOᵤSᵥ with theoretical (stoichiometric) dry air. The complete-combustion equation gives:

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_u\text{S}_v + \left(x + \frac{y}{4} + v - \frac{u}{2}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + v\text{SO}_2$$

For **incomplete** combustion, carbon burns partially to CO:

$$\text{C} + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad \text{(instead of CO}_2\text{)}$$

The **dry flue gas** therefore contains: CO₂, CO, SO₂, O₂ (excess), N₂.

### 2.2 Stoichiometric Air Requirement — Per kg of Carbon

For 1 kg of **pure carbon** burnt:
- Theoretical O₂ required = (8/3) kg = **2.667 kg O₂/kg C**
- Theoretical air = 2.667 / 0.232 = **11.49 kg air/kg C** (since O₂ is 23.2 % of air by mass)
- Theoretical flue gas (dry) from 1 kg C = (11/3) kg = **3.667 kg dry FG/kg C**

### 2.3 Key Combustion Performance Indicators

1. **Excess Air (EA)** — the percentage of theoretical air supplied in excess.
$$\text{EA \%} = \frac{\text{Actual Air} - \text{Theoretical Air}}{\text{Theoretical Air}} \times 100$$

2. **Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR)** — kg of dry air per kg of fuel.
3. **Equivalence Ratio (Φ)** — actual fuel–air ratio ÷ stoichiometric fuel–air ratio.
4. **Combustion Efficiency (ηc)** — fraction of fuel carbon converted to CO₂.
$$\eta_c = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]} \times 100 \;\;\text{(\%)}$$

5. **Dry Gas Loss (q₂)** — heat carried away by dry stack gas:
$$q_2 = \frac{m_g \, C_{pg} \, (T_g - T_a)}{GCV} \times 100 \;\;(\%)$$

### 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CHEAT SHEET)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD: MEMORIZE THIS TABLE VERBATIM.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical O₂ per kg C | O₂ₜₕ = 8/3 | kg/kg C | Fixed constant |
| 2 | Theoretical air per kg C | Aₜₕ = (8/3)/0.232 = 11.49 | kg/kg C | 23.2 % O₂ by mass |
| 3 | Dry flue gas per kg C | G = 11/3 = 3.667 | kg/kg C | CO₂ + N₂ only |
| 4 | N₂ per kg C (theoretical) | N₂ₜₕ = 8.773 | kg/kg C | From 79/21 ratio |
| 5 | % Excess Air (from O₂ in FG) | EA = (O₂ × 100) / (0.264 N₂ − O₂) | % | Standard KTU formula |
| 6 | % Excess Air (from CO₂ max) | EA = (CO₂ₘₐₓ − CO₂ₒᵦₛ)/(CO₂ₒᵦₛ) × 100 | % | Used when O₂ unmeasured |
| 7 | Actual O₂ in flue gas | O₂ₐ = 2.667 × (1 + EA/100) − (11/3)(CO₂+CO) | kg | Mass balance |
| 8 | Stoichiometric A/F | (A/F)ₛ = 14.4 (carbon), 14.7 (octane) | kg/kg | For hydrocarbons |
| 9 | % Theoretical Air | %TA = 100 + EA% | % | Used in boiler trials |
| 10 | Combustion efficiency | ηc = CO₂/(CO₂ + CO) × 100 | % | On dry basis |
| 11 | Stack gas mass | Gs = 11 CO₂ + 8 O₂ + 7(CO+N₂)/100 | kg/kg fuel | Siegert's rule |
| 12 | Dry flue gas ratio | CO₂ + CO = C/(1 + EA/100) × const | – | Carbon-balance trick |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:**
> In all KTU derivations, square brackets **[ ]** indicate percentage by volume on dry basis. Curly braces **{ }** indicate percentage by mass. Never interchange these in exam answers.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Flue Gas Analysis

1. **Boiler Tuning** — operators adjust dampers to maintain 10–15 % excess air for oil-fired and 15–25 % for coal-fired boilers.
2. **Emissions Compliance** — monitors ensure SO₂, NOₓ, CO do not exceed pollution-control norms.
3. **Heat Recovery** — Economizers and air-preheaters are sized from the flue gas mass flow.
4. **Process Control** — Continuous analyzers (Zirconia O₂ probes, NDIR CO/CO₂ sensors) feed back to the BMS (Burner Management System).
5. **Forensic / Troubleshooting** — High CO + low O₂ ⇒ clogged burner; high O₂ + low CO₂ ⇒ excessive air leakage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: % Excess Air from Orsat (Dry) Flue Gas Composition

**GIVEN:** Dry flue gas contains [CO₂], [CO], [O₂], [N₂] all in % v/v, with [CO₂] + [CO] + [O₂] + [N₂] = 100 %.

**Let**:
- C = kmol of carbon burnt per kg of fuel
- H = kmol of H₂ burnt per kg of fuel
- A = kmol of dry air actually supplied per kg of fuel

**STEP 1 — Write dry flue gas on a basis of 100 kmol dry flue gas (the KTU standard trick):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_2 &: \; 100 \cdot \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{100} = [\text{CO}_2] \text{ kmol} \\
\text{CO}   &: \; [\text{CO}] \text{ kmol} \\
\text{O}_2  &: \; [\text{O}_2] \text{ kmol} \\
\text{N}_2  &: \; [\text{N}_2] \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}$$

**STEP 2 — Apply CARBON BALANCE (carbon in = carbon out):**

Carbon entering the furnace is from the fuel. Carbon leaving is in CO₂ and CO only. By Avogadro's law, 1 kmol CO₂ contains 1 kmol C; 1 kmol CO contains 1 kmol C.

$$C_{\text{fuel}} \;(\text{kmol}) = [\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]$$

**STEP 3 — Apply NITROGEN BALANCE (inert gas tracer):**

N₂ in air = 79/21 × O₂ supplied (molar ratio of air). Since N₂ does not react:

$$\text{N}_2 \text{ in flue gas} = \frac{79}{21} \times \text{O}_2 \text{ supplied (kmol)}$$

The oxygen supplied splits into: oxygen in dry flue gas + oxygen in CO₂ + oxygen in CO + oxygen in H₂O. On a dry basis we exclude H₂O:

$$[\text{N}_2] = \frac{79}{21}\Big( [\text{O}_2] + [\text{CO}_2] + \tfrac{1}{2}[\text{CO}] \Big)$$

**STEP 4 — Solve for the actual O₂ supplied:**

$$\begin{aligned}
21\,[\text{N}_2] &= 79\,[\text{O}_2] + 79\,[\text{CO}_2] + \tfrac{79}{2}[\text{CO}] \\[4pt]
79\,[\text{O}_2] &= 21\,[\text{N}_2] - 79\,[\text{CO}_2] - \tfrac{79}{2}[\text{CO}] \\[4pt]
[\text{O}_2]_{\text{actual}} &= \frac{21\,[\text{N}_2] - 79\,[\text{CO}_2] - 39.5\,[\text{CO}]}{79}
\end{aligned}$$

**STEP 5 — Theoretical O₂ required (no excess, no incompleteness):**

If all C → CO₂, theoretical O₂ needed = [CO₂] + ½[CO].

$$[\text{O}_2]_{\text{theoretical}} = [\text{CO}_2] + \tfrac{1}{2}[\text{CO}]$$

**STEP 6 — Define % Excess Air:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{EA} &= \frac{[\text{O}_2]_{\text{actual}} - [\text{O}_2]_{\text{theoretical}}}{[\text{O}_2]_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \left(\frac{[\text{O}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + 0.5[\text{CO}]}\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}$$
