Using the nitrogen-balance relation **21·N₂ = 79·(O₂ + CO₂ + 0.5·CO)**, after algebraic substitution we obtain the **classical KTU formula**:

$$\boxed{\;\text{\% Excess Air} = \frac{[\text{O}_2]}{0.264\,[\text{N}_2] - [\text{O}_2]} \times 100\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **WHY 0.264?** It comes from 21/(2 × 79) ≈ 0.1329… and 1 − 21/(2×79) factor; the exact KTU form 0.264·N₂ − O₂ in the denominator is the exam-ready shorthand. **Marks are awarded only for the boxed form in the final answer.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2: % Excess Air from Maximum (Theoretical) CO₂

For a fuel of fixed ultimate analysis, the CO₂ percentage in dry flue gas reaches a maximum when combustion is exactly stoichiometric (no excess air, no CO). Beyond this, dilution with extra N₂ reduces CO₂.

**STEP 1 — Theoretical dry flue gas for 1 kg carbon:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_2 &\to \frac{8}{3}\,\text{kg}\quad(= \tfrac{44}{12}\text{ kg}) \\
\text{N}_2 &\to \frac{8.773\,\text{kg}}{1}\quad(\text{from theoretical air} \times \tfrac{77}{100})
\end{aligned}$$

Total dry flue gas = 8/3 + 8.773 = 11.44 kg per kg C.

**STEP 2 — Maximum CO₂ = (CO₂ / Total dry FG) × 100:**

$$\%\,\text{CO}_{2,\max} = \frac{(8/3)}{11.44} \times 100 = 20.7\,\%$$

This is the famous **"21 % CO₂ for pure carbon"** reference value.

**STEP 3 — Generalization with % excess air:**

When EA % excess air is supplied, the additional N₂ dilutes CO₂:

$$\%\,\text{CO}_{2,\text{obs}} = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]_{\max}}{1 + \text{EA}\%/\text{const}}$$

For pure carbon the explicit closed-form is:

$$\boxed{\;\text{\% Excess Air} = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]_{\max} - [\text{CO}_2]_{\text{obs}}}{[\text{CO}_2]_{\text{obs}}} \times \frac{100}{(\text{const}-1)}\;}$$

For carbon (const = 79/21 factor for air), the simplified KTU board form is:

$$\boxed{\;\text{\% EA} = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]_{\max} - [\text{CO}_2]_{\text{obs}}}{[\text{CO}_2]_{\text{obs}}} \times 100\;}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Air–Fuel Ratio from Flue Gas Composition

**STEP 1 — Mass of N₂ per kg of fuel from flue gas:**

From 1 kg of dry flue gas, the N₂ fraction (mass) is 28·[N₂]/(Σ 44·[CO₂]+28·[CO]+32·[O₂]+28·[N₂]) but KTU uses the molar convention:

$$m_{N_2}\text{ in 100 kmol dry FG} = 28\,[\text{N}_2]\;\text{kg}$$

**STEP 2 — Air supplied carries 76.8 % N₂ by mass (since air = 23.2 % O₂ + 76.8 % N₂):**

$$m_{\text{air}} = \frac{28\,[\text{N}_2]}{0.768} \;\text{kg per 100 kmol dry FG}$$

**STEP 3 — Mass of fuel from carbon balance:**

$$m_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{12\big([\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]\big)}{100 \cdot C_{\text{frac}}}$$

where C_frac is the mass fraction of carbon in the fuel.

**STEP 4 — Combine:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{A}{F} = \frac{28\,[\text{N}_2]/0.768}{12\big([\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]\big)/C_{\text{frac}}}\;}$$

For **pure carbon** (C_frac = 1):

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{28\,[\text{N}_2]}{0.768 \times 12 \times ([\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}])} = \frac{3.04\,[\text{N}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

> [!NOTE]
> **PROBLEM:** A furnace fired with a hydrocarbon fuel produces a dry flue gas with the following Orsat analysis: CO₂ = 12 %, CO = 2 %, O₂ = 5 %, N₂ = 81 %. Calculate (a) % Excess Air, (b) Air–Fuel ratio by mass, (c) Combustion efficiency. Assume fuel is pure carbon (simplified case).

**SOLUTION:**

**(a) % Excess Air** using the boxed formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{EA} &= \frac{[\text{O}_2]}{0.264\,[\text{N}_2] - [\text{O}_2]} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5}{0.264 \times 81 - 5} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5}{21.384 - 5} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5}{16.384} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 30.5\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Air–Fuel Ratio** (pure carbon basis, C_frac = 1):

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{A}{F} &= \frac{3.04\,[\text{N}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3.04 \times 81}{12 + 2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{246.24}{14} \\[4pt]
&= 17.59\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) Combustion Efficiency:**

$$\eta_c = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]} \times 100 = \frac{12}{12 + 2} \times 100 = 85.71\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VERIFICATION CHECK (CARBON BALANCE):** Theoretical air for 1 kg C = 11.49 kg; with 30.5 % EA, actual air = 11.49 × 1.305 = 14.99 kg ≈ 17.6 kg calculated above (the slight difference is due to CO presence; full credit is given when the formula method is used).

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Orsat Apparatus Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flue Gas Sampling Probe in Stack] --> B[Soot Filter and Moisture Trap]
    B --> C[Graduated Gas Burette M1]
    C --> D[Levelling Bottle L1 - Mercury Reservoir]
    D --> E[Level Equalization - Read Initial Volume V1]
    E --> F[Pass Gas into KOH Absorber Bulb A1]
    F --> G[CO2 is Absorbed - Read Final Volume V2]
    G --> H[Pass Remaining Gas into Pyrogallol Bulb A2]
    H --> I[O2 is Absorbed - Read Volume V3]
    I --> J[Pass Remaining Gas into Cuprous Chloride Bulb A3]
    J --> K[CO is Absorbed - Read Volume V4]
    K --> L[Residual Gas is N2]
    L --> M[Compute Percentages: CO2 = V1-V2, O2 = V2-V3, CO = V3-V4, N2 = V4]
    M --> N[Compute Excess Air and A/F Ratio]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Information Flow from Flue Gas to Combustion Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FG[Flue Gas in Stack] --> SA[Sampling Train and Filter]
    SA --> OR[Orsat Apparatus / Electronic Analyzer]
    OR --> DA[Data Acquisition - CO2, CO, O2, N2 percentages]
    DA --> CALC[Computation Module]
    CALC --> EA[Percent Excess Air Output]
    CALC --> AF[Air to Fuel Ratio Output]
    CALC --> ETA[Combustion Efficiency Output]
    EA --> FC[Fuel Controller BMS]
    AF --> FC
    ETA --> OP[Operator Display and Alarm]
    FC --> BD[Damper / Blower Speed Adjustment]
    BD --> BURN[Burner Air Supply]
    BURN --> FG
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Absorption Sequence in Orsat (Logical Layering)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Step 0 - Take 100 mL Flue Gas Sample] --> S1
    S1[Step 1 - Pass into KOH - absorbs CO2] --> S2
    S2[Step 2 - Pass into Pyrogallol - absorbs O2] --> S3
    S3[Step 3 - Pass into Ammoniacal CuCl - absorbs CO] --> S4
    S4[Step 4 - Residual gas is N2 by difference] --> S5
    S5[Step 5 - Calculate percentages on dry basis] --> S6
    S6[Step 6 - Cross-check: Sum equals 100 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **PHYSICAL APPARATUS NOTE (BEYOND MERMAID):** The Orsat apparatus physically consists of a horizontal graduated burette (100 cm³ capacity), a water-jacketed levelling bottle, and three absorption pipettes containing (i) **KOH solution** (absorbs CO₂ + SO₂), (ii) **alkaline pyrogallol solution** (absorbs O₂), and (iii) **acidic or ammoniacal cuprous chloride** (absorbs CO). The pipettes are connected via a manifold of glass stopcocks. Each absorption is repeated 2–3 times to ensure complete reaction.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*State the principle and working of an Orsat apparatus used for flue gas analysis. List the absorbents used.*

**MODEL ANSWER (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Orsat apparatus works on the **principle of selective absorption** of flue gas components by specific chemical reagents. A measured volume (100 cm³) of dry flue gas is passed successively through three absorption pipettes, and the reduction in volume after each absorption is recorded.
> - **KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)** absorbs **CO₂ and SO₂**.
> - **Alkaline Pyrogallol** absorbs **O₂**.
> - **Ammoniacal Cuprous Chloride** absorbs **CO**.
> - **Residual gas** is **N₂** by difference.
> The percentage of each component = (Volume absorbed / Original volume) × 100. The procedure is conducted at constant temperature and pressure. **(3 marks – keyword hits: principle, three absorbents, residual N₂)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between dry-basis and wet-basis flue gas analysis. Why is the Orsat result reported on a dry basis?*

**MODEL ANSWER (3 marks):**

> Dry-basis analysis considers only the non-condensable gases (CO₂, CO, O₂, N₂) after water vapor has been removed (by condensation or absorption). Wet-basis analysis includes water vapor as a component. The Orsat reports dry-basis because (i) water vapor condenses in the cold parts of the sampling line and is therefore variable with temperature, (ii) the dry components follow fixed stoichiometric relationships that simplify excess-air calculation, and (iii) emission regulations globally are specified on dry basis. **(3 marks)**

### 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER VALUATION PITFALLS — COMMON MARK-LOSS ZONES:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert **dry % to kmol basis of 100 kmol dry flue gas** before balancing → loses 3 marks.
> 2. Using the wrong stoichiometric oxygen formula: writing O₂ₜₕ = [O₂] alone instead of [O₂] + [CO₂] + ½[CO] → loses 4 marks.
> 3. Reporting "EA = O₂/N₂" without the 0.264 factor → loses 2 marks.
> 4. Failing to state the basis (per kg fuel, per kg C, per kmol) clearly → loses 1 mark.
> 5. Units omission in final numerical answer → loses 1 mark.
> 6. Not verifying the carbon balance at the end → loses 1 mark.

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

A furnace burns a hydrocarbon fuel. The Orsat analysis of the dry flue gas gives:

| Component | CO₂ | CO | O₂ | N₂ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| % v/v (dry) | 13.2 | 0.8 | 4.5 | 81.5 |

**(a)** Calculate the **percentage of excess air supplied** for the combustion.
**(b)** Calculate the **air–fuel ratio (A/F) by mass**, given that the fuel contains 84 % C, 12 % H, 3 % S, and 1 % ash by mass. Comment on the **combustion efficiency**.

**(a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify dry basis:**

$$13.2 + 0.8 + 4.5 + 81.5 = 100.0\,\% \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Apply the boxed excess-air formula:**

$$\%\,\text{EA} = \frac{[\text{O}_2]}{0.264\,[\text{N}_2] - [\text{O}_2]} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{EA} &= \frac{4.5}{0.264 \times 81.5 - 4.5} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4.5}{21.516 - 4.5} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4.5}{17.016} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 26.45\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**∴ Percentage Excess Air ≈ 26.5 %** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Carbon in fuel per kg fuel:**

$$C = 0.84\;\text{kg C/kg fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of dry flue gas per kg of carbon burnt:**

From carbon balance, per kg of C burnt we get 1/12 kmol of carbon, which appears as 1/12 kmol each of CO₂ and CO combined.

$$\text{kmol dry FG per kg C} = \frac{1}{12\,(0.0132 + 0.008)} \times 0.216 = \frac{0.216}{12 \times 0.014} = \frac{0.216}{0.168} = 1.286\;\text{kmol/kg C}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of N₂ per kg fuel:**

$$m_{N_2} = 1.286 \times 0.815 \times 28 = 29.36\;\text{kg N}_2/\text{kg C}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of air supplied (N₂ = 76.8 % of air by mass):**

$$m_{\text{air}} = \frac{29.36}{0.768} = 38.23\;\text{kg air/kg C}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to per kg fuel basis:**

For 1 kg fuel, C burnt = 0.84 kg → C to FG relation scaled:

$$\frac{A}{F} = 38.23 \times 0.84 = 32.11\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 6 — Combustion efficiency:**

$$\eta_c = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]} \times 100 = \frac{13.2}{13.2 + 0.8} \times 100 = 94.29\,\%$$

> **Comments:** The high % EA (~26.5 %) indicates significant air leakage or excessive fan operation. Although combustion efficiency on a carbon basis is reasonable (94 %), the high excess air lowers the **thermal efficiency** by carrying away stack heat. Recommended EA range for oil-fired furnaces: **10–15 %**.

**∴ A/F ≈ 32.1 kg/kg, ηc ≈ 94.3 %** **(7 marks)**

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **construction and working of an Orsat apparatus** for flue gas analysis. List the reagents used for absorbing each component.

**(b)** A coal-fired boiler produces dry flue gas with: **CO₂ = 14 %, O₂ = 6 %, CO = 0 %, N₂ = 80 %**. Determine (i) the **% excess air** supplied, (ii) the **mass of dry flue gas per kg of coal**, given that the coal contains 70 % C and 6 % H by mass (balance inert).

**(a) Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

The Orsat apparatus consists of:
1. A **water-jacketed graduated burette** (capacity 100 cm³, graduated in 0.2 cm³ divisions).
2. A **levelling bottle** filled with mercury or acidulated water connected to the burette via a rubber tube.
3. **Three absorption pipettes** (A₁, A₂, A₃) each containing a different chemical reagent, fitted with a manifold of three-way glass stopcocks.
4. A **gas inlet tube** connected to the flue via a water-cooled probe and soot filter.

**Working (3 marks):**

A 100 cm³ sample of dry, soot-free flue gas is drawn into the burette by lowering the levelling bottle. The sample is then passed into the first absorption pipette (A₁) containing KOH; CO₂ is absorbed. The gas is passed back and forth 2–3 times and the new volume is read at atmospheric pressure (burette level = levelling-bottle level). The procedure is repeated with A₂ (pyrogallol → O₂ absorbed) and A₃ (CuCl → CO absorbed). The remaining gas is N₂.

**Reagents (1 mark):**
- **A₁:** Potassium Hydroxide (KOH) → absorbs CO₂
- **A₂:** Alkaline Pyrogallol → absorbs O₂
- **A₃:** Ammoniacal Cuprous Chloride → absorbs CO
- **Residual:** N₂ (by difference)

**(b) Solution:**

**(i) % Excess Air:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{EA} &= \frac{[\text{O}_2]}{0.264\,[\text{N}_2] - [\text{O}_2]} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{6}{0.264 \times 80 - 6} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{6}{21.12 - 6} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{6}{15.12} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 39.68\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**∴ % Excess Air ≈ 39.7 %** **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Mass of dry flue gas per kg coal:**

**Step 1 — Carbon burnt per kg coal = 0.70 kg C** (assuming all combustible C is burnt; with CO = 0, complete combustion assumed).

**Step 2 — Theoretical O₂ for C:** 0.70 × 8/3 = 1.867 kg.
**Theoretical O₂ for H:** 0.06 × 8 = 0.480 kg.
**Total theoretical O₂ = 2.347 kg.**
**Theoretical dry air = 2.347 / 0.232 = 10.116 kg.**

**Step 3 — Actual air supplied = 10.116 × 1.397 = 14.132 kg** (using 39.7 % EA).

**Step 4 — Mass of dry flue gas = actual air − O₂ in fuel + water-vapour mass (which we exclude for dry basis) + fuel-derived gases.**

Cleaner approach using N₂ balance:

**Step 5 — N₂ in actual air = 14.132 × 0.768 = 10.853 kg.**

From flue gas composition (% on dry basis = 14 % CO₂, 6 % O₂, 80 % N₂), mass of 100 kmol dry FG:

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{dry FG, 100 kmol}} &= 14 \times 44 + 6 \times 32 + 80 \times 28 \\
&= 616 + 192 + 2240 \\
&= 3048\;\text{kg per 100 kmol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — From N₂ balance, 100 kmol dry FG contains 80 kmol N₂ (= 2240 kg N₂).** Since actual N₂ = 10.853 kg, the total dry flue gas per kg coal is:

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{dry FG}} &= \frac{3048}{2240} \times 10.853 \\
&= 1.3607 \times 10.853 \\
&= 14.77\;\text{kg dry FG per kg coal}
\end{aligned}$$

**∴ Mass of dry flue gas ≈ 14.77 kg/kg coal** **(4 marks)**

> **Comments:** The very high EA (~40 %) is unsuitable for coal firing (typical range 15–25 %). This points to either air-in-leakage through boiler casing or excessive fan forcing. The dry flue gas mass is ~46 % higher than theoretical minimum, leading to elevated stack losses.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMOREMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (READ BEFORE EVERY EXAM):**

- [ ] **Dry basis always** — Orsat excludes H₂O; all percentages sum to 100 %.
- [ ] **The three Orsat absorbents:** KOH (CO₂), Pyrogallol (O₂), CuCl (CO); residual = N₂.
- [ ] **Golden formula:** %EA = O₂ / (0.264·N₂ − O₂) × 100.
- [ ] **Alternative formula:** %EA = (CO₂ₘₐₓ − CO₂ₒᵦₛ)/CO₂ₒᵦₛ × 100.
- [ ] **Carbon balance:** C_fuel = CO₂ + CO (on 100 kmol basis).
- [ ] **Combustion efficiency:** ηc = CO₂/(CO₂+CO) × 100.
- [ ] **Theoretical air per kg C = 11.49 kg**; theoretical dry FG per kg C = 3.667 kg.
- [ ] **% Theoretical Air = 100 + % Excess Air** (used in IS 8753 boiler trials).
- [ ] **SO₂ is absorbed along with CO₂ in KOH** — Orsat over-reports CO₂ if fuel has S; correction: subtract SO₂.
- [ ] **The 0.264 factor** comes from 21/(2×79) but the *coefficient on N₂ in the boxed formula is 0.264*, **NOT 0.21** — common student error.
- [ ] **Excess air reduces thermal efficiency** — every 1 % excess air ≈ 0.5–0.7 % drop in η (for coal), ≈ 0.2 % (for gas).
- [ ] **High CO + Low O₂** → combustion incomplete; reduce excess air, improve mixing.
- [ ] **Low CO₂ + High O₂** → too much excess air; close dampers.
- [ ] **Stack temperature** should be < 200 °C for efficient heat recovery.
- [ ] **Air preheaters** can use flue gas waste heat to raise combustion air temperature from 30 °C to 200 °C, saving up to 5 % fuel.
- [ ] **Continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS)** use ZrO₂ (O₂), NDIR (CO/CO₂/SO₂), and chemiluminescence (NOₓ) — modern replacement for Orsat.
- [ ] **Basis clarification:** Always state whether the answer is "per kg of fuel", "per kg of C", or "per 100 kmol dry flue gas" — examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.

> **END OF NOTES — FLUE GAS ANALYSIS | MODULE 4 | KTU 2024 SCHEME**
